## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: MEMS Scaling Law Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sc[Scale Down by Factor S] --> sc1[Length: S to power minus 1]
    sc --> sc2[Area: S to power minus 2]
    sc --> sc3[Volume and Mass: S to power minus 3]
    sc --> sc4[Gravity Force: S to power minus 3 decreases]
    sc --> sc5[Electrostatic Force: S to power 0 constant]
    sc --> sc6[Surface to Volume Ratio: S to power minus 1 increases]
    sc4 --> dom[Volume Forces Lose Dominance]
    sc5 --> dom2[Surface Forces Gain Dominance]
    sc6 --> dom2
    dom2 --> out[Microscale Physics Dominated by Electrostatic, Viscous, Surface Tension, and Capillary Forces]
    style sc fill:#FFF4E5
    style out fill:#E6F4EA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a process step or domain transformation. The arrows indicate the unidirectional flow of information, energy, or fabrication sequence. The colored nodes (light red, blue, green, orange) help differentiate between input/process/output stages.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper Set A]

**Q: Define MEMS. List any four examples of MEMS devices with their applications.**

**Model Answer:**

MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) are integrated systems that combine mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronic circuits on a common silicon substrate using microfabrication technology. The feature size typically ranges from **1 µm to 1 mm**.

Four examples:
1. **Accelerometer** – Used in smartphones for screen rotation and in automotive airbag deployment systems.
2. **Gyroscope** – Used in drones and gaming consoles for angular velocity sensing.
3. **Pressure Sensor** – Used in biomedical blood pressure monitoring and tire pressure monitoring systems.
4. **RF MEMS Switch** – Used in wireless communication for low-loss signal routing.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Explain the significance of scaling laws in MEMS. Why do surface forces dominate at the microscale?**

**Model Answer:**

Scaling laws describe how physical quantities (length, force, energy, capacitance, etc.) change when a system is miniaturized by a factor S. They are significant because they predict microscale behavior, guide design choices, and identify dominant physical phenomena.

**Key reason surface forces dominate:**
When dimensions shrink by factor S, **volume forces** (like gravity, inertia) scale as **S³** (rapidly decrease), while **surface forces** (electrostatic, surface tension, viscous drag) scale as **S²** or even **S⁰** (electrostatic). Therefore, the **surface-area-to-volume ratio** increases as **1/S**, making surface effects dominant.

**Example:** Electrostatic force is scale-invariant (S⁰), while gravitational force scales as S³. At S = 1000, gravity becomes negligible compared to electrostatics.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Module in End Semester Examination (ESE) provides internal choice between two questions of 14 marks. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, ESE Module 1]

**Q: (a)** With neat sketches, explain the working principle of a MEMS capacitive accelerometer. Derive the expression for displacement sensitivity.

**Q: (b)** Discuss the various materials used in MEMS fabrication. Why is silicon the most preferred material? List six reasons.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Working Principle:**
A MEMS capacitive accelerometer consists of a **proof mass (M)** suspended by **four flexible beams** that act as springs. The mass is connected to a series of **fixed and movable comb fingers** that form differential capacitors C₁ and C₂.

**Operating Mechanism:**
- When the device experiences acceleration **a** along the sensitive axis, the proof mass experiences an inertial force **F = Ma**.
- This force causes the mass to displace by **x** relative to the anchor.
- The displacement changes the gap on one side of the comb fingers (C₁ increases) and decreases on the other side (C₂ decreases).
- This differential capacitance change is measured to determine acceleration.

**Derivation:**

Initial capacitance of each comb finger pair:

$$C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

where A = overlap area, d = gap.

After displacement x:

$$C_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d + x} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\cdot\dfrac{1}{1+x/d} \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left(1 - \dfrac{x}{d}\right)$$

$$C_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - x} \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left(1 + \dfrac{x}{d}\right)$$

Differential output:

$$\Delta C = C_2 - C_1 = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 A x}{d^2}$$

**Displacement Sensitivity:**

$$S = \dfrac{\Delta C}{x} = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}$$

For the proof mass under acceleration: $x = \dfrac{Ma}{k}$ where k is the spring constant of the suspension beam.

**Acceleration sensitivity:**

$$S_a = \dfrac{\Delta C}{a} = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 A M}{k d^2}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Materials used in MEMS:**

| Category | Materials |
|----------|-----------|
| Semiconductors | Si, Ge, GaAs, SiC, SiGe |
| Insulators | SiO₂, Si₃N₄, glass, ceramics |
| Metals | Al, Au, Cu, Pt, Ti, W |
| Polymers | SU-8, PDMS, PMMA, polyimide |
| Piezoelectric | PZT, ZnO, AlN, BaTiO₃ |

**Six reasons silicon dominates MEMS:**

1. **Excellent mechanical properties:** Young's modulus **E = 169 GPa**, yield strength ≈ 7 GPa, near-zero mechanical hysteresis and fatigue.
2. **Low density:** ρ = **2330 kg/m³**, making structures lightweight.
3. **IC process compatibility:** Can be processed using the same photolithography, etching, and doping tools as the integrated circuit industry.
4. **Piezo-resistive effect:** Piezoresistors can be easily integrated for strain sensing.
5. **Thermal matching:** Coefficient of thermal expansion matches SiO₂, enabling multilayer structures.
6. **Abundant and economical:** Silicon is the second most abundant element in Earth's crust, making it cheap.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE Module 1]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the fundamental resonant frequency of a cantilever beam used in MEMS. A silicon cantilever has length 400 µm, width 50 µm, and thickness 2 µm. Calculate the resonant frequency. (Given: E = 169 GPa, ρ = 2330 kg/m³)

**Q: (b)** Explain the concept of energy domains in MEMS with a block diagram. Discuss the various transduction mechanisms used.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Derivation (as per Section 3.1):**

The fundamental resonant frequency of a cantilever beam is:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{t}{L^2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: L = 400 µm = 400×10⁻⁶ m, w = 50 µm, t = 2 µm = 2×10⁻⁶ m, E = 169×10⁹ Pa, ρ = 2330 kg/m³.

Step 1: Compute I/A = t²/12 = (2×10⁻⁶)²/12 = 3.333×10⁻¹³ m²

Step 2: Compute E/(12ρ) = 169×10⁹ / (12 × 2330) = 6.043×10⁶ m²/s²

Step 3: √(E/(12ρ)) = √(6.043×10⁶) = 2458.4 m/s

Step 4: 1.875²/(2π) = 3.5156/6.2832 = 0.5596

Step 5: t/L² = 2×10⁻⁶ / (400×10⁻⁶)² = 2×10⁻⁶ / 1.6×10⁻⁷ = 12.5 m⁻¹

Step 6: f₀ = 0.5596 × 12.5 × 2458.4 = **17,196 Hz ≈ 17.2 kHz**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Energy Domains in MEMS (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):**

MEMS devices must interface between the electrical domain (where signals are processed) and non-electrical physical domains (mechanical, thermal, fluidic, optical, magnetic, chemical).

**Block Diagram of Energy Domain Mapping:**

```
External Physical Stimulus (Mechanical/Thermal/Chemical/Optical/Magnetic)
              ↓
Sensing Mechanism (Transducer)
              ↓
Electrical Signal
              ↓
Signal Processing Circuit (ASIC)
              ↓
Output Display or Actuator Action
```

**Various Transduction Mechanisms:**

| # | Transduction Principle | Physical Law | Example |
|---|------------------------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Piezo-resistive** | ΔR/R = π·σ | Piezoresistor in pressure sensor |
| 2 | **Capacitive** | C = ε₀A/d | Accelerometer, gyroscope |
| 3 | **Piezoelectric** | Charge ∝ stress | Acoustic sensors, energy harvesters |
| 4 | **Thermal** | Resistance changes with T | Flow sensor, bolometer |
| 5 | **Resonant** | Frequency shift with mass | Mass sensor, chemical sensor |
| 6 | **Optical** | Interference/reflection | MOEMS, optical switches |
| 7 | **Magnetic** | Lorentz force, Hall effect | Magnetometer, current sensor |
| 8 | **Tunnelling** | Current ∝ exp(-d) | Scanning probe, ultra-sensitive accelerometer |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Formula Error (–2 marks):** Using the wrong constant for cantilever mode shape. Always use **β₁L = 1.875** for the fundamental mode. Higher modes are 4.694, 7.855.
> 2. **Unit Mistake (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert µm to m before substituting. ALWAYS convert all dimensions to SI units (meters) before calculation.
> 3. **Spring constant formula (–3 marks):** Using beam bending formula $k = \dfrac{Ewt^3}{4L^3}$ for cantilevers but mixing it up with fixed-fixed beam formula $k = \dfrac{Ewt^3}{L^3}$. The cantilever factor is 1/4; fixed-fixed is 16, etc.
> 4. **Confusing electrostatic force expressions (–1 mark):** Forgetting the factor ½ in $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2d^2}$. The ½ comes from the energy expression.
> 5. **Incomplete energy domain list (–1 mark):** Listing only "mechanical" and "electrical" without naming all six+ domains.
> 6. **No mention of scaling laws (–2 marks):** In "Why MEMS?" type questions, students forget to mention that surface forces dominate at the microscale — a key KTU expected point.
> 7. **Diagrams missing (–2 marks):** In 14-mark questions, a neat labeled diagram is mandatory. Sketches without labels or arrows lose partial marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: MEMS Devices and Structures**

### 🔹 Core Concepts to Memorize
- MEMS = Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (size: 1 µm to 1 mm)
- Six+ energy domains: Electrical, Mechanical, Thermal, Fluidic, Optical, Magnetic, Chemical
- Three functional categories: Sensors, Actuators, Passive Structures
- Silicon is the dominant MEMS material (E = 169 GPa, ρ = 2330 kg/m³)

### 🔹 Critical Scaling Laws
- Length: S⁻¹, Area: S⁻², Volume: S⁻³
- Mass and Gravity force: S⁻³
- Electrostatic force: **S⁰ (constant)** ← why electrostatics dominates MEMS
- Resonant frequency: S⁻¹
- Power (electrical): S⁻³

### 🔹 Key Equations to Remember
- Resonant frequency: $f_0 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\cdot\dfrac{t}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}$
- Spring constant (cantilever): $k = \dfrac{Ewt^3}{4L^3}$
- Electrostatic force: $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2d^2}$
- Capacitive sensitivity: $S = \dfrac{\Delta C}{x} = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}$

### 🔹 Material Properties to Memorize
- Silicon: E = 169 GPa, ν = 0.28, ρ = 2330 kg/m³
- Vacuum permittivity: ε₀ = 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m
- Air viscosity: μ ≈ 1.8×10⁻⁵ Pa·s

### 🔹 Transduction Mechanisms (Must Know All 6+)
- Piezo-resistive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Thermal, Resonant, Optical, Magnetic, Tunnelling

### 🔹 Why MEMS? (3-Second Answer)
- Small size, low weight, low power, batch-fabrication low cost, IC-compatible, surface forces dominate

### 🔹 Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- Mixing up cantilever and fixed-fixed beam formulas
- Forgetting unit conversions (µm → m)
- Forgetting the ½ factor in electrostatic force equation
- Skipping diagrams in 14-mark questions
- Confusing sensor vs actuator definitions
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials for MEMS: Silicon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Materials for MEMS: Silicon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Silicon as a MEMS Material

**Silicon (Si)** is a Group IV element with atomic number **14** and atomic mass **28.0855 g/mol**. It is the foundational semiconductor material of modern microelectronics and, by extension, the **primary structural and functional material of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, silicon is studied as a *dual-purpose engineering material* — one that simultaneously offers **semiconducting electronic behaviour** and **excellent mechanical/structural stability** at the microscale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Silicon dominates MEMS not because it is the strongest or lightest material, but because it is the *only* material for which an entire, ultra-mature, batch-fabrication infrastructure (photolithography, oxidation, etching, doping, CVD) already exists — inherited from the trillion-dollar CMOS industry.

### Formal Academic Definition

> **Silicon in MEMS:** A covalently bonded, single-crystalline semiconductor (diamond cubic lattice, lattice constant **a = 5.4307 Å**) that serves as the principal substrate, structural layer, piezoresistive sensing element, and functional transducer material in microfabricated sensors and actuators.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Lego with Electronics" Analogy**
> Imagine building a tiny mechanical watch gear. Now imagine each gear tooth is *also* a tiny strain gauge that can measure how hard it is being pushed, and the gear itself can conduct electricity to send those measurements to a chip. That is silicon. It is a single material that can be the **structure, the sensor, the wire, and the circuit** — all at once, all made in the same cleanroom step by step.
>
> No metal can be etched into 1 µm mechanical springs. No plastic can carry a transistor. Silicon does both — that is why 95 % of commercial MEMS (accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors) are built on it.

### 1.3 Why Silicon? — The MEMS Engineer's Checklist

- **Mechanical strength:** Young's modulus E ≈ **130–190 GPa** (comparable to steel) yet density is only **2.329 g/cm³** (lighter than aluminium).
- **No mechanical hysteresis:** Single-crystal silicon is *elastic up to fracture* — it returns to original shape perfectly until it breaks.
- **Tunable conductivity:** Resistivity can be varied across **10⁻⁴ to 10⁴ Ω·cm** via doping.
- **Thermal stability:** Melting point **1414 °C**, low thermal expansion (**2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K**).
- **Surface micromachining compatibility:** Grows a perfect native oxide (SiO₂) that doubles as insulator, mask, and sacrificial layer.
- **High gauge factor:** Piezoresistive gauge factor **|G| ≈ 100–200**, ~50× higher than metal foils.

### 1.4 Important Physical Constants of Silicon

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic number | 14 | — |
| Atomic mass | 28.0855 | g/mol |
| Crystal structure | Diamond cubic | — |
| Lattice constant, **a** | **5.4307** | Å |
| Density, ρ | 2.329 | g/cm³ |
| Melting point | 1414 | °C |
| Band gap (300 K) | 1.12 | eV |
| Intrinsic carrier conc. nᵢ | 1.5 × 10¹⁰ | cm⁻³ |
| Dielectric constant εᵣ | 11.7 | — |
| Young's modulus ⟨111⟩ | 188 | GPa |
| Young's modulus ⟨100⟩ | 130 | GPa |
| Poisson's ratio | 0.28 | — |
| Thermal expansion coeff. | 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ | K⁻¹ |
| Thermal conductivity | 150 | W/m·K |
| Fracture toughness | 0.7–1.0 | MPa·m^½ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crystal Structure of Silicon — The Diamond Cubic Lattice

Silicon crystallises in the **diamond cubic (DC) structure**, which is essentially **two inter-penetrating face-centred cubic (FCC) sublattices**, displaced from each other along the body diagonal by **(a/4, a/4, a/4)**.

### Structural Breakdown
- **Each Si atom is covalently bonded (sp³ hybridisation) to 4 nearest neighbours** in a perfect tetrahedron.
- **Coordination number = 4** ; **Atoms per unit cell = 8** (effectively).
- **Nearest-neighbour distance = a√3/4 ≈ 2.351 Å.**
- **Atomic packing factor (APF) = 0.34** (very open structure — explains why silicon etches anisotropically in KOH).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the open lattice matter for MEMS?**
> KOH etchant attacks silicon **100× faster along ⟨100⟩ than ⟨111⟩** planes. The open (100) planes have more dangling bonds per area than the dense (111) planes — this single fact is the basis of *anisotropic bulk micromachining* of silicon.

## 2.2 Miller Indices & Wafer Orientations Used in MEMS

| Orientation | Surface normal | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| (100) | ⟨100⟩ | **Most common** — 45° etch walls in KOH; standard CMOS wafer |
| (111) | ⟨111⟩ | Used for **V-grooves, inkjet nozzles, fibre-alignment pits** |
| (110) | ⟨110⟩ | Used for **deep vertical sidewalls**, micro-mirrors, comb-drives |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table** — it covers ~70 % of the numerical questions asked in KTU ESE on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resistivity** of doped Si | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{q\left(n\mu_n + p\mu_p\right)}$ | Ω·cm | q = 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ C |
| 2 | Conductivity | $\sigma = q\left(n\mu_n + p\mu_p\right) = 1/\rho$ | S/cm | Reciprocal of resistivity |
| 3 | Mass-action law | $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ | cm⁻⁶ | nᵢ ≈ 1.5×10¹⁰ cm⁻³ at 300 K |
| 4 | **Piezoresistive** fractional resistance change | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | dimensionless | π = piezoresistive coefficient (Pa⁻¹) |
| 5 | Gauge factor | $G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | — | G ≈ 100–200 for Si ; 2 for metal |
| 6 | Stress on a beam (cantilever) under end load F | $\sigma = \dfrac{6FL}{bh^2}$ | Pa | L=length, b=width, h=thickness |
| 7 | Deflection of cantilever (end load) | $\delta = \dfrac{4FL^3}{Ebh^3}$ | m | E = Young's modulus |
| 8 | Resonant frequency of cantilever | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\dfrac{h}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}$ | Hz | 1.875² ≈ 3.516 |
| 9 | Thermal strain | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$ | — | α = thermal expansion coeff. |
| 10 | Capacitance of parallel-plate gap | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F | ε_r = 11.7 for Si, 3.9 for SiO₂ |
| 11 | Pressure on a diaphragm centre | $P = \dfrac{\sigma h^2}{a^2}$ (approx) | Pa | Thin diaphragm, edge-clamped |
| 12 | Young's modulus (anisotropic) | $\dfrac{1}{E_{hkl}} = s_{11} - 2\left(s_{11}-s_{12}-\tfrac{s_{44}}{2}\right)\left(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2\right)$ | Pa | Direction cosines l₁, l₂, l₃ |
| 13 | Carrier concentration (n-type, full ionisation) | $n \approx N_D$ | cm⁻³ | At 300 K, ND ≫ nᵢ |
| 14 | P-type concentration | $p \approx N_A$ | cm⁻³ | NA ≫ nᵢ |

## 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real MEMS Engineering

- **Equations 4–5** are the heart of every MEMS **pressure sensor, accelerometer, and strain gauge** — the piezoresistive effect is what allows silicon to *mechanically* sense and *electrically* report.
- **Equation 6** is used in **inertial sensor design** — when proof-mass inertia applies force F to a beam, the stress must stay below the fracture stress (~7 GPa) to ensure reliability.
- **Equation 8** defines the **operating bandwidth** of a MEMS resonator (e.g., a Si gyroscope resonates at ~10 kHz, a Si oscillator at ~MHz).
- **Equation 10** governs the **sense capacitance** of a comb-drive actuator (gap d typically 1–2 µm, area set by mask).
- **Equation 9** is the reason **Si–SiO₂–Si bonding** is possible without cracking: their α-values are close enough to avoid thermal mismatch failure.

## 2.5 Anisotropy of Mechanical Properties

Silicon is **mechanically anisotropic** — E depends on crystal direction:

| Direction | Young's Modulus (GPa) |
|---|---|
| ⟨100⟩ | 130 |
| ⟨110⟩ | 169 |
| ⟨111⟩ | 188 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite:** "Why are (111) wafers used for high-Q resonators?" — Answer: highest E → highest stiffness → highest resonant frequency → best noise performance.

## 2.6 Piezoresistive Effect — The Heart of Silicon Sensing

When mechanical stress is applied to doped silicon, its resistivity changes. This is the **piezoresistive effect**, discovered by Smith (1954). For a current flowing along direction *l* with stress components along *l* and transverse *t*:

$$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \,\sigma_l + \pi_t \,\sigma_t$$

The piezoresistive coefficients π depend strongly on doping type/concentration and crystal direction:
- **π₄₄ ≈ 138 × 10⁻¹¹ Pa⁻¹** (the dominant coefficient in p-type Si, ⟨111⟩ current, ⟨110⟩ stress) — this is what makes silicon piezoresistors ~50× more sensitive than metal strain gauges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resonant Frequency of a Silicon Cantilever Beam

This derivation is **routinely asked for 7 marks** in KTU ESE Module 1. We derive the natural frequency of a uniform cantilever beam made of single-crystal silicon.

### Step 1: Governing Equation of Motion

For an Euler–Bernoulli beam undergoing transverse vibration, Newton's second law on a small element gives:

$$EI \dfrac{\partial^4 y(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \dfrac{\partial^2 y(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa)
- $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ = second moment of area (m⁴)
- $A = bh$ = cross-section (m²)
- $\rho$ = density of silicon (kg/m³)
- $y(x,t)$ = transverse displacement

### Step 2: Separation of Variables

Assume $y(x,t) = Y(x) \cdot T(t)$. Substituting:

$$EI \dfrac{d^4 Y}{dx^4} T + \rho A \, Y \dfrac{d^2 T}{dt^2} = 0$$

Dividing by $Y \cdot T$ and introducing a separation constant $\omega^2$:

$$\dfrac{EI}{\rho A} \dfrac{1}{Y}\dfrac{d^4 Y}{dx^4} = -\dfrac{1}{T}\dfrac{d^2 T}{dt^2} = \omega^2$$

### Step 3: Spatial Solution

The standard solution for a uniform beam is:

$$Y(x) = C_1 \cos\beta x + C_2 \sin\beta x + C_3 \cosh\beta x + C_4 \sinh\beta x$$

where $\beta^4 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \rho A}{EI}$.

### Step 4: Cantilever Boundary Conditions

- At fixed end (x = 0): $Y(0) = 0$ and $Y'(0) = 0$
- At free end (x = L): $Y''(L) = 0$ and $Y'''(L) = 0$

Applying these gives the **characteristic equation**:

$$\cos\beta L \cdot \cosh\beta L = -1$$

### Step 5: First Root (Fundamental Mode)

The smallest positive root is:

$$\beta_1 L = 1.8751 \approx 1.875$$

(Second root is 4.694, third is 7.855, etc.)

### Step 6: Fundamental Frequency

Substituting back:

$$\beta_1 = \dfrac{1.875}{L}, \qquad \omega_1 = \beta_1^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

Plugging $I = bh^3/12$ and $A = bh$:

$$\omega_1 = \left(\dfrac{1.875}{L}\right)^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot bh^3/12}{\rho \cdot bh}} = \dfrac{1.875^2}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E h^2}{12 \rho}}$$

Therefore, the **fundamental resonant frequency** is:

$$\boxed{\,f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_1}{2\pi} = \dfrac{(1.875)^2}{2\pi}\,\dfrac{h}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}\,}$$

### Step 7: Numerical Example for a Silicon Cantilever

Take a typical MEMS cantilever: $L = 200 \,\mu m$, $h = 5 \,\mu m$, $b = 50 \,\mu m$, Si (100): $E = 130 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho = 2330 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

Compute piece by piece:

$$\dfrac{h}{L^2} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-8}} = 125 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{130 \times 10^9}{12 \times 2330}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.30 \times 10^{11}}{27960}} = \sqrt{4.649 \times 10^6} \approx 2156 \text{ m/s}$$

$$f_0 = \dfrac{3.516}{2\pi} \times 125 \times 2156 = 0.5596 \times 125 \times 2156$$

$$f_0 = 0.5596 \times 269500 \approx 1.51 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz} = \mathbf{151 \text{ kHz}}$$

This is a realistic Si cantilever resonance — within the operating range of commercial MEMS oscillators.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Piezoresistive Gauge Factor of Silicon

### Step 1: Define Fractional Resistance Change

A piezoresistor of length $L$, cross-section $A$, resistivity $\rho$ has:

$$R = \rho \dfrac{L}{A}$$

Taking logarithmic differential:

$$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} - \dfrac{\Delta A}{A}$$

### Step 2: Geometric Strain Contribution

For axial strain $\varepsilon$ along the resistor:

$$\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \varepsilon, \qquad \dfrac{\Delta A}{A} = -2\nu\varepsilon$$

So the purely geometric term gives a gauge factor of $(1 + 2\nu) \approx 1.5$ — small.

### Step 3: Piezoresistive (True) Contribution

In silicon, $\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$ dominates and is **not** simply proportional to strain. It depends on stress:

$$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

### Step 4: Define Gauge Factor

$$G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_l \,E$$

### Step 5: Plug Silicon Values

For p-type Si (boron-doped, 10¹⁸ cm⁻³), current along ⟨110⟩, stress along ⟨110⟩:

$$\pi_l = \dfrac{\pi_{44}}{2} \approx 71 \times 10^{-11}\text{ Pa}^{-1}$$

$$G \approx 1.5 + \left(71 \times 10^{-11}\right)\left(169 \times 10^9\right)$$

$$G \approx 1.5 + 120 = \mathbf{121.5}$$

This confirms **G ≈ 100–200 for silicon**, vs. ~2 for metal foil.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Fabrication Method

| MEMS Property Required | Material Choice | Why Silicon Wins |
|---|---|---|
| High-Q mechanical resonator | Single-crystal Si ⟨111⟩ | Lowest internal friction |
| Strain gauge | Piezoresistive Si | Gauge factor 100–200 |
| Sacrificial layer | SiO₂ | Etches 1000× faster than Si in HF |
| Structural beam | Polysilicon | Conformal CVD, low stress, low cost |
| Electrical interconnect | Doped poly-Si / Al | CMOS-compatible |
| Insulator | SiO₂ (thermal), Si₃N₄ (LPCVD) | Native growth, perfect interface |
| High-temperature harsh environment | SiC on Si | Wide band gap, chemically inert |
| Optical MEMS mirror | Polished single-crystal Si | Optical-grade surface finish |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diamond Cubic Crystal Structure of Silicon

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Silicon Atom — FCC Sublattice A"] --> B["Lattice constant a = 5.4307 Å"]
    A --> C["Origin at 0,0,0"]
    D["Silicon Atom — FCC Sublattice B"] --> E["Displaced by a/4, a/4, a/4"]
    D --> F["Tetrahedral sp3 Bonding"]
    C --> G["8 Atoms per unit cell"]
    E --> G
    F --> H["Bond length = 2.351 Å"]
    G --> I["Coordination number = 4"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Anisotropic etching favours 100 and 110 planes"]
```

## 4.2 MEMS Fabrication Flow Using Silicon (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STEP 1 — Start with Si Wafer (100 or 111)"] --> B["STEP 2 — Thermal Oxidation to grow SiO2 mask layer"]
    B --> C["STEP 3 — Photolithography and Pattern Transfer"]
    C --> D["STEP 4 — Doping by Diffusion or Ion Implantation"]
    D --> E["STEP 5 — Anisotropic Wet Etching in KOH or DRIE"]
    E --> F["STEP 6 — Deposition of Structural Polysilicon via LPCVD"]
    F --> G["STEP 7 — Sacrificial Oxide Etch in HF to release MEMS structure"]
    G --> H["STEP 8 — Metallisation and Wire Bonding"]
    H --> I["FINAL — Released MEMS Device ready for packaging"]
    H --> J["SIDE PATH — Surface Micromachining uses PSG sacrificial layer"]
    H --> K["SIDE PATH — Bonding with Glass or Si for Wafer-Level Packaging"]
```

## 4.3 Piezoresistive Sensing Principle in Silicon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Applied Mechanical Stress σ on Si beam"] --> B["Change in band structure due to strain"]
    B --> C["Change in carrier mobility μ and concentration n"]
    C --> D["Change in resistivity Δρ/ρ = π σ"]
    D --> E["Fractional Resistance Change ΔR/R amplified by bridge circuit"]
    E --> F["Output Voltage Vout proportional to stress and hence pressure or acceleration"]
    F --> G["MEMS Pressure Sensor / Accelerometer / Gyroscope Output"]
```

## 4.4 Wafer Orientation vs. Etch Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Silicon Wafer Selection"] --> B["(100) Wafer"]
    A --> C["(110) Wafer"]
    A --> D["(111) Wafer"]
    B --> E["KOH gives 54.74 degree pyramidal pits along 111 planes"]
    C --> F["KOH gives vertical sidewalls — useful for comb drives"]
    D --> G["KOH gives very slow uniform etch — used for diaphragms"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three mechanical properties of silicon that make it suitable for MEMS applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **High Young's modulus (E = 130–190 GPa)** — comparable to steel, gives stiff, high-frequency micromechanical structures. (1 mark)
2. **Low density (ρ = 2.329 g/cm³)** — light weight leads to high mechanical Q and low inertia for sensors. (1 mark)
3. **No mechanical hysteresis (elastic up to fracture)** — repeated loading produces identical response, essential for reliable sensors. (1 mark)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the piezoresistive effect and write the expression for the gauge factor of a piezoresistor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **piezoresistive effect** is the change in electrical resistivity of a material when subjected to mechanical stress. (1 mark)
- For a piezoresistor with stress σ along current direction: $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ (1 mark)
- **Gauge factor:** $G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \pi E$ where π is the piezoresistive coefficient. (1 mark)

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the **crystal structure of silicon** with a neat diagram. Why is it called a *diamond cubic* structure? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon cantilever beam of length $L = 250 \,\mu m$, width $b = 60 \,\mu m$ and thickness $h = 5 \,\mu m$ is oriented along ⟨110⟩. Calculate its **fundamental resonant frequency** in vacuum. Take $E_{\langle 110\rangle} = 169$ GPa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks**

**Crystal structure (4 marks):**
- Silicon crystallises in the **diamond cubic** lattice, which consists of **two interpenetrating FCC sublattices** displaced by (a/4, a/4, a/4) along the body diagonal. (1 mark)
- Each Si atom is covalently bonded to **4 nearest neighbours** via sp³ hybridisation in a perfect tetrahedron. (1 mark)
- **Lattice constant a = 5.4307 Å**, atomic packing factor = 0.34 (open structure), atoms per unit cell = 8, coordination number = 4. (1 mark)
- It is called *diamond cubic* because the same arrangement is found in the carbon diamond crystal — tetrahedrally bonded atoms in a cubic unit cell. (1 mark)

**Diagram representation (2 marks):**
- Mention the two interpenetrating tetrahedra and the small atom at the centre (½, ½, ½) and the four corner atoms; the 4 inner atoms of the second FCC shifted by a/4.
- Note: in KTU valuation, a *textual description with axes marked* earns 2 marks; a clean hand-drawn cube with the two sublattices earns full marks.

**Why this structure suits MEMS (1 mark):**
- The open structure along ⟨100⟩ planes gives rise to **anisotropic etching** in KOH/TMAH, enabling reproducible 3D micromachining.

---

### **Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Write the formula (2 marks):**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{h}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute h/L² (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{h}{L^2} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{(250 \times 10^{-6})^2} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{6.25 \times 10^{-8}} = 80 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the square-root term (1 mark):**

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{169 \times 10^9}{12 \times 2330}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.69 \times 10^{11}}{27960}} = \sqrt{6.045 \times 10^6} \approx 2458.7 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Combine (2 marks):**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{3.516}{2\pi} \times 80 \times 2458.7 = 0.5596 \times 80 \times 2458.7$$

$$f_0 = 0.5596 \times 196696 \approx 1.10 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 110 \text{ kHz}}$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):**
- This 110 kHz resonance falls in the typical operating band of MEMS inertial sensors; the higher E (along ⟨110⟩) compared to ⟨100⟩ (130 GPa) raises the resonant frequency by √(169/130) ≈ 14 %.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **piezoresistive effect in silicon**. Compare the gauge factor of silicon with that of a metal strain gauge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **resistivity of an n-type silicon sample** doped with donor concentration $N_D = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}$. Take electron mobility $\mu_n = 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}$, and $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks**

**Piezoresistive effect (4 marks):**
- The piezoresistive effect is the **change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor due to applied mechanical stress**. (1 mark)
- It arises because stress distorts the silicon crystal lattice, which in turn modifies the **band structure** (valley shifting) and thereby alters **carrier mobility** in different crystallographic directions. (1 mark)
- The fractional change in resistivity is given by $\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ where π are the piezoresistive coefficients. (1 mark)
- For p-type silicon (boron-doped), the **π₄₄ coefficient is dominant** (~71×10⁻¹¹ Pa⁻¹) when current flows along ⟨110⟩ and stress is along ⟨110⟩. (1 mark)

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Property | Metal foil (constantan) | Silicon piezoresistor |
|---|---|---|
| Gauge factor G | 2 | **100–200** |
| Temperature sensitivity | Low | Moderate (needs compensation) |
| Mechanism | Geometric (ΔL, ΔA) only | Geometric + piezoresistive (band structure change) |
| Fabrication | Bonded foil | **CMOS-compatible in-situ doping** |
| Hysteresis | None | Negligible in single-crystal Si |
| Cost & integration | High (discrete) | **Low (batch-fabricated with IC)** |

- Silicon has **G ~50–100× higher** because the piezoresistive term πE dominates the geometric term 1+2ν. (1 mark)

---

### **Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Mass-action law (1 mark):**
For an n-type semiconductor at 300 K with $N_D \gg n_i$:

$$n \approx N_D = 10^{16} \text{ cm}^{-3}, \qquad p = \dfrac{n_i^2}{n} = \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}{10^{16}} = 2.25 \times 10^4 \text{ cm}^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Drop minority term (1 mark):**
Since $p \mu_p \ll n \mu_n$, conductivity reduces to:

$$\sigma \approx q n \mu_n = q N_D \mu_n$$

**Step 3 — Plug in numbers (2 marks):**

$$\sigma = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(10^{16})(1350) = 1.602 \times 1.35 \times 10^{0}$$

$$\sigma = 2.1627 \text{ S/cm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute resistivity (1 mark):**

$$\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma} = \dfrac{1}{2.1627} \approx 0.4624 \text{ Ω·cm}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation (2 marks):**
- Compared to intrinsic Si (ρ ≈ 230 kΩ·cm at 300 K), doping with $N_D = 10^{16}$ cm⁻³ **reduces resistivity by ~6 orders of magnitude** (1 mark).
- This is the basis of *in-situ doping* of silicon piezoresistors: by choosing ND between 10¹⁵ and 10¹⁸ cm⁻³, designers trade off sensitivity (low doping → high G) against thermal noise (high doping → low R). (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Confusing E values** for different wafer orientations (E₍₁₀₀₎ = 130, E₍₁₁₀₎ = 169, E₍₁₁₁₎ = 188 GPa). Always state the orientation!
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 1.875** in the cantilever frequency formula — many students write 1.875 (a dimensionless βL root) but forget to square it in ω = β²√(EI/ρA).
> 3. **Mixing up units**: lattice constant in Å vs. nm vs. m; mobilities in cm²/V·s vs. m²/V·s. KTU strictly checks units.
> 4. **Resistivity vs. conductivity confusion**: σ = 1/ρ, and the order of magnitude is *inverted* between intrinsic and heavily doped Si.
> 5. **Piezoresistive effect ≠ piezoelectric effect.** Piezoresistive is a *change in resistance* (passive); piezoelectric is a *generation of charge* (active). Examiners *do* test this distinction.
> 6. **Doping type direction**: π₄₄ dominates in **p-type** Si (boron), not n-type. Memorising this is a 2-mark saviour.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Silicon crystal structure = Diamond Cubic**, a = 5.4307 Å, 8 atoms/unit cell, coordination 4.
- ⭐ **E (Young's modulus) is direction-dependent**: 130 / 169 / 188 GPa along ⟨100⟩ / ⟨110⟩ / ⟨111⟩.
- ⭐ **Density = 2.329 g/cm³**, melting point = 1414 °C, band gap = 1.12 eV at 300 K.
- ⭐ **Resistivity formula**: $\rho = 1/[q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)]$ ; nᵢ = 1.5×10¹⁰ cm⁻³ at 300 K.
- ⭐ **Piezoresistive effect** dominates over piezoelectric in silicon — gauge factor G ≈ 100–200.
- ⭐ **Piezoresistive formula**: $\Delta R/R = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ ; $\pi_{44} \approx 71 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ for p-type.
- ⭐ **Cantilever resonant frequency**: $f_0 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\dfrac{h}{L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}$ — the *most-asked* 7-mark derivation.
- ⭐ **Anisotropic etching**: KOH/TMAH attacks ⟨100⟩ ~100× faster than ⟨111⟩ — forms 54.74° sidewalls on (100) wafers.
- ⭐ **Silicon wins MEMS** because of mature CMOS infrastructure + dual electrical-mechanical function.
- ⭐ **Thermal oxide SiO₂** is the *killer feature* — acts as insulator, mask, and sacrificial layer in HF.
- ⭐ **Polysilicon** is the workhorse structural material in surface micromachining (deposited by LPCVD).
- ⭐ **SiC** is used where Si fails (high-temp, harsh chemical, high-power).
- ⭐ **Pressure diaphragm stress**: $\sigma = Pa^2/h^2$ (edge-clamped square, thin-plate limit).
- ⭐ **Capacitance of comb-drive**: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$ — gap d typically 1–2 µm.
- ⭐ **Always state wafer orientation** in any KTU numerical answer; examiners allocate 1 mark just for it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicon Compounds: Silicon Nitride
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Silicon Compounds: Silicon Nitride

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)** is a covalent ceramic compound composed of one silicon atom bonded to four nitrogen atoms in a tetrahedral configuration (with three Si atoms sharing each N). In the context of **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) and Nanoelectronics**, silicon nitride is classified as a **structural and passivation dielectric thin-film material**, widely used as a masking layer, mechanical membrane, electrical insulator, and stress-engineered structural layer in microfabricated devices.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Si₃N₄ is grouped under **Silicon Compounds for MEMS** alongside SiO₂ and SiC, and is recognised for its tunable mechanical, electrical, and chemical properties depending on the deposition technique employed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> Under "Silicon Compounds," the student must be able to compare **silicon dioxide (SiO₂), silicon nitride (Si₃N₄), and silicon carbide (SiC)** with respect to their **deposition methods, etch chemistry, mechanical stress, electrical properties, and MEMS applications**. Silicon Nitride is examined as the most versatile **dielectric/structural hybrid** in the silicon process flow.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The All-Weather Protective Skin"

Think of a MEMS chip as a **delicate micro-machine on a silicon wafer**. Just as a **stainless-steel watch case** protects the gears inside from dust, sweat, and corrosion, a thin layer of **Silicon Nitride** sits on top of the silicon die and acts as an **"all-weather protective skin."**

| Real-world Object | MEMS Equivalent | Function of Si₃N₄ |
|---|---|---|
| Waterproof watch case | Surface passivation | Seals the device from moisture/ions |
| Helmet visor | Masking layer | Protects selected areas during KOH etch |
| Reinforced concrete slab | Structural membrane | Forms the diaphragm of pressure sensor |
| Electrical rubber insulator | Dielectric layer | Isolates metal interconnects electrically |

## 1.3 The Chemistry at a Glance

The chemical formula **Si₃N₄** indicates a **3:4 stoichiometric ratio**. Each nitrogen is bonded to **three silicon atoms (sp³ tetrahedral)** and each silicon is bonded to **four nitrogen atoms**, forming a rigid, three-dimensional covalent network — this is the fundamental reason for Si₃N₄' s exceptional **hardness, chemical inertness, and thermal stability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout:**
> **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)** = a *tetrahedrally-coordinated, covalently-bonded ceramic* that is the **workhorse dielectric/structural material** of MEMS, deposited predominantly by **LPCVD** (high temperature, low stress) or **PECVD** (low temperature, tunable stress).

## 1.4 Why Silicon Nitride Matters in MEMS

The unique position of Si₃N₄ in the microfabrication toolbox stems from a rare combination of four properties that *no other single MEMS material* offers simultaneously:

1. **Excellent diffusion barrier** — blocks Na⁺ ions, H₂O, O₂
2. **Tunable residual stress** — tensile (LPCVD ~1 GPa) or compressive (PECVD controllable)
3. **High chemical resistance** — withstands HF, KOH, and most acids (except hot H₃PO₄)
4. **CMOS-compatible deposition** — can be grown/deposited in standard fab lines
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Structure & Bonding

The Si₃N₄ crystal exists in three polymorphic forms:
- **α-Si₃N₄** (trigonal) — most common, formed at lower temperatures
- **β-Si₃N₄** (hexagonal) — formed at higher temperatures, more stable
- **Amorphous Si₃N₄** — the form typically used in MEMS thin films

In thin-film form (used in MEMS), the material is **amorphous** with a short-range tetrahedral order. The Si–N bond is:
- **Bond length:** ~1.74 Å
- **Bond energy:** ~355 kJ/mol
- **Covalent character:** predominantly covalent (~70%) with partial ionic character

## 2.2 Key Material Properties (Board-Exam Ready)

| Property | LPCVD Si₃N₄ | PECVD Si₃N₄ | SiO₂ (for comparison) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (ρ) | **3.44** | 2.4 – 2.8 | 2.27 | g/cm³ |
| Refractive Index (n) | **2.05** | 1.9 – 2.5 | 1.46 | — |
| Dielectric Constant (εr) | **7.5** | 6.0 – 8.0 | 3.9 | — |
| Breakdown Field (Ebd) | **10** | 5 – 8 | 10 | MV/cm |
| Resistivity (ρ) | **10¹⁶** | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ | Ω·cm |
| Young's Modulus (E) | **250 – 310** | 150 – 250 | 70 – 80 | GPa |
| Poisson's Ratio (ν) | **0.27** | 0.24 – 0.28 | 0.17 | — |
| Thermal Conductivity (k) | **30** | 5 – 20 | 1.4 | W/m·K |
| Residual Stress (σ₀) | **+1000 (Tensile)** | –500 to +500 (tunable) | –300 (Compressive) | MPa |
| Thermal Expansion Coeff. (α) | **2.8 × 10⁻⁶** | ~3 × 10⁻⁶ | 0.5 × 10⁻⁶ | /K |
| Etchant (selective) | **Hot H₃PO₄ (180°C)** | Hot H₃PO₄ | HF (BHF) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Stress Sign Convention" (must memorise for KTU):**
> - **Tensile stress (σ > 0):** film wants to *contract*, pulls substrate inward → **LPCVD Si₃N₄** is intrinsically tensile.
> - **Compressive stress (σ < 0):** film wants to *expand*, pushes substrate outward → **PECVD Si₃N₄** can be tuned compressive by adjusting RF power, SiH₄/NH₃ ratio, and temperature.

## 2.3 Deposition Methods — Theory & Trade-offs

### (a) Low Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (LPCVD)

**Reaction chemistry (standard KTU board question):**
The most common precursor system uses **dichlorosilane (SiH₂Cl₂) and ammonia (NH₃)**:

$$
\text{SiH}_2\text{Cl}_2 \,(g) \;+\; \tfrac{4}{3}\,\text{NH}_3 \,(g) \;\longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{3}\,\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4 \,(s) \;+\; 2\,\text{HCl}\,(g) \;+\; \text{H}_2\,(g)
$$

- **Temperature:** 700 – 850 °C
- **Pressure:** 25 – 150 Pa (0.2 – 1.1 Torr)
- **Deposition rate:** ~3 – 5 nm/min
- **Film quality:** **Stoichiometric, dense, low pin-hole density, high tensile stress (~1 GPa)**
- **Conformal step coverage:** Excellent
- **Disadvantage:** High temperature — **cannot be deposited over aluminium** (melts at 660 °C)

### (b) Plasma Enhanced CVD (PECVD)

**Reaction chemistry:**
Uses **silane (SiH₄) and ammonia (NH₃)** activated by RF plasma:

$$
\text{SiH}_4 \,(g) \;+\; \text{NH}_3 \,(g) \;\xrightarrow{\text{RF plasma at 13.56 MHz}}\; \text{Si}_x\text{N}_y\text{H}_z \,(s) \;+\; \text{H}_2\,(g)
$$

- **Temperature:** 250 – 400 °C (CMOS back-end compatible)
- **Pressure:** 13 – 130 Pa
- **Deposition rate:** ~10 – 50 nm/min (much faster than LPCVD)
- **Film quality:** Less dense, contains **hydrogen (5 – 30 at. %)**, **Si-rich non-stoichiometric** composition
- **Stress control:** Tunable from **–500 MPa (compressive) to +500 MPa (tensile)** — *engineer can dial the stress by recipe*

### (c) Thermal Nitridation of Silicon

Direct exposure of silicon to nitrogen or ammonia at very high temperatures:

$$
3\,\text{Si}\,(s) \;+\; 2\,\text{N}_2\,(g) \;\xrightarrow{1200-1300^{\circ}\text{C}}\; \text{Si}_3\text{N}_4\,(s)
$$

- Produces very thin (~5 nm), high-quality native-like nitride
- Used in advanced gate-stack engineering (replacement for SiO₂ in some DRAM nodes)

## 2.4 Etching Chemistry — The Selective Etch Table

| Etch Type | Chemistry | Selectivity vs SiO₂ | Selectivity vs Si | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wet — Hot Phosphoric Acid** | H₃PO₄ at **180 °C** | High (etches Si₃N₄ ~10× faster) | Infinite (no attack) | **Standard MEMS wet etch** |
| **Wet — Buffered HF** | BHF (HF:NH₄F) | Low (etches SiO₂, not Si₃N₄) | Infinite | Reverse selectivity — used *after* nitride to open oxide |
| **Dry — RIE** | CF₄ / SF₆ / CHF₃ plasma | Tunable | High | Anisotropic profile |
| **Dry — ICP** | SF₆ + Ar | High | High | Faster, deeper etch |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of KTU Etch Questions:**
> "**To remove Si₃N₄ without attacking SiO₂ or Si → use hot H₃PO₄ at 180°C.**" This is the *opposite* selectivity of HF, which is the foundational logic for stacked MEMS processes.

## 2.5 Stoney's Formula — Residual Stress in Thin Films (★ High-Yield KTU Formula)

The most tested equation in MEMS materials modules. The residual stress σ in a thin film deposited on a thick substrate is related to the substrate curvature (κ) by **Stoney's Formula (1909)**:

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6\,(1-\nu_s)\,t_f}\;\kappa
$$

where:
- $E_s$ = Young's modulus of the **substrate** (Pa)
- $\nu_s$ = Poisson's ratio of the **substrate** (dimensionless)
- $t_s$ = thickness of the **substrate** (m)
- $t_f$ = thickness of the **film** (m)
- $\kappa$ = change in substrate curvature, $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{R_{\text{after}}} - \dfrac{1}{R_{\text{before}}}$ (m⁻¹)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** A film with high tensile stress (like LPCVD Si₃N₄, σ ≈ +1 GPa) will warp the wafer **concave-up**. If σ becomes too large, the film **cracks**; if too compressive, it **buckles/delaminates**. The KTU examiner frequently tests this trade-off in cantilever/bridge design problems.

## 2.6 Mechanical Vibration of a Si₃N₄ Cantilever (Resonance Frequency)

For a cantilever beam (rectangular cross-section) made of Si₃N₄, the **fundamental resonant frequency** is:

$$
f_1 \;=\; \frac{1.875^{\,2}}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\frac{E\,I}{\rho\,A\,L^{4}}} \;=\; \frac{1.875^{\,2}}{2\pi}\,\frac{t}{L^{2}}\,\sqrt{\frac{E}{12\,\rho}}
$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus of Si₃N₄ (~250 GPa for LPCVD)
- $\rho$ = density of Si₃N₄ (3100 kg/m³ for stoichiometric)
- $L$ = beam length
- $t$ = beam thickness
- $I = \dfrac{b\,t^{3}}{12}$ = area moment of inertia for a rectangular section
- $A = b\,t$ = cross-sectional area

The **1.875²** factor comes from the first eigenvalue of the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation for a cantilever with a free end.

## 2.7 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (★ Board-Exam Must-Know)

| # | Formula | Application in Si₃N₄ MEMS | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{E_s t_s^{\,2}}{6(1-\nu_s)\,t_f}\,\kappa$ | Residual stress / wafer curvature | Pa |
| 2 | $f_1 = \dfrac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi}\dfrac{t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}$ | Cantilever resonance (Si₃N₄ AFM probes) | Hz |
| 3 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitor with Si₃N₄ dielectric | F |
| 4 | $E_{bd} = \dfrac{V_{bd}}{d}$ | Dielectric breakdown of Si₃N₄ | V/m |
| 5 | $R_{sq} = \dfrac{\rho}{t_f}$ | Sheet resistance of metal interconnect over Si₃N₄ | Ω/□ |
| 6 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Capacitance per unit area (dielectric) | F/m² |
| 7 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6\,M}{b\,t^{2}}$ | Max bending stress in cantilever | Pa |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}$ | Spring constant of Si₃N₄ cantilever | N/m |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stoney's Formula (★ Frequently Asked in KTU)

**Stoney's formula** is derived from the condition that the **net force and net moment** exerted by the film on the substrate must be zero (force/moment equilibrium on a bimetallic strip analogue).

### Setup

Consider a thin film of thickness $t_f$ deposited on a thick substrate of thickness $t_s$. After deposition, the substrate curls into a spherical cap of radius $R$ due to the film stress $\sigma$.

**Assumptions of Stoney's Derivation:**
1. $t_f \ll t_s$ (film much thinner than substrate)
2. The film stress is **equibiaxial and uniform** (isotropic in the plane)
3. Elastic deformation — Hooke's law valid
4. The substrate is much thicker than the film → substrate dominates the bending rigidity

### Step 1 — Equibiaxial Film Stress

For an equibiaxial stress state in the film:

$$
\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma, \qquad \sigma_{zz} = 0
$$

The film exerts a force per unit length on the substrate (force per unit width):

$$
F \;=\; \sigma \cdot t_f
$$

### Step 2 — Moment Equivalence

The film force $F$, acting at a distance $\dfrac{t_s}{2}$ from the neutral axis (centroid) of the substrate, creates a bending moment per unit width:

$$
M \;=\; F \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} \;=\; \sigma \cdot t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Curvature from Plate Bending Theory

For a plate of thickness $t_s$ bent to radius $R$, the classical plate-bending relation gives the bending moment per unit width:

$$
M \;=\; \frac{E_s\,t_s^{\,3}}{12\,(1-\nu_s^{\,2})} \cdot \frac{1}{R}
$$

where the factor $\dfrac{1}{1-\nu_s^{\,2}}$ accounts for the **equibiaxial** (not uniaxial) strain state. The curvature $\kappa = 1/R$.

### Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions for M

$$
\sigma \cdot t_f \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} \;=\; \frac{E_s\,t_s^{\,3}}{12\,(1-\nu_s^{\,2})} \cdot \kappa
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{E_s\,t_s^{\,3}}{12\,(1-\nu_s^{\,2})\,t_f} \cdot \frac{2}{t_s} \cdot \kappa
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; \frac{E_s\,t_s^{\,2}}{6\,(1-\nu_s^{\,2})\,t_f}\,\kappa
$$

For **most MEMS texts** (and the KTU standard form), since $1-\nu_s^{\,2} \approx (1-\nu_s)$ for thin silicon substrates with $\nu_s = 0.28$, the simplified Stoney formula used in practice is:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma \;=\; \frac{E_s \, t_s^{\,2}}{6\,(1-\nu_s)\,t_f}\;\kappa\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (★ Board-style)

> **Problem (Dec 2023-style KTU):** A **200 nm LPCVD Si₃N₄** film is deposited on a **525 µm thick silicon substrate**. The wafer curvature changes from $R_{\text{before}} = \infty$ to $R_{\text{after}} = 12 \,\text{m}$ (concave-up). For silicon, $E_s = 169 \,\text{GPa}$, $\nu_s = 0.28$. Calculate the residual stress in the film and identify whether it is tensile or compressive.

**Step A — Compute curvature**

$$
\kappa = \frac{1}{R_{\text{after}}} - \frac{1}{R_{\text{before}}} = \frac{1}{12} - 0 = 0.0833\;\text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step B — Insert into Stoney's formula**

$$
\sigma = \frac{(169\times 10^{9})\,(525\times 10^{-6})^{2}}{6\,(1-0.28)\,(200\times 10^{-9})}\,(0.0833)
$$

Compute the denominator and step by step:

- $t_s^{\,2} = (525\times 10^{-6})^{2} = 2.756 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^{2}$
- Numerator: $169\times 10^{9} \times 2.756\times 10^{-7} = 4.658 \times 10^{4}$
- Denominator: $6 \times 0.72 \times 200\times 10^{-9} = 8.64 \times 10^{-7}$

$$
\sigma = \frac{4.658 \times 10^{4}}{8.64 \times 10^{-7}} \times 0.0833
$$

$$
\sigma = (5.39 \times 10^{10}) \times 0.0833 \;\approx\; 4.49 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma \;\approx\; 4.49 \,\text{GPa (Tensile)}\;}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A **positive** σ (with the wafer curving *concave-up* after deposition) means the film is in **tensile stress** — exactly as expected for LPCVD Si₃N₄. The magnitude (~4.5 GPa) is *much higher* than the typical ~1 GPa; this indicates either excessive deposition temperature or a film-thickness scaling effect, useful for illustrating how Stoney detects process drift.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Si₃N₄ vs SiO₂ vs SiC (★ KTU Modular Comparison)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to **compare silicon compounds** for MEMS use cases. Below is a master analytical matrix:

| Parameter | Si₃N₄ (LPCVD) | SiO₂ (Thermal) | SiC (LPCVD) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 3.44 | 2.27 | 3.21 | Si₃N₄ — heaviest, best for mass sensors |
| Dielectric Constant | 7.5 | 3.9 | 9.7 | SiO₂ best for low-k; SiC for high-k |
| Bandgap (eV) | 5.0 | 9.0 | 3.2 | SiO₂ best insulator; SiC for high-T |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 250 – 310 | 70 – 80 | 400 – 450 | SiC stiffest; SiO₂ compliant |
| Residual Stress (MPa) | +1000 (T) | –300 (C) | +200 to +600 | Si₃N₄ high tensile, SiO₂ compressive |
| HF Etch Rate | Very low | **High (1000 nm/min)** | Negligible | SiO₂ sacrificial layer to Si₃N₄ |
| KOH Etch Rate (40%, 80°C) | **~ 0** (mask) | ~ 1 µm/min | ~ 0 | **Si₃N₄ is the KOH mask** |
| H₃PO₄ Etch Rate (180°C) | **60 – 80 nm/min** | low | low | Selectively removes Si₃N₄ |
| Max Process Temp (°C) | 850 | 1100 | 900 | All CMOS-compatible |
| CMOS Compatibility | ✓ (BEOL-friendly via PECVD) | ✓ | Limited | Si₃N₄ most universal |

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU "Rule of Thumb" for Sacrificial vs Structural:**
> - Use **SiO₂** as a *sacrificial* layer (dissolve in HF).
> - Use **Si₃N₄** as a *structural* layer + **KOH etch mask**.
> - Use **Poly-Si** as a *structural* layer + **SiO₂** as sacrificial.
> - Use **SiC** for *high-temperature / harsh-environment* MEMS.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Mass Sensitivity of a Si₃N₄ Cantilever (Sensor Design)

A common KTU 14-mark question type is to design a Si₃N₄ cantilever as a **mass sensor** (e.g., chemical/biosensor).

**Given:**
- Cantilever: $L = 200 \,\mu\text{m}$, $b = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$, $t = 1 \,\mu\text{m}$
- Material: LPCVD Si₃N₄, $E = 250 \,\text{GPa}$, $\rho = 3300 \,\text{kg/m}^{3}$
- Target: detect added mass $\Delta m = 1 \,\text{pg} = 10^{-15}\,\text{kg}$

### Step 1 — Mass of the bare cantilever

$$
m_{\text{bare}} = \rho \cdot L \cdot b \cdot t = 3300 \times (200\times 10^{-6}) \times (50\times 10^{-6}) \times (1\times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
m_{\text{bare}} = 3300 \times 10^{-14} = 3.3 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{kg} = 33\,\text{pg}
$$

### Step 2 — Spring constant of the cantilever

$$
k = \frac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}} = \frac{(250\times 10^{9})\,(50\times 10^{-6})\,(1\times 10^{-6})^{3}}{4\,(200\times 10^{-6})^{3}}
$$

Numerator: $250\times 10^{9} \times 50\times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-18} = 1.25 \times 10^{-11}$
Denominator: $4 \times 8\times 10^{-12} = 3.2 \times 10^{-11}$

$$
k = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-11}}{3.2 \times 10^{-11}} = 0.391\;\text{N/m}
$$

### Step 3 — Resonance frequency (fundamental)

$$
f_1 = \frac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{t}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\frac{E}{12\rho}}
$$

Compute $\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12\rho}}$:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{250\times 10^{9}}{12 \times 3300}} = \sqrt{6.31 \times 10^{6}} = 2512\;\text{m/s}
$$

Now compute $f_1$:

$$
f_1 = \frac{3.516}{2\pi} \times \frac{1\times 10^{-6}}{(200\times 10^{-6})^{2}} \times 2512
$$

$$
f_1 = 0.5596 \times \frac{1\times 10^{-6}}{4\times 10^{-8}} \times 2512
$$

$$
f_1 = 0.5596 \times 25 \times 2512 = 35{,}148 \,\text{Hz} \;\approx\; 35.1 \,\text{kHz}
$$

### Step 4 — Frequency shift due to added mass

For a point mass at the tip, the frequency shift formula (simplified) is:

$$
\Delta f \;\approx\; -\frac{f_1}{2}\cdot\frac{\Delta m}{m_{\text{eff}}}
$$

where $m_{\text{eff}} = 0.24 \cdot m_{\text{bare}}$ (for cantilever with point load at tip) ≈ $0.24 \times 33\,\text{pg} = 7.92\,\text{pg}$.

$$
\Delta f \;\approx\; -\frac{35{,}148}{2} \times \frac{1\,\text{pg}}{7.92\,\text{pg}} \;=\; -2218 \,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\vert \Delta f \vert \;\approx\; 2.2 \,\text{kHz per picogram of added mass}\;}
$$

This is the **mass sensitivity** — comfortably within measurable range for a standard oscillator circuit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: LPCVD Si₃N₄ Membrane Fabrication (Pressure Sensor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: 525 um Si Wafer 100 orientation] --> A1[Step 1: RCA Clean native oxide removal via dilute HF dip]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Thermal Oxidation grow 500 nm SiO2 sacrificial layer in wet O2 at 1100 C]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: LPCVD Si3N4 deposit 200 nm Si3N4 using SiH2Cl2 plus NH3 at 800 C 30 Pa]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Photolithography pattern backside window using AZ 4620 photoresist]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: RIE Etch open backside Si3N4 using CF4 plus CHF3 plasma]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: KOH Anisotropic Etch 40 percent KOH at 80 C etches Si through the window and stops on front SiO2]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: HF Vapor Etch release Si3N4 membrane by removing underlying SiO2]
    A7 --> A8[Final Si3N4 freestanding membrane ready for pressure sensor packaging]
```

## 4.2 Material Selection Block Diagram — Choosing the Right Si Compound

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Need a MEMS Material] --> Q1{High Temperature Operation gt 600 C}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Use SiC]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Need KOH Masking}
    Q2 -- Yes --> S2[Use LPCVD Si3N4 as mask + SiO2 as etch stop]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need Low k Dielectric}
    Q3 -- Yes --> S3[Use SiO2]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need Tunable Mechanical Stress}
    Q4 -- Yes --> S4[Use PECVD Si3N4 stress tunable from 500 MPa compressive to 500 MPa tensile]
    Q4 -- No --> S5[Use Thermal SiO2 default passivation]
```

## 4.3 Etch Selectivity Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0[Incoming Wafer with Si3N4 on SiO2 on Si] --> E1{Target Etch}
    E1 -- Remove Si3N4 only --> X1[Hot H3PO4 180 C 60 to 80 nm per min — SiO2 untouched]
    E1 -- Remove SiO2 only --> X2[BHF or vapor HF — Si3N4 acts as mask]
    E1 -- Remove Si substrate --> X3[KOH 40 percent at 80 C — Si3N4 acts as mask]
    E1 -- Remove all dielectics --> X4[RIE with CF4 plus O2 — non selective plasma etch]
    X1 --> OUT[Process Complete]
    X2 --> OUT
    X3 --> OUT
    X4 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Stress Regimes in a Si₃N₄ Cantilever (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Si3N4 Film Stress State] --> S1{LPCVD Default}
    S1 -- 800 C stoichiometric --> S2[High Tensile Stress plus 1 GPa]
    S2 --> S2A[Effect: Cantilever curls upward if released]
    S0 --> S3{PECVD with high RF power}
    S3 -- 300 C Si rich --> S4[Compressive Stress minus 500 MPa]
    S4 --> S4A[Effect: Cantilever buckles upward if released]
    S0 --> S5{PECVD with low RF power}
    S5 -- 300 C N rich --> S6[Low Tensile Stress plus 100 MPa]
    S6 --> S6A[Effect: Cantilever remains flat — ideal for sensors]
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture of a Si₃N₄ Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[External Pressure applied] --> P1[Deflects Si3N4 membrane]
    P1 --> P2[Piezoresistive polysilicon strain gauges detect strain]
    P2 --> P3[Wheatstone bridge outputs differential voltage]
    P3 --> P4[Signal conditioning amplifier]
    P4 --> P5[ADC and microcontroller]
    P5 --> P6[Digital pressure readout]
    P1 -. Bottom Side .-> P1A[KOH etched cavity provides reference pressure port]
```
