## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
*List any three roles played by silicon dioxide in MEMS device fabrication.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Dielectric Layer:** Provides electrical insulation between conducting layers; used as gate oxide in MOS-based MEMS transducers (3 marks allocation: 1 mark per point).
2. **Sacrificial Layer:** Temporarily supports the polysilicon structural layer during fabrication and is selectively removed using HF in surface micromachining to release the MEMS structure.
3. **Structural / Passivation Layer:** Acts as a protective barrier against moisture, ionic contamination, and mechanical damage; also functions as a mask against dopant diffusion during ion implantation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
*Differentiate between dry and wet thermal oxidation of silicon.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Dry Oxidation | Wet Oxidation |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidant | Pure O₂ | Water vapour (H₂O) |
| Growth rate | Slow | Fast (~10× dry) |
| Temperature | 1000 – 1200 °C | 800 – 1000 °C |
| Oxide quality | High density, low defect | Slightly porous |
| Application | Thin gate oxide (< 100 nm) | Thick field oxide (> 100 nm) |

(Mark split: 1 mark dry, 1 mark wet, 1 mark difference.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions in ESE carry 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The internal choice allows the student to pick **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Thermal Oxidation Kinetics*

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 1]**
**(a)** Derive the **Deal–Grove linear-parabolic growth law** for thermal oxidation of silicon, clearly stating the three flux components and the underlying assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon wafer is oxidized in **wet O₂ at 1000 °C**. The parabolic rate constant is $B = 0.45 \, \mu\text{m}^2/\text{h}$ and the linear rate constant is $B/A = 0.85 \, \mu\text{m}/\text{h}$. Calculate the oxidation time required to grow an oxide of thickness **0.6 μm** on a bare wafer. Identify whether the growth is diffusion-limited or reaction-limited. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Deal–Grove Derivation (7 marks):**

The derivation proceeds in **five** logical steps, as presented in Section 3.1 of these notes:

1. **Define oxidant concentration $C(x,t)$** in the oxide layer; assume quasi-steady-state so $\partial C/\partial t \approx 0$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Express the three fluxes** in terms of mass-transfer coefficient $h$, diffusivity $D_e$, and surface reaction constant $k_s$:

$$F_1 = h(C_g - C^*), \quad F_2 = D_e \frac{C^* - C_i}{x_0}, \quad F_3 = k_s C_i$$

*(1 mark for writing expressions, 1 mark for variable definitions.)*
3. **Apply steady-state equality $F_1 = F_2 = F_3 = F$** and eliminate $C_i, C^*$:

$$F = \frac{C_g}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x_0}{D_e} + \dfrac{1}{k_s}}$$

*(1 mark)*
4. **Relate flux to growth rate** via $dx_0/dt = F/N_1$ and identify $A$ and $B$:

$$A = 2 D_e \left(\frac{1}{k_s} + \frac{1}{h}\right), \quad B = \frac{2 D_e C_g}{N_1}$$

*(1 mark)*
5. **Integrate** the resulting differential equation to obtain the **master equation**:

$$x_0^2 + A x_0 = B (t + \tau)$$

*(2 marks — 1 for the integrated form, 1 for explaining the limiting cases linear vs parabolic.)*

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute A:**

$$A = \frac{B}{(B/A)} = \frac{0.45}{0.85} = 0.529 \, \mu\text{m}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Write the time formula from the master equation (with $\tau = 0$):**

$$t = \frac{x_0^2 + A x_0}{B}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Substitute $x_0 = 0.6 \, \mu\text{m}$:**

$$t = \frac{(0.6)^2 + (0.529)(0.6)}{0.45} = \frac{0.36 + 0.3174}{0.45} = \frac{0.6774}{0.45} = 1.505 \, \text{hours}$$

*(3 marks — 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for arithmetic, 1 mark for final units.)*

**Step 4 — Identify the regime:**

Since $x_0 = 0.6 \, \mu\text{m} > A = 0.529 \, \mu\text{m}$, the **parabolic (diffusion-limited)** regime dominates. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Concluding statement:** Wet oxidation is preferred for thick oxides because the higher water solubility in SiO₂ raises the diffusion coefficient, increasing $B$. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Properties and Sacrificial Role in MEMS*

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 1, Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** Discuss the **key material properties of SiO₂** that make it suitable as a structural and sacrificial layer in MEMS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **process flow diagram**, explain the use of SiO₂ as a **sacrificial layer** in the fabrication of a polysilicon surface-micromachined cantilever. Mention the etchant used and justify its selectivity over silicon. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Key Material Properties (7 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Rigidity:** Young's modulus ~70 GPa provides sufficient stiffness for MEMS beams. *(1 mark)*
2. **High Electrical Resistivity:** 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ Ω·cm makes it an excellent insulator for electrical isolation. *(1 mark)*
3. **Low Dielectric Constant (κ ≈ 3.9):** Minimizes capacitive loading in RF MEMS switches. *(1 mark)*
4. **Thermal Stability:** Withstands high-temperature processing without decomposition. *(1 mark)*
5. **High HF Etch Rate:** Selectively dissolved by HF; etch rate ~1 μm/min in BOE. *(1 mark)*
6. **Controllable Residual Stress:** Tensile/compressive stress tuned by deposition method; LPCVD oxide is typically compressive (~300 MPa). *(1 mark)*
7. **Optical Transparency:** Useful for optical MEMS, with refractive index 1.46. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Sacrificial Layer Process Flow (7 marks):**

The standard **sacrificial oxide** surface-micromachining sequence is:

1. **Step 1:** Grow/deposit a thin SiO₂ layer (~1–2 μm) on a silicon wafer using thermal oxidation or LPCVD. *(1 mark)*
2. **Step 2:** Deposit a polysilicon structural layer (~2 μm) over the SiO₂ using LPCVD at 580–620 °C. *(1 mark)*
3. **Step 3:** Photolithographically pattern and dry-etch (RIE) the polysilicon to define the cantilever shape. *(1 mark)*
4. **Step 4:** Etch **access holes** in the polysilicon to expose the underlying SiO₂. *(1 mark)*
5. **Step 5:** Immerse the wafer in **49 % HF** or **Buffered Oxide Etch (BOE, NH₄F:HF)** to dissolve the SiO₂ beneath the cantilever. *(1 mark)*
6. **Step 6:** The cantilever is released and becomes free-standing after rinsing and supercritical drying. *(1 mark)*

**Etchant Justification (1 mark):**
HF attacks SiO₂ via $\text{SiO}_2 + 6\text{HF} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SiF}_6 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ but does **not** attack Si because Si is not soluble in HF in the absence of oxidizers. The **selectivity of HF for SiO₂ over Si is >100:1**, which is the reason HF is the universal etchant for SiO₂ in MEMS release steps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall — Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** $B/A$ (linear rate constant, units μm/h) with $B$ (parabolic rate constant, units μm²/h). Many students interchange them, leading to numerical errors. Remember: $\dfrac{B}{A} = \dfrac{k_s C^*}{N_1}$ and $B = \dfrac{2 D_e C^*}{N_1}$.
> 2. **Always quote units explicitly** for $x_0$, $A$, $B$, and $t$. KTU evaluators deduct **0.5 marks** per missing unit.
> 3. **Do not forget the SiO₂:Si volume ratio of 2.2** when answering questions on oxide consumption of silicon. **~54 %** of the oxide thickness grows above the original Si surface, and **~46 %** consumes the Si substrate.
> 4. **Mention both Dry and Wet** oxidation when comparing — partial comparison fetches only 50 % marks.
> 5. **Draw process-flow diagrams** in Part B answers; diagrams carry ~2 marks weightage.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Silicon Dioxide for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **SiO₂** is amorphous thermally grown glass on Si; quartz is its crystalline polymorph.
- **4 critical roles in MEMS:** Dielectric, Sacrificial, Structural, Masking/Passivation.
- **Density 2.27 g/cm³**, dielectric constant **3.9**, breakdown field **~10 MV/cm**.
- **Three formation methods:** Thermal oxidation (dry/wet), CVD, PVD.
- **Dry oxidation:** O₂ at 1000–1200 °C → slow, high quality, thin gate oxides.
- **Wet oxidation:** H₂O at 800–1000 °C → fast (~10× dry), thick field oxides.
- **Deal–Grove master equation:** $x_0^2 + A x_0 = B(t + \tau)$.
- **Linear regime (thin oxide):** $x_0 \approx (B/A)\,t$ → reaction-limited at Si/SiO₂ interface.
- **Parabolic regime (thick oxide):** $x_0 \approx \sqrt{B\,t}$ → diffusion-limited through oxide.
- **Si consumption ratio:** SiO₂:Si ≈ 2.2:1 in volume; ~54 % grows above the original Si surface.
- **Universal MEMS etchant:** HF or BOE; **selectivity over Si > 100:1**.
- **Sacrificial release sequence:** Deposit SiO₂ → deposit poly-Si → pattern poly-Si → etch access holes → HF release → dry.
- **Residual stress of thermal SiO₂:** ~300 MPa compressive (LPCVD oxide).
- **In examination answers always:** state the equation, define all symbols, include units, and end with a one-line engineering interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polysilicon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polysilicon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Polysilicon — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polysilicon** (also called *polycrystalline silicon* or simply *poly-Si*) is a high-purity, multi-grained form of elemental silicon composed of numerous small, randomly oriented single-crystal regions (called **crystallites** or **grains**) separated by disordered atomic boundaries known as **grain boundaries**. In the context of MEMS and integrated-circuit (IC) fabrication, polysilicon is conventionally deposited using **Low-Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (LPCVD)** at temperatures between **580 °C and 650 °C**, using silane (SiH₄) as the precursor gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Polysilicon is treated as the **workhorse structural and functional material** in surface-micromachined MEMS. It is the most widely used material after single-crystal silicon in microsystems engineering because it can be deposited in thin-film form, patterned lithographically, doped for electrical conductivity, and released as free-standing mechanical structures.

The standard textbook-style description (consistent with the KTU module objectives) defines polysilicon as a *thin-film engineering material* whose properties are *tunable* by controlling grain size, doping concentration, deposition temperature, and post-deposition annealing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a **mosaic tile floor**, where each tiny square tile is a perfect, uniform crystal of silicon (atoms arranged in an orderly, repeating pattern). Now, instead of laying these tiles in one continuous direction, every tile is rotated at a slightly different angle relative to its neighbor. The lines where two misaligned tiles meet are messy, irregular zones — these are the **grain boundaries**.

- **Single-crystal silicon** = one giant, perfectly aligned tile covering the entire floor (every atom in a flawless, repeating diamond-cubic pattern).
- **Polysilicon** = thousands of small, randomly oriented tiles bonded together with imperfect seams (grain boundaries).
- **Amorphous silicon (a-Si)** = no tiles at all; atoms are scattered with no long-range order.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Polysilicon is "many tiny single-crystals of silicon glued together at random angles." Each tiny crystal behaves like perfect silicon, but at the boundaries between them, atoms are disordered, which gives polysilicon its unique combination of mechanical strength and tunable electrical properties.

## 1.3 Why Polysilicon Matters in MEMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics (remember these for the exam):**
> - **Young's Modulus (E):** ≈ **160 GPa** (very close to single-crystal Si ≈ 169 GPa)
> - **Density (ρ):** ≈ **2.32 g/cm³**
> - **Residual Stress:** tunable from **tensile** to **compressive** by annealing
> - **Dopant Range:** phosphorus or boron at **10¹⁴ – 10²¹ atoms/cm³**
> - **Resistivity:** from **< 1 mΩ·cm** (heavily doped) to **> 10⁴ Ω·cm** (undoped)

These properties make polysilicon uniquely suited to build **beams, plates, membranes, gears, mirrors, and electrodes** in MEMS devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microstructural Anatomy of Polysilicon

The internal architecture of a polysilicon film can be broken into three distinct regions:

1. **Grains (Crystallites):** Each grain is a small, single-crystal domain where silicon atoms are arranged in the diamond-cubic lattice. Typical grain size ranges from **0.05 µm to 1 µm**, depending on deposition conditions and annealing.

2. **Grain Boundaries:** These are 2D defects (transition zones typically 0.5–1 nm wide) where adjacent grains meet. Atoms in the boundary have **dangling bonds**, **dislocations**, and **segregated dopants**. Grain boundaries act as:
   - **Trap states** for charge carriers
   - **Diffusion paths** for dopants
   - **Scattering centers** for phonons and electrons

3. **Interface Layers:** At the top and bottom of the film, there are transition regions where the film contacts the substrate (typically SiO₂). The bottom interface often has a thin "incubation layer" of fine grains or amorphous-like material.

## 2.2 Grain Size — The Master Control Knob

**Why is grain size so important?** Almost every property of polysilicon depends on it:

| Property | Effect of Increasing Grain Size |
|----------|-------------------------------|
| Mechanical strength (yield stress) | Increases |
| Electrical conductivity | Increases (fewer scattering centers) |
| Dopant diffusivity | Decreases (less boundary diffusion) |
| Surface roughness | Usually increases |
| Optical reflectivity | Increases (less grain-boundary scattering) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hall–Petch Relationship** (mechanical strengthening in polycrystals):
> $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k}{\sqrt{d}}$$
> where *σ_y* is the yield stress, *d* is the average grain diameter, and *k* is the Hall–Petch coefficient. This shows why smaller grains *strengthen* the film by blocking dislocation motion.

## 2.3 Deposition Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

LPCVD polysilicon deposition using silane proceeds as:

**Step 1: Gas transport** — SiH₄ molecules diffuse to the wafer surface.
**Step 2: Adsorption** — SiH₄ chemisorbs onto the surface.
**Step 3: Pyrolysis** — Surface reaction: SiH₄ → Si + 2H₂.
**Step 4: Nucleation** — Small Si clusters form on the substrate (SiO₂), creating an *incubation layer*.
**Step 5: Grain growth** — Existing grains grow laterally and vertically; new grains nucleate between them.
**Step 6: Coalescence** — Grains meet and form a continuous film.

The deposition rate follows an **Arrhenius-type** behavior:
$$R_d = R_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$$
with $E_a \approx 1.7$ eV for silane-based LPCVD. Above ~625 °C, the deposition becomes **surface-reaction limited** (good uniformity); below it, the process is **mass-transport limited** (poor uniformity but high rate).

## 2.4 Doping Polysilicon

Doping is essential to make polysilicon electrically conductive. Two main methods:

1. **In-situ doping:** Add PH₃ or B₂H₆ to the SiH₄ flow during deposition. Achieves uniform dopant distribution within each grain.
2. **Ion implantation / diffusion:** Dope after deposition. Dopants segregate heavily to grain boundaries.

The **effective carrier concentration** in polysilicon is given by:
$$n_{\text{eff}} = n_{\text{grain}} - n_{\text{GB,trapped}}$$
where grain-boundary trap states capture free carriers, reducing conductivity.

## 2.5 Residual Stress — The MEMS Designer's Nemesis

Residual stress in polysilicon arises from two sources:

- **Intrinsic stress ($\sigma_i$):** Due to grain-boundary relaxation, defect density, and growth dynamics during deposition.
- **Thermal stress ($\sigma_{th}$):** Due to the thermal-expansion mismatch between polysilicon and the substrate after high-temperature processing.

$$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_i + \sigma_{th} = \sigma_i + \frac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$$

where:
- $E_f$ = Young's modulus of the film
- $\nu_f$ = Poisson's ratio
- $\alpha_f, \alpha_s$ = thermal expansion coefficients of film and substrate
- $\Delta T$ = temperature change from deposition (zero-stress) temperature to room temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A free-standing polysilicon beam with **tensile** stress stays straight (good for resonant sensors). A beam with **compressive** stress buckles or curls — disastrous for mechanical devices. Annealing at **~1000 °C in N₂ for 1 hour** is used to convert intrinsic compressive stress to tensile stress, producing "stress-free" or slightly tensile films ideal for MEMS.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Equation | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|--------------------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Young's Modulus | $E$ | ≈ **160 GPa** |
| 2 | Density | $\rho$ | **2.32 g/cm³** |
| 3 | Poisson's ratio | $\nu$ | ≈ **0.22** |
| 4 | Yield stress (Hall–Petch) | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k / \sqrt{d}$ | 1–3 GPa |
| 5 | Deposition rate | $R_d = R_0 e^{-E_a/k_B T}$ | nm/min |
| 6 | Thermal stress | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_f}{1-\nu_f}(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$ | MPa–GPa |
| 7 | Residual stress | $\sigma_{res} = \sigma_i + \sigma_{th}$ | tunable |
| 8 | Effective carrier density | $n_{\text{eff}} = n_{\text{grain}} - n_{\text{GB,trap}}$ | cm⁻³ |
| 9 | Resistivity (heavily doped) | $\rho_e$ | < 1 mΩ·cm |
| 10 | Fracture strength | $\sigma_f$ | 1–3 GPa |
| 11 | Thermal conductivity | $k_{th}$ | ≈ 30 W/(m·K) |
| 12 | Coefficient of thermal expansion | $\alpha$ | 2.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Polysilicon is used in MEMS as:

- **Structural layer:** beams, plates, membranes, gears, accelerometers (Analog Devices ADXL series).
- **Sacrificial layer (less common):** etched away to release underlying structures.
- **Electrical interconnect / electrode:** doped poly-Si wires carry signals in integrated MEMS-CMOS systems.
- **Sense / actuator element:** piezoresistive strain gauges; thermistors; heaters.
- **Optical layer:** polysilicon micromirrors in DLP/DMD projectors (Texas Instruments).
- **Solar cells:** polysilicon forms the photovoltaic absorber in many commercial solar panels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Stress in a Polysilicon Film on SiO₂ Substrate

We will derive the expression for thermal stress that develops in a polysilicon thin film when cooled from the deposition/annealing temperature down to room temperature.

### Step 1: Define the physical situation

Consider a thin polysilicon film of thickness $t_f$ deposited on a thick SiO₂ substrate. Both the film and substrate are free to contract as temperature drops, but they are *bonded* at the interface. Because their coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) differ, this bonding produces internal stress.

### Step 2: Write the free (unconstrained) thermal strains

If the film and substrate were not bonded, each would contract by its own thermal strain:
$$\varepsilon_f^{\text{free}} = \alpha_f \Delta T, \qquad \varepsilon_s^{\text{free}} = \alpha_s \Delta T$$

### Step 3: Apply the biaxial stress–strain relation in the film

For a thin film under biaxial stress (no stress in the through-thickness direction, $\sigma_z = 0$), Hooke's law gives the in-plane strain as:
$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{\sigma_{th}}{E_f}(1 - \nu_f)$$

### Step 4: Enforce strain compatibility at the interface

Because the film is firmly bonded to the substrate, the in-plane strain in the film must equal the substrate strain (assuming the substrate is much thicker and is unaffected):
$$\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_s^{\text{free}}$$

The strain in the film = the free thermal strain of the substrate (since the substrate, being thick, dictates the deformation):
$$\frac{\sigma_{th}}{E_f}(1 - \nu_f) = \alpha_s \Delta T$$

But this neglects the film's intrinsic thermal contraction. The *mismatch strain* that the stress must accommodate is:
$$\Delta \varepsilon = (\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$$

### Step 5: Solve for the biaxial thermal stress

Substituting the mismatch strain into the biaxial stress–strain relation:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}\Delta \varepsilon = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$$

This is the final result. We can verify dimensions:
- $E_f$ has units of Pa.
- $(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)$ has units of 1/K.
- $\Delta T$ has units of K.
- $1 - \nu_f$ is dimensionless.
- Therefore, $\sigma_{th}$ has units of Pa. ✔

### Step 6: Numerical estimation (typical KTU problem)

Take $E_f = 160$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.22$, $\alpha_f = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K, $\alpha_s = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /K (SiO₂), and $\Delta T = 1000 - 25 = 975$ K.

$$\alpha_f - \alpha_s = (2.6 - 0.5) \times 10^{-6} = 2.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /K}$$

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{160 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.22} \times (2.1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 975$$

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{160 \times 10^9}{0.78} \times 2.0475 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_{th} \approx 205.13 \times 10^9 \times 2.0475 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sigma_{th} \approx 4.2 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 420 \text{ MPa}$$

This is a *substantial* tensile stress — close to the yield strength of polysilicon, which is why controlling $\alpha_s$ (e.g., by using a low-expansion substrate) is so important in MEMS design.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stress-Engineering Trade-offs in Polysilicon

| Engineering Goal | Mechanism | Process Lever | Risk |
|-----------------|-----------|---------------|------|
| Reduce tensile stress | Increase $\alpha_s$ to match $\alpha_f$ | Use different underlayer | May change electrical isolation |
| Convert compressive → tensile | Grain regrowth, void elimination | High-T anneal (1000 °C, 1 h, N₂) | Dopant redistribution |
| Increase conductivity | Higher dopant concentration | In-situ doping, implant + anneal | Stress increase, defect formation |
| Increase fracture strength | Smaller grain size | Lower deposition T, no anneal | Higher resistivity |
| Reduce surface roughness | Larger grains, longer anneal | High-T anneal | Increased stress gradient |
| Increase piezoresistance | Strong texture, large grains | Specific doping, anneal | Less mechanical strength |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microstructure of Polysilicon (Mermaid Block Diagram)

Below is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing the hierarchical structure of a polysilicon film, from atoms → grains → grain boundaries → film:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["ATOMIC LEVEL Silicon atoms in diamond cubic lattice"] --> B["GRAIN Single crystal region 50 nm to 1 micrometer diameter atoms perfectly ordered"]
    B --> C["GRAIN BOUNDARY 0.5 to 1 nm wide disordered atoms dangling bonds segregated dopants"]
    C --> D["POLYCRYSTALLITE Two or more grains meeting at a boundary"]
    D --> E["POLYSILICON FILM Continuous 0.1 to 10 micrometer thick layer covering substrate"]
    E --> F["MEMS STRUCTURE Beam Plate Membrane Comb finger Gear Mirror"]
    F --> G["MEMS DEVICE Accelerometer Gyroscope Pressure sensor Micromirror RF switch"]
```

## 4.2 Polysilicon Surface-Micromachining Process Flow

This schematic summarizes the standard **PolyMUMPs / SUMMiT** style process used to manufacture free-standing polysilicon MEMS structures:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Step 1 Start with silicon wafer covered with silicon nitride isolation layer"] --> P2["Step 2 Deposit and pattern PHOSPHOSILICATE GLASS PSG sacrificial layer"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3 Deposit first POLYSILICON POLY 0 structural layer by LPCVD"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4 Pattern POLY 0 by reactive ion etching"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5 Deposit second sacrificial layer"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6 Deposit second POLYSILICON POLY 1 structural layer"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7 Pattern POLY 1"]
    P7 --> P8["Step 8 Repeat for POLY 2 if needed"]
    P8 --> P9["Step 9 Final lithography to open release etch windows"]
    P9 --> P10["Step 10 Release in HYDROFLUORIC ACID HF vapor or liquid frees polysilicon structures"]
```

## 4.3 Stress-Engineering Decision Tree for Polysilicon Films

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Polysilicon Film After Deposition"] --> S2{"Measure Residual Stress"}
    S2 -- Compressive --> S3["Risk Buckling curling Delamination"]
    S3 --> S4["Apply HIGH TEMPERATURE ANNEAL 1000 C 1 hour N2 atmosphere"]
    S4 --> S5["Grain growth voids close stress converts to tensile"]
    S2 -- Tensile --> S6["Acceptable for most MEMS beams and plates"]
    S6 --> S7["Verify fracture margin stays below yield strength"]
    S2 -- Low stress ideal --> S8["Use for resonant sensors and precision mirrors"]
    S5 --> S9["Re measure stress confirm flat released structure"]
```

## 4.4 Electrical Conduction Model in Polysilicon

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["Dopant atom in grain bulk"] --> E2["Free carrier electron or hole in grain interior"]
    E2 --> E3["Carrier travels to grain boundary"]
    E3 --> E4{"Grain boundary trap state available"}
    E4 -- Yes --> E5["Carrier trapped potential barrier forms"]
    E4 -- No --> E6["Carrier crosses to next grain"]
    E5 --> E7["Thermionic emission over barrier"]
    E7 --> E6
    E6 --> E8["Conduction to next grain chain repeats across film"]
    E8 --> E9["Effective resistivity depends on doping and grain size"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This conduction model (the **Seto model**, 1975) explains why heavily doped polysilicon has near-single-crystal-like resistivity, while lightly doped polysilicon has very high resistivity — the boundary traps dominate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define polysilicon. List any three of its key properties that make it suitable for MEMS structural layers.**

**Model Answer:**
Polysilicon (polycrystalline silicon) is a thin-film form of silicon composed of many small, randomly oriented single-crystal grains separated by grain boundaries. It is typically deposited by LPCVD at 580–650 °C using silane as the precursor.

Three key properties for MEMS:
1. **High Young's modulus (~160 GPa)** — gives mechanical stiffness and predictable deflection.
2. **Tunable residual stress** — can be engineered to be slightly tensile, preventing buckling of released structures.
3. **Tunable electrical conductivity** — doping (in-situ or implant) allows resistivities from < 1 mΩ·cm to > 10⁴ Ω·cm, enabling its use as both a structural layer and an electrical conductor.

*(Any 3 valid properties earn full marks.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What are grain boundaries in polysilicon, and how do they affect its electrical and mechanical properties?**

**Model Answer:**
Grain boundaries are thin (0.5–1 nm wide) disordered regions where two adjacent silicon crystallites meet at a misorientation. They contain dangling bonds, dislocations, and often segregated dopant atoms.

**Effects:**
- **Electrical:** Boundary trap states capture free carriers, reducing effective carrier concentration and increasing resistivity. The Seto conduction model (thermionic emission over inter-grain barriers) describes this behavior.
- **Mechanical:** Grain boundaries block dislocation motion, so smaller grain size *increases* yield strength via the Hall–Petch relation. They also act as void-formation sites that influence residual stress.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the microstructure of polysilicon. Discuss how grain size affects the mechanical and electrical properties of polysilicon.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Derive the expression for thermal stress in a polysilicon film deposited on a SiO₂ substrate. A 2 µm thick polysilicon film ($E = 160$ GPa, $\nu = 0.22$, $\alpha_{\text{poly}} = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K) is deposited on SiO₂ ($\alpha_{\text{SiO}_2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /K) and cooled from a stress-free temperature of 1000 °C to 25 °C. Calculate the thermal stress developed.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Microstructure of polysilicon (3 marks for diagram + explanation):**

Polysilicon consists of:
- **Grains (crystallites):** Small, single-crystal silicon regions, typically 0.05–1 µm in size, each with a randomly oriented diamond-cubic lattice.
- **Grain boundaries:** Disordered transition zones (0.5–1 nm) between adjacent grains; contain dangling bonds and segregated dopants.
- **Interfaces:** Top surface (free surface) and bottom interface with the substrate (typically SiO₂).

```
[Diagram description — should show irregular polygonal grains with shaded
 boundaries and arrows pointing to grains, grain boundaries, and substrate]
```

**Effect of grain size (4 marks):**

| Property | Smaller grain size | Larger grain size |
|----------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Yield strength (Hall–Petch) | Higher | Lower |
| Resistivity (light doping) | Higher | Lower |
| Dopant diffusivity | Higher (boundary diffusion) | Lower |
| Surface roughness | Lower | Higher |
| Piezoresistive gauge factor | Variable | Higher for heavy doping |

*Conclusion:* Grain size is a *master control parameter* for polysilicon engineering, and it can be tuned by deposition temperature, pressure, and post-deposition annealing.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Derivation (4 marks):** See Section 3.1 above for the full step-by-step derivation, ending at:
$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$$

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- $E_f = 160$ GPa $= 160 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\nu_f = 0.22$
- $\alpha_f = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- $\alpha_s = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- $\Delta T = 1000 - 25 = 975$ °C $= 975$ K

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{160 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.22} \times (2.6 - 0.5) \times 10^{-6} \times 975$$

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{160 \times 10^9}{0.78} \times 2.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 975$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 205.13 \times 10^9 \times 2.0475 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} \approx 4.20 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 420 \text{ MPa (tensile)}}$$

The positive sign indicates **tensile stress**, which is desirable for flat, released MEMS beams.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the LPCVD process for depositing polysilicon. Explain the role of deposition temperature on film properties.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is residual stress in polysilicon films? Discuss the two main sources of residual stress and the techniques used to control it. Why is stress control critical in surface-micromachined MEMS?

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**LPCVD process description (4 marks):**

Low-Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (LPCVD) of polysilicon uses **silane (SiH₄)** as the precursor gas at a low pressure of **25–150 Pa (200–1000 mTorr)** and temperatures between **580 °C and 650 °C**. The reaction is:
$$\text{SiH}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Si} + 2\text{H}_2$$

Steps: (1) gas transport, (2) adsorption, (3) surface pyrolysis, (4) nucleation on the substrate, (5) grain growth, (6) coalescence into a continuous film.

**Role of deposition temperature (3 marks):**

| Deposition Temperature | Resulting Film |
|------------------------|---------------|
| 580–600 °C (low) | Amorphous or very fine-grained film; smooth surface; high resistivity; needs high-T anneal |
| 620–650 °C (typical) | Polycrystalline with columnar grains; standard MEMS film |
| > 650 °C (high) | Large grains; rough surface; high deposition rate; reduced throughput |

At lower temperatures, the surface reaction is the rate-limiting step, giving good wafer-to-wafer uniformity. At higher temperatures, mass transport limits the rate, leading to gas-phase depletion and poor uniformity.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Definition of residual stress (2 marks):**

Residual stress is the internal mechanical stress that remains in a polysilicon film after all deposition and processing steps, measured at room temperature with no external load.

**Two main sources (3 marks):**

1. **Intrinsic stress ($\sigma_i$):** Originates from the film growth process — incomplete grain boundary relaxation, atomic peening, defect incorporation, and trapped hydrogen. It can be **compressive** (low-T deposition) or **tensile** (high-T deposition).
2. **Thermal stress ($\sigma_{th}$):** Arises from the CTE mismatch between the film and substrate upon cooling:
$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$$

For polysilicon on SiO₂, $\alpha_f > \alpha_s$, so $\sigma_{th}$ is **tensile** and is typically the dominant stress component.

**Control techniques (1 mark):**
- **High-temperature anneal** (e.g., 1000 °C / 1 h in N₂): converts compressive to tensile stress; reduces overall stress magnitude.
- **Doping:** heavy in-situ phosphorus or boron doping reduces intrinsic stress.
- **Multi-layer deposition** (poly / oxide / poly stacks).
- **Process parameter tuning:** deposition T, pressure, gas flow.

**Why stress control is critical in MEMS (1 mark):**
- Excess **tensile** stress causes beam fracture or stiction.
- **Compressive** stress causes buckling, curling, or delamination of released structures.
- For resonant devices (gyroscopes, resonators), stress must be tightly controlled to set the resonant frequency precisely.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $(1 - \nu_f)$** in the thermal stress formula. It is a *biaxial* stress, not a uniaxial stress — losing this factor costs ~2 marks.
> 2. **Sign confusion:** polysilicon on SiO₂ is tensile on cool-down (positive $\sigma_{th}$); students often write "compressive" by mistake.
> 3. **Not specifying units clearly:** GPa vs. MPa vs. Pa — the examiner will deduct marks if units are inconsistent in the final answer.
> 4. **Confusing deposition temperature regimes:** below 580 °C gives amorphous silicon, *not* polysilicon. State the regime (580–650 °C) precisely.
> 5. **In Part A answers, avoid one-line answers:** write at least 3 sentences per question; define *and* explain with an example for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Polysilicon**

- ⭐ Polysilicon = **polycrystalline silicon** = many small single-crystal grains + grain boundaries.
- ⭐ Deposition method: **LPCVD** of **SiH₄** at **580–650 °C**, **25–150 Pa**.
- ⭐ Reaction: **SiH₄ → Si + 2H₂**.
- ⭐ Three regions: **grains**, **grain boundaries**, **interfaces**.
- ⭐ Grain size: **0.05–1 µm**; tunable by deposition T, anneal, doping.
- ⭐ Mechanical properties: **E ≈ 160 GPa**, $\rho = 2.32$ g/cm³, $\nu = 0.22$.
- ⭐ Yield strength follows **Hall–Petch relation**: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k / \sqrt{d}$.
- ⭐ Residual stress = **intrinsic + thermal**.
- ⭐ **Thermal stress formula:** $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}(\alpha_f - \alpha_s)\Delta T$ — memorize exactly.
- ⭐ Polysilicon on SiO₂ → tensile thermal stress on cool-down.
- ⭐ **Anneal at 1000 °C / 1 h / N₂** converts compressive → tensile (standard MEMS process).
- ⭐ Doping: **in-situ PH₃ / B₂H₆** or **ion implantation**; resistivity tunable over **>7 orders of magnitude**.
- ⭐ **Seto model:** conduction in polysilicon is limited by thermionic emission across grain-boundary barriers.
- ⭐ Polysilicon is used as: **structural layer, electrode, piezoresistor, heater, mirror, sacrificial layer** (less common).
- ⭐ Famous examples: **Texas Instruments DLP / DMD micromirrors**, **Analog Devices accelerometers**.
- ⭐ Typical MEMS film thickness: **0.5–10 µm**.
- ⭐ Compared to single-crystal Si, polysilicon is **cheaper to deposit, easier to integrate with CMOS, and has tunable stress** — the trade-off is **lower conductivity and slightly weaker mechanical strength**.

**One-line exam punchline to remember:**
> *"Polysilicon is the workhorse structural material of surface-micromachined MEMS — its grain size, stress, and conductivity are all tunable through deposition and annealing, making it the most versatile thin-film engineering material in microsystems technology."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicon Piezo Resistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET863 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Silicon Piezo Resistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silicon Piezo Resistors** are doped monocrystalline silicon elements that exploit the **piezoresistive effect** — the change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor under applied mechanical stress — to transduce mechanical quantities (force, pressure, acceleration, strain) into a measurable electrical signal. They form the fundamental sensing element of countless MEMS devices, including pressure sensors, inertial sensors, and strain gauges.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for MEMS & Nanoelectronics, piezo resistors are classified under **Transduction Mechanisms in MEMS**, specifically as **piezoresistive transducers**. They are typically fabricated using standard CMOS-compatible processes such as **ion implantation** or **diffusion** of dopants (Boron for p-type, Phosphorus/Arsenic for n-type) into a defined region of a silicon diaphragm or cantilever.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you have a long, thin rubber tube through which water flows. The flow rate depends on the tube's diameter. Now imagine gently stretching the tube — it becomes thinner and longer, restricting the water flow. The change in flow tells you exactly how much it was stretched. 

A **silicon piezo resistor works on the same principle**, but with **electrons instead of water**:

- The **silicon crystal** is the "tube."
- **Electrical current (electrons/holes)** is the "water flow."
- **Mechanical stress** (from bending, pressure, or acceleration) is the "stretching force."
- The **change in resistance** is what we measure to deduce the applied stress.

Unlike a metal strain gauge (which simply gets thinner and longer), silicon's resistance changes dramatically because the **atomic lattice itself deforms**, altering how easily charge carriers can move through the crystal. This is what makes silicon piezo resistors **30 to 100 times more sensitive** than metallic strain gauges.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Longitudinal Gauge Factor (GF) of silicon**: $\approx 100$ to $175$ (vs. $\approx 2$ for metal strain gauges)
> - **Standard Doping Concentration**: $10^{18}$ to $10^{20} \text{ cm}^{-3}$ (p-type Boron is most common)
> - **Operating Temperature Range**: $-55^\circ\text{C}$ to $+150^\circ\text{C}$ (typical)
> - **Sheet Resistance** ($R_s$): $100$ to $500\ \Omega/\square$ (commonly tuned)
> - **Young's Modulus of Silicon** $\langle 100 \rangle$: **$E = 130\ \text{GPa}$**
> - **Poisson's Ratio of Silicon**: **$\nu \approx 0.28$**

## 1.4 Why Silicon for Piezoresistance?

| Property | Value/Behaviour | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Single-crystal availability | High-purity wafers | Predictable, reproducible piezo coefficients |
| CMOS compatibility | Standard process | Low-cost monolithic integration |
| Gauge Factor | 100–175 | Far superior to metal ($\sim 2$) |
| Mechanical quality factor | High (low internal friction) | Excellent fatigue resistance |
| Thermal stability | Moderate (with compensation) | Requires careful design |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of the Piezoresistive Effect

In semiconductors, electrical conduction is governed by the **band structure** — the energy landscape that electrons and holes must navigate. When mechanical stress is applied, the **deformation of the crystal lattice** alters the effective mass and mobility of charge carriers. This is fundamentally different from the geometric effect seen in metals.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Mechanical Stress Application**: A diaphragm, cantilever, or bridge deforms due to external force.
2. **Lattice Distortion**: Atomic bonds stretch or compress, changing bond angles and inter-atomic distances.
3. **Band Structure Modification**: The conduction band valleys and valence band maxima shift in energy, and their effective masses become anisotropic.
4. **Mobility Redistribution**: Charge carrier mobility $\mu$ changes — the average drift velocity per unit electric field is altered.
5. **Resistance Change**: Since $\rho = 1/(q \cdot n \cdot \mu)$, even small mobility shifts produce large resistivity changes.
6. **Electrical Measurement**: A Wheatstone bridge or constant-current source detects $\Delta R / R$.

## 2.2 The Piezoresistive Tensor (Advanced Insight)

The piezoresistive effect in silicon is fundamentally a **tensor phenomenon** — its magnitude depends on the orientation of the resistor relative to the crystal axes and the direction of the applied stress. The relationship is governed by the **fourth-rank piezoresistive tensor** $\pi_{ijkl}$:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho_{ij}}{\rho} = \sum_{k,l} \pi_{ijkl} \, \sigma_{kl}$$

In **matrix (Voigt) notation**, this reduces to a 6×6 symmetric matrix $\pi_{\alpha\beta}$ for cubic crystals like silicon. Only **three independent coefficients** exist for silicon:

$$\pi_{11}, \quad \pi_{12}, \quad \pi_{44}$$

These are the **three fundamental piezoresistive coefficients** of silicon. Their values (in units of $10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$) at 300 K are:

| Coefficient | n-type Silicon (Lightly Doped) | p-type Silicon (Lightly Doped) |
|---|---|---|
| $\pi_{11}$ | $-102.2$ | $+6.6$ |
| $\pi_{12}$ | $+53.4$ | $-1.1$ |
| $\pi_{44}$ | $-13.6$ | $+138.1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The shear coefficient $\pi_{44}$ **dominates** in p-type silicon, making it the preferred material for MEMS piezoresistors. The longitudinal piezo coefficient in the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction reaches about $71.8 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ for p-type, which is enormous.

## 2.3 Longitudinal and Transverse Gauge Factors

The most useful engineering relations are the **two effective gauge factors** in any chosen direction:

$$GF_L = 1 + \nu + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_L} \cdot \left[\pi_{44}/2 + (\pi_{11} - \pi_{12} - \pi_{44}) \cdot (l_1^2 m_1^2 + m_1^2 n_1^2 + n_1^2 l_1^2)\right] \cdot E$$

$$GF_T = 1 + \nu + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_T} \cdot \left[(\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{44})(l_1^2 l_2^2 + m_1^2 m_2^2 + n_1^2 n_2^2) - 2\pi_{44}(l_1 l_2 m_1 m_2 + \ldots)\right] \cdot E$$

Where $l_1, m_1, n_1$ and $l_2, m_2, n_2$ are direction cosines. For practical design, we use the **simplified engineering form**:

$$GF_L \approx 1 + \nu + \pi_L \cdot E$$

$$GF_T \approx 1 - \nu + \pi_T \cdot E$$

## 2.4 Effect of Doping Concentration

Doping has a **drastic effect** on piezoresistive sensitivity. As doping increases, sensitivity decreases because:

- Higher dopant concentration → more **impurity scattering**
- Impurity scattering is **less sensitive to lattice distortion** than phonon scattering
- Therefore, $\Delta \mu / \mu$ per unit stress decreases

The empirical relation (often used in KTU problems):

$$\pi(N) = \pi(0) \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{N}{N_{ref}}\right)^\alpha\right]^{-1}$$

with $N_{ref} \approx 2 \times 10^{18}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ for p-type silicon and $\alpha \approx 0.6$ to $0.9$.

## 2.5 Temperature Effects (TCGF)

The **Temperature Coefficient of Gauge Factor (TCGF)** is a critical KTU-favorite parameter:

$$TCGF = \frac{1}{GF} \cdot \frac{\partial GF}{\partial T} \quad \left[\%/^\circ\text{C}\right]$$

Typical values:
- Lightly doped p-type: $-0.15\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$ (decreases with temperature)
- Heavily doped p-type: $-0.04\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$
- n-type silicon: $\sim +0.05\ \%/^\circ\text{C}$ (increases — different sign!)

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Rule**: For temperature-stable bridges, use **half n-type and half p-type resistors** in the Wheatstone bridge configuration. Their opposite TCGF signs cause temperature-induced errors to **cancel**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Fundamental gauge relation | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $GF = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Geometric + piezoresistive | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | Hooke's law (uniaxial) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $R_s = \dfrac{\rho}{t}$ | Sheet resistance | $\Omega/\square$ |
| 5 | $R = R_s \cdot \dfrac{L}{W}$ | Resistance of a rectangular resistor | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $GF_L = 1 + \nu + \pi_L E$ | Longitudinal gauge factor | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $GF_T = 1 - \nu + \pi_T E$ | Transverse gauge factor | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\pi_L = \dfrac{\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44}}{2}$ | Longitudinal piezo coef. in $\langle 110 \rangle$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $\pi_T = \dfrac{\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{44}}{2}$ | Transverse piezo coef. in $\langle 110 \rangle$ | $\text{Pa}^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $V_{out} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{2}$ | Half-bridge output | Volts |
| 11 | $V_{out} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Full-bridge output | Volts |
| 12 | $TCGF = \dfrac{1}{GF}\dfrac{\partial GF}{\partial T}$ | Temperature stability | $\%/^\circ\text{C}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember**: All $GF$ equations in this sheet use silicon with $E = 130\ \text{GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.28$. The "**1**" and "**$\nu$**" terms account for the geometric (Poisson) contribution. The piezoresistive term dominates in silicon, making $GF \approx \pi \cdot E$ a useful approximation.

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Applications

- **Automotive Airbag Sensors** — Bosch and Freescale use p-type piezo resistors on a bossed diaphragm to detect crash deceleration.
- **Blood Pressure Monitors** — Medical-grade silicon pressure sensors use 4 piezo resistors in a Wheatstone bridge.
- **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** — Self-sensing piezoresistive AFM cantilevers (e.g., IBM millipede).
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** — Accelerometers in smartphones (3-axis, 9-axis).
- **Respiratory/HVAC Flow Sensors** — Piezoresistive flow sensors measure very low flow rates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Gauge Equation

### Starting Point: Definition of Resistance

The resistance of a uniform conductor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ is:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

### Step 1: Express Fractional Change

Take the differential of $R$ with respect to all three variables. Divide both sides by $R$ to get the fractional change:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

> **Conversion Logic**: We treat $\Delta L / L$ as the longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_L$ and $\Delta A / A$ as the transverse strain response. The minus sign on $A$ reflects that resistance **increases** when cross-section **decreases**.

### Step 2: Substitute Strain Components

For a uniaxial stress $\sigma$ along the resistor axis, the strains are:

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{\sigma}{E} \quad \text{(longitudinal)}$$

$$\varepsilon_T = \frac{\Delta A}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta W}{W} = -2 \nu \cdot \frac{\sigma}{E} = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon_L$$

> **Conversion Logic**: The factor of 2 appears because area change involves **two** perpendicular dimensions. Poisson's ratio $\nu$ captures the lateral contraction per unit longitudinal strain.

### Step 3: Combine Geometric Terms

Substituting back:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon_L + 2\nu \cdot \varepsilon_L = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \varepsilon_L$$

> **Conversion Logic**: This is the **fundamental piezoresistive equation**. The first term is the **piezoresistive (intrinsic)** contribution; the second term is the **geometric (extrinsic)** contribution that exists even in metals.

### Step 4: Define the Gauge Factor

By definition, the gauge factor is the fractional resistance change per unit strain:

$$GF \equiv \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon_L} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon_L}$$

**Final Result**:

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon_L \qquad \text{where} \quad GF = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon_L}}$$

For silicon, the piezoresistive term $\Delta\rho/\rho / \varepsilon_L$ is roughly $100\times$ larger than the geometric term, hence $GF \approx 100$–$175$.

## 3.2 Derivation: $\pi_L$ and $\pi_T$ for a $\langle 110 \rangle$ Resistor

A resistor fabricated on a (100) silicon wafer aligned along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction experiences an effective longitudinal piezo coefficient that is the **average of multiple tensor components**.

For longitudinal stress along $\langle 110 \rangle$:

$$\pi_L^{\langle 110 \rangle} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44}\right]$$

**Derivation Logic**:

The stress tensor is $\sigma = \sigma_0 \cdot \hat{n}$ where $\hat{n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,1,0)$ for the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction.

The induced resistivity change in the current direction (also $\langle 110 \rangle$) is:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho_L}{\rho} = \pi_L \cdot \sigma_L = \pi_L \cdot \sigma_0$$

Using the tensor contraction for cubic symmetry:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho_L}{\rho} = \pi_{11} \sigma_{11} + \pi_{12}(\sigma_{22} + \sigma_{33}) + 0 \cdot \sigma_{12} \cdot (\text{geometric terms})$$

Since $\sigma_{11} = \sigma_{22} = \sigma_0 / 2$ and $\sigma_{33} = 0$ (perpendicular to wafer surface — usually unstressed), and accounting for the off-diagonal components from rotation:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho_L}{\rho} = \pi_{11} \cdot \frac{\sigma_0}{2} + \pi_{12} \cdot \frac{\sigma_0}{2} + \pi_{44} \cdot \frac{\sigma_0}{2}$$

$$\frac{\Delta \rho_L}{\rho} = \frac{\sigma_0}{2}\left(\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44}\right)$$

Comparing with $\Delta \rho_L / \rho = \pi_L \cdot \sigma_0$:

$$\pi_L = \frac{\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44}}{2}$$

By analogous derivation, the **transverse** coefficient is:

$$\pi_T = \frac{\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} - \pi_{44}}{2}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem**: A p-type silicon piezo resistor is oriented along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction on a (100) wafer. The applied stress is $\sigma = 50\ \text{MPa}$ along the resistor length. Calculate the fractional change in resistance, the gauge factor, and the output voltage when used in a half-bridge with a 5 V supply. Given: $\pi_{11} = 6.6 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\pi_{12} = -1.1 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$, $\pi_{44} = 138.1 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$, $E = 130\ \text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute $\pi_L$**

$$\pi_L = \frac{\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44}}{2} = \frac{(6.6 - 1.1 + 138.1) \times 10^{-11}}{2} = \frac{143.6 \times 10^{-11}}{2}$$

$$\pi_L = 71.8 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Longitudinal Gauge Factor**

$$GF_L = 1 + \nu + \pi_L \cdot E = 1 + 0.28 + (71.8 \times 10^{-11})(130 \times 10^9)$$

$$GF_L = 1.28 + 93.34 = 94.62$$

> **Note**: This is a typical $GF$ for p-type $\langle 110 \rangle$ silicon resistors.

**Step 3: Compute the Strain**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{50 \times 10^6}{130 \times 10^9} = 3.846 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Fractional Resistance Change**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF_L \cdot \varepsilon = 94.62 \times 3.846 \times 10^{-4} = 3.64 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} \approx 0.0364 = 3.64\%$$

**Step 5: Half-Bridge Output Voltage**

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R / R}{2} = 5 \times \frac{0.0364}{2} = 0.091\ \text{V} = 91\ \text{mV}$$

> **Result**: A 50 MPa stress produces a 91 mV output from a 5 V half-bridge — a perfectly readable signal for typical 12-bit ADCs (1 LSB ≈ 1.2 mV).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | p-type $\langle 110 \rangle$ | n-type $\langle 100 \rangle$ | n-type $\langle 110 \rangle$ | Metal Foil Gauge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal GF | $\sim 100$ | $\sim -50$ to $-100$ | $\sim -30$ to $-60$ | $\sim 2$ |
| TCGF ($\%/^\circ$C) | $-0.15$ | $+0.05$ | $+0.05$ | $+0.01$ |
| Doping sensitivity | High | Moderate | Moderate | N/A |
| Process compatibility | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Requires bonding |
| Linearity | Excellent | Good | Good | Excellent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: From Stress to Voltage

The diagram below maps the full signal path from external mechanical stimulus to readable electrical output — useful for KTU theory questions on "explain the working of a piezo resistive sensor."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Physical Stimulus: Pressure, Force, Acceleration] --> B[Silicon Diaphragm or Cantilever Structure]
    B --> C[Mechanical Stress Distribution sigma_xx, sigma_yy, sigma_xy]
    C --> D[Piezo Resistor on High-Stress Region]
    D --> E[Change in Resistivity Delta rho via piezoresistive effect]
    E --> F[Change in Resistance Delta R in active arms]
    F --> G[Wheatstone Bridge: 4 piezo resistors in full bridge]
    G --> H[Differential Output Voltage Vout = Vs x Delta R / R]
    H --> I[Amplifier and Signal Conditioning]
    I --> J[ADC and Microprocessor]
    J --> K[Digital Reading: Pressure, Force, or Acceleration]
```

## 4.2 Layout of a Wheatstone Bridge on a Square Diaphragm

A square diaphragm has **maximum stress at the midpoint of each edge**. Placing four piezo resistors there, oriented in a "diamond" configuration, gives a full bridge with maximum sensitivity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[R1: p-type lt110gt at LEFT edge] --- NodeTop[Top Node Vs]
    NodeTop --- R3[R3: p-type lt110gt at RIGHT edge]
    R1 --- NodeA[Node A: Vout+]
    R3 --- NodeB[Node B: Vout-]
    NodeA --- R2[R2: p-type lt110gt at BOTTOM edge]
    R2 --- NodeGND[Bottom Node: GND]
    NodeB --- R4[R4: p-type lt110gt at TOP edge]
    R4 --- NodeGND
```

> **Bridge Behaviour**: When pressure deflects the diaphragm, R1 and R3 experience longitudinal stress (resistance increases) while R2 and R4 experience transverse stress (resistance decreases) — or vice versa depending on orientation. This **doubles the bridge output** and provides **automatic temperature compensation** because all four resistors drift together with temperature.

## 4.3 Process Flow: Fabrication of a Silicon Piezo Resistor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Bare lt100gt Silicon Wafer] --> S1[Thermal Oxidation to grow SiO2 mask]
    S1 --> S2[Photolithography to define resistor region]
    S2 --> S3[Ion Implantation of Boron at 10 to 100 keV]
    S3 --> S4[Drive-in Anneal at 1000 C for 30 min]
    S4 --> S5[Deposit and pattern Aluminum contacts]
    S5 --> S6[Anisotropic KOH Etching of backside to form diaphragm]
    S6 --> S7[Final: Packaged Piezo resistive Pressure Sensor]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Block: Orientation Dependence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Silicon Crystal with lt100gt axes] --> B{Choose Resistor Orientation}
    B -- lt100gt direction --> C[Use pi_11 and pi_12 only]
    B -- lt110gt direction --> D[Combines pi_11, pi_12, AND pi_44]
    B -- lt111gt direction --> E[All three coefficients contribute symmetrically]
    C --> F[Moderate Sensitivity]
    D --> G[HIGHEST Sensitivity for p-type silicon]
    E --> H[Used in specialized Hall-effect-like sensors]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight**: When asked to design a high-sensitivity piezo resistor, **always** recommend the $\langle 110 \rangle$ orientation on a (100) wafer with **p-type doping** — this is the textbook "optimal" configuration and appears in nearly every KTU numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: Define the piezoresistive effect. Why is silicon preferred over metals for piezo resistive sensors?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

The **piezoresistive effect** is the change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor when subjected to mechanical stress, arising from the modification of the band structure and carrier mobility rather than purely geometric changes.

Silicon is preferred because:
- **High gauge factor** ($GF \approx 100$–$175$ for p-type) compared to $\sim 2$ for metals (1 mark).
- **CMOS-compatible** fabrication enables low-cost monolithic integration of sensor + circuitry (1 mark).
- **Tunable sensitivity** through doping concentration and crystal orientation (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: What is the significance of the $\langle 110 \rangle$ crystal orientation for a silicon piezo resistor on a (100) wafer?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

In a (100) silicon wafer, resistors aligned along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction experience a **maximum effective longitudinal piezo coefficient** given by $\pi_L = (\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44})/2$ (1 mark). For p-type silicon, $\pi_{44}$ is very large ($\sim 138 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$), and it adds constructively to the other coefficients in this direction (1 mark). This makes the $\langle 110 \rangle$ orientation the **preferred choice** for high-sensitivity MEMS pressure and acceleration sensors (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module 1 — With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the fundamental gauge factor equation for a piezo resistor, clearly distinguishing between the **geometric** and **piezoresistive** contributions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A p-type silicon piezo resistor of dimensions $L = 100\ \mu\text{m}$, $W = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, $t = 0.5\ \mu\text{m}$ is oriented along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction. The longitudinal piezo coefficient is $\pi_L = 71.8 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ and sheet resistance $R_s = 200\ \Omega/\square$. An axial stress of 40 MPa is applied.

Compute: (i) The longitudinal gauge factor, (ii) The unstrained resistance, (iii) The fractional change in resistance, (iv) The change in resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**:

Starting with $R = \rho L / A$, take the differential:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

Under uniaxial stress $\sigma$ along the resistor length:

$$\frac{\Delta L}{L} = \varepsilon_L = \frac{\sigma}{E}, \quad \frac{\Delta A}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon_L$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \varepsilon_L$$

Dividing by $\varepsilon_L$:

$$GF = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon_L}$$

The first term $(1 + 2\nu)$ is the **geometric contribution** (present in all conductors including metals), while the second term is the **intrinsic piezoresistive contribution** unique to semiconductors. For silicon, this term dominates, giving $GF \approx 100$ versus $\sim 2$ for metals.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**:

**(i) Longitudinal Gauge Factor** (2 marks):

$$GF_L = 1 + \nu + \pi_L \cdot E = 1 + 0.28 + (71.8 \times 10^{-11})(130 \times 10^9)$$

$$GF_L = 1.28 + 93.34 = 94.62$$

**(ii) Unstrained Resistance** (2 marks):

$$R = R_s \cdot \frac{L}{W} = 200 \cdot \frac{100}{10} = 2000\ \Omega$$

**(iii) Fractional Resistance Change** (1.5 marks):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{130 \times 10^9} = 3.077 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 94.62 \times 3.077 \times 10^{-4} = 2.91 \times 10^{-2} \approx 2.91\%$$

**(iv) Change in Resistance** (1.5 marks):

$$\Delta R = 0.0291 \times 2000 = 58.2\ \Omega$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **tensor nature of the piezoresistive effect** in silicon. List the three independent coefficients and explain why $\pi_{44}$ is particularly important for p-type silicon. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wheatstone bridge uses four identical p-type silicon piezo resistors ($R_0 = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ each) at the edges of a square diaphragm. Two opposite resistors (R1, R3) experience longitudinal stress and gain $\Delta R = +5\ \Omega$ each, while the other two (R2, R4) experience transverse stress and lose $\Delta R = -5\ \Omega$ each. If the bridge supply is $V_s = 5\ \text{V}$, compute the differential output voltage and the sensitivity in mV/V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Tensor Nature of Piezoresistivity (7 marks)**:

The piezoresistive effect in silicon is fundamentally **anisotropic** and must be described by a **fourth-rank tensor** $\pi_{ijkl}$ relating resistivity changes to stress components. For a cubic crystal like silicon, symmetry reduces the 81 tensor components to just **three independent coefficients**: $\pi_{11}$, $\pi_{12}$, and $\pi_{44}$ (3 marks).

The longitudinal gauge factor in any crystallographic direction is computed by combining these coefficients through the tensor transformation. For the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction, the effective coefficient involves all three:

$$\pi_L^{\langle 110 \rangle} = \frac{\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44}}{2}$$

The shear coefficient $\pi_{44}$ **dominates** in p-type silicon ($\sim 138 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$) because the valence band consists of heavy-hole and light-hole bands that have **highly anisotropic effective masses**. When the lattice is sheared, the populations of these bands shift dramatically, causing a large change in conductivity (2 marks). In contrast, the conduction band valleys of n-type silicon are more symmetric, giving a smaller $\pi_{44}$ (2 marks).

**(b) Bridge Output (7 marks)**:

For a full Wheatstone bridge with all four arms active, the differential output is:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \left[\frac{R_1 + \Delta R_1}{R_1 + \Delta R_1 + R_2 + \Delta R_2} - \frac{R_4 + \Delta R_4}{R_3 + \Delta R_3 + R_4 + \Delta R_4}\right]$$

With $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_0 = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $\Delta R_1 = +\Delta R = +5\ \Omega$, $\Delta R_2 = -\Delta R = -5\ \Omega$:

$$V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta R_1 - \Delta R_2}{2 R_0} = 5 \cdot \frac{5 - (-5)}{2 \times 1000} = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{2000}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 25\ \text{mV}}$$

**Sensitivity** (2 marks):

$$S = \frac{V_{out}}{V_s} = \frac{25\ \text{mV}}{5\ \text{V}} = 5\ \text{mV/V}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** (verified by past KTU paper patterns):
> 1. **Forgetting the geometric term** $(1 + 2\nu)$ — KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark for this omission in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing longitudinal and transverse** gauge factors — transverse uses $1 - \nu$, longitudinal uses $1 + \nu$.
> 3. **Using $\pi_{11}$ alone for $\langle 110 \rangle$** — must use the **combination** $\pi_L = (\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44})/2$. This is a favourite trap in 7-mark questions.
> 4. **Ignoring units** of $\pi$ in $10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$ — students often get 100× wrong answers.
> 5. **Half-bridge vs full-bridge confusion**: full-bridge output is $V_s \cdot \Delta R/R$, half-bridge is half of that.
> 6. **Sign of $\Delta R$**: longitudinal stress **increases** resistance in p-type; transverse stress **decreases** it.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition**: Silicon piezo resistor — a doped Si element whose resistivity changes with stress via the piezoresistive effect.
- **Key relation**: $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$, with $GF = (1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$.
- **Optimal configuration**: p-type, $\langle 110 \rangle$ orientation on (100) wafer.
- **Key coefficients**: $\pi_{11}$, $\pi_{12}$, $\pi_{44}$ — only 3 independent for cubic silicon.
- **Effective $\pi_L^{\langle 110\rangle} = (\pi_{11} + \pi_{12} + \pi_{44})/2$**.
- **Gauge factor range**: $100$ to $175$ for silicon vs $\sim 2$ for metals.
- **Doping**: Higher doping → lower sensitivity (impurity scattering is stress-insensitive).
- **Temperature**: TCGF of p-type is **negative**, of n-type is **positive** → use mixed-type bridge for compensation.
- **Bridge**: 4 piezo resistors on diaphragm edges form a full Wheatstone bridge with maximum sensitivity and built-in temperature compensation.
- **Process**: Boron ion implantation + KOH anisotropic etching for diaphragm.
- **Applications**: pressure sensors, accelerometers, AFM cantilevers, IMUs.
- **Constants to remember**: $E = 130\ \text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.28$, $\pi_{44}(p) \approx 138 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exams**: Always show the **derivation of the gauge factor** from $R = \rho L / A$ — this is a 7-mark question in nearly every KTU paper. Memorize the $\pi_{44}$ value and remember that p-type silicon has the largest sensitivity. Mention CMOS compatibility as the key practical advantage — examiners reward this point consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymers in MEMS: SU-8
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymers in MEMS: SU-8

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SU-8** is a high-aspect-ratio, **negative-tone, epoxy-based photoresist** originally developed by **IBM** in the late 1980s (later commercialized by MicroChem Corp. / Kayaku Advanced Materials). Chemically, it is a multifunctional **bisphenol-A novalac epoxy resin** dissolved in an organic solvent (typically **gamma-butyrolactone / GBL**), sensitized with a **triaryl sulfonium salt photoacid generator (PAG)**. The designation "SU-8" derives from the fact that each oligomer molecule nominally contains **8 epoxy rings**, which upon UV exposure and crosslinking forms a dense three-dimensional network.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, SU-8 is positioned as the **workhorse structural polymer** of the MEMS field, used wherever thick (1 µm – 500 µm) microstructures with near-vertical sidewalls are required.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Scaffolding"

Imagine you are constructing the **steel framework of a high-rise building** (the 3D microstructure). Ordinary photoresists behave like **thin wooden planks** — easy to cut, but they cannot stand tall without buckling. SU-8, by contrast, behaves like a **prefabricated steel reinforcement cage** poured into a mould and then chemically welded (crosslinked) by ultraviolet light.

- The **novalac backbone** = the steel rods (provides structural continuity).
- The **8 epoxy end-groups** = the welded joints (every group can chemically bond to another, creating a 3D cage).
- The **photoacid generator (PAG)** = the welding torch (UV light triggers acid release, which catalyses the crosslinking).
- The **UV exposure step** = the moment the welding torch is ignited.
- The **post-exposure bake (PEB)** = the cooling time required for the welds to harden.

Once the welding is done, the structure is **chemically and mechanically locked**; chemicals that dissolve the un-crosslinked resin (the developer) will wash away everything *except* the building frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Negative" tone?** Because the regions that are *exposed* to UV remain on the substrate after development. Light = solid. This is the opposite of positive resists like PMMA or AZ-series, where light = dissolved.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> Key SU-8 metrics the KTU board examiners frequently quote:
> - **Young's Modulus (E):** 4.0 – 4.4 GPa (fully crosslinked)
> - **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** ≈ **210 °C** (post-baked)
> - **Tensile Strength:** ≈ 34 – 60 MPa
> - **Density:** ≈ **1.19 g/cm³**
> - **Refractive Index (n @ 589 nm):** ≈ 1.59 – 1.80 (post-baked)
> - **Optical Transparency:** Excellent across 400 nm – 1600 nm
> - **Typical Film Thickness:** 1 µm – 500 µm
> - **Achievable Aspect Ratio:** > **20 : 1** (commonly 5:1 to 15:1 in standard processing)
> - **Exposure Source:** i-line (365 nm), h-line (405 nm), broadband UV
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of SU-8 — The Three Functional Components

| # | Component | Functional Role | Typical %wt |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **EPON Resin 825 (Bisphenol-A Novalac Epoxy)** | Crosslinkable base oligomer; provides 8 epoxy end-groups per molecule for 3D networking | 65 – 75 % |
| 2 | **Gamma-Butyrolactone (GBL)** | Casting solvent; enables spin-coating of thick uniform films | 20 – 30 % |
| 3 | **Triarylsulfonium Hexafluoroantimonate Salt (PAG)** | Photoacid Generator — absorbs UV, releases H⁺ (strong acid) on exposure | 1 – 5 % |

## 2.2 The Photo-Acid Catalyzed Crosslinking Mechanism — Step-by-Step

1. **UV Photon Absorption (λ ≈ 365 nm):** The triarylsulfonium PAG absorbs a UV photon and undergoes photolysis, generating a **strong Bronsted acid (H⁺SbF₆⁻)** plus by-products (e.g., diphenyl sulfide).
2. **Acid-Catalyzed Ring Opening:** During the **post-exposure bake (PEB)** at ≈ 95 °C, the photogenerated H⁺ diffuses and protonates the oxygen of the epoxide ring, opening it to form a reactive **secondary hydroxyl group** and a carbocation-type site.
3. **Cationic Chain Propagation:** The opened epoxide can attack and open the epoxide of an *adjacent* SU-8 molecule, propagating the crosslink in an **autocatalytic (chain-reaction) fashion**.
4. **Network Formation:** Because each oligomer carries **8 epoxy groups**, a single photoevent can trigger crosslinking at multiple sites → a dense, mechanically rigid **3D crosslinked network** is formed in the exposed regions.
5. **Development Selectivity:** Unexposed regions (where no H⁺ was generated) remain soluble in the developer (e.g., **SU-8 Developer = 1-methoxy-2-propanol acetate / PGMEA**). Exposed regions are insoluble → they remain on the wafer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Post-Exposure Bake is MANDATORY:** Without PEB, the diffusion-limited acid catalysis cannot progress to completion. Skipping PEB leads to under-crosslinking, "T-top" sidewall profiles, and delamination — a very common KTU practical-error scenario.

## 2.3 Standard SU-8 Photolithography Process (the "Seven Sacred Steps")

| Step | Process | Typical Parameters | Purpose |
|------|---------|--------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Substrate Preparation** | Dehydration bake 200 °C / 5 min + HMDS priming | Improve adhesion |
| 2 | **Spin Coating** | 500 rpm spread (10 s) → 1000–3000 rpm coat (30 s) | Deposit uniform film |
| 3 | **Soft Bake (Pre-Bake)** | 65 °C (2 min) → 95 °C (5–10 min) | Evaporate solvent; densify film |
| 4 | **UV Exposure** | 100 – 300 mJ/cm² (i-line, contact/proximity) | Photoacid generation |
| 5 | **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** | 65 °C (1 min) → 95 °C (3–5 min) | Activate crosslinking |
| 6 | **Development** | Immersion in PGMEA, 4 – 10 min with agitation | Dissolve unexposed SU-8 |
| 7 | **Hard Bake (Optional)** | 150 – 200 °C, 30 min | Further crosslink & stabilize |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus (Hooke's Law) | GPa |
| 2 | $\text{Aspect Ratio} = \dfrac{h}{w}$ | h = film thickness, w = minimum feature width | dimensionless |
| 3 | $d = \dfrac{k \cdot \omega^{2}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \cdot \rho$ | Cantilever static deflection (relevant for SU-8 cantilevers) | m |
| 4 | $\sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal residual stress in SU-8 film | Pa |
| 5 | $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot d \cdot NA}{m}$ | Rayleigh criterion for minimum resolvable feature | m |
| 6 | $h = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot t^{2}}{8 \eta}$ | Stokes-law film-thickness dependence (spin coating, simplified) | m |
| 7 | $T_g \approx 210 \text{ °C}$ | Glass transition temperature of crosslinked SU-8 | °C |
| 8 | $n_{SU\text{-}8} \approx 1.59 - 1.80$ | Refractive index (depends on hard-bake) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_{polymer}^{eff} \approx 2$ to $5$ GPa | Effective modulus range for crosslinked SU-8 | GPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Board-Examiner Tip:** Always quote the **Young's Modulus of fully crosslinked SU-8 as ≈ 4 GPa** unless the question explicitly asks for a specific hard-bake condition. Marks are routinely lost for stating "≈ 0.5 GPa" (which is the value for PDMS — an entirely different polymer!).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of SU-8

- **Microfluidics:** Channels, valves, mixers, droplet generators (lab-on-a-chip, organ-on-chip).
- **Micromechanics:** Cantilevers, membranes, inertial sensors (gyroscopes, accelerometers), micro-grippers.
- **BioMEMS:** Cell-culturing scaffolds, neural probes (biocompatible after proper treatment).
- **Optics & Photonics:** Waveguides, micro-lens arrays, optical MEMS mirrors.
- **RF-MEMS:** Low-loss dielectric membranes for tunable capacitors and switches.
- **Packaging:** Wafer-level capping, hermetic sealing, structural spacer layers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spin-Coating Film Thickness (Empirical Model)

The thickness of an SU-8 film after spin-coating is governed by an empirical relation derived from the balance between centrifugal force and viscous drag.

$$h = \dfrac{K \cdot \eta^{\beta}}{\sqrt{\omega}}$$

where:
- $h$ = final film thickness (µm)
- $K$ = empirical constant depending on resin formulation and solvent volatility
- $\eta$ = resin viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\omega$ = angular spin speed (rad/s)
- $\beta \approx 0.4$ (model-dependent exponent for non-Newtonian SU-8 behaviour)

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Mass Continuity (Conservation of Solvent + Solute on the wafer):**

Let $\rho$ be the SU-8 solution density, $A$ the wafer area, and $h(t)$ the instantaneous film thickness. The total mass on the wafer is $M(t) = \rho \cdot A \cdot h(t)$.

**Step 2 — Volumetric Outflow due to Centrifugal Force:**

The radial velocity $v_r$ at radius $r$ from the wafer centre is given by the balance of centrifugal force ($\rho \omega^{2} r$) and the viscous gradient $\left( \eta \dfrac{d^{2} v_r}{dz^{2}} \right)$. Solving the Navier-Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates with the no-slip boundary condition at the wafer surface and zero shear at the free surface gives:

$$v_r(z) = \dfrac{\rho \omega^{2} r}{2 \eta} \left( 2hz - z^{2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Outflow Rate (Integrating $v_r$ over film depth):**

$$Q(r,t) = \int_{0}^{h} v_r \, dz = \dfrac{\rho \omega^{2} r h^{3}}{3 \eta}$$

**Step 4 — Mass Balance on the Wafer:**

$$\dfrac{dh}{dt} = -\dfrac{2 Q(r,t)}{r} = -\dfrac{2 \rho \omega^{2} h^{3}}{3 \eta}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the ODE (separable variables):**

$$\int \dfrac{dh}{h^{3}} = -\int \dfrac{2 \rho \omega^{2}}{3 \eta} \, dt$$

$$-\dfrac{1}{2 h^{2}} = -\dfrac{2 \rho \omega^{2} t}{3 \eta} + C$$

Applying the initial condition $h(0) = h_0$ (thickness at $t=0$):

$$\dfrac{1}{h^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{h_0^{2}} + \dfrac{4 \rho \omega^{2} t}{3 \eta}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result for $t \gg h_0^{2}$ (solvent-evaporation stage):**

$$\boxed{\,h(t) \;\approx\; \dfrac{\sqrt{3 \eta / 4 \rho \omega^{2} t}}{1} \;=\; K \cdot \eta^{1/2} \cdot \omega^{-1}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The thickness scales as $\omega^{-1}$ (not $\omega^{-1/2}$ as in a Newtonian fluid) because SU-8 is a **shear-thinning, non-Newtonian** polymer. Doubling spin speed roughly halves the film thickness.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Residual Thermal Stress in SU-8 Film

When an SU-8 film is deposited on a silicon substrate and cooled from the hard-bake temperature $T_{HB}$ to room temperature $T_{RT}$, mismatch in coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) generates **thermal residual stress**.

**Step 1 — Define CTE Mismatch:**

$$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{SU\text{-}8} - \alpha_{Si}$$

where $\alpha_{SU\text{-}8} \approx 52 \times 10^{-6} / \text{K}$ and $\alpha_{Si} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6} / \text{K}$ → $\Delta \alpha \approx 49.4 \times 10^{-6} / \text{K}$.

**Step 2 — Unconstrained Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T = \Delta \alpha \cdot (T_{HB} - T_{RT})$$

**Step 3 — Constrained Stress (Stoney's Equation, biaxial film on thick substrate):**

$$\sigma_{film} = \dfrac{E_{SU\text{-}8}}{1 - \nu_{SU\text{-}8}} \cdot \varepsilon_{th}$$

**Step 4 — Stoney's Modified Form (substrate radius of curvature $R$):**

$$\sigma_{film} = \dfrac{E_{Si} \cdot t_{Si}^{2}}{6 \cdot (1 - \nu_{Si}) \cdot t_{f} \cdot R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why SU-8 structures can crack or delaminate if hard-bake temperature gradients are too steep — the residual tensile stress may exceed the adhesion strength of SU-8/Si interface.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SU-8 vs Other MEMS Polymers

| Property | **SU-8** | **PMMA** | **PDMS (Sylgard 184)** | **Polyimide (PI-2525)** |
|----------|----------|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Photoresist Type | Negative | Positive | Not a photoresist (cast/cure) | Negative |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 4.0 – 4.4 | 3.0 – 5.0 | **0.001 – 0.003** | 2.5 – 8.5 |
| Tg (°C) | 210 | 105 | – (elastomer) | 350 – 400 |
| Elongation at Break (%) | 6.5 | 4 – 6 | **> 100** | 30 – 80 |
| Max Aspect Ratio | **> 20:1** | 5:1 | N/A (cast) | 10:1 |
| Optical Transparency | Good (visible/IR) | Excellent (UV-vis) | Excellent | Moderate (yellowish) |
| Biocompatibility | Good (post-cured) | Excellent | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| Typical Thickness | 1 – 500 µm | 0.05 – 100 µm | 10 – 5000 µm | 1 – 50 µm |
| Process Mode | Photolithography | Photolithography | Soft lithography | Photolithography |
| Solvent/Cast | GBL | Anisole/CB | None (liquid prepolymer) | NMP |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SU-8 Photolithography Process — End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step1: Substrate Prep\nSi Wafer Cleaning + HMDS] --> A2
    A2[Step2: Spin Coating\nDispense SU-8 then spin 1000-3000 rpm] --> A3
    A3[Step3: Soft Bake\n65C 2 min then 95C 5-10 min] --> A4
    A4[Step4: UV Exposure\ni-line 365 nm via mask aligner] --> A5
    A5[Step5: Post Exposure Bake\n65C 1 min then 95C 3-5 min] --> A6
    A6[Step6: Development\nImmerse in PGMEA 4-10 min] --> A7
    A7[Step7: Hard Bake\n150-200C 30 min optional] --> A8[Final: Crosslinked SU-8 Microstructure]
```

```mermaid
    E1 -->|Cracking or delamination| E3[Cause: Excessive thermal stress ramp]
    E1 -->|Undercut or over-development| E4[Cause: Over-exposure or developer too long]
    E1 -->|Sticky or tacky surface| E5[Cause: Insufficient hard bake]
    E1 -->|Bubbles or voids| E6[Cause: Spin too fast or soft bake too aggressive]
```
