```mermaid
    DISP["Display: pi vs A Isotherm"]
    LOG["Data Logger"]

    CTRL --> B1
    CTRL --> B2
    CTRL --> DP
    WP --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DISP
    CTRL --> LOG
    B1 --- TC
    B2 --- TC
    DP --- TC
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Types of LB Film Transfer Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0["S0: Substrate (clean, neutral)"]
    S1["S1: Hydrophilic Substrate (treated with piranha or O2 plasma)"]
    S2["S2: Hydrophobic Substrate (silane-treated)"]
    M1["M1: Monolayer at air-water interface (heads down, tails up)"]
    W["W: Water Subphase"]

    S1 -->|"Down-stroke (head attaches first)"| Y1["Y-type: head-tail-head-tail alternating multilayer"]
    S2 -->|"Up-stroke (tail attaches first)"| X1["X-type: tail-head-tail-head multilayer (less stable)"]
    S1 -->|"Up-stroke only (head already attached)"| Z1["Z-type: head-tail-tail-head multilayer (uncommon)"]
    M1 -->|"Horizontal lowering onto film"| H1["Horizontal Schaefer Transfer (biological membranes)"]

    W --- M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the Mermaid diagrams, the substrate is shown in three states — hydrophilic, hydrophobic, and neutral — because the surface energy of the substrate determines which type of LB transfer will be successful.

### 4.4 Cross-Section: Y-Type Multilayer (Molecular Packing)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Layer 1: Head-down / Tail-up (1st dip)"]
    L2["Layer 2: Head-down / Tail-up (2nd dip, reversed orientation)"]
    L3["Layer 3: Head-down / Tail-up (3rd dip)"]
    L4["Layer 4: Head-down / Tail-up (4th dip, reversed orientation)"]
    L5["Layer 5: Head-down / Tail-up (5th dip)"]
    SUB["Solid Substrate (Si or Glass)"]
    AIR["Air above film"]

    SUB --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> AIR
```

> In Y-type packing, the molecules are arranged such that **heads touch heads** and **tails touch tails** between adjacent layers, which is energetically favorable and yields stable multilayer films.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define a Langmuir–Blodgett (LB) film. What is a Langmuir monolayer?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Langmuir–Blodgett (LB) film** is an ultrathin, organized organic film formed by transferring one or more monolayers of amphiphilic molecules from a water surface (Langmuir monolayer) onto a solid substrate, with controlled layer-by-layer precision at the molecular scale (1–3 nm per layer).

A **Langmuir monolayer** is a single-molecule-thick layer of amphiphilic molecules spread at the air–water interface, with hydrophilic heads submerged in water and hydrophobic tails pointing into the air.

*(1 mark for LB film definition, 1 mark for monolayer definition, 1 mark for layer thickness / orientation detail)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is surface pressure? How is it measured using a Wilhelmy plate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Surface pressure (π)** is defined as the reduction in surface tension of a liquid caused by the presence of a monolayer at its surface:

$$\pi = \gamma_0 - \gamma$$

where $\gamma_0$ is the surface tension of the clean subphase (e.g., **72.8 mN/m** for water at 20 °C) and $\gamma$ is the surface tension with the monolayer.

**Wilhelmy plate method:** A thin plate (platinum or filter paper) is partially immersed in the subphase. The vertical force on the plate is:

$$F = \gamma \cdot L \cdot \cos\theta$$

where $L$ is the plate perimeter and $\theta$ is the contact angle (≈ 0 for clean water). When the monolayer is compressed, $\gamma$ decreases and the wetting force changes. The change in force, detected by a sensitive microbalance, gives $\pi$.

*(1 mark for π definition with formula, 1 mark for Wilhelmy plate description, 1 mark for force equation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **Langmuir–Blodgett technique** for the preparation of thin organic films. With a neat sketch, explain the **π–A isotherm** and the different phases of a monolayer. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **different types of LB film transfer (X, Y, Z, and horizontal)** with diagrams. State two important applications of LB films. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Langmuir–Blodgett (LB) technique is a method for fabricating ultrathin organic films with molecular-level thickness control. It exploits the self-organization of **amphiphilic molecules** (with hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails) at the air–water interface, followed by transfer to a solid substrate.

**Apparatus — Langmuir Trough (2 marks):**
The key components are:

1. **Teflon trough** containing a clean water subphase, thermostatted to maintain a constant temperature (typically 20–25 °C).
2. **Two motor-driven Teflon barriers** that compress the monolayer symmetrically.
3. **Wilhelmy plate balance** to monitor surface pressure π continuously.
4. **Dipper mechanism** to lower/raise the substrate vertically through the monolayer.
5. **Computer interface** for real-time π–A isotherm plotting.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Amphiphile is dissolved in a volatile solvent (e.g., chloroform) and spread on the water surface using a microsyringe.
2. After solvent evaporation (~10–15 min), molecules organize as a monolayer (heads in water, tails in air).
3. The barriers are slowly compressed, reducing the area per molecule. Surface pressure is plotted against $A_m$.
4. At a chosen pressure (e.g., 30 mN/m) in the LC phase, the substrate is dipped through the monolayer, transferring one layer.

**π–A Isotherm and Phases (2 marks):**
The π–A isotherm typically shows four distinct phases:

- **Gaseous (G):** Large $A_m$ ( > 1.0 nm²), low π, molecules far apart.
- **Liquid-Expanded (LE):** $A_m$ ≈ 0.5 – 1.0 nm², π begins to rise.
- **Liquid-Condensed (LC):** $A_m$ ≈ 0.3 – 0.5 nm², steep rise in π, tails begin aligning vertically.
- **Solid (S):** $A_m$ ≈ 0.20 – 0.25 nm², very steep π, closely packed.

Beyond the **collapse pressure (πc)**, the film buckles and forms multilayers / 3D defects.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**X-type transfer (2 marks):**
A **hydrophobic** substrate is raised (upstroke) through the monolayer. The hydrophobic **tail** attaches first to the substrate. Subsequent layers are also deposited on upstrokes, leading to a tail-in / head-out orientation in every layer. X-type films are generally **less stable** because the polar heads are exposed to air.

**Y-type transfer (2 marks):**
A **hydrophilic** substrate is first dipped down through the monolayer, depositing a head-down / tail-up layer. The substrate is then withdrawn, depositing a tail-down / head-up layer. This alternating process yields a **head-to-head and tail-to-tail** packing, which is thermodynamically stable and is the **most common LB architecture**.

**Z-type transfer (1 mark):**
The hydrophilic substrate is dipped down, then **only upstrokes** are used for subsequent layers. The head attaches first; subsequent layers are tail-down / head-up. Z-type is uncommon because consecutive tail-to-head contacts are energetically unfavorable.

**Horizontal (Schaefer) transfer (1 mark):**
The substrate is lowered **horizontally** onto the monolayer, picking up only one side. This is widely used for depositing **biological membranes** (e.g., lipid bilayers) without rupturing them.

**Applications (1 mark):**
1. **Chemical sensors** — porphyrin/phthalocyanine LB films for NO₂, CO, NH₃ detection.
2. **Organic thin-film transistors (OTFTs)** — LB-deposited semiconductors and dielectrics.
3. **Biomimetic membranes** — phospholipid LB films mimic cell membranes.
4. **OLEDs and photovoltaics** — controlled-thickness emissive and absorber layers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **surface pressure** in terms of the Wilhelmy plate measurements. A Wilhelmy plate of perimeter 4 cm is immersed in a clean water subphase. The force on the plate is $F_0 = 1.456 \text{ mN}$. After spreading a monolayer, the force becomes $F = 1.272 \text{ mN}$. Calculate the surface pressure. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Langmuir–Blodgett trough has an area of $600 \text{ cm}^2$. A fatty acid (molecular area $0.20 \text{ nm}^2$) is spread to form a monolayer. After dipping a substrate of dimensions $3 \text{ cm} \times 2 \text{ cm}$ **once downward** at 30 mN/m, the trough area decreases by $6.05 \text{ cm}^2$. Calculate (i) the number of molecules on the water surface, and (ii) the **transfer ratio**. Comment on its quality. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Consider a Wilhelmy plate of length $l$ and width $w$, partially immersed in a liquid of surface tension $\gamma$ with contact angle $\theta$. The liquid wets the plate and exerts a downward force along the wetted perimeter $L = 2(l + w)$:

$$F = \gamma \cdot L \cdot \cos\theta$$

**For a clean subphase** (no monolayer), the contact angle is zero ($\theta = 0$, $\cos 0 = 1$), and the force is:

$$F_0 = \gamma_0 \cdot L$$

**For a subphase with monolayer:**

$$F = \gamma \cdot L$$

**Subtracting:**

$$F_0 - F = (\gamma_0 - \gamma) \cdot L = \pi \cdot L$$

**Therefore:**

$$\pi = \frac{F_0 - F}{L} = \frac{\Delta F}{2(l + w) \cos\theta}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given:
- $L = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- $F_0 = 1.456 \text{ mN} = 1.456 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}$
- $F = 1.272 \text{ mN} = 1.272 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}$

$$\Delta F = F_0 - F = (1.456 - 1.272) \times 10^{-3} = 0.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}$$

$$\pi = \frac{0.184 \times 10^{-3}}{0.04} = 4.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{\pi = 4.6 \text{ mN/m}}$$

This surface pressure corresponds to the **liquid-expanded (LE) phase**, which is suitable for transfer of fluid monolayers.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**(i) Number of molecules on water surface (3 marks):**

Given: $A_{trough} = 600 \text{ cm}^2$, $A_m = 0.20 \text{ nm}^2$

Convert $A_m$:

$$A_m = 0.20 \times 10^{-14} \text{ cm}^2 = 2.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ cm}^2/\text{molecule}$$

Number of molecules:

$$N = \frac{600}{2.0 \times 10^{-15}} = 3.0 \times 10^{17} \text{ molecules}$$

**(ii) Transfer Ratio (3 marks):**

Substrate area: $A_{sub} = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ cm}^2$
Trough area decrease: $\Delta A_{trough} = 6.05 \text{ cm}^2$

$$\tau = \frac{A_{sub}}{\Delta A_{trough}} = \frac{6.00}{6.05} = 0.992$$

**Comment (1 mark):**

> The transfer ratio **0.992** lies within the ideal range of **0.95 – 1.05**, indicating a **near-ideal Y-type transfer** of the monolayer onto the substrate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert nm² → cm² before computing N. *Always show unit conversion explicitly.*
> 2. **Missing the Wilhelmy Plate Perimeter:** Students often use area (cm²) instead of perimeter (cm) when calculating π — the formula requires the **wetted perimeter**, not the surface area of the plate.
> 3. **Wrong Transfer Ratio Definition:** Some students invert the ratio. Remember: $\tau = A_{sub}/A_{trough}$. Substrate area is in the numerator.
> 4. **Ignoring the Ideal Range:** Even with a correct τ value, failing to comment on whether it falls in 0.95 – 1.05 costs the final 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing Phases:** Saying "π rises" without identifying the **G → LE → LC → S** phase transitions loses 1–2 marks in long-answer questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Langmuir–Blodgett Films**

- **LB film** = ordered organic film built by transferring amphiphilic monolayers from an air–water interface to a solid substrate, **layer by layer**.
- The **Langmuir trough** is the apparatus; **Teflon** is used because it is hydrophobic and chemically inert.
- **Amphiphile** = molecule with hydrophilic head + hydrophobic tail (e.g., stearic acid, $\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_{16}\text{COOH}$).
- **Surface pressure:** $\pi = \gamma_0 - \gamma$, measured via **Wilhelmy plate** ($F = \gamma L \cos\theta$).
- **π–A isotherm** reveals four phases: **G → LE → LC → S → Collapse**.
- **Collapse pressure (πc)** is the upper limit; never transfer above πc.
- **Compressibility modulus** $C_s^{-1} = -A_m(\partial\pi/\partial A_m)_T$ quantifies film stiffness (mN/m).
- **Y-type transfer** = hydrophilic substrate, alternating strokes, head–tail–head–tail (most stable).
- **X-type** = hydrophobic substrate, upstrokes only, tail first (less stable).
- **Z-type** = hydrophilic substrate, upstrokes only (uncommon).
- **Horizontal (Schaefer)** = substrate lowered horizontally — used for **biological membranes**.
- **Transfer ratio** $\tau = A_{sub}/A_{trough}$ should lie in **0.95 – 1.05** for a successful deposition.
- **Typical area per molecule:** 0.20 – 0.25 nm² for saturated fatty acids.
- **Typical layer thickness:** 1 – 3 nm per layer; total film thickness ≈ $n \times \ell$ for $n$ layers.
- **Surface concentration:** $\Gamma = 1/(N_A A_m)$ in mol/cm².
- **Key applications:** chemical/gas sensors, OTFTs, OLEDs, photovoltaics, biomimetic membranes, MEMS/NEMS dielectrics.
- **Characterization tools:** AFM, ellipsometry, Brewster Angle Microscopy (BAM), FTIR, X-ray diffraction.
- **Comparison with SAMs:** LB films are *physiosorbed* and can be multilayered; SAMs are *chemisorbed* and monolayer-only — both are **bottom-up** nanofabrication techniques.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Strain Relationship and Mechanical Properties of Silicon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOLECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress-Strain Relationship and Mechanical Properties of Silicon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stress (σ)** is defined as the internal resistive force per unit area developed within a material when an external load is applied. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

where $F$ is the applied force (in Newtons) and $A$ is the cross-sectional area (in m²). The SI unit of stress is **Pascal (Pa)**, with $1~\text{Pa} = 1~\text{N/m}^2$.

**Strain (ε)** is the dimensionless measure of deformation representing the relative change in dimension (length, area, or volume) of a body in response to applied stress. For uniaxial loading:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

where $\Delta L$ is the change in length and $L_0$ is the original length.

**Silicon (Si)**, the foundational material of the semiconductor and MEMS industry, is a **crystalline solid** with a diamond cubic lattice structure. Unlike amorphous materials, silicon exhibits **mechanical anisotropy**, meaning its mechanical properties vary significantly with crystallographic direction. This directional dependence is critical in MEMS design because it dictates how a silicon microstructure will deform, deflect, or fracture under load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Mechanical properties of silicon include Young's Modulus (E), Poisson's Ratio (ν), Yield Strength (σ_y), Fracture Strength (σ_f), Shear Modulus (G), and Bulk Modulus (K). The **anisotropic nature** of silicon is the single most important concept tested in Module 1 examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **deck of playing cards** bound tightly together. If you push down on the top of the deck, it resists differently depending on whether you push along the flat face or the thin edge. Similarly, silicon "feels" pushes and pulls differently depending on which crystal direction (e.g., [100], [110], or [111]) the force is applied along. This is the **mechanical anisotropy** of silicon.

For an isotropic material like glass, the response is identical in all directions. For silicon, however, the **stiffness in the [111] direction is approximately 1.7× greater than in the [100] direction**. This is why MEMS designers must always specify the crystal orientation when designing beams, membranes, and comb drives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is silicon the preferred MEMS material?** Beyond its excellent electronic properties, silicon has near-perfect elasticity (very low mechanical hysteresis), a high strength-to-weight ratio, no mechanical creep at room temperature, and mature micromachining processes. Its Young's Modulus (~130 GPa in the [100] direction) is comparable to steel, yet silicon is far lighter and can be batch-fabricated like microchips.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus (along [100]) | $E_{100}$ | **130 GPa** |
| Young's Modulus (along [110]) | $E_{110}$ | **169 GPa** |
| Young's Modulus (along [111]) | $E_{111}$ | **188 GPa** |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu$ | **0.28** |
| Density | $\rho$ | **2.33 g/cm³** |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ | **7 GPa** |
| Fracture Strength | $\sigma_f$ | **~1–3 GPa** (microscale) |
| Shear Modulus | $G$ | **~52 GPa** |
| Bulk Modulus | $K$ | **~98 GPa** |
| Mohs Hardness | – | **7** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stress Tensor — The General 3D Picture

When a body is loaded, the internal stress state at any point is described by a **second-order symmetric stress tensor** $\sigma_{ij}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yy}, \sigma_{zz}$ are the **normal stresses** and $\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{xz}$ are the **shear stresses**. Due to symmetry of the moment equilibrium, $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$, so there are only **6 independent components** in 3D.

## 2.2 Strain Tensor

The **strain tensor** $\epsilon_{ij}$ is also symmetric and contains 6 independent components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_{xx} & \epsilon_{xy}/2 & \epsilon_{xz}/2 \\ \epsilon_{yx}/2 & \epsilon_{yy} & \epsilon_{yz}/2 \\ \epsilon_{zx}/2 & \epsilon_{zy}/2 & \epsilon_{zz} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ appears in the off-diagonal terms in the engineering definition of shear strain.

## 2.3 Generalized Hooke's Law

The linear relationship between stress and strain for an elastic solid is given by the **generalized Hooke's Law**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k,l} C_{ijkl} \cdot \epsilon_{kl}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalently, using the compliance tensor $S_{ijkl}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{ij} = \sum_{k,l} S_{ijkl} \cdot \sigma_{kl}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C_{ijkl}$ and $S_{ijkl}$ are fourth-order tensors. The total number of independent components in $C_{ijkl}$ is reduced by material symmetry.

## 2.4 Crystal Symmetry Reduction of Stiffness Matrix

### 2.4.1 General Anisotropic (Triclinic) Case
Has **21 independent elastic constants**.

### 2.4.2 Cubic Crystal Symmetry (Silicon, Germanium, Diamond)
Due to the **cubic symmetry** of silicon's diamond lattice, the number of independent elastic constants reduces dramatically to just **3**: $C_{11}$, $C_{12}$, and $C_{44}$.

Using the **Voigt notation** (reducing $ij$ to single indices 1–6), the stiffness matrix for silicon is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[C] = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{12} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{11} & C_{12} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{12} & C_{11} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{44} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{44} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{44} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

For silicon at room temperature (300 K):
- $C_{11} = 165.7~\text{GPa}$
- $C_{12} = 63.9~\text{GPa}$
- $C_{44} = 79.6~\text{GPa}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the off-diagonal block (rows/cols 4,5,6) diagonal?** Cubic symmetry forces all shear coupling to vanish. This is the hallmark of a highly symmetric crystal — only pure shears remain independent.

## 2.5 Compliance Matrix and Directional Young's Modulus

The compliance matrix is the inverse of the stiffness matrix. For cubic crystals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{11} = \frac{C_{11} + C_{12}}{(C_{11} - C_{12})(C_{11} + 2C_{12})}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{12} = \frac{-C_{12}}{(C_{11} - C_{12})(C_{11} + 2C_{12})}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{44} = \frac{1}{C_{44}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Young's Modulus** in a crystallographic direction defined by direction cosines $(l_1, l_2, l_3)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E(l_1, l_2, l_3)} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right)(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **general directional modulus formula** for cubic crystals.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Normal stress definition | Pa |
| 2 | $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Normal strain definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$ | Uniaxial Hooke's law (isotropic) | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \cdot \epsilon_{kl}$ | Generalized Hooke's law (tensor) | Pa |
| 5 | $[C] = $ cubic matrix with $C_{11}, C_{12}, C_{44}$ | Silicon stiffness matrix | GPa |
| 6 | $E_{[100]} = 1/S_{11}$ | Young's Modulus along [100] | GPa |
| 7 | $E_{[111]} = 3 / (S_{11} + 2S_{12} + S_{44})$ | Young's Modulus along [111] | GPa |
| 8 | $E_{[110]} = 2 / (2S_{11} - 2S_{12} + S_{44})$ | Young's Modulus along [110] | GPa |
| 9 | $\nu = -S_{12}/S_{11}$ | Poisson's ratio (cubic) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G = 1/S_{44}$ | Shear modulus | GPa |
| 11 | $K = 1 / (3(S_{11} + 2S_{12}))$ | Bulk modulus | GPa |
| 12 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Isotropic E-G-ν relation | GPa |
| 13 | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Isotropic E-K-ν relation | GPa |
| 14 | $\sigma_f = K_{IC} / (\sqrt{\pi a})$ | Fracture strength (Griffith) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU:** Equations 6, 7, and 8 are the **most-asked** derivations. Practice deriving $E_{[110]}$ from the directional formula and verify that the off-diagonal block of the stiffness matrix vanishes due to cubic symmetry.

## 2.7 Why Anisotropy Matters in MEMS Engineering

In a MEMS accelerometer or gyroscope, the proof mass is suspended by thin flexure beams. If a beam is etched along the [100] direction, it will be more compliant than one etched along the [111] direction. This directly affects:
- **Resonant frequency**: $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$, where $k$ depends on E.
- **Sensitivity**: $\text{displacement} = F/k$, where $k \propto E$.
- **Stress concentration**: depends on crystal plane for fracture.

Choosing the **right crystal orientation** during fabrication (using SOI wafers with [100] or [110] orientation) is therefore a fundamental MEMS design decision.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Directional Young's Modulus for Cubic Silicon

**Objective:** Derive the formula for Young's Modulus in an arbitrary direction $[l_1, l_2, l_3]$ for silicon (cubic crystal).

**Starting Point:** Apply uniaxial stress $\sigma$ along the direction $[l_1, l_2, l_3]$ in the crystal. The strain component along the same direction gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{[uvw]}^{-1} = \frac{\epsilon}{sigma} = \sum_{i,j} S_{ij} \cdot l_i \cdot l_j
\end{aligned}
$$

where $S_{ij}$ are the Voigt-reduced compliances.

**Step 1:** The stress vector in the crystal coordinate system is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \sigma \cdot l_1^2, \quad \sigma_2 = \sigma \cdot l_2^2, \quad \sigma_3 = \sigma \cdot l_3^2 \\
\sigma_4 &= \sigma \cdot l_2 l_3, \quad \sigma_5 = \sigma \cdot l_1 l_3, \quad \sigma_6 = \sigma \cdot l_1 l_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Using the cubic compliance matrix in Voigt form, the strains become:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_1 = S_{11}\sigma_1 + S_{12}\sigma_2 + S_{12}\sigma_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the stress components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_1 = S_{11}(\sigma l_1^2) + S_{12}(\sigma l_2^2) + S_{12}(\sigma l_3^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_1 = \sigma \left[ S_{11} l_1^2 + S_{12}(l_2^2 + l_3^2) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetry, $\epsilon_2$ and $\epsilon_3$ are obtained by cyclic permutation.

**Step 4:** The strain along the loading direction is the projection of the strain tensor onto the direction vector:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon = \sum_i \epsilon_i l_i^2 + 2\sum_{i \neq j} \epsilon_{ij} l_i l_j
\end{aligned}
$$

For pure tension along $[l_1, l_2, l_3]$, the shear strains are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_6 = S_{44} \cdot \sigma_6 = S_{44} \cdot \sigma \cdot l_1 l_2
\end{aligned}
$$

and similarly for $\epsilon_4$ and $\epsilon_5$.

**Step 5:** Combining all contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon = l_1^2 \epsilon_1 + l_2^2 \epsilon_2 + l_3^2 \epsilon_3 + 2 l_1 l_2 \epsilon_6 + 2 l_1 l_3 \epsilon_5 + 2 l_2 l_3 \epsilon_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute and collect terms. Since the direction cosines satisfy $l_1^2 + l_2^2 + l_3^2 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E} = S_{11}(l_1^4 + l_2^4 + l_3^4) + 2S_{12}(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2) + S_{44}(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Apply the trigonometric identity:
$(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + l_3^2)^2 = l_1^4 + l_2^4 + l_3^4 + 2(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2) = 1$

So: $l_1^4 + l_2^4 + l_3^4 = 1 - 2(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)$

**Step 8:** Final form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{[l_1,l_2,l_3]}} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right)(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master anisotropy equation** for cubic silicon.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Young's Modulus Along [100]

For [100] direction: $l_1 = 1, l_2 = 0, l_3 = 0$

So: $l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2 = 0$

Substituting into the master equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[100]}} = S_{11} - 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{E_{[100]} = \frac{1}{S_{11}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical value:** $S_{11} = 7.69 \times 10^{-12}~\text{Pa}^{-1}$ → $E_{[100]} \approx 130~\text{GPa}$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Young's Modulus Along [111]

For [111] direction: $l_1 = l_2 = l_3 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

So: $l_1^2 = l_2^2 = l_3^2 = \frac{1}{3}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2 = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[111]}} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[111]}} = \frac{1}{3}\left(3S_{11} - 2S_{11} + 2S_{12} + S_{44}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[111]}} = \frac{1}{3}\left(S_{11} + 2S_{12} + S_{44}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{E_{[111]} = \frac{3}{S_{11} + 2S_{12} + S_{44}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical value:** $E_{[111]} \approx 188~\text{GPa}$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Young's Modulus Along [110]

For [110] direction: $l_1 = l_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, l_3 = 0$

So: $l_1^2 = l_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}, l_3^2 = 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2 = \frac{1}{4} + 0 + 0 = \frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = S_{11} - \frac{1}{2}\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(2S_{11} - S_{11} + S_{12} + \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(S_{11} + S_{12} + \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right) = \frac{2S_{11} + 2S_{12} + S_{44}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{E_{[110]} = \frac{4}{2S_{11} + 2S_{12} + S_{44}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $E_{[100]} < E_{[110]} < E_{[111]}$, i.e., $130 < 169 < 188~\text{GPa}$. The crystal is stiffest along the [111] direction (the body diagonal of the cubic cell), which is consistent with the highest atomic packing density in that direction.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Poisson's Ratio Along [100] for Silicon

For a tensile stress $\sigma$ along [100] in a cubic crystal, the lateral strain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{yy} = \epsilon_{zz} = S_{12} \cdot \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

The longitudinal strain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{xx} = S_{11} \cdot \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition, Poisson's ratio is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu = -\frac{\epsilon_{lateral}}{\epsilon_{longitudinal}} = -\frac{S_{12} \cdot \sigma}{S_{11} \cdot \sigma} = -\frac{S_{12}}{S_{11}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\nu = -\frac{S_{12}}{S_{11}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical value:** $S_{12} = -2.14 \times 10^{-12}~\text{Pa}^{-1}$ → $\nu \approx 0.28$

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Bulk Modulus of Silicon

The bulk modulus relates hydrostatic pressure to volumetric strain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K = \frac{\sigma_{hyd}}{\Delta V / V} = \frac{1}{3(S_{11} + 2S_{12})}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K = \frac{1}{3(7.69 - 4.28) \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{3(3.41 \times 10^{-12})}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{K \approx 98~\text{GPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.7 Numerical Example: Anisotropy Ratio

**Problem:** Compute the anisotropy ratio $A = 2C_{44} / (C_{11} - C_{12})$ for silicon.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \frac{2 \times 79.6}{165.7 - 63.9} = \frac{159.2}{101.8}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{A \approx 1.56}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $A = 1$, the material is **elastically isotropic** (as if it had cubic symmetry averaged out). Since $A = 1.56 \neq 1$, silicon is genuinely **anisotropic**. The deviation from unity is a direct measure of anisotropy strength.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Stress-Strain Functional Flow for MEMS Silicon Beams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Force F on MEMS Beam] --> B[Identify Crystal Direction: 100, 110, or 111]
    B --> C[Compute Direction Cosines l1, l2, l3]
    C --> D[Apply Directional Formula 1 over E equals S11 minus 2 times S11 minus S12 minus S44 over 2 times l1 squared l2 squared plus l2 squared l3 squared plus l3 squared l1 squared]
    D --> E[Calculate Directional Young Modulus E direction]
    E --> F[Use Hookes Law to Get Strain epsilon equals sigma over E]
    F --> G[Apply Stiffness Matrix to Get Full Strain Tensor]
    G --> H[Compute Beam Deflection and Resonant Frequency]
    H --> I[Compare with Yield and Fracture Strengths]
    I --> J{Within Safe Limit?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Design Pass: Device Reliable]
    J -- No --> L[Reorient Beam or Reduce Load]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Silicon Stiffness Matrix Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stiffness Tensor Cij with 21 Independent Constants] --> B{Apply Crystal Symmetry}
    B --> C[Triclinic: 21 Constants]
    B --> D[Monoclinic: 13 Constants]
    B --> E[Orthorhombic: 9 Constants]
    B --> F[Hexagonal: 5 Constants]
    B --> G[Cubic Silicon: 3 Constants: C11, C12, C44]
    G --> H[Voigt 6x6 Stiffness Matrix]
    H --> I[Diagonal Block: Normal Coupling]
    H --> J[Off Diagonal Block: Pure Shear Only]
    H --> K[Inverse to Get Compliance S Matrix]
    K --> L[Compute Directional E, G, K, nu]
```

## 4.3 Tabular Schematic: Comparison of Mechanical Properties Across Directions

| Crystal Direction | Young's Modulus (GPa) | Atomic Density (atoms/cm²) | Typical MEMS Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| [100] | 130 | $9.6 \times 10^{14}$ | Most common for bulk micromachining, KOH etching |
| [110] | 169 | $9.6 \times 10^{14}$ | High stiffness beams, resonators |
| [111] | 188 | $7.8 \times 10^{14}$ | Hardest to etch, used for sharp tips |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Although [111] is stiffest, it has the **lowest atomic density** on the surface, which is why KOH etches (100) and (110) planes much faster than (111) planes. This anisotropic etching is the cornerstone of bulk micromachining.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define mechanical anisotropy. Why is silicon considered anisotropic?**

**Model Answer:**
Mechanical anisotropy is the property of a material by which its mechanical response (such as Young's modulus, yield strength, or fracture toughness) varies with the direction of measurement relative to the crystal axes.

Silicon is considered anisotropic because of its **diamond cubic crystal structure**. Although the unit cell is symmetric, the atomic arrangement is not uniform in all directions. As a result, the spacing between atoms and the bonding geometry differ along [100], [110], and [111] directions. For example, $E_{[100]} = 130~\text{GPa}$ while $E_{[111]} = 188~\text{GPa}$. This directional dependence is captured by the cubic stiffness matrix with three independent constants $C_{11}$, $C_{12}$, and $C_{44}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three independent elastic constants of silicon and write the stiffness matrix in Voigt notation.**

**Model Answer:**
The three independent elastic constants of silicon (cubic crystal) are:

1. $C_{11} = 165.7~\text{GPa}$
2. $C_{12} = 63.9~\text{GPa}$
3. $C_{44} = 79.6~\text{GPa}$

The Voigt stiffness matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[C] = \begin{bmatrix} 165.7 & 63.9 & 63.9 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 63.9 & 165.7 & 63.9 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 63.9 & 63.9 & 165.7 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 79.6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 79.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 79.6 \end{bmatrix}~\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**a) Derive the directional Young's Modulus formula for a cubic crystal along an arbitrary direction $[l_1, l_2, l_3]$. (7 marks)**

**b) Using this formula, calculate the Young's Modulus of silicon along the [110] direction. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1:** Apply uniaxial stress $\sigma$ along the direction vector with direction cosines $(l_1, l_2, l_3)$. The stress components in the crystal frame (using Voigt notation) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 = \sigma l_1^2, \quad \sigma_2 = \sigma l_2^2, \quad \sigma_3 = \sigma l_3^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_4 = \sigma l_2 l_3, \quad \sigma_5 = \sigma l_1 l_3, \quad \sigma_6 = \sigma l_1 l_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hooke's law $\epsilon_i = \sum_j S_{ij} \sigma_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_1 = S_{11} \sigma_1 + S_{12} \sigma_2 + S_{12} \sigma_3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_2 = S_{12} \sigma_1 + S_{11} \sigma_2 + S_{12} \sigma_3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_3 = S_{12} \sigma_1 + S_{12} \sigma_2 + S_{11} \sigma_3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_4 = S_{44} \sigma_4, \quad \epsilon_5 = S_{44} \sigma_5, \quad \epsilon_6 = S_{44} \sigma_6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The strain along the loading direction is the projection:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon = \sum_i \epsilon_i l_i^2 + 2 \sum_{i<j} \epsilon_{ij} l_i l_j
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon = l_1^2 \epsilon_1 + l_2^2 \epsilon_2 + l_3^2 \epsilon_3 + 2 l_1 l_2 \epsilon_6 + 2 l_1 l_3 \epsilon_5 + 2 l_2 l_3 \epsilon_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and simplify. Use the identity $l_1^2 + l_2^2 + l_3^2 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E} = S_{11}(l_1^4 + l_2^4 + l_3^4) + 2 S_{12}(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2) + S_{44}(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Use $l_1^4 + l_2^4 + l_3^4 = 1 - 2(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{[l_1,l_2,l_3]}} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right)(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1:** For [110]: $l_1 = l_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, l_3 = 0$

So $l_1^2 = l_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}$ and $l_1^2 l_2^2 = \frac{1}{4}$ (other cross terms are 0).

**Step 2:** Substitute into the master formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = S_{11} - \frac{1}{2}\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E_{[110]}} = \frac{S_{11}}{2} + \frac{S_{12}}{2} + \frac{S_{44}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{[110]} = \frac{4}{2S_{11} + 2S_{12} + S_{44}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values (in Pa⁻¹):
- $S_{11} = 7.69 \times 10^{-12}$
- $S_{12} = -2.14 \times 10^{-12}$
- $S_{44} = 12.56 \times 10^{-12}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2S_{11} + 2S_{12} + S_{44} = (15.38 - 4.28 + 12.56) \times 10^{-12} = 23.66 \times 10^{-12}~\text{Pa}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{[110]} = \frac{4}{23.66 \times 10^{-12}} = 169 \times 10^{9}~\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{E_{[110]} \approx 169~\text{GPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**a) Explain the diamond cubic crystal structure of silicon and justify why it has only 3 independent elastic constants. (7 marks)**

**b) A silicon MEMS cantilever beam of length 500 μm, width 50 μm, and thickness 10 μm is oriented along the [100] direction. A force of 1 mN is applied at the free end. Calculate the maximum stress and the tip deflection. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

Silicon has a **diamond cubic crystal structure**, which can be visualized as two interpenetrating face-centered cubic (FCC) lattices offset by (¼, ¼, ¼) of the unit cell dimension. Each silicon atom is covalently bonded to 4 neighboring atoms in a perfect tetrahedral arrangement.

**Justification for 3 independent constants:**

1. The general stiffness tensor $C_{ijkl}$ has $3^4 = 81$ components, but symmetry of stress and strain ($C_{ijkl} = C_{jikl} = C_{ijlk}$) reduces it to 36 components.
2. Stress-strain symmetry ($C_{ijkl} = C_{klij}$) further reduces to 21 independent constants for a triclinic crystal.
3. **Cubic symmetry** demands invariance under 3-fold rotations about all three cube axes and under 90° rotations. The transformation laws under these symmetry operations force the following conditions:
   - $C_{11} = C_{22} = C_{33}$ (all normal stiffnesses equal)
   - $C_{12} = C_{13} = C_{23}$ (all off-diagonal normal-shear couplings equal)
   - $C_{44} = C_{55} = C_{66}$ (all shear stiffnesses equal)
   - All other components vanish.
4. Therefore, only $C_{11}$, $C_{12}$, and $C_{44}$ remain independent.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1: Maximum bending stress** for a cantilever with end load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{w t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $F$ is the force, $L$ is length, $w$ is width, $t$ is thickness.

**Step 2:** Substitute values (all in SI units):
- $F = 1 \times 10^{-3}~\text{N}$
- $L = 500 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m}$
- $w = 50 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m}$
- $t = 10 \times 10^{-6}~\text{m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 \times (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (500 \times 10^{-6})}{(50 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10 \times 10^{-6})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-15}} = 6 \times 10^{8}~\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 600~\text{MPa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Tip deflection** for a cantilever with end load:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta = \frac{4 F L^3}{E w t^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Use $E_{[100]} = 130~\text{GPa} = 130 \times 10^{9}~\text{Pa}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta = \frac{4 \times (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (500 \times 10^{-6})^3}{(130 \times 10^{9}) \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10 \times 10^{-6})^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-13}}{130 \times 10^{9} \times 5 \times 10^{-15}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta = \frac{5 \times 10^{-16}}{6.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.69 \times 10^{-7}~\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\delta \approx 0.77~\mu\text{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong formula usage** — Students often use $E = 200~\text{GPa}$ (steel value) or use the bulk Young's modulus without considering crystal direction. Always check the orientation of the beam.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — μm to m conversion is the #1 source of numerical mistakes. Always convert all dimensions to meters before plugging into formulas.
> 3. **Confusing the formula** — The deflection formula $\delta = \frac{4FL^3}{Ewt^3}$ is for an end-loaded cantilever. The fixed-fixed beam has a different formula ($\delta = \frac{FL^3}{192EI}$). Read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Forgetting the directional nature of E** — Part (b) explicitly says [100] direction, so use $E_{[100]} = 130~\text{GPa}$, not 188 GPa or any other value.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Silicon's crystal structure is diamond cubic**, which gives it **3 independent elastic constants**: $C_{11}$, $C_{12}$, and $C_{44}$.
- **The stiffness matrix in Voigt notation** has the form where the upper-left 3×3 block couples normal stresses and strains, and the lower-right 3×3 block contains only pure shears (all off-diagonals zero).
- **Young's Modulus is direction-dependent**: $E_{[100]} = 130~\text{GPa}$, $E_{[110]} = 169~\text{GPa}$, $E_{[111]} = 188~\text{GPa}$.
- **Poisson's ratio** for silicon is $\nu \approx 0.28$ (cubic crystal, single value).
- **The directional E formula** is the most-asked KTU question: $\frac{1}{E} = S_{11} - 2\left(S_{11} - S_{12} - \frac{S_{44}}{2}\right)(l_1^2 l_2^2 + l_2^2 l_3^2 + l_3^2 l_1^2)$.
- **Anisotropy ratio** $A = \frac{2C_{44}}{C_{11} - C_{12}} = 1.56$ for silicon. $A = 1$ would mean isotropy.
- **Bulk modulus** $K = 98~\text{GPa}$; **Shear modulus** $G = 52~\text{GPa}$ (approximately).
- **Yield strength** of bulk silicon is ~7 GPa, but **fracture strength** at microscale is typically 1–3 GPa due to surface defects.
- **For MEMS design**, the choice of crystal orientation directly affects beam stiffness, resonant frequency, and stress distribution.
- **Bulk micromachining uses anisotropic etchants** like KOH and TMAH, which etch (100) and (110) planes ~100× faster than (111) planes.
- **In KTU exams, always state the crystal direction** explicitly when computing elastic properties — this is the mark-bearing phrase.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microsystem Fabrication Processes: Photolithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microsystem Fabrication Processes: Photolithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Photolithography

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photolithography** is a planar, pattern-transfer process used in microsystem and integrated-circuit (IC) fabrication. It uses **ultraviolet (UV) light** projected through a precisely engineered **photomask** to selectively expose regions of a light-sensitive polymeric film called **photoresist**, which is spun onto a substrate (typically silicon wafer). The exposed (or unexposed) regions of the resist are then chemically dissolved in a **developer** solution, thereby replicating the mask pattern onto the substrate for subsequent etching, doping, or deposition steps.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, photolithography is classified as the **master patterning technology** of all microsystem manufacturing because it defines the **lateral (X-Y) geometry** of every MEMS device, microchannel, cantilever, and transistor on a chip.

## 1.2 The Intuitive "Sticker-Printing" Analogy

Imagine you want to print a fancy design (say, your initials) on a white T-shirt. The process would be:

1. You buy a **stencil** (this is your **photomask**).
2. You place the stencil on a sheet that has a hidden ink layer that only appears when hit by sunlight (**photoresist**).
3. You expose sunlight onto the stencil (**UV exposure**).
4. Where sunlight passes through, the ink turns visible. Where the stencil blocks light, the ink stays hidden.
5. You wash the sheet — the parts that were hit by light wash away (or stay, depending on the sheet type), and your design appears (**development**).

Photolithography is essentially this same process, but executed with **sub-micrometer precision**, on a **silicon wafer**, using **deep UV light** (wavelengths as short as **193 nm** or **13.5 nm** in EUV), and the "T-shirt" is the future microchip or MEMS device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping (Analogy → Engineering)**
> - **T-shirt** → Silicon / Glass / Quartz substrate
> - **Stencil** → Photomask (chrome on quartz)
> - **Hidden ink sheet** → Photoresist
> - **Sunlight** → UV illumination source (mercury lamp / excimer laser)
> - **Washing** → Chemical development (TMAH / KOH / solvents)

## 1.3 Why Photolithography is the Heart of Microsystem Fabrication

- **Resolution**: Modern DUV/EUV tools can print features as small as **~7 nm** half-pitch.
- **Throughput**: A single 300 mm wafer can hold **hundreds of die**, all patterned in **<1 second per exposure field**.
- **Repeatability**: Defect densities in production fabs are **<0.01 defects/cm²**.
- **Versatility**: The same tool family patterns MEMS, CMOS, photonics, and bio-MEMS.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| UV wavelength (Hg i-line) | **365 nm** | nm |
| DUV (ArF excimer) | **193 nm** | nm |
| EUV | **13.5 nm** | nm |
| Wafer diameter | **75, 100, 150, 200, 300** | mm |
| Photoresist thickness | **0.1 – 2.0** | µm |
| Spinner rotation speed | **1,000 – 6,000** | rpm |
| Soft-bake temperature | **90 – 120** | °C |
| Typical exposure dose | **20 – 200** | mJ/cm² |
| Numerical Aperture (NA) | **0.16 – 1.35** | — |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Canonical Steps of Photolithography

The process is universally structured into the following sequential steps. KTU questions frequently test the **order, temperature, chemistry, and purpose** of each step.

1. **Wafer Cleaning (Piranha / RCA)** — Removes organic, ionic, and metallic contaminants using **H₂SO₄ + H₂O₂** (Piranha) and **NH₄OH + H₂O₂ + H₂O** (SC-1) followed by **HCl + H₂O₂ + H₂O** (SC-2). *Why?* Particulate contamination causes **pinholes and pattern defects** in the final resist image.

2. **Dehydration Bake (≥ 200 °C for 5–10 min)** — Drives off adsorbed moisture. *Why?* Water on the wafer causes **resist popping** and poor adhesion.

3. **Adhesion Promoter (HMDS)** — Hexamethyldisilazane is vapor-deposited to convert the hydrophilic Si–OH surface into a hydrophobic Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃ surface. *Why?* Photoresist is also hydrophobic; matching chemistries improves wetting and adhesion.

4. **Photoresist Coating (Spin Coating)** — Liquid resist is dispensed, and the wafer is spun at **1,000 – 6,000 rpm** for **20 – 60 s**. Centripetal force spreads a thin, uniform film. The film thickness $t$ follows:

   $$t = \frac{k \cdot C^{\beta} \cdot \eta^{\gamma}}{\omega^{\alpha}}$$

   where $C$ is resist solids content, $\eta$ is viscosity, $\omega$ is angular spin speed, and $k, \alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are empirically fitted constants. In practice, $t \propto \omega^{-1/2}$ is a useful approximation.

5. **Soft Bake (Pre-exposure Bake)** — Hot-plate bake at **90 – 120 °C for 60 s** removes residual solvent and densifies the film. *Why?* Excess solvent absorbs UV, causing **T-topping** and poor CD control.

6. **Alignment & Exposure (UV through mask)** — The mask is aligned to existing wafer features using **alignment marks**, and the photoresist is exposed to filtered UV. **Photoactive Compound (PAC)** molecules in the resist undergo a chemical change.

7. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** — Hot-plate bake at **100 – 130 °C for 60 – 120 s**. For chemically amplified resists, this drives the **acid-catalyzed deprotection reaction**. *Why?* Improves sidewall profile and reduces **standing-wave effects**.

8. **Development** — The wafer is immersed in developer (**TMAH 0.26 N** for positive DUV resists, or **TMAH 2.38 %** for older i-line resists). Either exposed (positive) or unexposed (negative) regions dissolve.

9. **Hard Bake (Post-development Bake)** — **120 – 150 °C for 5 – 30 min** to harden the resist prior to etching/implantation, improving **etch resistance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Positive vs. Negative Resist — At a Glance**
> - **Positive resist**: Exposed regions become **soluble** in developer (chains broken). Used for **fine-line patterning** because of better resolution and contrast.
> - **Negative resist**: Exposed regions become **insoluble** (cross-linked). Used when **thick, mechanically robust** films are needed (e.g., electroplating molds, LIGA).

## 2.2 Lithography Exposure Modes

| Mode | Gap (mask-to-wafer) | Resolution | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | 0 (hard contact) | High (~λ) | Lab/legacy MEMS |
| **Proximity** | 5 – 50 µm | Lower | MEMS with non-planar wafers |
| **Projection (Step-and-Scan)** | ∞ (lens in between) | **Sub-50 nm** | Modern IC fabs |

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they appear in **3-mark, 7-mark, and 14-mark** derivations alike.

| # | Equation | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$ | **Rayleigh Resolution** — Minimum resolvable half-pitch | $R$ in nm, $\lambda$ in nm, $NA$ dimensionless, $k_1 \approx 0.25 - 0.5$ |
| 2 | $$\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}$$ | **Depth of Focus** — Tolerance to wafer non-planarity | DOF in nm, $k_2 \approx 0.5 - 1.0$ |
| 3 | $$NA = n \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ | **Numerical Aperture** of the projection lens | $n$ = refractive index of medium, $\theta$ = half-angle of cone |
| 4 | $$E = I \cdot t$$ | **Exposure Dose** | $E$ in mJ/cm², $I$ in mW/cm², $t$ in s |
| 5 | $$\text{CD}_{\text{printed}} = \text{CD}_{\text{mask}} \cdot \frac{1}{M}$$ | **Critical Dimension** transfer (M = magnification) | CD in nm, M = 1, 4, or 5 |
| 6 | $$t = k \cdot \frac{\eta^{\gamma}}{\omega^{1/2}}$$ | **Spin-coated film thickness** (empirical) | $t$ in nm, $\omega$ in rpm |
| 7 | $$\text{Contrast} = \frac{\gamma_{1} - \gamma_{2}}{\gamma_{1} + \gamma_{2}}$$ | **Resist contrast** (gamma) — higher is sharper | dimensionless, ideal > 5 |
| 8 | $$D = \sqrt{\lambda \cdot g}$$ | **Proximity gap diffraction blur** | $D$ in µm, $\lambda$ in µm, $g$ in µm |
| 9 | $$R_{\text{blur}} = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda \cdot g}{d}$$ | **Contact/proximity blur diameter** | $d$ = feature size on mask |

> [!CAUTION]
> **NEVER** use the `|` character in a table cell — it breaks the markdown parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IC Manufacturing**: Defines transistor gate lengths in microprocessors (TSMC N3, Samsung 3 nm GAA).
- **MEMS**: Patterns photoresist sacrificial layers, etch masks for deep RIE, electroplating molds for LIGA.
- **Bio-MEMS / Lab-on-Chip**: Defines microchannel widths (often **10 – 100 µm** for cell sorting).
- **Optoelectronics**: Patterns gratings and photonic crystals.
- **NEMS**: Pushes resolution to <100 nm for nanocantilevers and nanowires.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Rayleigh Resolution Criterion (Line-by-Line)

We derive the minimum printable half-pitch from the diffraction of light through a finite aperture.

**Step 1 — Statement of the diffraction problem.**

When UV light of wavelength $\lambda$ passes through a slit of width $w$ on the photomask, it does not continue as a perfect beam. By **Huygens–Fresnel diffraction**, the slit acts as a source of secondary spherical wavefronts that interfere in the far field, producing an intensity pattern $I(x)$ described by the single-slit diffraction equation:

$$
I(\theta) = I_0 \left[ \frac{\sin\left(\frac{\pi w \sin\theta}{\lambda}\right)}{\left(\frac{\pi w \sin\theta}{\lambda}\right)} \right]^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Rayleigh criterion.**

Lord Rayleigh stated that two points are *just resolvable* when the central maximum of one diffraction pattern falls on the **first minimum** of the other. For a circular lens aperture, the first minimum occurs at angle:

$$
\sin \theta_{\min} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}
$$

where $D$ is the lens aperture diameter. Substituting $NA = n \sin\theta$ and the relation $f \approx D / (2 \, NA)$ from optics, we obtain:

$$
\sin \theta_{\min} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{2 f \, NA}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the wavelength to obtain the half-pitch.**

The minimum resolvable feature (the distance between two point images) is:

$$
R = f \cdot \sin \theta_{\min}
$$

Substituting $\sin\theta_{\min}$:

$$
R = f \cdot \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{2 f \, NA}
$$

The focal length $f$ cancels:

$$
R = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{2 \, NA}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to the engineering form.**

The factor $1.22/2 \approx 0.61$ is absorbed into an empirical process constant $k_1$, which lumps together lens aberrations, resist chemistry, mask effects, and illumination partial coherence. Hence:

$$
\boxed{R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}}
$$

with **typical $k_1$ values**:
- $k_1 \geq 0.8$ → easy, "old" processes
- $k_1 = 0.5$ → standard production
- $k_1 = 0.25$ → theoretical limit (single-exposure ArF immersion)
- $k_1 = 0.20$ → only achievable with **computational lithography** (OPC, SMO, EUV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: The path to sub-7 nm nodes came from *lowering* $k_1$ via **Optical Proximity Correction (OPC)**, **Source-Mask Optimization (SMO)**, and **multi-patterning**, since $\lambda$ cannot easily be reduced below 13.5 nm (EUV) due to the lack of suitable mask blank materials and resist sensitivity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Depth of Focus (DOF)

**Step 1 — Definition.** The depth of focus is the axial range over which the image remains acceptably sharp. Beyond this range, the spot diameter grows by **less than 30 %** (Rayleigh tolerance).

**Step 2 — Geometric argument.** A ray converging to the image plane at angle $\theta$ traces a cone. The transverse spot size at a defocus $\Delta z$ from the focal plane is:

$$
\delta = 2 \, \Delta z \, \tan \theta \approx 2 \, \Delta z \, \sin \theta
$$

**Step 3 — Rayleigh condition.** The defocus is acceptable when $\delta \leq R$, i.e.:

$$
2 \, \Delta z \, \sin\theta \leq k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

Solving for $\Delta z_{\max}$ (i.e. DOF/2):

$$
\Delta z_{\max} = \frac{k_1 \, \lambda}{2 \, NA \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\sin\theta = NA / n$** (for a lens imaging into medium of refractive index $n$):

$$
\Delta z_{\max} = \frac{k_1 \, \lambda}{2 \, (NA^2 / n)} = \frac{k_1 \, n \, \lambda}{2 \, NA^2}
$$

Absorbing the constants into $k_2 = k_1 n / 2$:

$$
\boxed{\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}}
$$

For air ($n=1$), $k_2 \approx 0.5$. For immersion ($n=1.44$, water), $k_2 \approx 0.72$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Exposure Dose and Resist Sensitivity

**Step 1 — Exposure Dose Definition.** The dose is the energy delivered per unit area:

$$
E = I \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Thresholding.** A positive resist dissolves only where $E \geq E_0$ (the clearing dose). The **image log-slope (NILS)** and resist contrast $\gamma$ determine how sharply the boundary forms.

**Step 3 — Resist Contrast.** The contrast $\gamma$ is defined as the slope of the normalized thickness-vs-log-dose curve:

$$
\gamma = \left[ \log_{10}\left( \frac{E_0}{E_1} \right) \right]^{-1}
$$

where $E_0$ is the dose at which the resist just clears, and $E_1$ is the dose at which the resist is fully removed. A higher $\gamma$ means a steeper sidewall and better CD control.

**Step 4 — Typical values.** For DUV chemically amplified resists, $\gamma \approx 5 - 10$. For EUV resists, $\gamma$ is usually lower (3 – 6) because of stochastic effects.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 193 nm ArF immersion scanner is used with $NA = 1.35$ and $k_1 = 0.27$. Calculate (a) the half-pitch resolution, (b) the depth of focus in air-equivalent terms, and (c) the required exposure dose if the intensity at the wafer is **25 mW/cm²** and the clearing dose is **30 mJ/cm²**.

### Solution

**(a) Resolution:**

$$
R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA} = 0.27 \cdot \frac{193 \, \text{nm}}{1.35}
$$

$$
R = 0.27 \cdot 142.96 \, \text{nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{R \approx 38.6 \, \text{nm}}
$$

**(b) Depth of Focus (using $k_2 = 0.5$ in air):**

$$
\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{193 \, \text{nm}}{(1.35)^2}
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{193}{1.8225} \, \text{nm} = 0.5 \cdot 105.9 \, \text{nm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{DOF} \approx 52.9 \, \text{nm}}
$$

**Engineering commentary:** A DOF of ~50 nm is razor-thin. Modern scanners compensate via **wafer-stage precision (± 0.3 nm)**, **active focusing interferometry**, and **tilt-correction hexapods**.

**(c) Exposure time:**

$$
t = \frac{E_0}{I} = \frac{30 \, \text{mJ/cm}^2}{25 \, \text{mW/cm}^2} = \frac{30}{0.025} \, \text{s} \quad \text{(Note: 25 mW = 0.025 W)}
$$

$$
\boxed{t = 1.2 \, \text{s}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly mix units. Always convert **mW → W** and **mJ → J** consistently: $1 \, \text{mW} = 10^{-3} \, \text{W}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Photolithography Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Bare Silicon Wafer 300mm"] --> B["Step 1: Piranha + RCA Cleaning"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Dehydration Bake 200C 5 min"]
    C --> D["Step 3: HMDS Adhesion Promoter Vapor Prime"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Spin Coat Photoresist 3000 rpm 30s"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Soft Bake 100C 60s Hot Plate"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Mask Alignment under Microscope"]
    G --> H["Step 7: UV Exposure DUV 193nm Dose 30 mJ per cm2"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Post Exposure Bake 110C 90s"]
    I --> J["Step 9: TMAH Development 60s"]
    J --> K{"Resist Type?"}
    K -->|Positive| L["Exposed regions DISSOLVE"]
    K -->|Negative| M["Exposed regions REMAIN crosslinked"]
    L --> N["Step 10: Hard Bake 130C 10 min"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["Step 11: Etch or Implant Transfer Pattern"]
    O --> P["Step 12: Resist Strip O2 Plasma or NMP"]
    P --> Q["END: Patterned Wafer Ready for Next Module"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Exposure Tool Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Excimer Laser Source 193nm or EUV 13.5nm"] --> B["Beam Homogenizer and Shaper"]
    B --> C["Illuminator Optics Condenser NA 0.2 to 0.85"]
    C --> D["Reticle Mask with Chrome Pattern"]
    D --> E["Projection Optics Reduction Lens NA 1.35"]
    E --> F["Immersion Fluid Water n 1.44"]
    F --> G["Photoresist on Silicon Wafer"]
    G --> H["Wafer Stage Hexapod with Laser Interferometer"]
    H --> I["Alignment System Through the Lens TTL"]
    I --> J["CD Metrology and Overlay Sensors"]
    J --> K["Patterned Image on Wafer"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Resolution-DOF Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Goal: Smaller Transistors"] --> B["Reduce Wavelength lambda"]
    A --> C["Increase Numerical Aperture NA"]
    A --> D["Reduce k1 via Resolution Enhancement Techniques RET"]
    B --> E["Smaller R Resolution"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    C --> F["Smaller DOF Depth of Focus"]
    B --> F
    F --> G["Tighter Wafer Flatness and Stage Precision"]
    G --> H["Higher Tool Cost"]
    E --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the trade-off diagram**: The lithography engineer's three "knobs" ($\lambda$, $NA$, $k_1$) all push resolution down, but increasing $NA$ **squared-drops** the depth of focus, demanding astronomically precise stages. This is the **central engineering tension** in advanced lithography — and is why EUV + computational lithography became essential at the 7 nm node.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAET864]`** Differentiate between **positive** and **negative photoresist** with one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Positive Resist | Negative Resist |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Exposed regions become **soluble** in developer due to chain scission | Exposed regions become **insoluble** due to crosslinking |
| Tone | Same as mask (light→dissolved) | Opposite of mask (light→remains) |
| Resolution | **Better** (no swelling) | Lower (swelling limits aspect ratio) |
| Example | **PMMA**, **DNQ-novolac** (i-line) | **SU-8**, **AZ-nLOF** |

*Marking scheme: 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for resolution/swelling, 1 mark for examples.*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET864]`** What is **HMDS** and why is it used in the photolithography process?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
HMDS stands for **Hexamethyldisilazane**, formula $(CH_3)_3Si–NH–Si(CH_3)_3$. It is a **silylation agent** vapor-deposited onto the silicon wafer before resist coating. Its function is to convert the hydrophilic native oxide (Si–OH) surface into a hydrophobic Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃ surface, which **chemically matches** the hydrophobic photoresist and **promotes adhesion**, preventing resist delamination during development and subsequent processing.

*Marking scheme: 1 mark for full form + formula, 1 mark for chemistry, 1 mark for adhesion purpose.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAET864]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **eight major steps** involved in the photolithography process. State the typical temperature, time, and chemistry at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Rayleigh resolution criterion** $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$ and the **depth of focus** $\text{DOF} = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$. Explain the engineering trade-offs implied by these equations for modern sub-50 nm lithography. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) (7 marks)

The eight steps are tabulated below (1.5 marks for the diagram and ordering, 5.5 marks for the table content):

| # | Step | Equipment | Chemistry / Parameter | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wafer Cleaning** | Wet bench | H₂SO₄/H₂O₂ (Piranha) + SC-1 (NH₄OH/H₂O₂/H₂O) + SC-2 (HCl/H₂O₂/H₂O) | Remove organics, ions, metals |
| 2 | **Dehydration Bake** | Hot plate | 200 °C, 5 min, in N₂ | Remove adsorbed water |
| 3 | **HMDS Priming** | Vapor prime oven | HMDS vapor, 150 °C, 5 min | Hydrophobize surface for adhesion |
| 4 | **Photoresist Coat** | Coater | 3000 rpm, 30 s, ~1 µm thick | Form uniform polymer film |
| 5 | **Soft Bake** | Hot plate | 100 °C, 60 s | Remove solvent, densify |
| 6 | **Exposure** | Stepper / scanner | DUV dose 30 mJ/cm² | Photochemical reaction in resist |
| 7 | **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** | Hot plate | 110 °C, 90 s | Drive acid diffusion (CAR) |
| 8 | **Development** | Wet track | TMAH 2.38 %, 60 s puddle | Dissolve exposed (positive) regions |
| 9 | **Hard Bake** | Oven | 130 °C, 10 min | Harden for etch/implant |

*(Step 9 is sometimes counted as part of step 8 in shorter KTU versions; both are accepted.)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) (7 marks)

**Derivation of Resolution (3.5 marks):**

Starting from single-slit diffraction, the first minimum of the intensity pattern lies at:

$$
\sin\theta_{\min} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D}
$$

where $D$ is the lens diameter. The numerical aperture is $NA = n \sin\theta$, so:

$$
R = f \cdot \sin\theta_{\min} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{2 \, NA} \approx 0.61 \, \frac{\lambda}{NA}
$$

Absorbing the constant into a process factor $k_1$:

$$
\boxed{R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}}
$$

**Derivation of DOF (2 marks):** Following the geometric argument (defocus $\delta \leq R$):

$$
\boxed{\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}}
$$

**Trade-off Discussion (1.5 marks):**
- Pushing **smaller $R$** requires reducing $\lambda$, increasing $NA$, and decreasing $k_1$.
- However, $\text{DOF} \propto 1/NA^2$, so **resolution gains via NA are rapidly paid for by loss of focus tolerance**.
- Sub-50 nm nodes therefore require $\lambda = 13.5$ nm (EUV), $NA = 0.33 - 0.55$, $k_1 = 0.20$ via OPC/SMO, and **wafer-stage precision of < 0.3 nm**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What are the three main **exposure modes** in photolithography? Compare their resolution, mask-to-wafer gap, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A photolithography system uses a **mercury i-line (365 nm)** source with $NA = 0.65$ and $k_1 = 0.45$. Calculate:
  (i) the minimum printable half-pitch,
  (ii) the depth of focus,
  (iii) the exposure time needed if the wafer-stage intensity is **15 mW/cm²** and the clearing dose is **45 mJ/cm²**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) (7 marks)

| Mode | Mask-Wafer Gap | Resolution Limit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | 0 µm (hard contact) | ~λ (≈ 365 nm for i-line) | University labs, MEMS R&D |
| **Proximity** | 5 – 50 µm | $\sqrt{\lambda g}$ Fresnel diffraction | MEMS on non-planar wafers |
| **Projection** | ∞ (lens-coupled) | $k_1 \lambda / NA$ (sub-30 nm) | Modern IC and MEMS fabs |

**Resolution derivation for proximity mode (3 marks):**

The Fresnel diffraction blur at a gap $g$ for feature of size $d$ is:

$$
D = \sqrt{\lambda \cdot g}
$$

For $g = 20 \, \mu m$, $\lambda = 365 \, \text{nm} = 0.365 \, \mu m$:

$$
D = \sqrt{0.365 \times 20} = \sqrt{7.3} \approx 2.7 \, \mu m
$$

Thus, proximity mode is limited to multi-micrometer features, hence its use in **thick-resist MEMS** but never in cutting-edge ICs.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) (7 marks)

**(i) Resolution (2 marks):**

$$
R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA} = 0.45 \cdot \frac{365 \, \text{nm}}{0.65}
$$

$$
R = 0.45 \cdot 561.5 \, \text{nm} \approx \boxed{252.7 \, \text{nm}}
$$

**(ii) Depth of Focus (2 marks):**

$$
\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{365 \, \text{nm}}{(0.65)^2}
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{365}{0.4225} = 0.5 \cdot 864.5 \approx \boxed{432.2 \, \text{nm}}
$$

**(iii) Exposure Time (3 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{E_0}{I} = \frac{45 \, \text{mJ/cm}^2}{15 \, \text{mW/cm}^2}
$$

Convert: $15 \, \text{mW/cm}^2 = 15 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W/cm}^2$ and $45 \, \text{mJ/cm}^2 = 45 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J/cm}^2$:

$$
t = \frac{45 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J/cm}^2}{15 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W/cm}^2} = \frac{45}{15} \, \text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t = 3.0 \, \text{s}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (where students lose 2–4 marks each):**
> 1. **Mixing mW and mJ units** — Always convert to base SI (W, J) **before** dividing. Many students write $t = 45/15 = 3$ directly (which is correct numerically) but **fail to state the unit conversion**, costing the unit mark.
> 2. **Confusing $k_1$ and $k_2$** — $k_1$ is the resolution constant (0.25 – 0.5); $k_2$ is the DOF constant (0.5 – 1.0). Swapping them is the **#1 reason for full-mark loss** in 7-mark numericals.
> 3. **Forgetting the Rayleigh constant $1.22$** when deriving from first principles. If you derive using the small-angle approximation, justify it; otherwise the examiner deducts.
> 4. **Writing "photoresist" instead of "photoresist" or "PR"** — accepted, but consistently calling it **"photoresist"** (one word) is the **KTU-blessed spelling**.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why"** in step-description questions — always give **one sentence on the engineering reason** for every step, or the examiner deducts 1 mark per skipped "Why".
> 6. **Drawing the photolithography steps as a circle** — the process is **linear and sequential**, not cyclic. Circular flowcharts are an instant 0.5-mark penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photolithography — Key Takeaways for KTU 2024 Exam Day**
> - **Photolithography** is a planar pattern-transfer process that defines lateral geometry in MEMS/IC fabrication.
> - **Eight (or nine) sequential steps**: Cleaning → Dehydration → HMDS → Spin Coat → Soft Bake → Expose → PEB → Develop → Hard Bake.
> - **Positive resist** (e.g., PMMA, DNQ-novolac) gives higher resolution; **negative resist** (e.g., SU-8) gives thicker, more robust films.
> - **Three exposure modes**: Contact (best resolution, mask damage), Proximity (no contact, blurred), Projection (lens-based, modern).
> - **Rayleigh Resolution**: $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$ — push $\lambda \downarrow$, $NA \uparrow$, $k_1 \downarrow$ for smaller features.
> - **Depth of Focus**: $\text{DOF} = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$ — **quadratically** shrinks as $NA$ grows; major engineering pain point.
> - **Exposure Dose**: $E = I \cdot t$, with $E_0$ (clearing dose) typically **20 – 200 mJ/cm²** for DUV.
> - **HMDS** is the adhesion promoter; **TMAH 2.38 %** is the standard developer; **O₂ plasma** strips residual resist.
> - **Numerical Aperture** $NA = n \sin\theta$; water-immersion lithography pushes $n$ from 1.0 to 1.44.
> - **Modern highlights**: DUV 193 nm, EUV 13.5 nm, multi-patterning, OPC, SMO — all aimed at pushing $k_1$ below 0.25.
> - **MEMS-specific twist**: photolithography also patterns **sacrificial layers** and **electroplating molds** (LIGA, UV-LIGA) — expect a question on this!
> - **Spin-coating thickness** scales as $t \propto 1/\sqrt{\omega}$ — higher spin speed = thinner film.
> - **Soft-bake and PEB** are **non-optional** — they govern resist contrast, sidewall angle, and CD uniformity.
> - **Always justify units** in numericals; the KTU evaluator is strict on this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ion Implantation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ion Implantation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ion Implantation** is a directed, non-equilibrium, low-temperature physical process used in semiconductor and MEMS fabrication in which energetic, charged particles (ions) of a desired dopant species are accelerated by a high electric field (typically **1 keV to several MeV**) and bombarded into a solid target substrate. The dopant atoms come to rest at depths of nanometers to micrometers below the surface, modifying the **electrical, mechanical, chemical, and optical** properties of the host material.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, ion implantation is positioned as a **key doping and surface modification technique** that is indispensable for:
- Controlled dopant introduction in CMOS, BiCMOS, and MEMS structures
- Threshold voltage adjustment in MOSFETs
- Formation of piezoresistors in silicon pressure sensors
- Localized doping of nano-scale electronic devices
- Creating etch stops and buried layers in MEMS bulk micromachining

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Ion implantation is a **physical, not chemical, process** — there is no thermal diffusion. The dopant profile is therefore **non-equilibrium**, allowing concentrations far exceeding the solid solubility limit of the host material.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Atomic Billiards Model

Imagine firing a stream of **marbles (ions)** from a powerful air-gun (the accelerator) into a tightly packed bed of **sand grains (target atoms)**. Each marble ricochets, slows down through countless tiny collisions, and finally comes to rest somewhere inside the sand. The deeper the marble's initial energy, the deeper it travels.

- The **stopping mechanism** is analogous to friction: marbles lose energy to both the sand grains (nuclear stopping) and to vibration of individual sand particles (electronic stopping).
- If you tilt the sand bed to align with a column of grains (a crystallographic direction), marbles can travel **much deeper** — this is the **channeling effect**.
- Craters form where high-energy marbles smash sand grains apart — analogous to **lattice damage**, which must later be repaired by **annealing**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ion Energy | $E$ | 1 – 1000 | keV |
| Dose (fluence) | $\phi$ | $10^{11}$ – $10^{16}$ | ions/cm² |
| Beam Current | $I$ | 0.1 – 10 | mA |
| Vacuum Level | $P$ | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-8}$ | Torr |
| Projected Range | $R_p$ | 10 – 1000 | nm |
| Projected Straggle | $\Delta R_p$ | 5 – 100 | nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Constants used in this Module:**
> - Boltzmann constant: **$k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
> - Elementary charge: **$e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**
> - Avogadro's number: **$N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹**
> - Permittivity of free space: **$\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m**
> - Bohr radius: **$a_0 = 5.29 \times 10^{-11}$ m**

## 1.4 Why Ion Implantation is Preferred in Nanoelectronics

- **Precise dose control** (electrical current integration $\rightarrow$ exact ion count)
- **Reproducible sub-100 nm junction depths** for nanoscale CMOS
- **Sharp, abrupt dopant profiles** impossible with thermal diffusion
- **Mask-free selective doping** using photoresist / oxide / metal masks
- **Low processing temperature** ($< 100°C$) — compatible with pre-fabricated MEMS
- **Wide range of dopants** (B, P, As, Sb, Si, Ge, O, N, Au, etc.)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Ion–Solid Interaction

When an accelerated ion enters a crystalline target, it undergoes a sequence of **binary collisions** with both the lattice nuclei and the bound electrons. The energy loss per unit path length is partitioned into two independent mechanisms:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = N \left[ S_n(E) + S_e(E) \right]$$

where:
- $N$ = atomic density of the target (atoms/cm³)
- $S_n(E)$ = **nuclear stopping cross-section** (elastic energy transfer to target nuclei)
- $S_e(E)$ = **electronic stopping cross-section** (inelastic energy transfer to target electrons)
- $x$ = path length traversed

### 2.1.1 Nuclear Stopping — Dominant at LOW energies ($E < 50$ keV)
Caused by **elastic Coulomb scattering** between the moving ion and the screened nucleus of a target atom. Results in large-angle deflections, significant displacement of lattice atoms, and **lattice damage**.

**Thomas–Fermi screened Coulomb potential model (LSS theory):**

$$S_n(E) = C_k \, Z_1 Z_2 \, e^2 \, a \left( \frac{M_1}{M_2} \right) \left[ \frac{1}{2} \ln\left( \frac{E}{E_{th}} \right) - \text{const} \right]$$

where:
- $Z_1$, $Z_2$ = atomic numbers of ion and target
- $M_1$, $M_2$ = atomic masses of ion and target
- $a$ = **Thomas–Fermi screening length** $a = 0.8853 \, a_0 \, Z^{-1/3}$ with $Z = (Z_1^{2/3} + Z_2^{2/3})^{1/2}$
- $C_k \approx 4\pi$ for a power-law potential

### 2.1.2 Electronic Stopping — Dominant at HIGH energies ($E > 50$ keV)
Caused by **inelastic excitation and ionization** of target electrons. The ion transfers energy to the electron cloud via long-range Coulomb interaction, behaving like a **viscous drag**.

**Bethe–Bloch (high-energy) approximation:**

$$S_e(E) = \frac{2\pi Z_1^2 Z_2 e^4}{m_e v^2} \left[ \ln\left( \frac{2 m_e v^2}{I} \right) \right]$$

where:
- $m_e$ = electron mass = $9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $v$ = ion velocity
- $I$ = mean ionization potential of the target ($\approx 10$ eV for Si)

**Lindhard–Scharff (low-to-medium energy) approximation:**

$$S_e(E) = k_e \sqrt{E} \quad \text{where} \quad k_e \propto Z_1^{1/6} Z_2^{1/2} M_1^{1/2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crossover energy $E_c$ where $S_n = S_e$ is approximately:
> $$E_c \approx \frac{2 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \times (Z_1 Z_2)^{4/3} \times 100 \text{ keV}$$
> For B in Si: $E_c \approx 15$ keV. For As in Si: $E_c \approx 130$ keV.

## 2.2 Range Theory — Where Does the Ion Stop?

### 2.2.1 Total Range (R)
The total path length the ion travels before coming to rest:

$$R = \int_0^{E_0} \left( -\frac{dE}{dx} \right)^{-1} dE = \int_0^{E_0} \frac{dE}{N[S_n(E) + S_e(E)]}$$

### 2.2.2 Projected Range ($R_p$) and Straggle ($\Delta R_p$)
Since the ion scatters in 3-D, the **component along the incident direction** is what we measure as depth:

$$R_p = R \cos(\bar{\theta})$$

For an **amorphous target**, the distribution of final ion positions along the incident direction follows a **Gaussian profile**:

$$N(x) = \frac{\phi}{\sqrt{2\pi} \Delta R_p} \exp\left[ -\frac{(x - R_p)^2}{2 (\Delta R_p)^2} \right]$$

This is the **PRB (Pearson IV / Gaussian) profile** that board questions expect.

### 2.2.3 Lateral Straggle ($\Delta R_\perp$)
The transverse spread of the implanted profile, critical for narrow-line devices:

$$\Delta R_\perp \approx \frac{\Delta R_p}{\sqrt{2}}$$

for amorphous targets.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-dE/dx = N[S_n + S_e]$ | Stopping power | eV/nm |
| 2 | $R = \int_0^E dE / (N(S_n+S_e))$ | Total range | nm |
| 3 | $R_p \approx E^k / (N \, q)$ (Schiott) | Projected range, $k \approx 1.5-1.7$ | nm |
| 4 | $N(x) = (\phi / \sqrt{2\pi}\Delta R_p) e^{-(x-R_p)^2/2\Delta R_p^2}$ | Gaussian profile | atoms/cm³ |
| 5 | $n_s = \phi = \int_0^\infty N(x) \, dx$ | Sheet dose / fluence | ions/cm² |
| 6 | $a = 0.8853 a_0 Z^{-1/3}$ | TF screening radius | m |
| 7 | $N_{\text{atoms}} = \rho N_A / M$ | Atomic density of target | atoms/cm³ |
| 8 | $S_e(E) = k_e \sqrt{E}$ | Electronic stopping (LSS) | eV·cm²/atom |
| 9 | $\Delta R_\perp \approx \Delta R_p / \sqrt{2}$ | Lateral straggle (amorphous) | nm |
| 10 | $t_{\text{anneal}} = $ ms – 60 min | RTA duration range | time |
| 11 | $C_{\text{peak}} = \phi / (\sqrt{2\pi} \Delta R_p)$ | Peak concentration | atoms/cm³ |
| 12 | $E_c \propto Z_1 Z_2 (M_1/M_2)$ | Nuclear–electronic crossover | keV |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule in Tables:** All absolute-value / magnitude bars in the formulas above use `\vert` (e.g., $Z_1 \vert Z_2$) when rendered inside the table — this prevents Markdown table-parsing errors. The displayed $\vert$ or \mid separator is intentional.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS Source/Drain Doping:** Sub-10 nm nodes require $R_p < 15$ nm → ultra-low-energy ($< 1$ keV) implants.
- **MEMS Piezoresistors:** Boron implant into p-type Si sets gauge factor in pressure sensors.
- **SOI / Buried Oxide:** High-dose O⁺ implant + annealing → SIMOX wafers used in advanced MEMS.
- **Threshold Voltage Tuning:** $\phi$ controls $V_T$ in MOSFETs ($\Delta V_T \propto \phi$).
- **Quantum Dots / Dopant Atoms:** Single-ion implant with nanometer-precision apertures.
