<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define LPCVD. Why is it operated at sub-atmospheric pressures?**

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Low Pressure Chemical Vapor Deposition (LPCVD) is a thermal CVD process in which thin films are formed by chemical reaction of gaseous precursors on heated substrates, with the reaction chamber maintained at sub-atmospheric pressures of 0.1 to 10 Torr.

**Reason for Low Pressure (1.5 marks):** Operating at low pressure increases the **mean free path** of reactant gas molecules by 2–3 orders of magnitude, transitioning gas flow from the viscous regime to the **molecular (Knudsen) flow regime**. This enables:
1. Excellent **step coverage** on high-aspect-ratio MEMS/IC features.
2. Reduction of unwanted **gas-phase nucleation** and particle formation.
3. Lower **boundary layer thickness**, improving film thickness uniformity across the wafer batch.
4. Higher **wafer throughput** due to the elimination of carrier-gas-dominated viscous transport.

---

### **Question 2: What is the activation energy of polysilicon deposition from silane, and what regime does it indicate?**

> `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Exam]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The activation energy $E_a$ for polysilicon LPCVD from silane (SiH₄) is approximately **1.6 – 1.9 eV** (or ~160 kJ/mol).

This high value indicates that the deposition is operating in the **surface-reaction-limited (kinetically controlled) regime**, where the rate-limiting step is the thermal decomposition of the silane molecule on the substrate surface. The Arrhenius behavior is dominant, and small changes in temperature produce large changes in deposition rate. Industrial reactors are typically operated near the **transition knee** between the kinetic and mass-transport regimes to achieve the best compromise between throughput and within-batch uniformity.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Choice 1]`

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the working of a **hot-wall LPCVD reactor** for polysilicon deposition. Discuss the role of the three-zone furnace and the vacuum system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **mean free path scaling** for a silane molecule in LPCVD, and explain how the resulting molecular flow regime enables **conformal step coverage** in MEMS trench structures of aspect ratio 10:1. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

A hot-wall LPCVD reactor (see Section 4.1) consists of:

1. **Quartz Reaction Tube (1 mark):** A high-purity fused-silica tube of ~150–200 mm internal diameter, sealed at both ends with O-ring end caps. Holds the wafer boat.

2. **Three-Zone Resistive Heating Coil (2 marks):** The furnace is divided into three independently controlled heating zones (front, center, rear). Each zone is driven by a thyristor-controlled heater. The center zone is set slightly higher than the end zones to compensate for **end thermal losses**, achieving a flat axial temperature profile within ±0.5°C over the wafer load.

3. **Quartz Wafer Boat (1 mark):** Holds 100–200 wafers vertically with uniform spacing (3–5 mm) to ensure symmetric gas flow and avoid "bullseye" thickness non-uniformity.

4. **Vacuum Pumping System (2 marks):** A combination of a **rotary vane rough pump** (to 10⁻³ Torr) backed by a **Roots blower** or **dry pump** to achieve the working pressure of 0.1–10 Torr. A **throttle valve** regulates chamber pressure independently of pump speed.

5. **Gas Delivery and Control (1 mark):** A gas cabinet with MFCs feeds precursors (SiH₄) and carrier gas (N₂) into the tube inlet. Pressure is monitored by a **capacitance manometer**.

The working sequence: (a) load wafers → (b) pump to base vacuum → (c) establish working pressure with N₂ → (d) heat to deposition temperature → (e) introduce SiH₄ → (f) deposit for calculated time → (g) pump out and cool in N₂.

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

Starting from the kinetic theory equation:

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P}$$

For a SiH₄ molecule with effective diameter $d \approx 4.1 \times 10^{-10}$ m, at $T = 900$ K and $P = 1$ Torr = 133.3 Pa:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 900}{\sqrt{2} \pi (4.1 \times 10^{-10})^2 \cdot 133.3}$$

Calculate the denominator term-by-term:

- $(4.1 \times 10^{-10})^2 = 1.681 \times 10^{-19}$ m²
- $\sqrt{2} \pi = 4.443$
- $4.443 \cdot 1.681 \times 10^{-19} = 7.469 \times 10^{-19}$ m²
- $7.469 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 133.3 = 9.956 \times 10^{-17}$ m²·Pa

Therefore:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.242 \times 10^{-20}}{9.956 \times 10^{-17}} = 1.247 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 124.7 \text{ µm}$$

**Conformality on High-Aspect-Ratio Trenches (4 marks):**

For a MEMS trench of width $w = 1$ µm and depth $d = 10$ µm (aspect ratio 10:1), the Knudsen number is:

$$K_n = \frac{\lambda}{w} = \frac{124.7 \text{ µm}}{1 \text{ µm}} = 124.7 \gg 1$$

This confirms the **molecular flow regime**, in which molecules travel in straight-line trajectories with negligible inter-molecular collisions. The flux of SiH₄ molecules incident on the **sidewalls and bottom** of the trench is therefore nearly equal to the flux on the **top surface**, yielding a **conformality ratio (sidewall thickness / top thickness) > 90%**. This is the key advantage of LPCVD over APCVD for MEMS fabrication of deep reactive-ion-etched (DRIE) structures, capacitive sensors, and microfluidic channels.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Internal Choice*

> `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Choice 2]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **LPCVD, APCVD, and PECVD** with respect to operating pressure, temperature, film quality, and step coverage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polysilicon LPCVD process at 1 Torr has a deposition rate of 12 nm/min at 600°C and 32 nm/min at 640°C. Calculate the **activation energy** and identify the **rate-limiting regime**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

| Feature | APCVD | LPCVD | PECVD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure (Torr) | 760 | 0.1–10 | 0.1–10 |
| Temperature (°C) | 400–1100 | 550–900 | 200–400 |
| Activation | Thermal | Thermal | RF plasma + thermal |
| Step Coverage | Poor (~50%) | Excellent (>90%) | Good (~75%) |
| Film Density | Moderate | High | Lower (H incorporation) |
| Throughput | Low (single wafer) | Very high (batch) | High (batch) |
| Capital Cost | Low | Moderate | High |
| Common Films | PSG, BPSG, SiO₂ | Poly-Si, Si₃N₄, W, SiO₂ | a-Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄ |

**Analysis (3 marks):** APCVD uses atmospheric transport; the short mean free path (~65 nm) causes the gas boundary layer to be thicker than the trench depth, leading to poor sidewall coverage. LPCVD's low pressure extends $\lambda$ to 50–150 µm, allowing molecules to penetrate deep trenches before colliding. PECVD uses plasma to provide additional reaction energy at low temperatures, trading some film quality for thermal budget savings — ideal for temperature-sensitive substrates (e.g., post-metallization inter-layer dielectrics).

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

Given: $R_1 = 12$ nm/min at $T_1 = 600°C = 873$ K; $R_2 = 32$ nm/min at $T_2 = 640°C = 913$ K.

**Step 1:** Compute inverse temperatures.

$$\frac{1}{T_1} = 1.1455 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}, \quad \frac{1}{T_2} = 1.0953 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the difference in inverse temperature.

$$\Delta\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = (1.1455 - 1.0953) \times 10^{-3} = 5.02 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the logarithmic rate ratio.

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{32}{12}\right) = \ln(2.667) = 0.9808$$

**Step 4:** Apply the two-point Arrhenius equation.

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln(R_2/R_1)}{\Delta(1/T)} = \frac{8.314 \cdot 0.9808}{5.02 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$E_a = \frac{8.154}{5.02 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.624 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/mol}$$

Convert to eV per molecule:

$$E_a = \frac{1.624 \times 10^5}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 2.697 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

$$E_a = \frac{2.697 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.68 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 5: Regime Identification (2 marks).**

An $E_a$ value of **1.68 eV** is well within the **surface-reaction-limited (kinetic) regime** range of 1.5–2.0 eV for silane pyrolysis. This is consistent with deposition temperatures of 600–640°C, which lie below the transition knee (~650°C for SiH₄ at 1 Torr).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in LPCVD Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to Convert Units:** The activation energy must be expressed either in J/mol (using $R = 8.314$) or in eV/molecule (using $k_B$). Mixing units is the #1 reason for incorrect numerical answers in KTU scripts.
> 2. **Confusing $k_B$ and $R$:** $R = N_A \cdot k_B$. Use $R$ when working in per-mole quantities; use $k_B$ when working with single molecules or Boltzmann statistics.
> 3. **Mean Free Path Pressure Units:** Always convert Torr to Pascals (1 Torr = 133.3 Pa) before substituting into the kinetic theory formula.
> 4. **Ignoring the Operating Regime:** Always conclude your Arrhenius numerical by explicitly stating whether the process is *kinetically limited* or *mass-transport limited*. A numerical answer without regime identification loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Step Coverage Confusion:** Step coverage is the *thickness ratio* between the sidewall/bottom and the top surface, not just the film thickness. Mention *conformal* vs. *non-conformal* explicitly.
> 6. **Skipping the Diagram:** In 7-mark sub-parts asking for "neat diagram," a missing or unlabeled reactor schematic can cost up to 2 marks. Always include the tube, wafer boat, heater zones, and vacuum port.
> 7. **Confusing Reactor Type:** "Hot-wall" reactors are standard for LPCVD (uniform batch heating). "Cold-wall" reactors are used for selective/area-defined epitaxy. Mislabeling is a frequent deduction trigger.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for LPCVD**

- ✅ **LPCVD** = Thermal CVD at **0.1 – 10 Torr** with **hot-wall** batch furnace geometry.
- ✅ Mean free path at 1 Torr is **~50–150 µm** (vs ~200 nm at 760 Torr).
- ✅ Knudsen number $K_n = \lambda / L$ determines the flow regime: $K_n > 0.5$ = molecular flow.
- ✅ **Two Regimes:** Surface-reaction limited (low $T$, high $E_a$) vs. mass-transport limited (high $T$, low $E_a$).
- ✅ **Arrhenius Rate Law:** $R = R_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ governs the kinetic regime.
- ✅ $E_a$ for **Polysilicon (SiH₄):** 1.6 – 1.9 eV.
- ✅ $E_a$ for **SiO₂ (TEOS):** 1.0 – 1.4 eV.
- ✅ $E_a$ for **Si₃N₄ (DCS + NH₃):** ~1.8 eV.
- ✅ **Three-Zone Furnace** is essential to maintain $\pm 0.5°C$ axial temperature uniformity.
- ✅ **Conformal step coverage** is the *defining advantage* of LPCVD over APCVD.
- ✅ Operating temperature window for Poly-Si: **580 – 650°C**; for Si₃N₄: **750 – 850°C**; for SiO₂ (TEOS): **650 – 750°C**.
- ✅ **PECVD** is the *low-temperature alternative* (200 – 400°C) but at the cost of film density and hydrogen content.
- ✅ LPCVD is **batch-mode** (100 – 200 wafers per run), giving it a **throughput** advantage over single-wafer APCVD systems.
- ✅ Critical films produced: **Poly-Si, Si₃N₄, SiO₂, W, PSG, BPSG** — the backbone of MEMS and CMOS process flows.
- ✅ **By-product removal** (HCl, H₂, HF) is critical to prevent particle contamination and film stress buildup.
- ✅ **Sticking coefficient** $S_c$ controls the conformality at low pressures; lower $S_c$ = better sidewall coverage.
- ✅ Always quote **pressure in Torr** (industry standard) and convert to Pa only for formula substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sputtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sputtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEAET864 – Module 2)**
> **Sputtering** is a Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) process in which atoms of a solid *target* material are ejected into the gas phase by the bombardment of the target surface with high-energy ionized particles (typically inert gas ions such as **Ar⁺** accelerated through a potential difference of **0.5 – 5 kV**). The ejected (sputtered) atoms traverse a low-pressure chamber and condense on a *substrate* to form a thin film of thickness ranging from a few **nanometers** to several **micrometers**.

The phenomenon is fundamentally governed by **momentum transfer** from the incident ion to the surface atoms of the target, and is classified as a non-thermal, momentum-driven ejection process (as opposed to thermal evaporation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The "Billiards Table" Model**
> Imagine a billiards (pool) table:
> - The **white cue ball** = the high-energy **Ar⁺ ion** fired from the plasma.
> - The **racked colored balls** = the surface atoms of the **target** held in their crystal lattice.
> - The **table cushions** = the underlying bulk atoms of the target that hold the surface atoms in place.
>
> When the cue ball strikes the rack with sufficient momentum, a few colored balls are knocked *outward* (ejected) — these are the sputtered atoms that travel to the substrate. The cue ball itself may bounce back (reflected neutral Ar atom) or implant into the target. The *cushions* (bulk lattice) absorb most of the remaining energy as heat, but a small, measurable fraction of atoms gets ejected with energies between **1 – 100 eV**.

This "atomic-scale billiards" picture highlights the three essential features of sputtering:
1. It is a **momentum-transfer process**, not a thermal evaporation.
2. A plasma (cloud of ions) is required as the "cannon" of projectiles.
3. The chamber must be at **low pressure** (typically **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁷ Torr** or **0.1 – 10 mTorr**) so sputtered atoms travel ballistically to the substrate.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE] **Key Numerical Benchmarks (must memorize)**
> - **Operating Pressure:** 0.5 – 100 mTorr (≈ 0.07 – 13 Pa)
> - **Discharge Voltage:** 0.3 – 5 kV (DC), 13.56 MHz RF
> - **Sputtering Yield (S):** typically **0.1 – 3 atoms/ion** for most metals at 500 eV Ar⁺
> - **Energy of Sputtered Atoms:** 5 – 100 eV (≈ 10× higher than thermal evaporation)
> - **Mean Free Path (λ) at 10 mTorr:** ≈ **1 cm**
> - **Deposition Rate:** 1 – 100 nm/min for metals

## 1.4 Why Sputtering is the Workhorse of MEMS/Nanoelectronics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Significance**
> Sputtering is the **dominant industrial deposition technique** for MEMS and CMOS metallization because it can deposit:
> - **Alloys and compounds** (e.g., Al-Si-Cu, TiN, ITO) with stoichiometric fidelity.
> - **Refractory metals** (W, Mo, Ta) with melting points > 2500 °C — impossible to evaporate thermally without crucible contamination.
> - **Step coverage** into high-aspect-ratio trenches (key for DRAM capacitor electrodes).
> - **Sub-nanometer film thickness control** via precise time/power monitoring.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

The sputtering process is broken down into **five sequential stages**:

### Stage 1 — Plasma Generation (Gas Ionization)
A low-pressure inert gas (typically **Argon, Ar**, 99.999% purity) is introduced into a vacuum chamber. A high negative DC voltage (cathode = target) or 13.56 MHz RF signal ionizes the gas:

$$Ar + e^{-} \rightarrow Ar^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

This creates a self-sustaining glow-discharge plasma consisting of:
- **Ar⁺ ions** (mass = 39.95 amu, charge = +e)
- **Free electrons** (which sustain the discharge by further impact ionization)
- **Neutral Ar atoms** (background)

### Stage 2 — Ion Acceleration (Bombardment)
The **target (cathode)** is biased to a high negative potential (e.g., **−500 V**). The positively charged Ar⁺ ions are accelerated across the dark space (cathode sheath) and strike the target surface with kinetic energy:

$$E_{ion} = eV_{cathode} \quad \text{(eV)}$$

For V = 500 V, ions hit with **E = 500 eV** — a sweet spot for maximum sputtering yield.

### Stage 3 — Momentum Transfer & Atom Ejection
The energetic ion collides with a surface target atom through a binary elastic collision. If the **recoil energy transferred** exceeds the **surface binding energy** (≈ 2 – 10 eV, often approximated as the heat of sublimation, **U_s**), the target atom is ejected. This is governed by the **kinematic factor**:

$$T = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \, E_{ion} \, \cos^2\theta$$

where **M₁** = ion mass, **M₂** = target atom mass, **θ** = scattering angle in lab frame, **E_ion** = incident ion energy.

> [!NOTE] **Why Ar is preferred as the bombarding gas:**
> Argon is a **noble gas** (chemically inert — does not react with target or film) and has a mass (39.95 amu) reasonably close to common metal targets (Al: 27, Cu: 64, Ti: 48, W: 184), giving a high kinematic transfer factor (T close to 1 for Cu).

### Stage 4 — Transport Through the Chamber
Ejected (sputtered) atoms travel ballistically across the chamber. The mean free path **λ** must be larger than the target-to-substrate distance **d** to avoid gas-phase scattering losses:

$$\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d_{atom}^2 \, P}$$

where **d_atom** = effective atomic diameter of gas, **P** = chamber pressure, **k_B** = Boltzmann constant, **T** = gas temperature.

### Stage 5 — Condensation & Film Growth on Substrate
Sputtered atoms strike the substrate (anode) with energies of **5 – 100 eV** and condense, forming nuclei that grow into a continuous thin film via Volmer–Weber, Frank–van der Merwe, or Stranski–Krastanov mechanisms.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Sputtering Yield | $S = \dfrac{\text{Number of ejected atoms}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$ | atoms/ion (dimensionless) | Predicts deposition rate |
| 2 | Energy transferred in elastic collision | $T = \dfrac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \, E \cos^2\theta$ | eV | Determines if ejection occurs |
| 3 | Sputtering threshold energy | $E_{th} \approx \dfrac{U_s}{\gamma (1 - \gamma)}$ where $\gamma = \dfrac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2}$ | eV | Minimum ion energy for sputtering |
| 4 | Mean free path | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d^2 P}$ | m | Set chamber pressure |
| 5 | Deposition rate | $R = \dfrac{S \, \Gamma \, M_t}{\rho \, N_A} \times \dfrac{1}{A}$ | nm/s | Process planning |
| 6 | Power density at target | $P_d = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A_{target}}$ | W/cm² | Thermal load check |
| 7 | Plasma frequency (electron) | $\omega_{pe} = \sqrt{\dfrac{n_e e^2}{m_e \varepsilon_0}}$ | rad/s | RF matching |
| 8 | Bohm sheath thickness | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_0 V}{e n_e}}$ | m | Ion acceleration distance |
| 9 | Number density of ions | $n_i = \dfrac{J_{ion}}{e \, v_{Bohm}}$ | m⁻³ | Plasma diagnostics |
| 10 | Average sputtered atom energy | $\langle E \rangle \approx \dfrac{U_s}{\gamma}$ | eV | Adatom mobility on substrate |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Boundary Conditions to Always Quote in KTU Answers**
> 1. Yield **S ≈ 0** when **E_ion < E_th** (no sputtering).
> 2. Yield **maximizes** at ion energies of **500 eV – 1 keV**.
> 3. For **E_ion > 10 keV**, ion implantation dominates → yield drops.
> 4. The condition **λ ≥ d** must hold (otherwise thermalized atoms lose directionality and resputter the film).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS Metallization:** Al/Cu interconnect layers in ICs.
- **MEMS Structural Layers:** Si, SiO₂, Si₃N₄ deposition.
- **Hard Coatings:** TiN, CrN, DLC on cutting tools.
- **Optical Coatings:** Anti-reflective multi-layer stacks.
- **Magnetic Films:** CoPt, NiFe for HDD read heads.
- **Photovoltaics:** TCO (transparent conducting oxide) electrodes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Energy Transfer in an Elastic Binary Collision

**Goal:** Find the condition under which the *maximum* kinetic energy is transferred from an incident ion of mass **M₁** and velocity **v₁** to a stationary target atom of mass **M₂** in the lab frame.

### Step 1 — Set up conservation laws
For a perfectly elastic head-on collision, both **momentum** and **kinetic energy** are conserved:

$$M_1 v_1 = M_1 v_1' + M_2 v_2' \quad \text{(momentum)}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2} M_1 v_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} M_1 (v_1')^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} M_2 (v_2')^2 \quad \text{(energy)}$$

### Step 2 — Solve the simultaneous equations
Multiply momentum equation by **M₁ v₁'** and subtract from the energy equation (or, more elegantly, convert to center-of-mass frame). Solving for the target recoil velocity:

$$v_2' = \frac{2 M_1}{M_1 + M_2} \, v_1$$

### Step 3 — Compute the kinetic energy of the recoiling target atom
$$T = \tfrac{1}{2} M_2 (v_2')^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} M_2 \left( \frac{2 M_1}{M_1 + M_2} \right)^2 v_1^2$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \, E_{ion} = \gamma \, E_{ion}}$$

where the **kinematic factor** is:

$$\gamma = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2}$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the result
- $\gamma = 1$ (maximum) when **M₁ = M₂** (e.g., Ar⁺ on Ca⁺ — rare in practice).
- $\gamma \to 0$ when **M₁ ≪ M₂** (light ions cannot eject heavy atoms) or **M₁ ≫ M₂** (heavy ions punch through without transferring energy).
- For **Ar⁺ (40) on Cu (64):** $\gamma = 4(40)(64) / (104)^2 = 10240 / 10816 \approx 0.946$ — excellent momentum match.
- For **Ar⁺ (40) on W (184):** $\gamma = 4(40)(184) / (224)^2 = 29440 / 50176 \approx 0.587$ — still useful but yield is lower.

> [!NOTE] **Physical meaning:** The factor $\gamma$ is the *fraction* of the incident ion's energy that can be transferred to a target atom in a *single head-on collision*. The cosine-squared dependence generalizes this to off-axis impacts.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sputtering Threshold Energy

**Goal:** Determine the minimum ion energy **E_th** at which sputtering becomes possible.

### Step 1 — Energy balance at threshold
At threshold, the energy transferred to the surface atom equals the **surface binding energy** $U_s$ (often approximated as the heat of sublimation):

$$T = U_s \quad \text{at threshold}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the kinematic transfer expression
$$\gamma \, E_{th} = U_s$$

### Step 3 — Account for off-normal incidence and cascade effects
Empirically refined by **Matsunami, Yamamura, and Itoh** (and simplified form):

$$\boxed{E_{th} = \frac{U_s}{\gamma \, (1 - \gamma)}}$$

### Step 4 — Physical interpretation
- The denominator $(1 - \gamma)$ accounts for the back-reflected component of the incident ion — when $\gamma \to 1$, nearly all energy is transferred, so the threshold approaches $U_s$.
- Typical **U_s** values: Al → 3.4 eV, Cu → 3.5 eV, W → 8.7 eV, Au → 3.8 eV.
- For Ar⁺ on Cu: $E_{th} = 3.5 / (0.946 \times 0.054) = 3.5 / 0.051 \approx 68$ eV (matches experiments: ≈ 17 – 30 eV; the discrepancy arises from cascade contributions).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Deposition Rate from Sputtering Yield

**Goal:** Express the film growth rate **R** (nm/s) on the substrate in terms of the ion current density **J**, sputtering yield **S**, target material molar mass **M_t**, and density **ρ_t**.

### Step 1 — Number of atoms ejected per second
If the ion current is **I** and each ion produces **S** ejected atoms:

$$\dot{N} = \frac{I}{e} \cdot S \quad \text{(atoms/sec)}$$

### Step 2 — Mass deposition rate
$$\dot{m} = \dot{N} \cdot \frac{M_t}{N_A} = \frac{I \, S \, M_t}{e \, N_A}$$

### Step 3 — Volumetric deposition rate
$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_t} = \frac{I \, S \, M_t}{e \, N_A \, \rho_t}$$

### Step 4 — Linear deposition rate (thickness per second) on the substrate
If the deposited material spreads uniformly over a substrate area **A_sub**:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{\dot{V}}{A_{sub}} = \frac{I \, S \, M_t}{e \, N_A \, \rho_t \, A_{sub}} \quad \text{(m/s)}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical illustration
**Problem:** A Cu target is sputtered by Ar⁺ at I = 0.5 A, S = 2.0, M_t = 63.5 g/mol, ρ_t = 8.96 g/cm³, A_sub = 100 cm². Find the deposition rate.

**Solution:**

$$R = \frac{(0.5)(2.0)(63.5 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(6.022 \times 10^{23})(8.96 \times 10^{6})(100 \times 10^{-4})}$$

Numerator = (0.5)(2.0)(0.0635) = **0.0635 g/s**

Denominator = (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹)(6.022 × 10²³)(8.96 × 10⁶)(0.01) = **8.63 × 10⁹** (mixed units; the g and cm³ must be reconciled carefully)

After unit normalization, the rate is approximately **R ≈ 70 nm/s = 4.2 μm/min** — a realistic industrial value.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** The 14-mark questions frequently ask for a numerical deposition rate or a comparison of sputter rates between two target materials. Master the *unit-cancellation* step explicitly — it is where students lose the most marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | DC Sputtering | RF Sputtering | Magnetron Sputtering |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Target type | Conducting only | Conducting & dielectric | Conducting (mostly) |
| Voltage / Frequency | 0.3 – 5 kV DC | 13.56 MHz RF | 0.3 – 1 kV DC/RF + B-field |
| Sputtering rate | Moderate | Low (due to lower ion energy) | Very high (10× DC) |
| Substrate heating | High | Moderate | Low (good for temperature-sensitive films) |
| Plasma density | 10⁹ – 10¹⁰ /cm³ | 10⁹ – 10¹⁰ /cm³ | 10¹¹ – 10¹² /cm³ |
| Typical use | Simple metal films | Insulators (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) | Production-line metallization |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Sputtering System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vacuum Chamber Stainless Steel] --> B[Residual Gas Pumping System]
    B --> C[Turbo Molecular Pump 10 to the power of 7 Torr]
    C --> D[Base Pressure Achieved less than 10 to the power of 6 Torr]
    D --> E[Argon Gas Inlet via Mass Flow Controller]
    E --> F[Working Pressure 1 to 10 mTorr]
    F --> G[Plasma Generation Region]
    G --> H[Target Cathode Negatively Biased 0.3 to 5 kV]
    H --> I[Ar Positive Ion Bombardment of Target]
    I --> J[Sputtered Atom Ejection]
    J --> K[Ballistic Transport through Chamber]
    K --> L[Substrate Holder Anode Grounded or Biased]
    L --> M[Film Nucleation and Growth]
    M --> N[Quartz Crystal Monitor for Rate Sensing]
    N --> O[Shutter for Process Control]
    O --> P[Thin Film on Wafer Substrate]

    subgraph PowerSupply [DC or RF Power Supply]
        Q1[DC Supply 0 to 5 kV] --> H
        Q2[13.56 MHz RF Generator] --> H
        Q3[Matching Network for RF] --> Q2
    end

    subgraph Cooling [Target Cooling Loop]
        R1[Water Chiller 15 to 20 deg C] --> H
    end
```

## 4.2 Magnetron Sputtering — Cross-Sectional Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outer Ring Magnet North] --> C[Target Surface]
    B[Central Magnet South] --> C
    C --> D[Magnetic Field Lines B above target]
    D --> E[Trapped Electrons Drift in Cycloid Path]
    E --> F[Enhanced Ionization near Target]
    F --> G[Increased Ar Positive Ion Flux]
    G --> H[Higher Sputter Rate and Lower Voltage]
    H --> I[Substrate below at 5 to 10 cm]
```

> [!NOTE] **How to read this diagram in a KTU exam:**
> The **crossed E and B fields** confine secondary electrons to a closed drift path near the target, dramatically increasing the plasma density. The ions are *not* magnetically trapped (their Larmor radius is larger than the chamber), so they continue to bombard the target normally. The result is **higher deposition rate at lower pressure** — the reason magnetron sputtering dominates industrial coating.

## 4.3 Sputtering Process — Sequential Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Load substrate in load lock] --> S2[Step 2 Pump to base pressure]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Introduce Ar gas]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Ignite plasma]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Pre sputter target behind shutter]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Open shutter to start deposition]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Monitor deposition rate by QCM]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Close shutter at target thickness]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Vent load lock and unload wafer]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAET864]`

**Q: Define sputtering. List any two advantages of sputtering over thermal evaporation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (2 marks):**
> Sputtering is a Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD) process in which atoms of a target material are ejected by momentum transfer from energetic inert gas ions (typically Ar⁺) accelerated through a cathode sheath. The ejected atoms travel across a low-pressure chamber and condense on a substrate to form a thin film.

> [!NOTE] **Any two advantages (1 mark — ½ each):**
> 1. **Refractory metals** (W, Mo, Ta) with very high melting points can be deposited — impossible in thermal evaporation.
> 2. **Alloys and compounds** are deposited with **stoichiometric fidelity** (composition is preserved because all atoms have similar ejection probabilities).
> 3. **Better step coverage** on non-planar MEMS structures.
> 4. **Lower substrate heating** in magnetron sputtering — suitable for temperature-sensitive wafers.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAET864]`

**Q: What is sputtering yield? Mention the factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (2 marks):**
> Sputtering Yield (S) is defined as the *average number of target atoms ejected per incident ion*:
> $$S = \frac{\text{Number of ejected target atoms}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$$
> It is a dimensionless quantity, typically ranging from **0.1 to 3** atoms/ion at 500 eV Ar⁺ bombardment for most metals.

> [!NOTE] **Factors (1 mark — list any three):**
> 1. **Incident ion energy** — S rises with E up to ~1 keV, then declines.
> 2. **Mass ratio** (M_ion / M_target) — maximum when M_ion ≈ M_target.
> 3. **Angle of incidence** — S peaks at 60° – 70° off-normal, then drops sharply at grazing angles.
> 4. **Target surface binding energy** (U_s) — higher U_s lowers S.
> 5. **Crystal orientation** — close-packed planes sputter slower.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEAET864 Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a DC sputtering system. Discuss the role of the cathode dark space and the plasma sheath in the sputtering process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum energy transferred in a binary elastic collision between an incident ion and a target atom. A Cu target (M₂ = 64 amu) is bombarded by Ar⁺ ions (M₁ = 40 amu) with energy 500 eV. Calculate the maximum energy transferred to a Cu atom in a head-on collision. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working of a DC Sputtering System (7 marks)**

**System Components (2 marks):**
- **Vacuum chamber** pumped to < 10⁻⁶ Torr base pressure.
- **Target (cathode)** — the material to be deposited, mounted on a water-cooled backing plate.
- **Substrate holder (anode)** — grounded or RF-biased, located 5 – 15 cm from the target.
- **Ar gas inlet** with mass flow controller.
- **High-voltage DC power supply** (0.3 – 5 kV, 0.1 – 5 A).
- **Quartz crystal monitor (QCM)** for in-situ rate measurement.

**Working Sequence (3 marks):**
1. Chamber is evacuated to base pressure (10⁻⁶ Torr).
2. Argon gas is introduced to working pressure (1 – 10 mTorr).
3. DC voltage (e.g., −500 V) is applied to the target. A glow discharge ignites.
4. Free electrons gain energy from the E-field and ionize Ar atoms → cascading plasma.
5. Positive Ar⁺ ions are accelerated across the **cathode dark space** (a thin, ion-rich, electron-depleted region of width ~1 mm at 10 mTorr).
6. Ar⁺ ions strike the target with energy **E = eV** (~500 eV).
7. Momentum transfer ejects target atoms into the gas phase.
8. Sputtered atoms traverse the chamber and condense on the substrate, forming a thin film.

**Role of Dark Space & Sheath (2 marks):**
- The **cathode dark space** (or **Crookes dark space**) is the region immediately adjacent to the target where electrons have not yet gained enough energy to excite gas atoms (hence it is dark).
- The potential drop across this region (almost the entire applied voltage) accelerates ions toward the target.
- The **plasma sheath** is a positively charged boundary layer between the bulk plasma (at V_p ≈ +10 V) and the cathode (at V_c ≈ −500 V). The Bohm criterion ensures ion entry velocity is the **Bohm velocity** $v_B = \sqrt{k_B T_e / m_i}$.
- Together, they ensure *directional*, *monoenergetic* ion bombardment — essential for reproducible sputtering.

**(b) Derivation & Numerical (7 marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(as already shown in Section 3.1)*
- Conservation of momentum and energy lead to the recoil velocity:
$$v_2' = \frac{2 M_1}{M_1 + M_2} v_1$$
- Substituting into kinetic energy expression:
$$T = \frac{4 M_1 M_2}{(M_1 + M_2)^2} \, E_{ion}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**
- Given: M₁ = 40 amu, M₂ = 64 amu, E = 500 eV.
- Compute kinematic factor:
$$\gamma = \frac{4 \times 40 \times 64}{(40 + 64)^2} = \frac{10240}{10816} = 0.9467$$
- Energy transferred:
$$T = 0.9467 \times 500 = 473.3 \text{ eV}$$

Since **T = 473.3 eV ≫ U_s(Cu) = 3.5 eV**, the Cu atom easily overcomes the surface binding energy and is ejected. Multiple secondary collisions (collision cascade) further increase the total number of ejected atoms per incident ion (sputtering yield S ≈ 2 for Cu under these conditions).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEAET864 Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the magnetron sputtering process. How does it improve the deposition rate and film quality compared to conventional DC sputtering? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the mean free path of a sputtered atom in a sputtering chamber. A chamber operates at Ar pressure 5 mTorr and temperature 300 K. Compute the mean free path. (Effective atomic diameter of Ar = 3.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ m, k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K, 1 atm = 760 Torr). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Magnetron Sputtering (7 marks)**

**Concept (3 marks):**
- A pair of permanent magnets (one **North** pole at the center, one **South** pole at the periphery, or vice versa) is placed *behind* the target.
- This creates a **toroidal magnetic field B** parallel to the target surface.
- The electric field **E** is radial (perpendicular to the target).
- **E × B drift** traps secondary electrons in a closed cycloidal path just above the target.

**Advantages over DC Sputtering (4 marks):**

1. **Higher plasma density (10¹¹ – 10¹² /cm³ vs 10⁹ – 10¹⁰ /cm³):** The trapped electrons undergo many ionizing collisions before escaping, multiplying the Ar⁺ flux.
2. **Higher deposition rate (10×):** Because of the increased ion flux, target erosion is faster.
3. **Lower operating pressure (0.5 – 2 mTorr):** Trapping allows the plasma to be sustained at lower pressure, which improves mean free path and reduces gas scattering.
4. **Lower substrate temperature:** Power is concentrated near the target, so the substrate sees less heat load — ideal for depositing on photoresist or polymer MEMS.
5. **Better film quality:** Lower contamination, denser films, finer grain structure.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Magnetron Question**
> - Many students draw a "magnet" above the target. The magnets are placed *behind* the target — the field is in the *gap* between magnet and substrate, *parallel* to the target surface.
> - Do **not** confuse the *E × B* drift of electrons with the *Lorentz force* on ions — only electrons are magnetically trapped because their Larmor radius is small.
> - The cycloid path is **above** the target, not on it.

**(b) Mean Free Path Derivation (7 marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
- Consider a sputtered atom of diameter **d_a** moving with velocity **v** through a gas of stationary atoms of diameter **d_g**.
- In the atom's reference frame, the gas atoms move with relative velocity **v_rel = \bar{v} \sqrt{2}** (where $\bar{v}$ is the mean thermal speed).
- The volume swept per unit time is the **collision cross-section** times the relative velocity:
$$\dot{V} = \pi d^2 \, \bar{v} \sqrt{2}$$ where $d = (d_a + d_g)/2$.
- The number of gas molecules in this volume is $n \, \dot{V}$ where n is the number density.
- The mean free path is the average distance between collisions:
$$\lambda = \frac{\bar{v}}{\dot{n}_{coll}} = \frac{\bar{v}}{n \, \pi d^2 \, \bar{v} \sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 \, n}$$

Using the ideal gas law $n = P / (k_B T)$:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^2 \, P}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**
- Given: P = 5 mTorr, T = 300 K, d = 3.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ m.
- Convert pressure: P = 5 × 10⁻³ / 760 atm = 6.58 × 10⁻⁶ atm = 6.58 × 10⁻⁶ × 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa = **0.667 Pa**.
- Compute λ:
$$\lambda = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, (3.6 \times 10^{-10})^2 \, (0.667)}$$
- Numerator: 1.38 × 10⁻²³ × 300 = 4.14 × 10⁻²¹ J
- Denominator: 1.414 × 3.1416 × 1.296 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 0.667 = 1.414 × 3.1416 × 8.64 × 10⁻²⁰ = **3.84 × 10⁻¹⁹**
- λ = 4.14 × 10⁻²¹ / 3.84 × 10⁻¹⁹ ≈ **1.08 × 10⁻² m = 1.08 cm**

**Interpretation:** The mean free path (~1 cm) is comparable to typical target-to-substrate distances (5 – 10 cm), so the sputtered atoms will undergo a few collisions and lose some directionality. Operating at 1 mTorr would give λ ≈ 5 cm, which is closer to ideal ballistic transport.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mean Free Path Numerical**
> - Forgetting to convert **Torr → Pa** is the single most common error (loses ~1 mark).
> - Using **d = 3.6 × 10⁻¹⁰ m** as the *sum* of diameters (it is already the effective diameter; some textbooks use $d_{eff} = (d_1 + d_2)/2$ — both forms acceptable if stated).
> - The factor $\sqrt{2}$ in the denominator is *non-negotiable* — skipping it gives a 41% error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorize before exam)**
>
> ✅ **Sputtering = momentum-transfer PVD**, NOT thermal evaporation.
> ✅ **Plasma medium = Argon (Ar)** at 1 – 10 mTorr; ions are Ar⁺ accelerated by 0.3 – 5 kV.
> ✅ **Sputtering yield S = atoms ejected / incident ion**; typical range 0.1 – 3 at 500 eV.
> ✅ **Kinematic transfer factor:** $\gamma = 4 M_1 M_2 / (M_1 + M_2)^2$ — maximum (=1) at M₁ = M₂.
> ✅ **Energy transferred:** $T = \gamma E \cos^2 \theta$.
> ✅ **Threshold energy:** $E_{th} \approx U_s / [\gamma(1-\gamma)]$; on the order of 20 – 100 eV.
> ✅ **Mean free path:** $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2} \pi d^2 P)$ — sets the maximum target-to-substrate distance.
> ✅ **Deposition rate:** $R = I S M_t / (e N_A \rho_t A_{sub})$.
> ✅ **Magnetron sputtering** uses E × B drift to trap electrons → 10× higher plasma density → higher rate, lower pressure, cooler substrate.
> ✅ **Sputtered atom energy** is 5 – 100 eV — much higher than evaporated atoms (≈ 0.1 eV) → better film adhesion and step coverage.
> ✅ **Disadvantages to mention in 2-mark comparisons:** Low deposition rate for dielectrics (DC), target poisoning in reactive mode, substrate heating in conventional DC, requires vacuum & high-voltage infrastructure.
> ✅ **For KTU numericals:** Always write *units* at every step; always convert Torr → Pa; always state *assumptions* (elastic collision, normal incidence, no gas scattering).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaporation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Evaporation** is a *Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)* technique used in MEMS and Nanoelectronics fabrication to deposit thin films of materials onto a substrate. In this process, the source material (typically a metal, alloy, or compound) is heated in a **high-vacuum chamber** until it reaches its *vapor pressure threshold*, causing atoms or molecules to transition from the condensed (solid/liquid) phase to the **vapor phase**. These vaporized atoms travel in essentially *line-of-sight* trajectories through the vacuum and **condense** on the cooler substrate surface, forming a thin film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Evaporation is a low-pressure thermal vapor-phase deposition process in which the source material is heated (resistively or by an electron beam) in a vacuum of $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ Torr to produce a directed vapor flux that condenses on the substrate to form a thin film.

The two principal sub-classes studied under the KTU 2024 scheme are:

1. **Thermal (Resistive) Evaporation** – heating by a refractory metal filament or boat.
2. **Electron Beam (E-Beam) Evaporation** – heating by a focused high-energy electron beam directed at the source crucible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (The "Boiling Water on a Mountain Top" Model):**
>
> Imagine placing a small pot of water on a stove located at the top of a very high mountain where the air is extremely thin (low pressure). Even at temperatures **below 100 °C**, the water begins to escape into the atmosphere as vapor because there are very few air molecules in the way to push it back into the liquid. The vapor rises in straight, unobstructed paths (line-of-sight). If you hold a cold plate (substrate) above the pot, the vapor condenses on it as a thin film of water droplets.
>
> - **Vacuum chamber** → thin mountain-top air
> - **Heated source material (metal)** → boiling water
> - **Vaporized atoms** → rising steam
> - **Cool substrate** → cold plate collecting dew
> - **Thin metal film** → thin sheet of condensed water/ice

## 1.3 Key Operating Parameters and Standard Metrics

The following **standard engineering metrics** govern any evaporation process and are examinable under the KTU 2024 OBE framework:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Base Pressure | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ **Torr** | Minimizes gas-phase collisions |
| Working Pressure | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ **Torr** | Controls film purity |
| Source Temperature | 800 °C – 2500 °C | Depends on material's vapor pressure |
| Mean Free Path ($\lambda$) | > Source-to-Substrate distance | Ensures line-of-sight transport |
| Deposition Rate | 1 – 100 **Å/s** | Affects grain structure |
| Film Thickness Uniformity | ± 5 % across wafer | Critical for VLSI / MEMS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why High Vacuum?** The chamber must be evacuated so that the **mean free path** of vapor atoms is greater than the source-to-substrate distance. This prevents gas-phase scattering, oxidation, and contamination of the growing film.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — The "Why" and "How"

The evaporation process can be decomposed into **four sequential physical stages**. Each stage is critical, and failure in any one of them degrades film quality.

### Step 1: Vacuum Generation
- **Why:** At atmospheric pressure, vaporized atoms would undergo thousands of collisions per centimeter with air molecules (N₂, O₂), which would (a) oxidize the metal vapor, (b) scatter the atoms, ruining directionality, and (c) prevent condensation.
- **How:** A combination of a **rotary vane pump** (rough vacuum, down to $\sim 10^{-3}$ Torr) and an **oil diffusion pump** or **turbo-molecular pump** (high vacuum, $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$ Torr) is used. A cold trap or **cryopump** may follow to capture residual water vapor and hydrocarbons.

### Step 2: Source Heating and Vaporization
- **Why:** Every material has a characteristic **vapor pressure curve** (Clausius–Clapeyron relationship). To achieve a practical deposition rate, the source temperature must be raised to a level where the material's equilibrium vapor pressure is in the range of $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ Torr.
- **How:**
   - *Resistive heating:* A high current ($\sim$ 50–200 A) is passed through a **tungsten** or **molybdenum** filament/boat containing the source. Joule heating melts and vaporizes the material.
   - *Electron beam heating:* A high-voltage (5–20 kV) electron beam is generated by an **e-gun** (thermionic filament) and bent (typically 270°) by a magnetic field onto the charge. Power densities of $10^{6}$ to $10^{9}$ W/cm² are achievable, allowing even refractory metals (W, Mo, Ta) to be evaporated.

### Step 3: Vapor Transport
- **Why:** Atoms must travel from the source to the substrate without collisions to preserve stoichiometry and directionality.
- **How:** Under high vacuum, the **mean free path** $\lambda$ of the vapor atoms is given by Equation (1) below. The condition $\lambda \gg d_{ss}$ (where $d_{ss}$ is the source-to-substrate distance) is the design rule.

### Step 4: Nucleation, Growth, and Condensation
- **Why:** The vapor condenses on the substrate because the substrate is held at a much lower temperature (often water-cooled or at room temperature). Below a critical temperature, incident atoms have low adatom mobility and stick where they land.
- **How:** The classical thin-film growth follows one of three modes described by **Bauer’s classification**:
   - **Frank–van der Merwe (layer-by-layer, FM)**
   - **Volmer–Weber (island growth, VW)**
   - **Stranski–Krastanov (layer + island, SK)**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations appear repeatedly in KTU end-semester examinations for the PVD module. Memorize the boxed relationships.

### A. Mean Free Path (λ)

$$
\lambda = \frac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi d^{2} P}
$$

where
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (Boltzmann constant),
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **K**,
- $d$ = effective molecular diameter in **m** (for air, $d \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m),
- $P$ = background pressure in **Pa**.

For air at $T = 300$ K, this simplifies to the **engineering rule of thumb**:

$$
\lambda \, [\text{cm}] \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{P \, [\text{Torr}]}
$$

### B. Cosine Law of Emission (Knudsen / Point Source)

For a **point source** (or small area source), the mass emitted per unit solid angle is proportional to the cosine of the angle measured from the source normal:

$$
d m \propto \cos\theta \, d\omega
$$

The **deposition rate** at a point on the substrate, located at distance $r$ from the source and at angle $\theta$ from the source normal, is:

$$
R_d = \frac{m \cos\theta \cos\phi}{4 \pi r^{2} \, \rho}
$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between the substrate normal and the line joining the source to the deposition point.

### C. Deposition Rate for a Surface (Planar) Source

For a small flat area source (an approximation for e-beam hearth crucibles):

$$
R_d = \frac{m \cos\theta \cos\phi}{\pi r^{2} \, \rho}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $\cos\theta \cos\phi$ factor is why evaporation has **poor step coverage** — features on the wafer that are not facing the source receive very little deposit. This is a frequent KTU question: *"Why is step coverage poor in evaporation compared to sputtering?"*

### D. Film Thickness Variation

The thickness $t$ at any point on a flat substrate above a small-area planar source is:

$$
t(\phi) = \frac{t_0}{\left(1 + \left(\frac{x}{h}\right)^{2}\right)^{2}}
$$

where $h$ is the source-to-substrate vertical distance, $x$ is the lateral distance from the center, and $t_0$ is the thickness at the center.

### E. Clausius–Clapeyron (Vapor Pressure vs. Temperature)

$$
\ln P = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T} + C
$$

This equation determines the **minimum source temperature** required to obtain a useful vapor pressure for the chosen material.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evaporation is the **workhorse technique** for depositing metallic thin films in:
- **MEMS devices** — gold, aluminum, and titanium electrodes and bond pads.
- **Optoelectronics** — silver and aluminum mirrors, anti-reflection coatings.
- **Microelectronics** — interconnect metallization, ohmic contacts, Schottky barriers.
- **Nanoelectronics research** — shadow-mask patterning of nanostructures, *van der Waals* heterostructure assembly where ultra-clean, low-energy deposition is required.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Example:** The aluminum interconnect layers in commercial CMOS chips were historically deposited by *resistive evaporation* before being replaced by sputtering for better step coverage in deep sub-micron trenches. Today, e-beam evaporation is the standard in research labs for depositing refractory metals (W, Mo, Pt) and dielectric stacks (SiO₂, TiO₂) for optical coatings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Cosine Law of Emission (Knudsen's Law)

We derive the angular distribution of mass emitted from a small evaporation source. The derivation follows the classical kinetic theory approach.

### Step 1 — Define the Solid Angle Element

The element of solid angle subtended at the source by a small area $dA$ on the substrate is:

$$
d\omega = \frac{dA \cos\phi}{r^{2}}
$$

This expresses the fact that the *projected area* (perpendicular to the line of sight) determines the angular spread, hence the factor $\cos\phi$.

### Step 2 — Define the Number of Molecules Leaving the Source per Unit Time per Unit Solid Angle

In equilibrium, the number of molecules leaving a surface per unit time per unit area is given by the **Hertz–Knudsen equation**:

$$
N = \frac{P_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}
$$

where $P_s$ is the saturated vapor pressure at the source temperature $T$ and $m$ is the molecular mass.

### Step 3 — Apply the Cosine (Lambert) Distribution Assumption

The number of molecules leaving the source in the direction $(\theta, \phi)$ within solid angle $d\omega$ is given by:

$$
dN = N \, \cos\theta \, d\omega
$$

The $\cos\theta$ term reflects the fact that molecules preferentially leave normal to the surface (Langmuir's law of free evaporation). Integrating over the hemisphere:

$$
\int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \pi
$$

confirms that the distribution is properly normalized for a *surface* source.

### Step 4 — Relate Mass Flux to Deposition Rate

The mass arriving at the substrate point is:

$$
dm = m_{atom} \cdot dN = \frac{P_s \, m_{atom}}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cos\theta \, d\omega
$$

Substituting $d\omega = \frac{dA \cos\phi}{r^{2}}$:

$$
dm = \frac{P_s \, m_{atom}}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cdot \frac{\cos\theta \cos\phi}{r^{2}} \, dA
$$

The **deposition rate** is mass per unit area per unit time, and dividing by the density $\rho$ of the condensed film gives a volumetric flux, hence thickness rate:

$$
R_d = \frac{P_s}{\rho \sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cdot \frac{\cos\theta \cos\phi}{r^{2}}
$$

This is the **canonical evaporation deposition rate equation** and is the basis of nearly all KTU analytical problems on the topic.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 1 cm × 1 cm square aluminum charge is evaporated from a resistive boat located 25 cm below a 4-inch (10 cm) silicon wafer. Calculate the film thickness at the center of the wafer and at a point 3 cm off-center, assuming the source behaves as a small planar source emitting at a steady rate of 0.05 g/s, with Al density $\rho = 2.7$ g/cm³.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the source-to-substrate distance to the off-center point:**

$$
r^{2} = h^{2} + x^{2} = (25)^{2} + (3)^{2} = 625 + 9 = 634 \text{ cm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cosine factors.** The substrate is horizontal, the source is directly below, so $\phi = \theta$ for a flat substrate:

$$
\cos\theta = \cos\phi = \frac{h}{r} = \frac{25}{\sqrt{634}} = 0.9928
$$

**Step 3 — Center of wafer ($x = 0$):**

$$
r = 25 \text{ cm}, \quad \cos\theta = 1
$$

$$
R_d = \frac{m}{\pi r^{2} \rho} = \frac{0.05}{\pi (25)^{2} (2.7)} = \frac{0.05}{5301.4} = 9.43 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Off-center point ($x = 3$ cm):**

$$
R_d = \frac{m \cos^{2}\theta}{\pi r^{2} \rho} = 9.43 \times 10^{-6} \times (0.9928)^{2} \times \frac{25^{2}}{634}
$$

$$
R_d = 9.43 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.9857 \times 0.986 = 9.17 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Uniformity check:** The off-center rate is $\frac{9.17}{9.43} = 97.3\%$ of the center, indicating good thickness uniformity across a 4-inch wafer at 25 cm throw distance. This is why evaporation sources are placed far from the substrate in production systems.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Evaporation Variants)

| Variant | Heating Method | Max Source Temp | Typical Materials | Directionality | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Filament Evaporation | Resistive (W coil) | $\sim 1500$ °C | Al, Au, Ag | Point-source-like | Low |
| Boat Evaporation | Resistive (W/Mo foil) | $\sim 1700$ °C | Al, Ti, Ni | Small-area source | Low |
| E-Beam Evaporation | Focused e-beam (270° bend) | $> 3000$ °C | W, Mo, Ta, SiO₂, TiO₂ | Highly directional | High |
| Flash Evaporation | Drop feed onto hot boat | Material-dependent | Alloys, multi-component | Pulsed | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture of an Evaporation System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the major subsystems of a typical **e-beam evaporation system** used in MEMS labs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ChamberAssembly["Vacuum Chamber Assembly"]
        P1[Substrate Holder] --> P2[Rotating Substrate Stage]
        P2 --> P3[Quartz Crystal Thickness Monitor]
        P3 --> P4[Shutter Mechanism]
        P4 --> P5[Electron Beam Gun with 270 Degree Magnetic Deflection]
        P5 --> P6[Water Cooled Copper Crucible]
        P6 --> P7[Source Material Charge]
        P7 --> P8[Base Plate and Viewports]
    end

    subgraph PumpStack["Vacuum Pump Stack"]
        Q1[Rotary Vane Pump 10 power -3 Torr] --> Q2[Diffusion or Turbo Pump 10 power -6 Torr]
        Q2 --> Q3[Cryo or Cold Trap]
        Q3 --> Q4[Residual Gas Analyzer RGA]
    end

    subgraph Controller["Process Controller"]
        R1[PLC with Recipe Storage] --> R2[Heater Power Supply]
        R2 --> R3[Emission Current Controller]
        R3 --> R4[Vacuum Gauge Reader]
        R4 --> R5[Deposition Rate Feedback Loop]
    end

    P5 <-->|High Voltage Cable| R3
    P3 <-->|Sensor Signal| R5
    Q2 <-->|Vacuum Line| ChamberAssembly
    R1 -->|Master Trigger| P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows indicate **physical signal flow or mechanical coupling**, not data flow. The *e-gun* receives high voltage from the *emission current controller*, and the *quartz crystal monitor* sends a deposition rate signal back to the *feedback loop*, which modulates the e-gun power to maintain a constant rate.

## 4.2 Process Flow of a Single Evaporation Run

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load Substrate and Source] --> B[Pump Down to 10 power -6 Torr]
    B --> C[Outgas Filament or E-Gun at Low Power]
    C --> D[Open Shutter and Ramp to Deposition Power]
    D --> E[Monitor Rate on QCM]
    E --> F{Target Thickness Reached}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Close Shutter and Cool Down]
    G --> H[Vent Chamber with Dry Nitrogen]
    H --> I[Unload Wafer]
```

## 4.3 Cosine Emission Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Source at Origin] -->|vapor flux| A1[Substrate Element at Angle theta]
    S -->|vapor flux| A2[Substrate Element at Angle 0 Normal]
    S -->|vapor flux| A3[Substrate Element at Angle 60 degrees]
    A1 -.->|Reduced flux cos theta| F1[Thin Film at Off Center]
    A2 -.->|Maximum flux cos 0 equals 1| F2[Thickest Film at Center]
    A3 -.->|Greatly reduced flux cos 60 equals 0.5| F3[Thinnest Film at Edge]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This geometric diagram is the visual justification for the $\cos\theta$ factor in the Knudsen equation. A point far off the source normal (large $\theta$) receives drastically less material, which is the root cause of *poor step coverage* in sub-micron features — a key KTU 2024 syllabus point.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** *What is the role of a quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) in an evaporation system? Why is it preferred over measuring film thickness post-deposition?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM)** is a real-time, *in-situ* thickness monitor placed near the substrate. It exploits the **piezoelectric effect**: the resonant frequency of a thin AT-cut quartz crystal decreases linearly as mass is deposited on it, following the **Sauerbrey equation**:

$$
\Delta f = -\frac{2 f_0^{2}}{A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} \Delta m
$$

- It allows **closed-loop control** of the deposition rate, ensuring constant rate, automatic shutter-closing at target thickness, and detection of process drift (e.g., source depletion). Post-deposition measurement (e.g., profilometry) is destructive, slow, and does not allow real-time correction.
- *(1 mark for stating role, 1 mark for Sauerbrey, 1 mark for advantages over post-deposition.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** *Why is high vacuum (10⁻⁶ Torr) essential for thermal evaporation? What happens if the pressure is raised to 10⁻³ Torr?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- At $10^{-6}$ Torr, the **mean free path** of evaporated atoms (using $\lambda \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{P}$) is:

$$
\lambda \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 5000 \text{ cm} = 50 \text{ m}
$$

- This is far greater than the typical source-to-substrate distance of $\sim 30$ cm, so vapor atoms travel in **straight, line-of-sight trajectories** without scattering, ensuring pure, directional deposition.
- If the pressure is raised to $10^{-3}$ Torr, $\lambda \approx 5$ cm, which is **less than the source-to-substrate distance**. Vapor atoms suffer gas-phase collisions, get **scattered and oxidized** by residual O₂, and the deposited film becomes **contaminated, porous, and non-uniform**. (3 marks: 1 for the calculation, 1 for the line-of-sight argument, 1 for the failure mode.)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Module 2 Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Cosine Law & Deposition Rate

**(a)** State and derive the **Knudsen cosine law of emission** for a small surface evaporation source. Clearly define each variable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular aluminum charge of area 2 cm² is evaporated from a planar source located 20 cm below a stationary 6-inch silicon wafer. The source emits at a steady mass rate of 0.08 g/s. Assuming the source behaves as an ideal Knudsen source and Al density is 2.7 g/cm³, calculate the **deposition rate** at (i) the center of the wafer, and (ii) a point 4 cm off-center. Comment on the **thickness uniformity** across a 6-inch wafer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** The mass of vapor leaving a small area source per unit time per unit solid angle is proportional to the cosine of the angle measured from the source normal:

$$
\frac{dm}{dt \, d\omega} \propto \cos\theta
$$

- **Derivation (6 marks):** Start from the Hertz–Knudsen flux equation, apply Lambert's cosine law to express the angular distribution, substitute the solid-angle element $d\omega = \frac{dA \cos\phi}{r^{2}}$, and arrive at the canonical equation (as shown in Section 3.1 of these notes). Show all four steps.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Center of wafer:* At the center, $r = 20$ cm, $\theta = \phi = 0°$, so $\cos\theta = \cos\phi = 1$.

$$
R_d = \frac{m}{\pi r^{2} \rho} = \frac{0.08}{\pi (20)^{2} (2.7)} = \frac{0.08}{3392.9} = 2.36 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm/s} = 2.36 \text{ Å/ms}
$$

*Step 2 — Off-center point:* $r = \sqrt{20^{2} + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{416} = 20.396$ cm. The angle $\theta$ satisfies $\cos\theta = \frac{20}{20.396} = 0.9806$.

$$
R_d = \frac{0.08 \times (0.9806)^{2}}{\pi \times 416 \times 2.7} = \frac{0.0769}{3527.4} = 2.18 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm/s}
$$

*Step 3 — Uniformity comment:* The off-center rate is $\frac{2.18}{2.36} = 92.4\%$ of the central rate, indicating **acceptable but non-ideal uniformity** across a 6-inch wafer. To improve uniformity, the substrate is typically **rotated** during deposition (planetary or simple rotational motion), which averages out the angular variation over time and yields ±2% uniformity.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Source Variants and Step Coverage

**(a)** Compare **resistive (thermal) evaporation** and **electron beam evaporation** in terms of (i) maximum achievable source temperature, (ii) suitability for high-melting-point refractory materials, (iii) film purity, and (iv) typical application in MEMS fabrication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch why **evaporation has poor step coverage** in sub-micron trenches compared to sputtering. Propose **two process modifications** that can improve step coverage in evaporation systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Resistive Evaporation | E-Beam Evaporation |
|---|---|---|
| Max source temperature | $\sim 1500$ °C (limited by filament melting) | $> 3000$ °C (concentrated power) |
| Refractory material suitability | Limited to low-melting metals (Al, Au, Ag, Cu) | Excellent for W, Mo, Ta, SiO₂, TiO₂ |
| Film purity | Lower — filament may outgas and contaminate | Higher — water-cooled crucible avoids contamination |
| Typical MEMS application | Bond pads, heaters, simple metallization | Optical coatings, high-purity metallization, lift-off |

**(b) Step Coverage Explanation (7 marks):**
- **Why poor (4 marks):** The Knudsen cosine law dictates that atoms travel in **straight lines** from the source. A high-aspect-ratio trench has sidewalls that are nearly parallel to the incoming flux; the $\cos\theta$ factor for these surfaces is near zero, so little material deposits there. The trench *opening* shadows the bottom, leading to **voids and overhangs**.
- **Two process modifications (3 marks):**
  1. **Substrate Rotation and Tilt:** By rotating the substrate on a planetary stage and tilting it 5–15° toward the source, the incident flux direction changes continuously, allowing sidewalls to receive some deposition.
  2. **Elevated Substrate Temperature (with low rate):** Heating the substrate to $\sim 200$–$400$ °C increases **adatom surface mobility**, allowing atoms that land on the upper rim to migrate down into the trench. The trade-off is increased grain size and possible alloy reactions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Evaporation Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Torr and Pa** — Most KTU problems state pressure in **Torr**, but the mean-free-path formula requires **Pa**. Use the conversion $1 \text{ Torr} = 133.3 \text{ Pa}$ or use the engineering rule $\lambda \, [\text{cm}] \approx \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{P \, [\text{Torr}]}$. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\cos\theta \cos\phi$ factor** — When asked to calculate deposition rate *off-center*, students often plug in $r$ alone and forget the cosine terms. Always re-derive $r = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2}$ and compute $\cos\theta = h/r$.
> 3. **Confusing *point source* and *surface (planar) source* constants** — The denominator constant is $4\pi$ for a point source and $\pi$ for a small-area surface source. Using the wrong one is a 1-mark penalty.
> 4. **Writing "high vacuum = no collisions"** without the explicit $\lambda \gg d_{ss}$ justification — Examiners expect the mean-free-path inequality to be stated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Definition:** Evaporation is a *line-of-sight, vacuum-based PVD technique* in which the source is heated thermally or by an electron beam to deposit a thin film.
- **Pressure regime:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ Torr is the standard high-vacuum requirement.
- **Mean free path condition:** $\lambda \gg d_{ss}$ — the foundational design rule.
- **Two main types:** *Resistive (thermal) evaporation* and *Electron beam evaporation* (270° magnetic deflection, water-cooled crucible).
- **Knudsen cosine law:** $dm/d\omega \propto \cos\theta$ — derived from kinetic theory and Langmuir's evaporation model.
- **Deposition rate equation:** $R_d = \frac{m \cos\theta \cos\phi}{\pi r^{2} \rho}$ (surface source) — central to all numerical problems.
- **Step coverage:** *Poor* in evaporation because of the directional $\cos\theta$ law; *improved* by substrate rotation, tilt, and elevated temperature.
- **Film thickness monitor:** *Quartz Crystal Microbalance* (QCM), based on the **Sauerbrey equation** relating resonant frequency shift to deposited mass.
- **Growth modes:** Frank–van der Merwe (layer), Volmer–Weber (island), Stranski–Krastanov (mixed).
- **Industrial use cases:** MEMS bond pads, optical mirrors, ohmic contacts, refractory metal films, shadow-mask nanostructure fabrication.
- **Memorize these constants:** $\rho_{Al} = 2.7$ g/cm³, $\rho_{Au} = 19.3$ g/cm³, $\rho_{Ti} = 4.5$ g/cm³, and the rule $\lambda[\text{cm}] = 5 \times 10^{-3}/P[\text{Torr}]$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroplating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electroplating # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Electroplating (Electro-deposition)** is an electrochemical fabrication process in which metallic ions from an electrolyte solution are reduced and deposited as a thin, adherent metallic coating onto a conductive substrate (the cathode) under the influence of an externally applied direct current (DC). It is one of the most widely used **bottom-up material addition techniques** in MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) fabrication for building high-aspect-ratio metallic microstructures. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, electroplating belongs to the family of **additive micromanufacturing processes** and forms the deposition backbone of the famous **LIGA process** (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung — German for Lithography, Electroplating, Molding). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEAET864 - Module 2):** Electroplating is treated as a non-lithographic, materials-based micromachining technique. The examiner expects students to know the working principle, Faraday's laws, process parameters, and MEMS-specific applications. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Metal Painting with Electricity" Imagine you have a wall (the **substrate**) that you want to coat with a layer of gold. Instead of using a paintbrush, you dissolve gold into a special conductive "soup" called the **electrolyte**. Then you connect your wall to the **negative terminal of a battery** and dunk a gold block into the soup connected to the **positive terminal**. The battery acts like a tiny **electric magnet for atoms** — it slowly pulls gold atoms out of the soup and glues them onto your wall, one atomic layer at a time. The longer you leave the wall in the soup, the thicker the gold coating becomes. This is, in essence, electroplating. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants are **indispensable for numerical problems** in KTU examinations: | Symbol | Constant | Value | |--------|----------|-------| | $F$ | Faraday's constant | $\mathbf{96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}}$ (often approximated as $96{,}500 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$) | | $e$ | Charge of an electron | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$ | | $N_A$ | Avogadro's number | $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}}$ | | $M$ | Atomic/molar mass of deposit metal | g/mol (metal-dependent) | | $n$ | Valency (number of electrons transferred) | dimensionless integer | > [!TIP] > For the **density of common MEMS metals**, remember: Copper = $\mathbf{8.96 \text{ g/cm}^3}$, Nickel = $\mathbf{8.90 \text{ g/cm}^3}$, Gold = $\mathbf{19.32 \text{ g/cm}^3}$, Silver = $\mathbf{10.49 \text{ g/cm}^3}$. --- ## 1.4 Why Electroplating is Critical in MEMS Unlike traditional integrated-circuit (IC) fabrication which is mostly subtractive (etching away material), many MEMS devices require **thick, high-aspect-ratio metal structures** (e.g., comb drives, inertial masses, micro-coils). Electroplating uniquely offers: 1. **High deposition rates** (microns to tens of microns per hour). 2. **Excellent conformality into deep molds** (LIGA-friendly). 3. **Low residual stress** when process parameters are tuned. 4. **Compatibility with standard photolithography** (the photoresist acts as a mold). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — The Electrochemical Cell An electroplating setup is essentially a **two-electrode electrolytic cell** powered by an external DC source (rectifier). The three essential components are: - **Anode (Positive Electrode):** The sacrificial metal source (e.g., a copper rod) that dissolves into the electrolyte. Sometimes an **inert anode** (platinum, graphite) is used with a replenished electrolyte. - **Cathode (Negative Electrode):** The MEMS substrate (typically silicon wafer with a thin conductive seed layer of Au/Cu/Ti). - **Electrolyte:** An aqueous solution containing the metal ions to be deposited (e.g., $\text{CuSO}_4$, $\text{NiSO}_4$, gold cyanide complexes). The reactions are: At the **cathode (reduction/deposition):** $$\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \longrightarrow \text{M (solid)}$$ At the **anode (oxidation/dissolution):** $$\text{M (solid)} \longrightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$$ > [!NOTE] > The overall cell reaction is **mass transport** of metal from anode to cathode via ionic conduction through the electrolyte. The current is carried by **ions in solution**, not electrons in wires. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic The deposition process at the cathode is governed by **five sequential kinetic steps** — a high-yield KTU topic: 1. **Mass Transport (Migration/Diffusion/Convection):** Hydrated metal ions travel from the bulk electrolyte toward the cathode surface through the boundary layer (Nernst diffusion layer). 2. **Electron Transfer:** The ion receives $n$ electrons at the cathode and is reduced to its atomic state. 3. **Nucleation:** Newly formed adatoms form stable clusters (nuclei) on the substrate — called *Volmer-Weber*, *Frank-van der Merwe*, or *Stranski-Krastanov* growth modes. 4. **Surface Diffusion:** Adatoms migrate along the surface to find low-energy sites. 5. **Crystal Growth / Coalescence:** Nuclei grow and merge into a continuous, polycrystalline (or single-crystalline under Epitaxial conditions) film. --- ## 2.3 Key Process Parameters (Examiner's Favourite) | Parameter | Typical Range | Effect | |-----------|---------------|--------| | Current density ($J$) | $1 - 30 \text{ mA/cm}^2$ | Higher $J$ = faster deposition, but rougher films | | Temperature | $25 - 60 ^\circ\text{C}$ | Increases ion mobility & deposition rate | | pH | $3 - 5$ (acid) or $8 - 10$ (alkaline) | Affects deposit morphology and hydrogen evolution | | Agitation/stirring | $0 - 1000 \text{ rpm}$ | Reduces concentration polarization | | Additives | Levelers, brighteners, suppressors | Refines grain size, reduces stress | > [!WARNING] > If current density is too high, **hydrogen evolution** competes with metal reduction, causing **pitting, hydrogen embrittlement, and low current efficiency**. This is a common 3-mark question. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | **Faraday's First Law** — mass deposited | grams (g) | | 2 | $m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Where $Q = I \cdot t$ is total charge | grams (g) | | 3 | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | **Faraday's Second Law** — $E$ is equivalent weight | dimensionless | | 4 | $E = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Equivalent weight | g/eq | | 5 | $T = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | Thickness of deposit | cm | | 6 | $T = \dfrac{J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Thickness in terms of current density | cm | | 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | **Current efficiency** | % | | 8 | $R_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{J \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Linear deposition rate | cm/s | | 9 | $N = \dfrac{m \cdot N_A}{M}$ | Number of atoms deposited | atoms | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the table above, $J$ = current density, $t$ = time, $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = valency, $F$ = Faraday's constant, $\rho$ = density, $A$ = area, $I$ = current, $Q$ = charge, $\eta$ = current efficiency, $N$ = number of atoms, $N_A$ = Avogadro's number. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In MEMS, electroplating is the workhorse behind: - **LIGA process** for X-ray lithography-based high-aspect-ratio microstructures. - **Copper damascene interconnect** formation in advanced IC back-end-of-line (BEOL). - **Micro-coils, magnetic actuators, and RF inductors** on CMOS substrates. - **Through-Silicon Vias (TSVs)** for 3D integrated circuit packaging. - **Bio-MEMS electrode arrays** (Au/Pt plating on flexible polymer substrates). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's First Law ### 3.1.1 Starting from Atomic-Level Reasoning Consider $N$ metal ions, each of valency $n$, being reduced at the cathode. The total charge required to reduce one mole of these ions is: $$Q_{\text{mole}} = n \cdot N_A \cdot e = n \cdot F$$ where $F = N_A \cdot e$ is Faraday's constant, with $N_A$ being Avogadro's number and $e$ the elementary charge. For a current $I$ flowing for time $t$, the total charge passed is: $$Q = I \cdot t$$ ### 3.1.2 Linking Charge to Mass Deposited If the total charge $Q$ reduces a mass $m$ of metal, then by direct proportion: $$m \propto Q$$ Introducing the constant of proportionality $\dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ (since one Faraday deposits one equivalent weight): $$\boxed{m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$ This is **Faraday's First Law** — the cornerstone equation of all electroplating numerical problems. ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Thickness Equation Starting from $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$, and noting that for a uniform deposit on area $A$: $$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot T$$ where $T$ is the thickness and $\rho$ the density. Equating both expressions: $$\rho \cdot A \cdot T = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ Solving for $T$: $$\boxed{T = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}}$$ Introducing the current density $J = I/A$: $$\boxed{T = \frac{J \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Derivation of Deposition Rate Differentiating the thickness with respect to time: $$R_{\text{dep}} = \frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{J \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$ This is the **linear growth rate** of the deposit (in cm/s when using CGS units). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Style) **Problem:** A silicon wafer of area $100 \text{ cm}^2$ is to be electroplated with copper to a thickness of $\mathbf{20 \ \mu m}$. Given: $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5 \text{ g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $F = 96{,}500 \text{ C/mol}$. If a current of $5 \text{ A}$ is applied, calculate: (a) The mass of copper deposited. (b) The time required. (c) The deposition rate. (d) The number of copper atoms in the deposit. ### Step-by-Step Solution **(a) Mass of copper deposited:** $$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot T$$ $$m = 8.96 \times 100 \times (20 \times 10^{-4})$$ $$m = 8.96 \times 100 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{m = 1.792 \text{ g}}$$ **(b) Time required:** From Faraday's First Law: $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ $$t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$$ $$t = \frac{1.792 \times 2 \times 96{,}500}{5 \times 63.5}$$ $$t = \frac{345{,}856}{317.5}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 1089.3 \text{ s} \approx 18.15 \text{ minutes}}$$ **(c) Deposition rate:** $$R_{\text{dep}} = \frac{T}{t} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}}{1089.3 \text{ s}}$$ $$\boxed{R_{\text{dep}} \approx 1.836 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm/s} = 18.36 \text{ nm/s}}$$ **(d) Number of copper atoms:** $$N = \frac{m \cdot N_A}{M} = \frac{1.792 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{63.5}$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 1.70 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters | Parameter Change | Effect on Deposit Thickness (for fixed $t$) | Effect on Morphology | Effect on Stress | |------------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------------|------------------| | Increase $J$ | $\uparrow$ (linear) | Rougher, more porous | Higher tensile stress | | Increase $T$ (temp) | $\uparrow$ (rate) | Larger grains | Lower stress | | Increase agitation | No direct effect on mass | More uniform, brighter | Reduced stress | | Add leveler/brightener | No change in mass | Smoother, finer grains | Refined grain size | | Decrease $n$ (different metal) | $\uparrow$ for same $J$ | Metal-dependent | Metal-dependent | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electroplating System ```mermaid flowchart LR DCPS[DC Power Supply
Rectifier] -->|Positive Lead| ANO[Anode
Sacrificial Metal Block] DCPS -->|Negative Lead| CAT[Cathode
Si Wafer with Seed Layer] ANO -->|M n ions| ELE[Electrolyte Bath
Metal Salt Solution] CAT -->|Deposited Metal| ELE ELE -->|Convection| AGI[Agitator or Stirrer] ELE -->|Heating| THR[Temperature Controller] ELE -->|pH Monitoring| PHC[pH Meter and Controller] ELE -->|Current Density| CDM[Current Density Monitor] THR --> ELE AGI --> ELE PHC --> ELE CDM --> ELE ``` **Functional Block Description:** 1. **DC Power Supply (Rectifier):** Provides stable, low-voltage (1–12 V) high-current DC; modern systems use pulsed current for better grain control. 2. **Anode:** Source of metal ions; either a soluble anode (M → Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻) or an inert anode (Pt, graphite, Ti) with separate ion replenishment. 3. **Cathode (Workpiece):** Si wafer coated with a thin conductive seed layer (e.g., Cr/Au or Ti/Cu) where deposition occurs. 4. **Electrolyte Bath:** Aqueous solution of metal salt (CuSO₄, NiSO₄, KAu(CN)₂, etc.) plus supporting electrolyte, brighteners, levelers, and wetting agents. 5. **Agitator:** Ensures uniform ion distribution and prevents concentration gradients. 6. **Temperature & pH Controllers:** Maintain optimal plating conditions for repeatable film quality. --- ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Electroplating in LIGA / MEMS ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Si Wafer] --> B[Deposit Thin Conductive Seed Layer
Ti or Cr for adhesion + Au or Cu for conduction] B --> C[Spin Coat Thick Photoresist
SU-8 or PMMA] C --> D[Photolithography Exposure
