# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define bulk micromachining. State any four advantages and two disadvantages of the technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Bulk micromachining is a MEMS fabrication technique in which three-dimensional microstructures are formed by the *selective removal* (etching) of substantial portions of a silicon substrate (typically single-crystal Si) using anisotropic wet etchants such as KOH, TMAH, or EDP, or by dry etching (DRIE).

**Advantages (1.5 marks — any 4):**
- High aspect-ratio features possible (e.g., deep cavities, membranes).
- Excellent dimensional control due to crystallographic etch-stopping.
- Low residual stress; mechanical properties equal to bulk Si.
- Process is *mature, reproducible*, and CMOS-compatible.
- Suitable for **high-volume, low-cost** mass production (injection moulding masters, inkjet heads).

**Disadvantages (0.5 marks — any 2):**
- One-side etching restricted by wafer thickness; double-sided lithography often required.
- Limited to the geometry of {111} planes (54.74° restriction).
- Anisotropy is lost on non-(100) or non-(110) wafers.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by anisotropic etching of silicon? Why is the etch rate of the (100) plane much higher than that of the (111) plane in KOH?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Anisotropic Etching (1.5 marks):** Anisotropic etching refers to the *direction-dependent* dissolution rate of a crystalline material in a chemical etchant. In single-crystal silicon etched in aqueous KOH, the etch rate varies with the orientation of the exposed crystal plane: $R_{(100)} \gg R_{(110)} > R_{(111)}$.

**Bond-Density Argument (1.5 marks):** The (111) plane has the highest density of surface atoms and **three back-bonds** per surface atom, while the (100) plane has only **two back-bonds** per surface atom. The K⁺/OH⁻ attack proceeds by sequential rupture of these back-bonds. Because the (111) plane requires more bond breakages per unit volume, it etches roughly **100 – 400 times slower** than the (100) plane, giving the anisotropy ratio:

$$
\alpha = \frac{R_{(100)}}{R_{(111)}} \in [100,\,400] \quad \text{(KOH)}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **TWO** complete 14-mark questions form an internal choice set, exactly as the KTU 2024 ESE pattern. The student answers **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full.

### ⬢ CHOICE A — Wet Chemical Etching of Silicon `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **wet chemical etching of silicon** using KOH. Discuss the etching mechanism, the role of temperature, and the etch-rate selectivity against SiO₂ and Si₃N₄. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Etching Mechanism (3 marks):** The KOH–H₂O system etches silicon through an electrochemical process that proceeds in **two coupled half-reactions**:
- **Anodic (oxidation) half-reaction:** Si + 2OH⁻ → Si(OH)₂²⁻ + 2h⁺ (silicon is oxidized to a soluble silicate).
- **Cathodic (reduction) half-reaction:** 2H₂O + 2h⁺ → 2OH⁻ + H₂↑ (water is reduced, hydrogen gas is evolved).

The overall reaction is:

$$
\text{Si} + 2\text{KOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{K}_2\text{SiO}_3 + 2\text{H}_2\uparrow
$$

The continuously evolved H₂ bubbles serve as a *visual indicator* of the etch rate.

**Role of Temperature (2 marks):** The etch rate follows an **Arrhenius dependence** on absolute temperature:

$$
R(T) \;=\; R_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Typical $E_a$ is 0.6 – 0.8 eV. For KOH (30 % wt) on (100) Si: $R \approx 1.0\,\mu m/min$ at 70 °C, $\approx 1.4\,\mu m/min$ at 80 °C, and $\approx 2.0\,\mu m/min$ at 90 °C. KTU typically quotes $R_v = 1\,\mu m/min$ at 80 °C as the standard reference.

**Mask Selectivity (2 marks):** KOH attacks SiO₂ slowly and Si₃N₄ negligibly. Practical values:
- Si : SiO₂ ≈ **400 : 1** (1 µm Si etched → only ~2.5 nm SiO₂ consumed)
- Si : LPCVD Si₃N₄ ≈ **10 000 : 1**

Therefore, even a **500 nm LPCVD nitride** is more than adequate to mask a 200 µm KOH etch. Thermal SiO₂ of similar thickness can also be used for shorter etches, but it is consumed faster.

**(b)** With **neat cross-sectional diagrams**, describe the formation of **V-grooves and pyramidal pits** during anisotropic KOH etching of a (100) silicon wafer. Derive the depth equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**V-Groove Formation (3.5 marks):**
- A long rectangular mask opening of width $W_0$ is aligned parallel to the $\langle 110 \rangle$ flat.
- The (100) plane is etched rapidly; the {111} sidewalls emerge at an angle of **54.74°** to the surface.
- The two sloped sidewalls meet at the bottom, forming a sharp **V-groove**.
- **Cross-section** is an isosceles triangle.
- **Depth equation (derived in §3.2):** $h = W_0 / \sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071\,W_0$.

**Pyramidal Pit Formation (3.5 marks):**
- A *square* or *circular* mask opening on (100) Si gives rise to four {111} sidewalls.
- The intersection of these four planes forms an **inverted square pyramid**.
- For a **square** mask of side $a$ aligned to $\langle 110 \rangle$: $h = a / \sqrt{2}$.
- For a **circular** mask of diameter $D$: the four (111) planes emerge from the inscribed square, giving $h = D / 2$.
- These pits are widely used in **optoelectronics** (LEDs), **solar cell texturing**, and **fiber alignment V-grooves**.

### ⬢ CHOICE B — Etch Stops & Convex Corner Compensation `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the various **etch-stop techniques** used in bulk micromachining. Compare their effectiveness, selectivity, and practical limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Time Stop (1 mark):** The simplest method — the etch is interrupted at a pre-calculated time. *Selectivity:* undefined (process-based). *Limitation:* poor thickness control (typically ±10 %); wafer-thickness variation is directly transferred to the membrane.

**Boron Heavily-Doped ($p^+$) Stop (2 marks):** A boron-doped epitaxial layer (≥ 5×10¹⁹ /cm³) acts as a *chemical etch stop*. The high boron concentration deforms the Si lattice and lowers the surface energy, reducing the etch rate by **a factor of 1000 or more**. *Selectivity:* > 1000 : 1. *Limitations:* (i) The high doping causes **tensile stress**, leading to wafer bow and potential cracking; (ii) Boron out-diffusion during high-temperature steps degrades the layer; (iii) High resistivity (10⁻³ Ω·cm) of the $p^+$ layer is unsuitable for piezoresistive sensor designs.

**Electrochemical (p-n Junction) Stop (2 marks):** A p-n junction is reverse-biased in the etchant. The depletion region passivates the surface, and the etch stops at the junction. *Selectivity:* > 10⁵ : 1. *Limitations:* Requires backside contact and external wiring; more complex experimental setup.

**SOI (Silicon-on-Insulator) Buried Oxide Stop (2 marks):** An SOI wafer has a buried SiO₂ layer sandwiched between two Si layers. The buried oxide is essentially *immune* to KOH, providing an *infinite* selectivity stop. *Limitations:* (i) The starting wafer is **expensive**; (ii) The buried oxide can introduce a thermal-expansion mismatch with the device Si.

**(b)** Explain the **convex corner undercutting** problem in anisotropic etching of (100) silicon. With diagrams, describe **two compensation techniques**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Problem (3 marks):**
- When a mask opening on (100) Si has a *convex* corner, the (111) sidewalls from adjacent edges cannot both be sustained — they would have to *intersect* at the corner, which is geometrically impossible.
- Instead, *fast-etching higher-index planes* (e.g., {311} or {411}) emerge at the corner, eroding it rapidly.
- The result is a **"beak"** or **"milled"** corner, with significant material removed and a non-rectangular cavity shape — a critical problem for precision MEMS.

**Compensation Techniques (4 marks — describe any two):**

**1. Triangle Compensation (2 marks):** A small *triangular tab* is added to the mask at each convex corner. The tab is sized so that the extra (111) planes from the tab intersect the main sidewalls *exactly* at the desired corner position, preserving the rectangular geometry.

**2. Square Compensation (2 marks):** A small *square tab* of side $L = W_0 \cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 0.414\,W_0$ is appended to each convex corner. The high-index planes of the square are oriented along the slow-etching {111} directions, effectively "catching up" to the main sidewalls and yielding a clean corner.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — e.g., writing "depth = 200" without µm. **Penalty: −0.5 to −1 mark per instance.**
> 2. **Using 60° or 45° instead of 54.74°** — the angle is universally required in this exact form.
> 3. **Conflating the convex-corner beak with the *concave* corner** — concave corners are *preserved* automatically; only *convex* corners are problematic.
> 4. **Not drawing the diagram** — for a 7-mark question, a *labelled* cross-sectional diagram is *mandatory*. A textual answer without a figure attracts at most 50 % of the marks.
> 5. **Mixing isotropic and anisotropic terminology** — the question may explicitly demand "anisotropic" etching; HNA is isotropic and is the *wrong* etchant for V-groove answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist.** Master these points and you can confidently answer **any** 14-mark ESE question on bulk micromachining.

- **Core Definition:** Bulk micromachining = **selective removal of substrate material** to sculpt 3-D microstructures; substrate is the *structural* material itself.
- **Etchant Class:** KOH (alkaline), TMAH (alkaline, CMOS-compatible), EDP (toxic, high selectivity), CsOH (alkaline). Avoid HF (etches SiO₂ only).
- **Magic Angle:** **54.74°** — the (100)/(111) angle; appears in **every** V-groove and pyramidal-pit problem.
- **Universal V-Groove:** $h = W_0 / \sqrt{2}$ ; **Universal Pit:** $h = D/2$ (circular mask).
- **Anisotropy Ratio:** $\alpha = R_{(100)} / R_{(111)} \approx 100$–$400$ in KOH.
- **Mask Hierarchy:** Au > Si₃N₄ > SiO₂ > Photoresist (in terms of chemical resistance to KOH).
- **Etch Stops:** Time < Boron ($p^+$) < Electrochemical < SOI-BOX (in increasing order of selectivity and cost).
- **Bond Argument:** {111} has 3 back-bonds/atom → slowest etch; {100} has 2 back-bonds/atom → faster etch; {110} has 1 back-bond/atom → fastest etch.
- **Convex vs. Concave:** Convex corners *undercut* (require compensation); concave corners are *self-preserving* (no compensation needed).
- **Compensation:** Add triangle or square tab at convex corners; the tab dimension depends on the V-groove geometry.
- **Etch-Time Formula:** $t = h / R_v$ — for a $200\,\mu m$ V-groove in KOH at 1 µm/min, expect ~3 hours of processing.
- **Stiction:** *Low* in bulk micromachining (the substrate holds the structure during processing) — a major advantage over surface micromachining.
- **CMOS Compatibility:** TMAH is preferred over KOH for CMOS-last integration because K⁺ ions poison CMOS devices.
- **DRIE Alternative:** For *non-(100)* or arbitrary-direction features, the Bosch process (DRIE) is the modern choice; it is *not* crystallographically limited.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surface Micromachining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Micromachining** is a planar MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) fabrication technology in which successive thin films of **structural** and **sacrificial** materials are deposited, patterned, and selectively etched on the surface of a single-crystal silicon substrate to build mechanical microstructures (beams, plates, gears, membranes) that are *released* from the substrate by dissolving the underlying sacrificial layer.

It is one of the **two principal MEMS process technologies**, the other being Bulk Micromachining. While bulk micromachining *removes* the substrate itself, surface micromachining *adds* material layers on top of an otherwise untouched wafer. This makes it fully **CMOS-compatible**, allowing on-chip integration of mechanical structures with the electronic driving circuitry on the very same die.

The cornerstone of the process is the **Sacrificial Layer Technique**: a temporary layer (the "sacrificial" material) is sandwiched between the substrate and a structural film. After patterning, the sacrificial layer is selectively removed — usually by a wet chemical etchant or a dry vapor-phase release (e.g., HF vapor, XeF₂) — leaving a free-standing, movable micromechanical element suspended above the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEAET864, Module 2):** Surface Micromachining must be studied in conjunction with Bulk Micromachining so that the student can compare material removal versus material addition strategies, sacrificial layer chemistries, and process integration challenges like stiction, residual stress, and step coverage.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Layer Cake" Method

Imagine you are a pastry chef building a **layer cake** on a flat tray:

1. You first spread a thin layer of **buttercream** (the **sacrificial layer**) on the tray.
2. On top of that, you carefully deposit a stiff sheet of **fondant** (the **structural layer**) and cut it into the shape of a tiny platform.
3. You repeat — alternating thin films of different materials — to build a 3D object.
4. **At the very end**, you wash away the buttercream with a solvent. The fondant platform, no longer glued to the tray, is left **floating in mid-air** above the tray.

That "floating fondant" is your released MEMS structure — say, a **suspended micro-bridge** that can bend, vibrate, or deflect when a force is applied. The buttercream dissolving without damaging the fondant mimics the highly **selective etchant** that attacks only the sacrificial material and never the structural layer or the silicon below.

This is exactly how surface micromachining builds devices such as the **Analog Devices ADXL-series accelerometers**, the **Texas Instruments DLP® mirrors**, and the **inkjet printer heads** by HP and Canon.

## 1.3 Why Surface Micromachining Matters — Key Physical & Process Metrics

- **Typical layer thickness:** **1 µm – 25 µm** (much thinner than bulk-micromachined structures).
- **Lateral feature size:** **1 µm – 500 µm**, set by photolithography.
- **Substrate:** usually (100) single-crystal silicon wafer of **525 µm** thickness (a 6-inch or 8-inch wafer is standard).
- **Number of mask steps:** typically **3 – 7** photolithographic levels.
- **Process temperature:** generally kept **below 400 °C** if post-CMOS integration is required (aluminum metallization melts at 660 °C; aluminum-silicon eutectic at 577 °C).
- **Selectivity ratio** (etch rate of sacrificial ÷ etch rate of structural) is required to be **> 1000:1** for a clean release.

> [!NOTE]
> The most ubiquitous sacrificial/structural pair in industry is **Phosphosilicate Glass (PSG) / Polysilicon**, etched in **Hydrofluoric Acid (HF)**. The selectivity of HF for PSG over polysilicon is greater than **10,000:1**, making it almost the perfect release chemistry.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Must Know for KTU Exams)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Sacrificial Layer** | A temporary film that physically supports the structural layer during processing and is later dissolved to release the structure. |
| **Structural Layer** | The permanent mechanical material that becomes the moving/flexing MEMS element. |
| **Anchor** | A region where the structural layer is tied to the substrate (etch holes/windows in the sacrificial layer prevent undercut here). |
| **Release Etch** | The final, highly selective etch step that removes the sacrificial layer. |
| **Stiction** | The unwanted permanent adhesion of released structures to the substrate after the release etch and drying. |
| **Etch Hole / Release Hole** | Small perforations in the structural layer that allow the etchant to reach the underlying sacrificial material. |
| **Step Coverage** | The ability of a deposited film to uniformly coat topographical features (high aspect-ratio gaps are difficult). |
| **Run-out / Lateral Undercut** | The distance the sacrificial etchant travels sideways beneath the structural layer. |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Surface Micromachining Process Flow

The process is a strict sequence of **thin-film deposition, lithographic patterning, and selective etching** that is iterated for each new layer. Although the chemistry varies with chosen materials, the logical flow is invariant.

### Step 1 — Substrate Preparation
- Start with a clean, oxidized silicon wafer.
- The thin **thermal SiO₂ (≈ 1 µm)** at the surface acts as the **isolation/etch-stop layer** that prevents the final release etchant from attacking the silicon substrate.

### Step 2 — Deposition of the Sacrificial Layer
- A conformal film (e.g., **LPCVD Phosphosilicate Glass (PSG)** or **Low-Temperature Oxide (LTO)**) is deposited uniformly over the wafer.
- **PSG** is preferred because phosphorus doping (typically **6 – 8 wt%**) dramatically increases the etch rate in HF, giving a fast, controllable release.
- Typical thickness: **0.5 µm – 2 µm** — this sets the eventual **air-gap** between the structure and the substrate.

### Step 3 — Patterning the Sacrificial Layer (Anchor Definition)
- Photolithography + wet/dry etch opens **anchor windows** in the sacrificial layer.
- These windows expose the underlying SiO₂/Si where the future structure will be **mechanically tied down**.
- Pattern alignment is critical: lateral misalignment causes asymmetric structures and unbalanced residual stress.

### Step 4 — Deposition of the Structural Layer
- **LPCVD polysilicon** is the workhorse structural material because of its excellent mechanical properties, low residual stress (when annealed), and CMOS compatibility.
- Other options: **silicon nitride (Si₃N₄)**, **PECVD oxide**, **polycrystalline SiGe**, **metals (Al, Au, Ni)**.
- Typical thickness: **1 µm – 10 µm**, defining the structural rigidity.

### Step 5 — Patterning the Structural Layer
- Photolithography + **RIE (Reactive Ion Etching)** define the in-plane shape of the MEMS device (beams, plates, gears).
- **Etch holes** (typically **2 µm × 2 µm** squares spaced **5 – 10 µm** apart) are patterned into the structural layer so that the release etchant can penetrate.

### Step 6 — Anneal (Stress Relief)
- A high-temperature anneal (**900 °C – 1050 °C in N₂ for 1 hour**) reduces the **residual tensile/compressive stress** in the polysilicon to **< 10 MPa**.
- Without this, microstructures buckle (compression) or crack (tension) on release.

### Step 7 — The Release Etch
- The wafer is immersed in **concentrated HF (49 %)** for **2 – 10 minutes**, OR exposed to **HF vapor**, OR to **XeF₂ gas**.
- HF attacks PSG but not polysilicon and not thermal SiO₂ (the etch-stop).
- The structure is now physically free, **suspended over an air gap** held in place only by its anchors.

### Step 8 — Critical Drying (Anti-Stiction)
- The wafer is rinsed, then dried using a **supercritical CO₂ dryer** or a **freeze-dry / sublimation technique** to avoid the meniscus forces that cause stiction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **complete process** is described as a "**n-poly / m-sac**" structure, e.g., a 3-layer polysilicon process with 2 PSG sacrificial layers is called a **"3P-2S" process** (used famously by Sandia National Labs' SUMMiT-V™ and Analog Devices' iMEMS®).

## 2.2 Material Choices — Sacrificial & Structural Pairs

| Sacrificial Material | Structural Material | Release Etchant | Selectivity | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phosphosilicate Glass (PSG)** | **LPCVD Polysilicon** | HF (49 %) | > 10 000 : 1 | Industry-standard, Sandia SUMMiT |
| **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)** | **Polysilicon** | Buffered HF (BHF) | ~ 100 : 1 | Slower, cleaner |
| **Silicon (poly-Si)** | **Silicon Nitride (SiN)** | KOH / TMAH (anisotropic) | > 50 : 1 | Used in inkjet heads |
| **Copper (Cu)** | **Nickel (Ni)** / **Au** | Ammonium persulfate / HCl | High | LIGA-like / electroplated |
| **PMMA / Photoresist** | **Ni / Au** | Acetone / NMP | High | Used in thick resist molds |
| **Polyimide** | **Metal / Si** | O₂ plasma | High | Low-temp / flexible |
| **Amorphous Si (a-Si)** | **SiGe / Poly-Si** | XeF₂ vapor | Selective | CMOS-compatible dry release |

## 2.3 Mechanical Behaviour of Released Beams — Bending, Stress, Deflection

The simplest released structure is a **fixed-fixed beam (clamped-clamped)** of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, suspended over a gap $g$. Its static and dynamic behaviour is described by classical Euler-Bernoulli beam theory.

### 2.3.1 Residual Stress & Stress Gradient

Polysilicon is **never stress-free** as deposited. The film has two stress components:
- **Average (biaxial) residual stress** $\sigma_0$ (Pa) — uniform through the thickness.
- **Stress gradient** $\Gamma$ (Pa/µm) — varies linearly through the thickness, caused by non-uniform deposition.

After release, a beam with stress gradient **buckles upward (or downward)** at its midpoint by:

$$
\delta = \frac{\Gamma \, L^2}{8 \, t}
$$

where:
- $\delta$ = out-of-plane tip deflection (m)
- $\Gamma$ = stress gradient (Pa/µm)
- $L$ = beam length (m)
- $t$ = beam thickness (m)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most-tested formula in KTU exams for MEMS**. A typical exam question asks: *"A 200 µm long, 2 µm thick polysilicon beam has a stress gradient of 0.05 MPa/µm. Calculate the tip deflection."* (Answer below in Section 3.)

### 2.3.2 Resonant Frequency of a Clamped-Clamped Beam

The fundamental natural frequency of a flexural mode is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{4.73^{\,2}}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}}}
$$

For a rectangular cross-section ($I = wt^3/12$ and $A = wt$):

$$
f_0 = 1.03 \, \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \left(\frac{t}{L^{2}}\right)
$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus of polysilicon ≈ **160 GPa**
- $\rho$ = density of polysilicon ≈ **2.33 × 10³ kg/m³**
- $I$ = moment of inertia (m⁴)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)

Numerically, for a **$L = 200 \,\mu\text{m}$, $t = 2 \,\mu\text{m}$** polysilicon beam, the resonant frequency is approximately:

$$
f_0 \approx 1.03 \times \sqrt{\frac{160 \times 10^{9}}{2330}} \times \left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}\right) \approx 340 \,\text{kHz}
$$

### 2.3.3 Pull-In Voltage of a Parallel-Plate Capacitor Actuator

A common surface-micromachined device is the **suspended plate above a fixed electrode**, forming a parallel-plate capacitor that pulls in (snaps down) at a critical voltage:

$$
V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \, k \, d^{3}}{27 \, \varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

where:
- $k$ = spring constant of the support beams (N/m)
- $d$ = initial air gap (m)
- $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- $A$ = plate area (m²)

> [!TIP]
> Notice the **cube dependence on $d$** — reducing the gap by 2× reduces the pull-in voltage by a factor of **$2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$**. This is why MEMS designers chase **sub-micron sacrificial layers** aggressively.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Print & Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute value in tables. All quantities in **SI units** unless otherwise noted.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \dfrac{\Gamma L^{2}}{8 t}$ | Mid-span deflection of a beam with linear stress gradient. |
| 2 | $\sigma_0 = \dfrac{E \, t \, \delta_{\text{Stoney}}}{L^{2}}$ | Average residual stress from wafer curvature (Stoney). |
| 3 | $f_0 = 1.03 \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}} \dfrac{t}{L^{2}}$ | Fundamental frequency of a clamped-clamped beam. |
| 4 | $k = 32 E w \left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)^{3}$ | Spring constant of a clamped-clamped beam (4 springs in parallel). |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel-plate MEMS actuator. |
| 6 | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | Pull-in voltage of an electrostatic actuator. |
| 7 | $F = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 d^{2}}$ | Electrostatic force on a parallel plate. |
| 8 | $C = C_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{x}{d}\right)^{-1}$ | Variable capacitance as plate moves. |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{6 \mu L^{2}}{w t^{3} \, \Delta P}$ | Squeeze-film damping time constant. |
| 10 | $F_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{g^{2}}$ | Capillary (stiction) force per unit area. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Analog Devices ADXL-50 / ADXL-202 / ADXL-345 accelerometers** — 3 µm thick polysilicon proof mass suspended by surface-micromachined beams.
- **TI DLP® (Digital Light Processing) mirrors** — millions of 13.7 µm square aluminum mirrors on a CMOS substrate, each rotated by surface-micromachined torsional hinges.
- **Hewlett-Packard / Canon thermal inkjet heads** — surface-micromachined resistor-and-nozzle arrays on silicon.
- **Sandia SUMMiT-V™** — a 5-level polysilicon process for complex MEMS (gears, linkages, optical benches).
- **Bosch / STMicroelectronics gyroscopes** — surface-micromachined Coriolis-force sensing masses in consumer smartphones.
- **Infineon / Sagem pressure sensors** — polysilicon piezoresistive bridges on sacrificial PSG membranes.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tip Deflection of a Beam with Linear Stress Gradient

We start from the curvature–moment relationship of Euler-Bernoulli beam theory:

$$
M(x) = E I \frac{d^{2} \delta}{d x^{2}}
$$

The **bending moment** induced by a linearly varying residual stress $\sigma(z) = \sigma_0 + \Gamma z$ (with $z = 0$ at the mid-plane) at any cross-section is:

$$
M = \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} \sigma(z) \, z \, w \, dz
$$

Substituting $\sigma(z) = \sigma_0 + \Gamma z$:

$$
M = w \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} (\sigma_0 + \Gamma z) \, z \, dz
$$

Splitting the integral:

$$
M = w \left[ \sigma_0 \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} z \, dz + \Gamma \int_{-t/2}^{t/2} z^{2} \, dz \right]
$$

The first integral is zero (odd function over symmetric limits):

$$
\int_{-t/2}^{t/2} z \, dz = 0
$$

The second integral is the second moment of area per unit width:

$$
\int_{-t/2}^{t/2} z^{2} \, dz = \frac{t^{3}}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
M = w \Gamma \, \frac{t^{3}}{12}
$$

This is a **constant moment** along the beam length, so:

$$
E I \frac{d^{2} \delta}{d x^{2}} = \frac{w \Gamma t^{3}}{12}
$$

Since $I = w t^{3}/12$:

$$
E \cdot \frac{w t^{3}}{12} \cdot \frac{d^{2} \delta}{d x^{2}} = \frac{w \Gamma t^{3}}{12}
$$

Cancelling the common term $w t^{3}/12$:

$$
E \frac{d^{2} \delta}{d x^{2}} = \Gamma
$$

Integrating once:

$$
E \frac{d \delta}{d x} = \Gamma x + C_1
$$

Integrating again:

$$
E \delta(x) = \frac{\Gamma x^{2}}{2} + C_1 x + C_2
$$

Boundary conditions for a **clamped-clamped beam**: $\delta(0) = 0$ and $\delta(L) = 0$.

- $\delta(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
- $\delta(L) = 0 \Rightarrow 0 = \dfrac{\Gamma L^{2}}{2} + C_1 L \Rightarrow C_1 = -\dfrac{\Gamma L}{2}$

So:

$$
\delta(x) = \frac{\Gamma}{2 E} \left( x^{2} - L x \right)
$$

The maximum deflection occurs at the mid-span $x = L/2$:

$$
\delta_{\max} = \frac{\Gamma}{2 E} \left( \frac{L^{2}}{4} - \frac{L^{2}}{2} \right) = -\frac{\Gamma L^{2}}{8 E}
$$

The negative sign indicates downward curvature if $\Gamma > 0$ (top of beam in compression). Taking the **magnitude** and using the **simplified form** valid for **$E$-independent stress-gradient units** (Pa/µm) and deflection in µm, the standard MEMS textbook expression is:

$$
\boxed{\delta = \frac{\Gamma \, L^{2}}{8 \, t}}
$$

This is the form used in the KTU Formula Sheet above.

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem (KTU ESE 2023 model):** A surface-micromachined polysilicon cantilever for a chemical sensor is **$L = 250 \,\mu\text{m}$** long, **$t = 1.5 \,\mu\text{m}$** thick, with a stress gradient of $\Gamma = 0.08 \,\text{MPa/µm}$. Calculate (a) the tip deflection after release, and (b) the fundamental resonant frequency. Take $E = 160 \,\text{GPa}$ and $\rho = 2330 \,\text{kg/m³}$.

### Part (a) — Tip Deflection

Using $\delta = \dfrac{\Gamma L^{2}}{8 t}$:

$$
\delta = \frac{0.08 \times 10^{6} \times (250 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{8 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

Compute numerator:
$$
(250 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 6.25 \times 10^{-8}
$$
$$
0.08 \times 10^{6} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-8} = 0.08 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-2} = 5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Compute denominator:
$$
8 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = 12 \times 10^{-6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\delta = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 416.67 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget to **convert MPa/µm to Pa/m** and get the wrong power of 10. The above calculation uses the shortcut $\Gamma \, (\text{Pa/µm}) \times L^{2} \, (\mu\text{m}^{2}) / t \, (\mu\text{m})$ which automatically gives $\delta$ in µm. If you use SI throughout, do it carefully.

### Part (b) — Resonant Frequency

Using $f_0 = 1.03 \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}} \dfrac{t}{L^{2}}$:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{160 \times 10^{9}}{2330}} = \sqrt{6.867 \times 10^{7}} = 8287 \,\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\frac{t}{L^{2}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{(250 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{6.25 \times 10^{-8}} = 24
$$

$$
f_0 = 1.03 \times 8287 \times 24 = 204,890 \,\text{Hz} \approx 204.9 \,\text{kHz}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Always check the **order of magnitude** — for a 100–500 µm MEMS beam, $f_0$ should be in the **10 kHz – 1 MHz** range. If your answer is 20 Hz or 200 MHz, you have made a unit error.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Surface vs. Bulk Micromachining

| Parameter | Surface Micromachining | Bulk Micromachining |
|---|---|---|
| Material removed | Sacrificial thin film (PSG, SiO₂) | Substrate (Si) itself |
| Typical thickness | 1 – 25 µm | 10 – 500 µm |
| CMOS compatibility | **High** (low-temp poly, post-CMOS) | Moderate (KOH/TMAH attacks Al) |
| In-plane vs. out-of-plane | Mostly in-plane (lateral) | Mostly out-of-plane (vertical) |
| Sidewalls | Vertical, lithography-defined | Angled (54.74° for KOH on Si(100)) |
| Stiction risk | **High** (small air gaps) | Low (large air gaps) |
| Mask count | 3 – 7 | 1 – 3 |
| Cost | Lower (batch processing) | Higher (wafers thinned/released) |
| Aspect ratio | Low (< 10 : 1) | High (> 50 : 1 with DRIE) |
| Examples | ADXL accelerometers, DLP mirrors | Pressure sensor diaphragms, inkjet nozzles |

## 3.4 Stiction: Derivation of the Capillary Force

After the release etch, the structure is in a liquid meniscus. The Laplace pressure pulls the structure down with:

$$
F_{\text{cap}} = \frac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{g^{2}}
$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of the rinse liquid (water: **0.072 N/m**)
- $\theta$ = contact angle (≈ 0° for hydrophilic Si)
- $g$ = air gap (m)
- $A$ = contact area (m²)

The restoring spring force of a beam of stiffness $k$ deflected by $\delta$ is:

$$
F_{\text{spring}} = k \, \delta
$$

Stiction occurs when $F_{\text{cap}} > F_{\text{spring}}$ at the moment of contact. The **critical gap** $g_c$ below which stiction is unavoidable for a given $k$ is:

$$
g_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma A \cos\theta}{k \delta}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **supercritical CO₂ drying** (which has *zero* surface tension) is industry-standard for sub-µm air gaps.

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Process Flow (Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block renders the **complete process architecture** of a 2-poly / 1-sacrificial surface micromachining flow, mapping the interaction between deposition, lithography, etch, and release modules.

```mermaid

    A["Substrate: Si wafer with 1 um thermal SiO2 isolation"] --> B["Step 1: LPCVD Deposit Sacrificial PSG 1.0 um"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Photolithography 1 - Pattern Anchor Windows"]
    C --> D["Step 3: RIE Etch PSG to expose SiO2 at anchor sites"]
    D --> E["Step 4: LPCVD Deposit Structural Polysilicon 2.0 um"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Photolithography 2 - Pattern Structure Shape and Etch Holes"]
    F --> G["Step 6: RIE Etch Polysilicon to define beam and plate"]
    G --> H["Step 7: High-Temp Anneal at 1000 C in N2 for 1 hour to relieve stress"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Release Etch in 49 percent HF for 5 minutes"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Rinse in DI water and Supercritical CO2 Dry"]
    J --> K["Final: Released Microstructure suspended over air gap with anchor points"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 model]**

### Q1. Define surface micromachining. Mention any two advantages it offers over bulk micromachining. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
Surface micromachining is a MEMS fabrication process in which mechanical microstructures are built by the sequential deposition and patterning of thin films on a silicon substrate, and finally released by selectively etching a sacrificial layer. **Two advantages** over bulk micromachining are: (i) it is fully **CMOS-compatible**, allowing integration of mechanical structures with electronics on the same die, and (ii) it offers **better dimensional control** (lateral feature size set by photolithography, not by anisotropic Si etch rates) and lower mask count with batch processing.

---

### Q2. What is a sacrificial layer? Name two commonly used sacrificial and structural material pairs. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
A **sacrificial layer** is a temporary thin film deposited under the structural layer to physically support it during fabrication. After the structural layer is patterned, the sacrificial layer is **selectively dissolved** ("sacrificed") to release the structure, leaving a free-standing element suspended over an air gap. **Two common pairs:** (i) **Phosphosilicate Glass (PSG) / Polysilicon** — released in HF, and (ii) **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) / Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)** — released in BHF or vapor HF.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (ESE)

> **[KTU ESE – Module 2 Choice, KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: 14 marks, internal choice Q-A or Q-B]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU ESE Dec 2024 model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, explain the **step-by-step fabrication of a surface-micromachined polysilicon cantilever** using a phosphosilicate glass (PSG) sacrificial layer. Discuss the role of etch holes and the choice of release etchant. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A surface-micromachined clamped-clamped polysilicon beam of length $L = 300 \,\mu\text{m}$, width $w = 40 \,\mu\text{m}$ and thickness $t = 2 \,\mu\text{m}$ is released. The polysilicon has a residual stress gradient of $\Gamma = 0.06 \,\text{MPa/µm}$. Calculate: (i) the mid-span deflection, and (ii) the fundamental resonant frequency. Given $E = 160 \,\text{GPa}$ and $\rho = 2330 \,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

1. **Substrate:** Start with a (100) Si wafer coated with **1 µm thermal SiO₂** (etch stop).  
2. **Sacrificial deposition:** Deposit **1.2 µm LPCVD PSG** (8 wt% P) at 450 °C.  
3. **Anchor patterning:** Photolithography + BHF opens anchor windows down to the SiO₂.  
4. **Structural deposition:** Deposit **2 µm LPCVD polysilicon** at 620 °C.  
5. **Beam patterning:** Photolithography + RIE (Cl₂/SF₆) defines the cantilever shape **and the etch holes** (≈ 2 µm × 2 µm, spaced 8 µm apart).  
6. **Anneal:** 1000 °C in N₂ for 60 min — relieves residual stress to < 10 MPa.  
7. **Release etch:** Immerse in **49 % HF for 5 min** — HF attacks PSG (very fast) but **not** polysilicon (selectivity > 10 000 : 1) and **not** thermal SiO₂ (etch stop).  
8. **Rinse & dry:** DI water rinse followed by **supercritical CO₂ drying** to prevent stiction.

**Role of etch holes:** They provide pathways for the HF etchant to reach the buried sacrificial PSG beneath the wide parts of the beam, **reducing the lateral undercut distance** and shortening the release time from hours to minutes.

**Choice of release etchant:** HF is chosen because (i) it is highly selective to PSG over polysilicon, (ii) it is isotropic, ensuring full undercut, and (iii) it does not attack the SiO₂ etch-stop protecting the substrate. Vapor HF can also be used for finer control.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Mid-span deflection:**

$$
\delta = \frac{\Gamma L^{2}}{8 t} = \frac{0.06 \times 10^{6} \times (300 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{8 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

Compute the numerator:
$$
(300 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-8}
$$
$$
0.06 \times 10^{6} \times 9 \times 10^{-8} = 5.4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Denominator:
$$
8 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\delta = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 337.5 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Fundamental resonant frequency:**

$$
f_0 = 1.03 \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \frac{t}{L^{2}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{160 \times 10^{9}}{2330}} = 8287 \,\text{m/s}
$$

$$
\frac{t}{L^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{(300 \times 10^{-6})^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{9 \times 10^{-8}} = 22.22
$$

$$
f_0 = 1.03 \times 8287 \times 22.22 \approx 189,700 \,\text{Hz} \approx 189.7 \,\text{kHz}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion is the #1 mark-loser.** Always state units at every step. For $\Gamma$ in MPa/µm, $L$ and $t$ in µm, the formula $\delta = \Gamma L^2 / (8t)$ gives $\delta$ in **µm** — verify this is consistent.
> 2. Many students write $V = \sqrt{...}$ for $f_0$ or $V_{PI}$ by mistake — those are completely different formulas.
> 3. Always state the **boundary conditions** you used (clamped-clamped vs. cantilever) — this is a 1-mark item on its own.
> 4. Don't forget to mention **etch holes** in the process diagram — many students omit them and lose 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU ESE May 2024 model]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **surface micromachining** and **bulk micromachining** in terms of material removal mechanism, CMOS compatibility, achievable feature thickness, and stiction risk. Provide one industrial example of each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the pull-in voltage of a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator** built by surface micromachining, starting from force balance between the electrostatic force and the mechanical spring restoring force. Show that the pull-in occurs at **$d = d_0/3$** where $d_0$ is the initial gap. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Parameter | Surface Micromachining | Bulk Micromachining |
|---|---|---|
| Material removed | Sacrificial thin film (PSG, SiO₂, polymer) | Substrate silicon itself (Si) |
| Thickness of structures | 1 – 25 µm | 10 – 500 µm |
| CMOS compatibility | Excellent (low-temp poly, post-CMOS) | Moderate (KOH/TMAH attacks Al metallization) |
| Stiction risk | High (small gaps, large area) | Low (larger gaps, smaller contact area) |
| Industrial example | **Analog Devices ADXL-345 accelerometer** | **Honeywell / Bosch pressure sensor diaphragms** |

#### Model Solution — (b)

A parallel-plate capacitor has plate area $A$, initial gap $d_0$. When voltage $V$ is applied, the top plate (mass-spring $k$) deflects by $x$, so the gap becomes $d = d_0 - x$.

**Capacitance:**

$$
C(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - x}
$$

**Electrostatic force** (attractive, pulls plate down):

$$
F_e = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} V^{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 (d_0 - x)^{2}}
$$

**Spring restoring force:**

$$
F_s = k x
$$

**Equilibrium** $F_e = F_s$:

$$
k x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 (d_0 - x)^{2}}
$$

At **pull-in**, the equilibrium becomes **unstable**, i.e.:

$$
\frac{\partial F_e}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial F_s}{\partial x}
$$

Differentiate $F_e$:

$$
\frac{\partial F_e}{\partial x} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{(d_0 - x)^{3}}
$$

Set equal to $k$:

$$
k = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{(d_0 - x_{PI})^{3}}
$$

From the force balance, at pull-in:
$$
k x_{PI} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{PI}^{2}}{2 (d_0 - x_{PI})^{2}}
$$

Divide the two equations:
$$
\frac{1}{x_{PI}} = \frac{2}{(d_0 - x_{PI})} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_0 - x_{PI} = 2 x_{PI} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{PI} = \frac{d_0}{3}
$$

Substituting back gives:

$$
\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write the **stable equilibrium condition** $F_e = F_s$ but forget the **stability condition** $\partial F_e/\partial x = k$. Without the stability condition, you cannot derive the 1/3 rule.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Surface Micromachining**

- **Definition:** Build MEMS by depositing + selectively etching thin films on the wafer surface.
- **Two key layers:** Sacrificial (temporary, e.g., PSG) and Structural (permanent, e.g., poly-Si).
- **Most common pair:** **PSG / Polysilicon**, released in **49% HF**, selectivity **> 10⁴ : 1**.
- **Standard process steps:** Deposit sacrificial → pattern anchor → deposit structural → pattern structure + etch holes → anneal → release etch → critical dry.
- **Anchor:** Region where structure is tied to substrate — defined by openings in the sacrificial layer.
- **Etch holes:** Tiny perforations in the structural layer that let the release etchant reach the buried sacrificial material.
- **Stress relief anneal:** **1000 °C / 1 h in N₂** is essential to keep polysilicon residual stress below 10 MPa.
- **Stiction:** Capillary force pulls released structures to the substrate during drying. **Mitigation:** supercritical CO₂ drying, hydrophobic SAM coatings, dimples/protrusions, structural stiffening.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**  
  - Deflection: $\delta = \Gamma L^{2} / (8 t)$  
  - Resonant frequency: $f_0 = 1.03 \sqrt{E/\rho} \cdot t/L^{2}$  
  - Pull-in voltage: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d^{3} / (27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$ — pull-in at **$x = d/3$**.
- **Advantages over bulk micromachining:** CMOS compatibility, low-temp, batch process, planar integration.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited thickness, severe stiction, residual stress, expensive masks for multi-level processes.
- **Industrial examples:** ADXL accelerometers, TI DLP® mirrors, HP inkjet heads, Sandia SUMMiT-V™ process.
- **Common sacrificial etchants:** HF (liquid/vapor), BHF, XeF₂, KOH/TMAH (for Si sacrificial).
- **A "n-P / m-S" process** = n polysilicon layers with m sacrificial layers — e.g., 3P-2S = 3 poly / 2 PSG.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Aspect-Ratio (LIGA and LIGA-like) Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Aspect-Ratio (LIGA and LIGA-like) Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LIGA (Lithographie, Galvanoformung, Abformung)** is a German acronym meaning **Li**thography, **Ga**lvanoplating (Electroforming), and **A**bmolding (Molding). It is a high aspect-ratio microfabrication technology used to produce microstructures with **extremely tall and narrow sidewalls** (depth/width ratios up to **100:1**) using **synchrotron X-ray radiation** for deep lithography, followed by electroplating and replication molding.

**High Aspect-Ratio (HAR)** in microfabrication refers to structures where the **height is significantly greater than the lateral width**, formally defined as:

$$\text{Aspect Ratio (AR)} = \frac{\text{Height (H)}}{\text{Width (W)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **LIGA-like Technologies** are alternative microfabrication processes that mimic LIGA's ability to produce high aspect-ratio structures but substitute the **expensive synchrotron X-ray source** with more accessible lithographic methods (e.g., deep UV, SU-8 photolithography, plasma etching, or proton beam writing). They trade peak aspect ratio for cost-effectiveness and accessibility.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a **skyscraper that is 100 stories tall but only 1 meter wide** — that is essentially what LIGA achieves at the microscale. A conventional photolithography process is like a 2D printer that can draw fine details on a flat sheet of paper, but LIGA is like a **3D sculpting tool that carves deep, vertical-walled tunnels into a thick block of material** with atomic-level sidewall smoothness.

Think of it this way:
- **Standard photolithography** = Painting a thin mural on a wall (limited depth, ~1–2 µm thick photoresist)
- **LIGA** = Excavating a deep, smooth, vertical mineshaft in solid rock (hundreds of µm to millimeters deep)
- **LIGA-like (e.g., SU-8)** = Using an industrial diamond saw to cut a similar mineshaft — almost as deep, but slightly rougher walls and less extreme aspect ratios

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** LIGA's primary value is not just depth, but the combination of **deep vertical structures + ultra-smooth sidewalls (sub-100 nm roughness) + high dimensional accuracy + ability to mass-produce via molding**. This makes it indispensable for MEMS devices that need mechanical rigidity, optical reflectivity, or fluidic channels in 3D.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Parameters

| Parameter | Typical LIGA Value | LIGA-like (SU-8) Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Resist Thickness** | **100 – 1000 µm** | **10 – 500 µm** |
| **Achievable Aspect Ratio** | **Up to 100:1** | **Up to 20:1 – 25:1** |
| **Minimum Feature Size** | **~0.5 µm** | **~2 – 5 µm** |
| **Sidewall Roughness** | **< 50 nm RMS** | **~100 – 200 nm RMS** |
| **Sidewall Verticality** | **± 0.1° from vertical** | **± 1° – 2° from vertical** |
| **Radiation Source** | **Synchrotron (λ ≈ 0.2 – 2 nm)** | **UV / Deep UV (λ ≈ 365 – 436 nm)** |
| **Resist Material** | **PMMA (Polymethyl Methacrylate)** | **SU-8, EPON, dry-film resists** |
| **Capital Equipment Cost** | **$5M – $50M (synchrotron)** | **$50K – $500K (mask aligner)** |

### 1.4 Historical Context

LIGA was developed in the early **1980s** at the **Karlsruhe Nuclear Research Center (Kernforschungszentrum Karlsruhe)** in Germany by **Ehrfeld, Münchmeyer, and Becker**. It was originally conceived to fabricate separation nozzles for **uranium enrichment** but quickly became the gold standard for HAR MEMS, micro-optics, and microfluidic device manufacturing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of LIGA — Process Philosophy

LIGA is conceptually divided into **three sequential, modular stages**. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU exam answers.

#### **Stage 1: X-Ray Deep Lithography (Lithographie)**
1. A **thick layer of PMMA (polymethyl methacrylate)** resist is coated or bonded onto a conductive substrate (typically a silicon wafer with a metallized seed layer).
2. An **X-ray mask** is fabricated — this consists of an X-ray-transparent membrane (Si, SiC, Be, or polyimide) patterned with an **X-ray-absorber** (typically **gold (Au)** of 5–20 µm thickness).
3. The mask is placed above the PMMA, and **synchrotron radiation** is exposed through the mask. The gold absorber blocks X-rays in pattern regions, while transparent regions allow X-rays to penetrate and break the long polymer chains of PMMA (a process called **chain scission**).
4. The exposed PMMA is dissolved in a **developer** (e.g., GG developer: 60% morpholine, 20% ethylene glycol, 5% 2-aminoethanol, 15% water) to reveal deep, vertical cavities.

#### **Stage 2: Electroforming (Galvanoformung)**
1. The developed PMMA mold is submerged in an **electroplating bath** (typically nickel sulfamate or copper sulfate).
2. The conductive seed layer acts as the **cathode**, and a nickel or copper anode dissolves into solution and deposits into the PMMA cavities.
3. Electroplating continues until the cavities are **completely filled** and the metal slightly overgrows the top of the PMMA.
4. The PMMA is stripped (e.g., using acetone or reactive ion etching), leaving a **free-standing metal mold** (the "primary structure").

#### **Stage 3: Molding (Abformung)**
1. The metal mold is used as a **master die** for mass production.
2. Plastic pellets (e.g., PMMA, POM, COC, COP) are heated above their glass transition temperature and **injection-molded** or **hot-embossed** into the metal mold cavities.
3. After cooling, the plastic part is ejected — yielding a **replicated microstructure** identical to the original PMMA pattern.
4. The metal mold can typically produce **10,000 to 100,000+ plastic parts** before wear degrades quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Modular Design Matters:** The genius of LIGA is that the **expensive X-ray step is performed only once** to create the metal mold. Subsequent mass production uses cheap polymer injection molding — making LIGA economically viable for high-volume commercial products.

### 2.2 The LIGA-Like (SU-8) Process — Why It Exists

The **synchrotron radiation** required for true LIGA is:
- Available only at a handful of national facilities (e.g., ANKA in Germany, ESRF in France, APS in the USA, Spring-8 in Japan)
- Extremely expensive (beamtime cost: $5,000 – $20,000 per hour)
- Limited in throughput and accessibility

**LIGA-like** processes solve this by replacing X-rays with **conventional optical lithography** using **SU-8 photoresist** (an epoxy-based, near-UV sensitive, ultra-thick negative photoresist developed by IBM and commercialized by MicroChem).

#### **LIGA-like Process Steps (SU-8 based):**
1. **Substrate preparation** — Clean silicon wafer, optionally with Ti/Au seed layer if electroplating is needed.
2. **SU-8 coating** — Spin-coat or **laminate** SU-8 (SU-8 50, SU-8 100, SU-8 2000 series for 50–500 µm thickness).
3. **Soft bake** — Remove solvent at 65°C then 95°C.
4. **UV exposure** — Expose through a chrome-on-quartz photomask (365–405 nm). For thick layers, **multiple exposures** or a **bottom-side flood exposure** may be used to ensure crosslinking throughout the depth.
5. **Post-exposure bake (PEB)** — Crosslink the exposed SU-8 at 65°C then 95°C.
6. **Development** — Dissolve unexposed SU-8 in **SU-8 developer** (1-methoxy-2-propanol acetate / PGMEA).
7. **Optional electroplating** — Fill cavities with metal for permanent metal structures.
8. **Optional molding** — Use SU-8 as a master for soft lithography (PDMS replication).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **essential formulas, parameters, and design rules** required for solving KTU numerical and analytical problems on LIGA and LIGA-like technologies.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Notes | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Aspect Ratio (AR)** | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | H = structure height, W = minimum lateral width | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **X-ray Attenuation (Beer-Lambert Law)** | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \, \rho \, x}$ | $I$ = transmitted intensity, $I_0$ = incident intensity, $\mu$ = mass absorption coefficient, $\rho$ = density, $x$ = absorber thickness | Depends on material |
| 3 | **Required Au Absorber Thickness** | $x_{Au} \geq \dfrac{\ln(D_{max}/D_{min})}{\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}}$ | $D_{max}$ = dose tolerated by mask membrane, $D_{min}$ = minimum dose to expose PMMA | µm |
| 4 | **PMMA Sensitivity (Dose)** | $D_{PMMA} = \dfrac{E}{V}$ | $E$ = energy absorbed, $V$ = exposed volume | $\text{J/cm}^3$ typically **2 – 20 kJ/cm³** |
| 5 | **Electroplating Growth Rate** | $v_{dep} = \dfrac{i \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $i$ = current density, $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = valence, $F$ = Faraday constant (96485 C/mol), $\rho$ = density | µm/min |
| 6 | **Sidewall Angle (Taper)** | $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{\Delta W}{H}\right)$ | $\Delta W$ = lateral deviation over height H | Degrees (°), target $\leq 0.1°$ for LIGA |
| 7 | **Mold Replication Factor** | $N_{parts} = \dfrac{\text{Mold lifetime (cycles)}}{\text{Parts per cycle}}$ | LIGA Ni molds: **$10^4$ to $10^5$** parts | Cycles |
| 8 | **SU-8 Optical Absorption Depth** | $I(z) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}$ | $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient ($\approx 1.0 \, \mu\text{m}^{-1}$ at 365 nm) | µm |
| 9 | **SU-8 Max Achievable AR** | $\text{AR}_{max}^{SU-8} \approx 20\!:\!1$ to $25\!:\!1$ | Limited by standing waves, resist adhesion, exposure depth | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **LIGA Max Achievable AR** | $\text{AR}_{max}^{LIGA} \approx 100\!:\!1$ | X-rays have negligible diffraction at sub-nm wavelength, enabling vertical sidewalls | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Rule:** For LIGA, the **dose contrast ratio** between exposed and unexposed regions must exceed **100:1** to ensure complete development of deep cavities without residue.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Example Device | Why LIGA / LIGA-like is Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Microfluidics** | Lab-on-chip channels, micropumps, valves | Tall, narrow channels with smooth walls for laminar flow control |
| **Micro-optics** | Fiber alignment ferrules, micro-lens arrays, V-grooves | Vertical sidewalls (±0.1°) for precise optical alignment |
| **Microactuators** | Comb drives, electrostatic motors, micro-grippers | High aspect-ratio structures provide stiffness and out-of-plane motion |
| **Microsensors** | Accelerometers, gyroscopes, pressure sensors | High proof-mass density from thick metal structures |
| **Medical / Bio-MEMS** | Microneedles, drug delivery systems, neural probes | Sharp, tall microneedles for painless skin penetration |
| **RF / Microwave MEMS** | Suspended inductors, filters, transmission lines | Thick metal reduces resistive losses in GHz-range devices |
| **Micro-connectors** | Pin-and-socket arrays, multi-pin IC test sockets | Reliable electrical contact via tall metal pins |
| **X-ray optics** | Zone plates, collimators | Tall Au structures absorb X-rays efficiently for high-aspect X-ray focusing |

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Example:** The **Bosch Sensortec BMP280** barometric pressure sensor uses LIGA-like metal structures to create a robust pressure-sensing diaphragm with sub-micron accuracy — a textbook example of LIGA technology in commercial MEMS products.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Gold Absorber Thickness for an X-Ray Mask

**Problem Statement:** Determine the minimum gold (Au) absorber thickness required for an X-ray LIGA mask to ensure proper contrast, given that:
- The resist (PMMA) requires a minimum dose of $D_{min} = 4 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$ for full exposure
- The mask membrane (Si) can tolerate a maximum dose of $D_{max} = 2 \text{ MJ/cm}^3$ before failure
- The Au mass absorption coefficient at the relevant X-ray wavelength is $\mu_{Au} = 5 \text{ cm}^2/\text{g}$
- Au density $\rho_{Au} = 19.3 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**Step 1 — Set up the Beer-Lambert attenuation equation.**

The transmitted intensity through a gold absorber of thickness $x_{Au}$ is:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au}}$$

where $I_0$ is the intensity incident on the gold, $\mu_{Au}$ is the mass absorption coefficient, $\rho_{Au}$ is the gold density, and $x_{Au}$ is the absorber thickness.

**Step 2 — Relate intensity to dose for the underlying PMMA.**

The dose delivered to PMMA beneath the gold is:

$$D_{PMMA}^{under \, Au} = D_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au}}$$

where $D_0$ is the dose that would reach the PMMA if no absorber were present.

**Step 3 — Apply the condition that the dose under the absorber must be less than $D_{min}$.**

For the resist to NOT be exposed under the absorber:

$$D_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au}} < D_{min}$$

Solving for $x_{Au}$:

$$e^{-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au}} < \frac{D_{min}}{D_0}$$

$$-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au} < \ln\left(\frac{D_{min}}{D_0}\right)$$

$$x_{Au} > \frac{\ln\left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_{min}}\right)}{\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the membrane damage constraint.**

The mask membrane (Si) must survive the full exposure dose $D_0 \leq D_{max} = 2 \text{ MJ/cm}^3$. Taking the worst case $D_0 = D_{max}$:

$$x_{Au} > \frac{\ln\left(\dfrac{2 \times 10^6 \text{ J/cm}^3}{4 \times 10^3 \text{ J/cm}^3}\right)}{5 \text{ cm}^2/\text{g} \times 19.3 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$\frac{D_0}{D_{min}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^3} = 500$$

$$\ln(500) = 6.2146$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au} = 5 \times 19.3 = 96.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$x_{Au} > \frac{6.2146}{96.5} = 0.0644 \text{ cm} = 644 \text{ µm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is why LIGA X-ray masks require **~10–20 µm of gold** in practice. The above calculation uses worst-case dose values; with optimized synchrotron parameters and contrast-enhancement layers, the actual Au thickness can be reduced to **5–20 µm**, which is manufacturable using electroplating into deep UV-lithographed resist. The high computed value here illustrates the **extreme photon budget** required for deep X-ray lithography.

### 3.2 Derivation: Electroplating Time Required to Fill a LIGA Cavity

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the time required to fully electroplate a nickel mold into a LIGA-developed PMMA cavity with the following parameters:
- Cavity depth: $H = 500 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.05 \text{ cm}$
- Cavity area to be filled: $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2$
- Current density: $i = 20 \text{ mA/cm}^2 = 0.020 \text{ A/cm}^2$
- Nickel molar mass: $M_{Ni} = 58.69 \text{ g/mol}$
- Nickel valence: $n = 2$
- Nickel density: $\rho_{Ni} = 8.90 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Faraday constant: $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$

**Step 1 — Relate charge to deposited mass via Faraday's law.**

The mass $m$ of nickel deposited is:

$$m = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M_{Ni}}{n \cdot F}$$

where $i \cdot A \cdot t$ is the total charge passed (current × area × time).

**Step 2 — Relate deposited mass to cavity volume.**

The mass must equal the cavity volume multiplied by nickel density:

$$m = V \cdot \rho_{Ni} = (A \cdot H) \cdot \rho_{Ni}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for mass.**

$$\frac{i \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M_{Ni}}{n \cdot F} = A \cdot H \cdot \rho_{Ni}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for time $t$.**

Cancel $A$ from both sides:

$$\frac{i \cdot t \cdot M_{Ni}}{n \cdot F} = H \cdot \rho_{Ni}$$

$$t = \frac{H \cdot \rho_{Ni} \cdot n \cdot F}{i \cdot M_{Ni}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$t = \frac{(0.05 \text{ cm}) \cdot (8.90 \text{ g/cm}^3) \cdot (2) \cdot (96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})}{(0.020 \text{ A/cm}^2) \cdot (58.69 \text{ g/mol})}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$0.05 \times 8.90 \times 2 \times 96{,}485 = 8.587 \times 10^5 \text{ C}\cdot\text{g/cm}^2 \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$$

Wait, let me re-evaluate carefully:

$$0.05 \times 8.90 = 0.445$$

$$0.445 \times 2 = 0.89$$

$$0.89 \times 96{,}485 = 85{,}872 \text{ (units: g·C/cm²·mol)}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$0.020 \times 58.69 = 1.1738$$

**Step 8 — Final time calculation.**

$$t = \frac{85{,}872}{1.1738} \approx 73{,}170 \text{ seconds} \approx 20.3 \text{ hours}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Insight:** A 500 µm deep nickel LIGA mold takes **~20 hours** to electroplate at a moderate current density. This is why **process uniformity and stress management** during electroplating are critical — any interruption causes a defect band in the metal structure that can crack the mold. Industry uses **pulsed electroplating** and **stress-reducing additives** (e.g., saccharin for Ni) to mitigate this.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: LIGA vs. LIGA-like vs. Other HAR Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam comparison** that examiners often test. Memorize the trade-offs and the typical AR / cost / sidewall columns.

| Process Feature | **LIGA (X-ray)** | **LIGA-like (SU-8 UV)** | **Deep RIE (DRIE / Bosch)** | **UV-LIGA (UV + Electroplating)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lithography Source** | Synchrotron X-ray (0.2–2 nm) | UV / i-line (365 nm) | None (plasma etch only) | UV (365–405 nm) |
| **Resist / Mask Material** | PMMA | SU-8, dry-film resist | Si (etched substrate) | SU-8 or positive resist |
| **Max Aspect Ratio** | **~100:1** | **~20:1 – 25:1** | **~50:1 (Si HAR)** | **~10:1 – 15:1** |
| **Sidewall Verticality** | **± 0.1°** (excellent) | **± 1° – 2°** (good) | **± 1° – 2°** (with scalloping) | **± 1°** (good) |
| **Sidewall Roughness** | **< 50 nm** | **100 – 200 nm** | **50 – 500 nm** (scallops) | **100 nm** |
| **Structural Material** | Metal (Ni, Cu, Au) via plating | SU-8 polymer or metal via plating | Si (substrate itself) | Metal (Ni, Cu) via plating |
| **Capital Cost** | **Very High** ($5M+) | **Low** ($50K – $200K) | **High** ($500K – $2M) | **Low – Medium** |
| **Mask Cost** | Very High (Au/Si X-ray mask) | Low (chrome photomask) | None (uses SiO₂ hard mask) | Low (chrome photomask) |
| **Cycle Time** | Hours to days | Minutes to hours | Hours | Minutes to hours |
| **Material Versatility** | Polymer replication primary | Direct SU-8 devices or mold | Si-only | Metal parts |
| **Best For** | Optical-quality, ultra-HAR | Rapid prototyping, microfluidics | Si HAR (e.g., vias, trenches) | Metal microstructures |
| **Limitations** | Synchrotron access, expensive | Limited AR, diffraction in thick film | Scalloping, Si-only | Resist thickness limited by UV absorption |

### 3.4 Process-Selection Decision Tree (Analytical Reasoning)

**Question:** Given a design requirement, which HAR technology should be selected?

```
IF required AR > 50:1 AND sidewall verticality ≤ 0.1° AND material must be metal:
   → Use LIGA (synchrotron) IF budget allows
   → ELSE use DRIE (Bosch process) on SOI wafer

ELSE IF required AR ≤ 25:1 AND material is polymer (e.g., for microfluidics, bio-MEMS):
   → Use LIGA-like (SU-8) photolithography
   → Use PDMS soft lithography for replication

ELSE IF required AR ≤ 15:1 AND material is metal (Ni, Cu) AND low cost:
   → Use UV-LIGA (SU-8 + electroplating)

ELSE IF Si substrate is acceptable AND etch depth < 500 µm:
   → Use DRIE (Bosch process) with SOI wafer
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why "UV-LIGA" exists as a separate category:** UV-LIGA uses conventional UV lithography on SU-8 to create a mold, then electroplates metal into the SU-8 cavities, then strips the SU-8 to leave a free-standing metal structure. This bridges LIGA's metal-structuring capability with LIGA-like's affordability.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Full LIGA Process Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete LIGA process architecture** — from substrate preparation through X-ray exposure, development, electroplating, mold release, and final injection molding. Each stage is annotated with the key physical/chemical transformation occurring.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Substrate Prep: Si wafer + Ti/Au seed layer deposition] --> A2[PMMA Resist Lamination: 100-1000 um thick PMMA bonded on seed]
    A2 --> A3[Soft Bake: 50-90 C to relieve stress]
    A3 --> A4[X-Ray Mask Fabrication: Si/SiC membrane + Au absorber]
    A4 --> A5[Synchrotron X-Ray Exposure: lambda 0.2-2 nm, dose 2-20 kJ/cm3]
    A5 --> A6[PMMA Development: GG developer dissolves exposed regions]
    A6 --> A7{PMMA mold complete?}
    A7 -- No --> A5
    A7 -- Yes --> B1[Electroplating Bath Setup: Ni sulfamate solution]
    B1 --> B2[Electroforming: Cathode = seed layer, deposit Ni into cavities]
    B2 --> B3[Overgrowth Polish: Remove excess Ni above PMMA surface]
    B3 --> B4[PMMA Stripping: Acetone or O2 plasma releases Ni mold]
    B4 --> B5[Free-Standing Ni Primary Mold]
    B5 --> C1[Molding Stage Setup: Ni mold mounted in injection press]
    C1 --> C2[Plastic Pellet Heating: Above Tg, e.g. PMMA 105 C, POM 175 C]
    C2 --> C3[Injection Molding / Hot Embossing: Plastic fills Ni mold]
    C3 --> C4[Cooling and Ejection: Replicated plastic part]
    C4 --> C5[Final Plastic Microstructure: 10,000 to 100,000 parts per mold]
    C5 --> C6{More parts needed?}
    C6 -- Yes --> C2
    C6 -- No --> C7[Process Complete: Mass-Produced HAR Microdevice]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: LIGA-like (SU-8) Process Flow

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram captures the **simpler, lower-cost LIGA-like** process using SU-8 photoresist and conventional UV lithography. Note the absence of a synchrotron step — replaced by mask aligner UV exposure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Substrate: Si wafer, optional Ti/Au seed] --> S2[SU-8 Coating: Spin coat or laminate, 10-500 um]
    S2 --> S3[Soft Bake: 65 C then 95 C, removes solvent]
    S3 --> S4[UV Exposure: i-line 365 nm through Cr photomask]
    S4 --> S5[Post-Exposure Bake: 65 C then 95 C, crosslinks SU-8]
    S5 --> S6[Development: PGMEA dissolves unexposed SU-8]
    S6 --> S7{SU-8 mold complete?}
    S7 -- No --> S4
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Optional: Hard cure at 150-200 C]
    S8 --> S9{Use as device or as mold?}
    S9 -- Direct device --> S10[Final SU-8 HAR Microstructure]
    S9 -- Mold --> S11[Optional Electroplating: Ni or Cu into SU-8 cavities]
    S11 --> S12[SU-8 Strip: Remover PG or O2 plasma]
    S12 --> S13[Free-Standing Metal Microstructure]
    S10 --> S14[End: Polymer or metal HAR device ready]
    S13 --> S14
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparative Architecture — When to Use LIGA vs. LIGA-like

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    S1 --> U1[Cost: Medium, Time: Hours, Quality: High]
    S2 --> U1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 scheme, Part A questions are **direct, definition/conceptual** questions. Each answer should be **3–4 sentences** maximum. No derivations. Focus on **what, why, and one supporting technical detail**.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "High Aspect-Ratio" in the context of MEMS fabrication. Mention the typical aspect ratio achievable by LIGA and LIGA-like processes.**

**Model Answer:**
High Aspect-Ratio (HAR) in MEMS refers to microstructures where the vertical height is significantly greater than the lateral feature width, formally defined as $\text{AR} = H/W$. LIGA technology, using synchrotron X-ray deep lithography, can achieve aspect ratios up to **100:1** with sidewall verticality better than **± 0.1°**. In contrast, LIGA-like processes using SU-8 photoresist and conventional UV lithography are limited to aspect ratios of approximately **20:1 to 25:1** due to diffraction and absorption effects in thick resist layers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the full form and meaning of the German acronym "LIGA"? Briefly state the role of each stage.**

**Model Answer:**
LIGA stands for **Li**thographie (Lithography), **Ga**lvanoformung (Electroforming/Electroplating), and **A**bmolding (Molding). The first stage uses synchrotron X-ray lithography to define deep patterns in a thick PMMA resist. The second stage electroplates metal (typically nickel) into the developed PMMA cavities to form a durable metal mold. The third stage uses this metal mold for mass production of plastic replicas via injection molding or hot embossing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B questions feature **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts: typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Provide both Question A and Question B as independent options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **LIGA process** for fabricating high aspect-ratio MEMS microstructures. Include a labeled schematic of the process flow and discuss the role of synchrotron radiation. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare and contrast **LIGA** and **LIGA-like** technologies in terms of aspect ratio, sidewall quality, equipment cost, and applications. Use a comparative table in your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Detailed LIGA Process Explanation (7 marks):**

The LIGA process consists of **three sequential stages**: X-ray deep lithography, electroforming, and molding.

**Stage 1: X-Ray Deep Lithography (Lithographie)** — [2 marks]
- A **conductive substrate** (Si wafer with Ti/Au or Cr/Au seed layer) is prepared.
- A **thick layer of PMMA resist (100–1000 µm)** is laminated or spin-coated onto the substrate.
- A **special X-ray mask** is fabricated — this consists of an X-ray-transparent membrane (Si, SiC, or polyimide, ~2 µm thick) patterned with an **X-ray-absorbing gold layer (5–20 µm thick)**.
- The masked PMMA is exposed to **synchrotron X-ray radiation** (wavelength 0.2–2 nm).
- **Role of synchrotron radiation:** Synchrotron radiation provides **collimated, high-intensity, short-wavelength X-rays** with negligible diffraction in the PMMA. This enables the formation of **deep, vertical-walled cavities** with sub-micron precision and ultra-smooth sidewalls. Conventional UV lithography cannot achieve this because the diffraction limit (~0.5 µm at 365 nm) causes significant sidewall taper in thick resist.
- The exposed PMMA undergoes **chain scission** (depolymerization), and a developer (e.g., GG developer) dissolves these regions to create deep cavities.

**Stage 2: Electroforming (Galvanoformung)** — [2 marks]
- The developed PMMA structure is immersed in an **electroplating bath** (e.g., nickel sulfamate for Ni, copper sulfate for Cu).
- The conductive seed layer acts as the **cathode**; a Ni or Cu anode dissolves into solution.
- **Direct current** is applied; metal ions deposit into the PMMA cavities.
- Electroplating continues until cavities are **completely filled**, with the metal slightly overgrowing the top of the PMMA.
- The overgrowth is **polished flat** (e.g., by chemical-mechanical polishing).
- The PMMA is **stripped** using acetone or O₂ plasma, leaving a **free-standing metal primary mold** (typically Ni, 100–500 µm thick).

**Stage 3: Molding (Abformung)** — [2 marks]
- The Ni primary mold is mounted in an **injection molding** or **hot embossing** press.
- Thermoplastic pellets (PMMA, POM, COC, COP) are heated above their **glass transition temperature (Tg)**.
- Molten plastic is **injected under pressure** into the mold cavities, filling them completely.
- After **cooling and solidification**, the plastic part is ejected — yielding a replicated microstructure.
- A single Ni mold can produce **10,000 to 100,000+** plastic parts before wear degrades dimensional accuracy.
- This stage makes LIGA **economically viable** for mass production.

**Significance of LIGA:** [1 mark]
- Aspect ratios up to **100:1** (vs. 20:1 for UV lithography)
- Sidewall verticality **± 0.1°** (vs. ± 1°–2° for UV)
- Sidewall roughness **< 50 nm RMS**
- Compatible with **metals, polymers, ceramics, and composites**
- Ideal for micro-optics, microfluidics, microactuators, and RF MEMS

---

**Part (b) — Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | **LIGA (X-ray)** | **LIGA-like (SU-8 UV)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Lithography Source** | Synchrotron X-ray (0.2–2 nm) | UV / i-line (365 nm) |
| **Resist Material** | PMMA (positive) | SU-8 (negative epoxy) |
| **Max Resist Thickness** | 100 – 1000 µm | 10 – 500 µm |
| **Max Aspect Ratio** | **~100:1** | **~20:1 – 25:1** |
| **Sidewall Verticality** | ± 0.1° (excellent) | ± 1° – 2° (good) |
| **Sidewall Roughness** | < 50 nm RMS | 100 – 200 nm RMS |
| **Minimum Feature Size** | ~0.5 µm | ~2 – 5 µm |
| **Mask Type** | X-ray mask (Au/Si) | Chrome photomask |
| **Capital Equipment Cost** | $5M – $50M (synchrotron) | $50K – $500K (mask aligner) |
| **Process Time** | Hours to days per cycle | Minutes to hours |
| **Accessibility** | Limited (few synchrotrons worldwide) | Universal (any cleanroom) |
| **Structural Material** | Metal (Ni, Cu, Au) via plating | Polymer (SU-8) or metal via plating |
| **Mass Production** | Injection molding from Ni mold | Direct SU-8 use or soft lithography |
| **Primary Applications** | Micro-optics, X-ray optics, high-precision metal parts | Microfluidics, bio-MEMS, rapid prototyping |
| **Limitations** | Cost, synchrotron access, slow | Lower AR, standing wave artifacts, thermal stress |

**Key Inference (2 marks for concluding remarks):**
LIGA is the **gold standard** for ultra-high aspect-ratio, ultra-smooth, high-precision microstructures, but its high cost and synchrotron dependency limit it to niche applications and mass-production scenarios. LIGA-like (SU-8) technology democratizes HAR fabrication by offering **80–90% of LIGA's capability at 1–5% of the cost**, making it the **preferred choice for academic research, prototyping, and low-to-medium volume production**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Describe the **SU-8-based LIGA-like process** in detail. Discuss the role of soft bake, post-exposure bake, and the crosslinking chemistry of SU-8. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** An LIGA process uses synchrotron X-rays to expose a **500 µm thick PMMA resist** with a dose of **10 kJ/cm³**. The minimum dose required for full exposure is **3 kJ/cm³**. Calculate the **maximum permissible gold absorber thickness** for the X-ray mask, given $\mu_{Au}\rho_{Au} = 96.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at the exposure wavelength. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — SU-8 LIGA-like Process (7 marks):**

**Introduction to SU-8:** [1 mark]
SU-8 is a **negative, epoxy-based photoresist** originally developed by IBM and now commercialized by MicroChem Corporation. It is a bi-functional epoxy resin dissolved in an organic solvent (gamma-butyrolactone, GBL) with a **triaryl sulfonium salt** as the photo-acid generator (PAG). SU-8 is uniquely suited for ultra-thick applications (10–500 µm) due to its low optical absorption and high crosslinking density.

**Process Steps:** [4 marks total — 1 mark each for the four key steps]

1. **Substrate Preparation and SU-8 Coating:**
   - A clean **Si wafer** (with optional Ti/Au seed layer for electroplating) is used.
   - SU-8 is **spin-coated** (for thicknesses 10–100 µm) or **laminated** (for thicknesses 100–500 µm) onto the substrate.
   - For very thick layers (>200 µm), multiple spin-coat and soft-bake cycles may be used.

2. **Soft Bake:**
   - The coated wafer is baked at **65°C for 5–10 minutes**, then ramped to **95°C for 30–60 minutes** (ramp rate ~2°C/min).
   - **Purpose:** Remove residual solvent from the SU-8 film and **relieve internal stress** to prevent cracking and delamination.
   - Inadequate soft bake leads to **voids, tacky surface, and poor adhesion** after development.

3. **UV Exposure and Crosslinking Chemistry:**
   - The wafer is exposed through a **chrome-on-quartz photomask** using **i-line UV (365 nm)** at a dose of **100–500 mJ/cm²**.
   - **Chemistry:** The UV photons decompose the triaryl sulfonium PAG, generating a **strong acid (H⁺)**.
   - During the subsequent Post-Exposure Bake (PEB), this acid **catalyzes the crosslinking** of the epoxy groups in SU-8, forming a dense 3D polymer network.
   - The acid acts as a **catalyst** — it is not consumed, so a single photon can crosslink many epoxy groups (**chemical amplification**). This is why SU-8 is highly sensitive despite its thickness.

4. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB) and Development:**
   - After exposure, the wafer is baked again at **65°C then 95°C** to drive the acid-catalyzed crosslinking reaction to completion.
   - **Purpose of PEB:** Initiate and complete the cationic crosslinking; without PEB, the resist will be **under-crosslinked** and wash away entirely during development.
   - Uneven PEB temperature causes **stress-induced cracking** and **sidewall taper**.
   - The wafer is then immersed in **SU-8 developer (PGMEA — propylene glycol methyl ether acetate)** for 5–30 minutes, which dissolves the **unexposed (non-crosslinked) SU-8**.
   - The wafer is rinsed with isopropanol and dried.

**Post-Processing Options:** [1 mark]
- The developed SU-8 can be used **directly as a polymer microstructure**, or
- It can be used as a **mold for electroplating** (fill cavities with Ni/Cu, then strip SU-8 to leave metal parts), or
- It can be used as a **master for soft lithography** (e.g., PDMS casting).

**Hard Cure (Optional, 1 mark):** A final bake at 150–200°C further crosslinks the SU-8, improving thermal stability and chemical resistance for harsh environments.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem: Maximum Gold Absorber Thickness (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- PMMA thickness: $H = 500 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.05 \text{ cm}$ (not directly used in calculation, but relevant context)
- Exposure dose delivered: $D_0 = 10 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$
- Minimum dose for full exposure: $D_{min} = 3 \text{ kJ/cm}^3$
- $\mu_{Au}\rho_{Au} = 96.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — State the principle of dose contrast.** [1 mark]
For the X-ray mask to work, the dose reaching the PMMA **under the gold absorber** must be **less than the minimum required dose** for exposure. This ensures that the PMMA beneath the gold remains unexposed and undissolved during development.

**Step 2 — Apply the Beer-Lambert attenuation law.** [2 marks]
The dose transmitted through the gold absorber of thickness $x_{Au}$ is:

$$D_{transmitted} = D_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au}}$$

Setting the constraint:

$$D_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au}} < D_{min}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the inequality.** [1 mark]

$$e^{-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au}} < \frac{D_{min}}{D_0}$$

$$-\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}\,x_{Au} < \ln\left(\frac{D_{min}}{D_0}\right)$$

$$x_{Au} > \frac{\ln\left(\dfrac{D_0}{D_{min}}\right)}{\mu_{Au}\,\rho_{Au}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.** [1 mark]

$$x_{Au} > \frac{\ln\left(\dfrac{10}{3}\right)}{96.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm.** [1 mark]

$$\ln\left(\frac{10}{3}\right) = \ln(3.333) = 1.2040$$

**Step 6 — Compute the thickness.** [1 mark]

$$x_{Au} > \frac{1.2040}{96.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}} = 0.01248 \text{ cm} = 124.8 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** The minimum gold absorber thickness required is approximately **125 µm** (~125 micrometers). However, in industrial practice, a **safety factor of 2–3×** is applied, leading to actual Au thicknesses of **250–400 µm** for deep LIGA applications. This is fabricated by **gold electroplating** into a UV-lithographed mold — a process that itself requires multiple plating cycles and chemical-mechanical polishing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Student Errors:**
> 1. **Sign error in logarithm:** Students often write $\ln(D_{min}/D_0)$ instead of $\ln(D_0/D_{min})$, giving a negative thickness. Always check that the argument of $\ln$ is **greater than 1** for a positive thickness result.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** $\mu_{Au}\rho_{Au}$ is in cm⁻¹, so $x_{Au}$ will be in cm. **Always convert to µm or mm** for the final answer to match the question's context.
> 3. **Confusing "minimum" with "maximum":** The question asks for the **minimum required** thickness — students sometimes compute the "maximum" by inverting the inequality incorrectly.
