> 4. **Forgetting to consider the "greater than" boundary:** The Au must be **at least** this thick; thinner Au will leak X-rays and partially expose the underlying PMMA, leading to defective patterns.
> 5. **Not stating the safety factor:** Real-world masks always have a safety margin. Mentioning this shows engineering maturity and earns bonus marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ **LIGA = Lithography + Electroforming + Molding** (German acronym)
- ☐ **LIGA-like = LIGA's process philosophy using non-synchrotron lithography** (typically SU-8 + UV)
- ☐ **High Aspect Ratio (AR) = H/W**, with LIGA achieving **up to 100:1** and LIGA-like achieving **~20:1 – 25:1**
- ☐ **Synchrotron radiation** is essential for true LIGA because its short wavelength (0.2–2 nm) avoids diffraction in thick PMMA
- ☐ **SU-8 is a negative, epoxy-based, chemically amplified photoresist** that enables ultra-thick (up to 500 µm) UV lithography

**Process Flow — Must Memorize:**
- ☐ **LIGA stages:** Substrate prep → PMMA lamination → X-ray exposure (synchrotron) → Development → Electroplating (Ni) → Polish → PMMA strip → Injection molding
- ☐ **LIGA-like stages:** Substrate prep → SU-8 coat → Soft bake → UV expose → Post-exposure bake → Develop → (Optional: electroplate)

**Critical Formulas (must know by heart):**
- ☐ $\text{AR} = H/W$
- ☐ $I = I_0 \exp(-\mu\rho x)$ (Beer-Lambert law)
- ☐ $D_{transmitted} = D_0 \exp(-\mu_{Au}\rho_{Au}x_{Au})$
- ☐ $t_{plating} = \dfrac{H \cdot \rho \cdot n \cdot F}{i \cdot M}$ (Faraday's law)
- ☐ $x_{Au} > \dfrac{\ln(D_0 / D_{min})}{\mu_{Au}\rho_{Au}}$ (mask thickness)

**Key Numbers to Memorize:**
- ☐ LIGA max AR = **100:1**, LIGA-like max AR = **20:1 – 25:1**
- ☐ Sidewall verticality: LIGA = **± 0.1°**, LIGA-like = **± 1° – 2°**
- ☐ LIGA sidewall roughness: **< 50 nm RMS**
- ☐ PMMA typical thickness: **100 – 1000 µm**
- ☐ SU-8 typical thickness: **10 – 500 µm**
- ☐ Ni mold lifetime: **10,000 – 100,000 parts**
- ☐ Faraday constant: **96,485 C/mol**

**Comparative Knowledge:**
- ☐ **LIGA = high cost, ultra-high quality, limited access**
- ☐ **LIGA-like (SU-8) = low cost, good quality, universal access**
- ☐ **DRIE (Bosch) = Si-only, scalloping sidewalls, AR ~50:1**
- ☐ **UV-LIGA = metal parts via UV + electroplating, AR ~15:1**

**Applications to Remember (at least 4):**
- ☐ Microfluidics (channels, valves, pumps)
- ☐ Micro-optics (fiber ferrules, micro-lenses)
- ☐ Microactuators (comb drives, grippers)
- ☐ Microneedles (drug delivery, neural probes)
- ☐ RF MEMS (inductors, filters)
- ☐ X-ray optics (zone plates, collimators)

**Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☐ ❌ Confusing "LIGA" (X-ray) with "LIGA-like" (UV/SU-8) — they are **not** the same
- ☐ ❌ Stating PMMA is a negative resist — PMMA is a **positive** resist (exposed regions are removed)
- ☐ ❌ Stating SU-8 is a positive resist — SU-8 is a **negative** resist (exposed regions remain)
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting the **post-exposure bake (PEB)** step in SU-8 — without PEB, the acid-catalyzed crosslinking will not occur
- ☐ ❌ Confusing the role of the **conductive seed layer** — it is required for electroplating, not for lithography

**Final Mnemonic — The 3-Stage LIGA Process:**
- ⭐ **"L-E-M"** = **L**ithography (X-ray) → **E**lectroforming (Ni plating) → **M**olding (injection)

> [!NOTE]
> **Good luck with your exam! Master these concepts, practice the numerical problem, and remember the comparative table — these are the highest-yield items for KTU 2024 scheme evaluation.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MEMS Sensors: Design of Acoustic Wave Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** MEMS Sensors: Design of Acoustic Wave Sensors # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Acoustic Wave Sensors (AWS)** are a class of MEMS devices that exploit the **piezoelectric effect** to convert mechanical acoustic wave propagation characteristics (velocity, amplitude, or attenuation) into measurable electrical signals, enabling the detection of physical, chemical, and biological measurands with ultra-high sensitivity. An acoustic wave sensor operates on the principle that a **piezoelectric substrate** (e.g., Lithium Niobate $\text{LiNbO}_3$, Quartz $\text{SiO}_2$, Zinc Oxide $\text{ZnO}$, or Aluminum Nitride $\text{AlN}$) supports the propagation of mechanical waves. The interaction of these waves with the surrounding medium perturbs the wave's properties, which are then transduced into an electrical output via **Interdigital Transducers (IDTs)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):** Acoustic wave sensors are categorized into two principal families: > 1. **Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW)** sensors — wave energy confined near the surface > 2. **Bulk Acoustic Wave (BAW)** sensors — wave energy propagates through the bulk of the substrate > Both rely on the **inverse piezoelectric effect** for actuation and the **direct piezoelectric effect** for sensing. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ripple Detector" Imagine throwing a pebble into a perfectly still pond. The ripples (mechanical waves) travel outward at a speed determined by the water's surface tension and depth. Now imagine placing your finger gently on the water's surface — the ripples slow down, diminish, or shift. An **acoustic wave sensor** works on exactly this principle: - The "pond" is the **piezoelectric substrate**. - The "pebble" is an **electrical signal** applied to metallic IDT fingers, which (via the inverse piezoelectric effect) launches a wave. - The "finger touching the water" is the **analyte** (mass, gas, biological species, humidity, temperature, pressure) that perturbs the wave. - The "change in ripple speed/pattern" is detected as a **frequency shift** or **phase shift** in the output electrical signal. > [!TIP] > **Why piezoelectric materials?** They are the only materials that naturally couple mechanical strain and electric field. This means a single material can both **generate** (actuate) and **detect** (sense) acoustic waves — no external transducer is needed. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value | |--------|----------|---------------| | $v_R$ | Rayleigh wave velocity (free surface) | **~4000 m/s** on ST-cut quartz | | $f_0$ | Operating frequency | **10 MHz – 10 GHz** | | $\rho_q$ | Density of AT-cut quartz | **2650 kg/m³** | | $\mu_q$ | Shear modulus of quartz | **2.947 × 10¹⁰ N/m²** | | $k^2$ | Electromechanical coupling coefficient | **0.5% – 6%** (material dependent) | | $Q$ | Quality factor | **10³ – 10⁵** | ## 1.4 Classification of Acoustic Wave Sensors ``` Acoustic Wave Sensors ├── Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) sensors │ ├── Rayleigh SAW │ ├── Shear Horizontal SAW (SH-SAW) │ ├── Love Wave sensors │ └── Surface Transverse Wave (STW) │ └── Bulk Acoustic Wave (BAW) sensors ├── Thickness Shear Mode (TSM) — e.g., QCM ├── Thickness Longitudinal Mode ├── Acoustic Plate Mode (APM) └── Film Bulk Acoustic Resonator (FBAR) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The distinction between **SAW** and **BAW** is purely based on the **mode of wave propagation**: SAW devices confine energy within ~1 wavelength of the surface, while BAW devices launch waves that travel through the entire thickness of the substrate. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Piezoelectric Foundation The operational foundation of every acoustic wave sensor rests on the **linear piezoelectric constitutive equations**: $$ \begin{aligned} T &= c^E \cdot S - e \cdot E \\ D &= e \cdot S + \varepsilon^S \cdot E \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $T$ = mechanical stress tensor [N/m²] - $S$ = mechanical strain tensor [dimensionless] - $E$ = electric field vector [V/m] - $D$ = electric displacement vector [C/m²] - $c^E$ = elastic stiffness tensor at constant electric field [N/m²] - $e$ = piezoelectric stress tensor [C/m²] - $\varepsilon^S$ = permittivity tensor at constant strain [F/m] > **Why does this matter?** The off-diagonal term $e$ is the *coupling agent*. Without it, mechanical and electrical domains are independent; with it, an input voltage generates a strain (inverse effect), and a strain generates a charge (direct effect). ## 2.2 SAW Sensor Architecture (Step-by-Step) A typical SAW device consists of: 1. **Input IDT** — converts RF electrical signal into a surface acoustic wave 2. **Output IDT** — reconverts the SAW into an electrical signal 3. **Delay line / resonator** — the propagation path between IDTs 4. **Piezoelectric substrate** — wave propagation medium 5. **Sensing layer / sensitive coating** — selective binding region ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic: - **Step 1:** An RF signal $V(t) = V_0 \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ is applied to the input IDT. - **Step 2:** Adjacent IDT fingers have opposite polarity → the spatially periodic electric field at period $p$ excites a mechanical wave at the substrate surface (inverse piezoelectric effect). - **Step 3:** The Rayleigh wave propagates with velocity $v_R$ along the surface. Wavelength $\lambda = v_R / f_0$, and since IDT finger spacing $p = \lambda / 2$, the IDT period directly sets the operating frequency. - **Step 4:** The wave interacts with the analyte (mass loading, viscoelastic loading, or conductivity change) on the sensing surface. - **Step 5:** The modified wave reaches the output IDT and is converted back to an electrical signal (direct piezoelectric effect). - **Step 6:** A frequency counter, network analyzer, or phase detector measures the **frequency shift $\Delta f$**, **phase shift $\Delta \phi$**, or **amplitude change $\Delta A$** — these are the primary outputs. ## 2.3 BAW Sensor Architecture In a BAW device, the wave propagates **perpendicular to the substrate surface**. Two major configurations exist: ### a) Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) - A thin AT-cut quartz disc with **circular electrodes** on both faces. - Excited at its **thickness shear mode (TSM)** resonance. - Operating frequency: $f_0 = v_q / (2 t_q)$ where $t_q$ is the quartz thickness. - Operates typically at **5–20 MHz** for fundamental mode (or higher harmonics). ### b) Film Bulk Acoustic Resonator (FBAR) - A thin **piezoelectric film** (e.g., AlN, ZnO) sandwiched between two metal electrodes. - Acoustic mirrors or air gaps provide acoustic isolation. - Operating frequencies: **1–10 GHz** — ideal for RF and high-sensitivity sensing. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it contains 95% of the equations tested in KTU Module 3 questions on acoustic wave sensors.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f_0}$ | Acoustic wavelength | m | | 2 | $f_0 = \dfrac{v_R}{\lambda}$ | Center/resonant frequency | Hz | | 3 | $f_0 = \dfrac{v_q}{2 t_q}$ | QCM / FBAR thickness-mode frequency | Hz | | 4 | $p = \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ | IDT finger pitch | m | | 5 | $v_R = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2\rho(1+\sigma)}}$ | Rayleigh wave velocity (approx., isotropic) | m/s | | 6 | $\Delta f = -\dfrac{2 f_0^2 \, \Delta m}{A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}}$ | **Sauerbrey equation** (rigid mass loading, QCM) | Hz | | 7 | $C_f = \dfrac{\Delta f}{\Delta m} = -\dfrac{2 f_0^2}{A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}}$ | Mass sensitivity constant | Hz/kg | | 8 | $\Delta f \cdot \lambda = \text{const}$ | Mass sensitivity per unit wavelength (SAW) | Hz·m | | 9 | $S_m = \dfrac{\Delta f / f_0}{\Delta m / A}$ | Normalized mass sensitivity | m²/kg | | 10 | $k^2 = \dfrac{e^2}{c^E \varepsilon^S}$ | Electromechanical coupling coefficient | dimensionless | | 11 | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f_{3dB}}$ | Quality factor (resonator) | dimensionless | | 12 | $\text{IL} = -20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Insertion loss | dB | | 13 | $N_{\text{pairs}} = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f_{\text{device}}}$ | Number of IDT finger pairs (selectivity) | dimensionless | | 14 | $\Delta \phi = 2\pi f_0 \cdot \dfrac{L}{v_R} \cdot \Delta\left(\dfrac{\Delta v}{v_R}\right)$ | Phase shift due to velocity perturbation | rad | > [!CAUTION] > **Table Safety Note:** In markdown tables, the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ and condition $\mid \text{condition} \mid$ must always be written with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table syntax. ## 2.5 Why Acoustic Wave Sensors Are Engineering Marvels - **Extreme sensitivity:** QCM can detect mass changes as small as **~1 ng/cm²**; FBAR and SAW devices reach **pg/cm²** and even **fg/cm²** levels. - **Wireless & passive operation:** SAW sensors can be interrogated **remotely by RF** — no on-board power source needed. Used in harsh environments (tire pressure monitoring, high-temperature industrial settings). - **Real-time, label-free detection:** No fluorescent labels or chemical tags required. - **CMOS compatibility:** Especially for AlN-based FBARs and ZnO SAW — integrates directly with IC processes. - **Wide dynamic range:** From gas-phase chemical sensing to bio-molecular interaction analysis (BIA). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive derivation policy in effect.** Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. No shortcuts, no skipped transitions. --- ## 3.1 Derivation of the Sauerbrey Equation (QCM Mass Loading) The Sauerbrey equation is the foundational result for QCM-based gravimetric sensing. We derive it from first principles. ### Starting Point: Standing-Wave Resonance Condition For a quartz plate of thickness $t_q$ oscillating in **thickness-shear mode**, the fundamental resonant frequency satisfies the boundary condition that an integer number of half-wavelengths fits in the plate: $$ t_q = \dfrac{n \lambda_q}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots $$ Since $\lambda_q = v_q / f_0$ where $v_q$ is the shear wave velocity in quartz: $$ t_q = \dfrac{n v_q}{2 f_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_0 = \dfrac{n v_q}{2 t_q} $$ For the **fundamental mode** ($n = 1$): $$ f_0 = \dfrac{v_q}{2 t_q} $$ ### Step 1: Frequency Sensitivity to Thickness Change A small mass addition $\Delta m$ distributed uniformly over electrode area $A$ acts as an equivalent quartz thickness change: $$ \Delta t_q = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho_q A} $$ Differentiating $f_0 = v_q / (2 t_q)$ with respect to $t_q$: $$ \dfrac{df_0}{dt_q} = -\dfrac{v_q}{2 t_q^2} $$ Therefore the frequency shift is: $$ \Delta f = \dfrac{df_0}{dt_q} \cdot \Delta t_q = -\dfrac{v_q}{2 t_q^2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho_q A} $$ ### Step 2: Substitute the Resonance Condition Back Using $f_0 = v_q / (2 t_q)$, we have $v_q = 2 f_0 t_q$. Substituting: $$ \Delta f = -\dfrac{2 f_0 t_q}{2 t_q^2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho_q A} = -\dfrac{f_0}{t_q} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho_q A} $$ Eliminate $t_q$ via $t_q = v_q / (2 f_0)$: $$ \Delta f = -\dfrac{f_0 \cdot 2 f_0}{v_q} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho_q A} = -\dfrac{2 f_0^2}{v_q \rho_q A} \cdot \Delta m $$ ### Step 3: Introduce Shear Wave Velocity For the shear mode, $v_q = \sqrt{\mu_q / \rho_q}$, hence $v_q \rho_q = \sqrt{\mu_q \rho_q}$: $$ \Delta f = -\dfrac{2 f_0^2}{\sqrt{\mu_q \rho_q} \cdot A} \cdot \Delta m $$ Rearranging yields the **Sauerbrey equation**: $$ \boxed{\;\Delta f = -\dfrac{2 f_0^2 \, \Delta m}{A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}}\;} $$ > **Sign Convention:** The negative sign indicates that mass loading **decreases** the resonant frequency (it acts as a heavier oscillator). This is the key reason QCMs are used as **microbalances**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the SAW Operating Frequency from IDT Geometry A uniform IDT has finger pairs spaced at half-wavelength intervals. For constructive interference of the launched waves from all $N$ finger pairs, the spatial periodicity must match the acoustic wavelength. ### Step 1: IDT Spatial Periodicity With finger width $a$ and gap $b$, the period of one finger pair is: $$ p = a + b $$ ### Step 2: Synchronous Frequency Condition The synchronous (Bragg) frequency at which constructive interference occurs is: $$ f_0 = \dfrac{v_R}{2p} $$ ### Step 3: Design for a Target Frequency For example, to design a SAW device at **433 MHz** on ST-cut quartz ($v_R \approx 3158$ m/s): $$ p = \dfrac{v_R}{2 f_0} = \dfrac{3158}{2 \times 433 \times 10^6} $$ $$ p = \dfrac{3158}{8.66 \times 10^8} = 3.646 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 3.65 \, \mu m $$ Hence the **finger width** $a = b = p/2 \approx 1.82 \, \mu m$. > **Interpretation:** Higher operating frequency demands finer lithography. This is why SAW device frequency is limited by the photolithographic resolution of the IDT fabrication process. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Mass Sensitivity for SAW Devices Consider a SAW delay line of center frequency $f_0$, wavelength $\lambda$, and length $L$. A thin mass film of density $\rho_f$ and thickness $h_f$ is deposited on the propagation path. The added areal mass density is $\Delta m = \rho_f h_f$. ### Step 1: Perturbation Theory Foundation A small perturbation of mass at the surface changes the wave's phase velocity. The fractional velocity change is, to first order: $$ \dfrac{\Delta v}{v_R} = -k_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{A} $$ where $k_s$ is a substrate-dependent sensitivity coefficient (units: m²/kg). ### Step 2: Velocity-to-Frequency Conversion For a fixed-IDT delay line, the wave transit time is $\tau = L / v_R$. The change in transit time: $$ \Delta \tau = -\dfrac{L \Delta v}{v_R^2} $$ This translates to a phase shift at the operating frequency: $$ \Delta \phi = 2\pi f_0 \cdot \Delta \tau = -\dfrac{2\pi f_0 L \Delta v}{v_R^2} $$ ### Step 3: Resonator Frequency Shift If the device is a resonator (oscillator loop), the phase condition is locked, so $f$ must adjust to maintain $\phi = 2\pi n$: $$ \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} = -\dfrac{\Delta v}{v_R} = k_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{A} $$ Hence the **mass sensitivity** of a SAW device: $$ S_m = \dfrac{\Delta f / f_0}{\Delta m / A} = k_s \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{kg}] $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** SAW mass sensitivity is **proportional to $f_0$** (since $k_s$ scales as $1/v_R$ and $f_0 = v_R / \lambda$ implies higher frequency = higher sensitivity). This is why modern SAW sensors push to **GHz** frequencies. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of QCM Frequency in the Presence of Viscoelastic Loading For non-rigid films (e.g., biological layers, polymers), the Sauerbrey equation must be extended. A viscoelastic load introduces both a mass component and a complex shear modulus contribution. The complex frequency shift is: $$ \dfrac{\Delta f^*}{f_0} = -\dfrac{Z_L}{\pi Z_q} $$ where: - $Z_q = \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}$ = acoustic impedance of quartz (real, lossless) - $Z_L = \sqrt{\rho_f G_f^*}$ = load impedance, with $G_f^* = G_f' + i G_f''$ the complex shear modulus Separating real and imaginary parts: $$ \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} = -\dfrac{1}{\pi Z_q} \sqrt{\rho_f} \left[ \sqrt{\vert G_f^* \vert + G_f''} \cdot \cos\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \right] $$ $$ \dfrac{\Delta \Gamma}{f_0} = -\dfrac{1}{\pi Z_q} \sqrt{\rho_f} \left[ \sqrt{\vert G_f^* \vert - G_f''} \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \right] $$ where $\theta = \arctan(G_f'' / G_f')$. > **Engineering takeaway:** In KTU-style problems, if the loading is described as "rigid" or "thin and elastic", use the Sauerbrey equation directly. If "viscoelastic" is mentioned, the full complex formalism applies. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A 5 MHz AT-cut quartz crystal is used as a QCM with electrode area $A = 0.20 \text{ cm}^2$. A rigid thin film of mass $2.0 \, \mu g$ is deposited uniformly. Calculate the frequency shift. Given $\rho_q = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_q = 2.947 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^2$. **Solution:** Step 1: Compute $\sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}$: $$ \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q} = \sqrt{2650 \times 2.947 \times 10^{10}} = \sqrt{7.81 \times 10^{13}} = 8.84 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \text{·s)} $$ Step 2: Apply the Sauerbrey equation: $$ \Delta f = -\dfrac{2 f_0^2 \, \Delta m}{A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} $$ $$ \Delta f = -\dfrac{2 \times (5 \times 10^6)^2 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-9}}{0.20 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.84 \times 10^6} $$ Step 3: Simplify numerator: $$ 2 \times 25 \times 10^{12} \times 2.0 \times 10^{-9} = 100 \times 10^3 = 1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ kg·Hz/m}^2 $$ Step 4: Simplify denominator: $$ 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 8.84 \times 10^6 = 176.8 \text{ kg/m}^2 \text{ (numerically)} $$ Step 5: Compute: $$ \Delta f = -\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{176.8} \approx -565.6 \text{ Hz} $$ **Final Answer:** $\Delta f \approx -566$ Hz (negative sign = frequency drop due to mass loading). > [!TIP] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show units at every step. Examiners award partial credit for unit consistency even if the final number is slightly off. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SAW Delay-Line Sensor — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR src[RF Signal Source
f0 = 100 MHz to 2.5 GHz] --> idtIn subgraph piezoelectric[PIEZOELECTRIC SUBSTRATE LiNbO3 or Quartz] direction LR idtIn[Input IDT
N finger pairs] --> propPath[Propagation Path
Length L, Aperture W] propPath --> idtOut[Output IDT
N finger pairs] end idtOut --> det[Output Detector
Phase or Amplitude] det --> out[Frequency Shift Delta f
or Phase Shift Delta phi] sensing[Analyte / Sensing Layer
Mass, Gas, Bio-molecule] -.->|perturbs| propPath piezo[Inverse Piezoelectric Effect] -.->|generates SAW| idtIn piezo2[Direct Piezoelectric Effect] -.->|recovers voltage| idtOut ``` ## 4.2 QCM / BAW Sensor — Cross-Sectional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB top[Top Electrode
Au or Pt, thickness 100 nm] --> piezo[Quartz Disc AT cut
thickness tq, shear mode] piezo --> bot[Bottom Electrode
Au or Pt] load[Mass Load or Viscoelastic Film
Delta m] -->|coats| top osc[Oscillator Circuit] -->|drives at f0| top bot -->|returns signal| osc freq[Frequency Counter
reads f0 minus Delta f] --> osc ``` ## 4.3 FBAR Sensor — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB top[Top Metal Electrode Mo or Al] --> film[AlN Piezoelectric Film
thickness ~ 1 to 2 micrometer] film --> bot[Bottom Metal Electrode Mo or Al] air[Air Cavity or Acoustic Bragg Mirror] -->|acoustically isolates| bot sig[RF Input 1 to 10 GHz] --> top bot --> out[Resonance Peak f_series and f_parallel] load[Analyte Bound to Top Surface] -->|mass loading| top ``` ## 4.4 Design-Parameter Interdependence Map ```mermaid flowchart TD lambda[Acoustic Wavelength lambda] -->|defines| pitch[IDT Finger Pitch p equals lambda over 2] lambda --> freq[Operating Frequency f0 equals vR over lambda] freq --> sens[Mass Sensitivity Sm
rises with f0] Npairs[Number of Finger Pairs N] -->|controls| bw[Bandwidth Delta f equals f0 over N] Npairs -->|controls| IL[Insertion Loss IL in dB] W[Aperture Width W] -->|controls| beam[Acoustic Beam Width] W -->|controls| R[Beam Steering Distortion] vR[Substrate Velocity vR] -->|set by| mat[Material LiNbO3 Quartz ZnO AlN] mat --> k2[Electromechanical Coupling k2] k2 -->|determines| eff[Conversion Efficiency and IL] ``` ## 4.5 Sensor Operating Mode Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Choose Acoustic Wave Sensor] --> B{Analyte Type?} B -->|Gas or Vapor| C[SAW with Polymer Coating] B -->|Bio-molecule| D[Love Wave or QCM with Gold] B -->|Mass at pg level| E[FBAR or High Freq SAW] B -->|Liquid Phase| F[SH SAW or QCM] C --> G{Operating Frequency?} G -->|< 500 MHz| H[Standard Photolithography OK] G -->|> 1 GHz| I[E-beam or Nanoimprint Required] D --> J{High Q Required?} J -->|Yes| K[SAW Resonator] J -->|No| L[SAW Delay Line] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `idtIn`, `piezo`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels are plain uppercase text to ensure clean rendering. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAET864]` **Define the term "piezoelectric coupling coefficient $k^2$" and state its typical range for common SAW substrate materials.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The electromechanical coupling coefficient $k^2$ is a dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the efficiency of energy conversion between electrical and mechanical (acoustic) domains in a piezoelectric material. It is defined as: $$ k^2 = \dfrac{U_{\text{mech}}}{U_{\text{elec}}} = \dfrac{e^2}{c^E \cdot \varepsilon^S} $$ where $e$ is the piezoelectric stress constant, $c^E$ is the elastic stiffness at constant electric field, and $\varepsilon^S$ is the permittivity at constant strain. Typical values: - **Quartz (ST-cut):** $\approx 0.16\%$ - **LiNbO₃ (Y-Z cut):** $\approx 4.5\%$ - **LiTaO₃ (Y-X cut):** $\approx 0.7\%$ - **ZnO / AlN thin films:** $\approx 1\% - 8\%$ (process dependent) *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical range)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAET864]` **Compare SAW and BAW sensors in terms of (a) wave propagation medium, (b) typical operating frequency, and (c) one application each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | SAW | BAW | |---------|-----|-----| | (a) Propagation medium | Energy confined to ~1 wavelength of surface | Energy propagates through entire substrate thickness | | (b) Typical frequency | 10 MHz – 3 GHz | 1 – 10 GHz (FBAR), 5 – 20 MHz (QCM) | | (c) Application | Wireless tire pressure sensors; chemical gas sensing | Film bulk filters in RF front-ends; QCM in biosensing | *(1 mark per row)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAET864, Module 3]` **Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) sensor. Describe the role of Interdigital Transducers (IDTs). **Part (b) [7 marks]:** A SAW delay-line sensor is fabricated on Y-Z LiNbO₃ ($v_R = 3488$ m/s) to detect a chemical vapor. The IDT has 50 finger pairs with finger pitch $p = 4 \mu m$. If a thin polymer film of mass $2 \mu g$ is adsorbed uniformly over a sensing area of $4 \text{ mm}^2$ and the device's mass sensitivity coefficient $k_s = 1.4 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$, calculate the expected fractional frequency shift. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Construction and Working of SAW Sensor (7 marks):** 1. **Construction** *(3 marks)*: - The sensor consists of a **piezoelectric substrate** (e.g., LiNbO₃, quartz, GaAs) on which two metallic Interdigital Transducers (IDTs) are photolithographically patterned. - Each IDT is composed of two interleaved comb-like metal electrodes, with finger width $a$ and gap $b$ such that the period $p = a + b = \lambda/2$. - Between the input and output IDTs lies a **propagation path / delay line** of length $L$ and aperture $W$. - A **sensitive coating** (e.g., polymer, antibody, or metallic film) is applied on the propagation path to selectively interact with the target analyte. - Optional reflectors form a **SAW resonator** configuration. 2. **Working Principle** *(3 marks)*: - An RF signal at frequency $f_0 = v_R / (2p)$ is applied to the input IDT. - The spatially periodic electric field of the IDT, via the **inverse piezoelectric effect**, excites a Rayleigh surface wave that propagates along the substrate surface. - The wave interacts with the sensitive coating and any adsorbed analyte, causing a perturbation in **wave velocity** and/or **amplitude**. - The perturbed wave reaches the output IDT, where the **direct piezoelectric effect** regenerates an electrical signal. - The **frequency shift $\Delta f$** (or amplitude / phase change) is proportional to the mass / property change of the analyte. 3. **Role of IDTs** *(1 mark)*: - IDTs serve a **dual function**: they are the **actuators** (launching SAWs via inverse piezoelectricity) and the **detectors** (converting SAWs back to electrical signals). Their geometry (period, number of pairs, aperture) sets the **operating frequency**, **bandwidth**, and **insertion loss** of the device. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Operating frequency of the SAW device: $$ f_0 = \dfrac{v_R}{2p} = \dfrac{3488}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{3488}{8 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ f_0 = 4.36 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz} = 436 \text{ MHz} $$ **Step 2:** Areal mass density of the adsorbed film: $$ \dfrac{\Delta m}{A} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg}}{4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^2 $$ **Step 3:** Apply the SAW mass sensitivity relation: $$ \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} = k_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta m}{A} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute: $$ \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} = 1.4 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 7.0 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 5:** Compute the absolute frequency shift: $$ \Delta f = 7.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 436 \times 10^6 = 3.05 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta f \approx 305 \text{ kHz}} $$ **Step 6 (Optional Interpretation):** For a typical SAW oscillator with $Q = 1000$, the minimum resolvable frequency is $\sim f_0/Q = 436$ kHz. Hence the sensor can **easily resolve** this analyte mass — confirming its viability for trace chemical detection. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAET864, Module 3]` **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the Sauerbrey equation for a Quartz Crystal Microbalance. State clearly the assumptions under which the equation is valid. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** A 10 MHz QCM with electrode area $A = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2$ registers a frequency drop of $\Delta f = 750 \text{ Hz}$ when exposed to a gas that adsorbs a thin rigid film on the electrode. Use the Sauerbrey equation to determine the mass deposited. Given $\rho_q = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\mu_q = 2.947 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^2$. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Derivation of the Sauerbrey Equation (7 marks):** *(Full derivation reproduced below for student reference — see Section 3.1 for the line-by-line version.)* The Sauerbrey equation describes the frequency shift $\Delta f$ of a quartz crystal microbalance due to a thin rigid mass layer: $$ \Delta f = -\dfrac{2 f_0^2 \, \Delta m}{A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}} $$ **Derivation outline (7 marks distributed as below):** 1. Starting from the resonance condition $f_0 = v_q / (2 t_q)$ *(1 mark)*. 2. Differentiating with respect to $t_q$: $df_0/dt_q = -v_q / (2t_q^2)$ *(1 mark)*. 3. Expressing mass addition as equivalent thickness: $\Delta t_q = \Delta m / (\rho_q A)$ *(1 mark)*. 4. Combining: $\Delta f = -f_0 \cdot \Delta m / (t_q \rho_q A)$ *(1 mark)*. 5. Substituting $t_q = v_q / (2f_0)$: $\Delta f = -2 f_0^2 \cdot \Delta m / (v_q \rho_q A)$ *(1 mark)*. 6. Using $v_q = \sqrt{\mu_q/\rho_q}$ to obtain the final Sauerbrey form *(1 mark)*. 7. **Assumptions** *(1 mark)*: (i) Mass is rigidly coupled to the oscillator (no slip); (ii) The film is thin compared to quartz thickness ($h_f \ll t_q$); (iii) The film's elastic properties are negligible; (iv) Uniform mass distribution over the electrode. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Compute $\sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}$: $$ \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q} = \sqrt{2650 \times 2.947 \times 10^{10}} = 8.84 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2 \text{·s)} $$ **Step 2:** Rearrange the Sauerbrey equation for $\Delta m$: $$ \Delta m = -\dfrac{\Delta f \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q}}{2 f_0^2} $$ **Step 3:** Insert values (using $|\Delta f|$ since mass is positive): $$ \Delta m = \dfrac{750 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.84 \times 10^6}{2 \times (10 \times 10^6)^2} $$ **Step 4:** Compute numerator: $$ 750 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 8.84 \times 10^6 = 750 \times 442 = 3.315 \times 10^5 $$ **Step 5:** Compute denominator: $$ 2 \times 10^{14} $$ **Step 6:** Final calculation: $$ \Delta m = \dfrac{3.315 \times 10^5}{2 \times 10^{14}} = 1.66 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta m \approx 1.66 \, \mu g} $$ **Step 7 (Sanity Check):** The areal mass density is $\Delta m / A = 1.66 \times 10^{-9} / 5 \times 10^{-5} = 3.32 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m}^2 = 33.2 \text{ ng/cm}^2$, which is well within the detection range of a QCM (typical limit ~ 1 ng/cm²). ✓ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign errors:** Mass loading always produces a *negative* $\Delta f$. Forgetting the minus sign costs 1 mark. > 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing cm² with m², or kHz with Hz, is a frequent 0.5-mark deduction. > 3. **IDT period confusion:** Writing $p = \lambda$ instead of $p = \lambda/2$ will propagate an error through the entire frequency calculation. > 4. **Wrong quartz constant:** Some students use $2.65 \times 10^3$ vs $2.65 \times 10^{-3}$. Always write the unit explicitly. > 5. **Skipping the Sauerbrey assumptions:** Even if the derivation is mathematically correct, omitting the validity conditions loses the final "understanding" mark. > 6. **Mixing SAW and QCM formulas:** SAW sensitivity uses $k_s \cdot \Delta m / A$, not the Sauerbrey form. The two are distinct. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Acoustic wave sensors** convert perturbations in mechanical wave propagation into electrical signals via the **piezoelectric effect**. - ✅ **Two principal families:** **SAW** (surface-confined Rayleigh or shear-horizontal waves) and **BAW** (thickness-mode vibrations through the substrate). - ✅ **Core piezoelectric equations** couple stress, strain, electric field, and electric displacement via the $e$, $c^E$, $\varepsilon^S$ tensors. - ✅ **SAW key relation:** $f_0 = v_R / \lambda$, with IDT pitch $p = \lambda/2$. - ✅ **QCM key relation:** $f_0 = v_q / (2 t_q)$. - ✅ **Sauerbrey equation:** $\Delta f = -2 f_0^2 \Delta m / (A \sqrt{\rho_q \mu_q})$ — valid for **rigid, thin, uniform** mass layers. - ✅ **Mass sensitivity scales with $f_0$:** Higher frequency sensors are more sensitive — drives GHz-scale FBAR/SAW design. - ✅ **Coupling coefficient $k^2$** determines the efficiency of electromechanical energy conversion; LiNbO₃ is high-coupling, quartz is low-coupling but temperature-stable. - ✅ **IDT design parameters:** Number of finger pairs $N$ sets bandwidth $\Delta f_{\text{device}} = f_0 / N$; aperture $W$ controls acoustic beam width; finger pitch $p$ sets $f_0$. - ✅ **SAW devices are wireless-readable** — uniquely useful for passive, harsh-environment sensing. - ✅ **Viscoelastic loading** requires the complex frequency-shift formalism, not the Sauerbrey equation. - ✅ **Applications include:** gas sensing, humidity sensing, biosensing, pressure sensing, temperature sensing, mass loading analysis, RF filters (FBAR), tire pressure monitoring systems (TPMS), and chemical warfare agent detection. - ✅ **Material selection trade-off:** Higher $k^2$ (LiNbO₃) gives stronger transduction but worse temperature stability; quartz is preferred when thermal drift must be minimized.

topics.title: Resonant Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resonant Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Resonant Sensor** is a class of MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) transducer in which the measurand (the physical, chemical, or biological quantity being measured) is converted into a shift in the **natural resonant frequency** of a micro-mechanical vibrating structure. The structure is deliberately excited to oscillate at (or near) its fundamental mechanical resonance, and any change in the surrounding physical variable — such as mass, force, pressure, acceleration, temperature, or gas concentration — alters the effective stiffness, effective mass, or damping of the resonator, which in turn shifts the resonance frequency. This frequency shift is the primary output signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> "A resonant sensor is a frequency-output MEMS device in which a micro-mechanical element is driven into steady-state vibration at its natural frequency, and the input measurand is detected as a proportional change in that resonant frequency."

The natural frequency of a generic single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) mechanical resonator is governed by:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{eff}}{m_{eff}}}$$

where:
- $k_{eff}$ = effective mechanical stiffness of the structure (N/m)
- $m_{eff}$ = effective mass of the structure (kg)
- $f_0$ = natural (resonant) frequency in **Hz** (often in the kHz–MHz range for MEMS devices)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why frequency is the preferred output in MEMS:**
> Frequency is a quantity that can be measured with extreme precision (down to parts-per-billion) using digital counters, and it is **virtually immune to amplitude noise, power-supply drift, and electromagnetic interference**. This makes resonant sensors inherently more accurate and stable than their analog (voltage/current) counterparts.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Tuning Fork Analogy**
> Imagine striking a tuning fork. It produces a clean, fixed musical pitch (a precise frequency). Now, suppose tiny grains of sand are slowly sprinkled onto the prongs of the tuning fork. The pitch drops — the fork sounds lower. This is exactly how a **resonant mass sensor** works in MEMS: when analyte molecules adsorb onto a vibrating micro-beam, the added mass lowers the resonance frequency, and the magnitude of that drop tells us how much mass was deposited.
>
> **Another Analogy — The Guitar String:**
> If you tighten a guitar string (increase tension/stiffness), the pitch rises; if you make it heavier (more mass) or looser, the pitch falls. A resonant MEMS sensor behaves the same way — it is a "micro guitar string" whose pitch changes in response to the physical world around it.

## 1.3 Classification of Resonant Sensors

| Category | Measurand Detected | Typical Structure | Frequency Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass Sensor** | Adsorbed mass / molecules | Cantilever, beam | kHz – MHz |
| **Pressure Sensor** | Pressure / Force | Diaphragm + beam | kHz – hundreds of kHz |
| **Accelerometer** | Inertial acceleration | Seismic mass + beam | kHz |
| **Gas / Chemical Sensor** | Gas concentration (via mass loading of coating) | Coated resonator | kHz – MHz |
| **Temperature Sensor** | Thermal expansion / stiffness change | Beam, ring | kHz – MHz |
| **Viscosity / Density Sensor** | Damping / added mass in fluid | Vibrating beam in liquid | kHz |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants Used in Resonant Sensor Design

- Young's Modulus of Silicon (single crystal): **$E_{Si} \approx 169$ GPa** (along \<110\>)
- Density of Silicon: **$\rho_{Si} \approx 2330$ kg/m³**
- Quality factor in vacuum: typically **$Q = 10^3$ – $10^5$**
- Quality factor in air/liquid: typically **$Q = 10$ – $10^3$**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher Q → Higher Resolution.** The quality factor $Q$ quantifies how "sharp" the resonance peak is. A high-$Q$ resonator exhibits a narrow resonance peak, allowing tiny frequency shifts to be detected with high accuracy. This is the central reason why vacuum packaging is often used for high-performance resonant sensors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

A resonant sensor operates in a continuous four-stage loop. Understanding each stage is essential for both design and KTU exam answers.

1. **Excitation (Actuation):** An external energy source drives the mechanical structure into oscillation. Common excitation mechanisms include:
   - **Electrostatic** — applying an AC voltage between a fixed electrode and the moving structure (capacitive force).
   - **Piezoelectric** — applying an AC voltage across a piezo layer (e.g., AlN, ZnO, PZT) bonded to the resonator.
   - **Electromagnetic** — Lorentz force from current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.
   - **Thermal** — AC heating element causing differential thermal expansion.
   - **Optical** — pulsed laser or photothermal excitation (used in research-grade devices).

2. **Steady-State Resonance:** The drive frequency is locked to the natural frequency $f_0$ of the structure using a feedback loop (such as a **Phase-Locked Loop, PLL**). The structure then vibrates at maximum amplitude with minimal drive power.

3. **Perturbation by Measurand:** The physical input (mass, force, pressure, etc.) alters $k_{eff}$ and/or $m_{eff}$ and/or the damping coefficient $c$, shifting the natural frequency to a new value $f_0'$.

4. **Detection (Readout):** The shift $\Delta f = f_0' - f_0$ is measured using:
   - Capacitive (change in capacitance as the structure moves).
   - Piezoelectric (charge generated by strain).
   - Piezoresistive (change in resistance due to strain).
   - Optical (interferometric or diffraction-based).
   - Electromagnetic (induced EMF / eddy current).

## 2.2 Why Frequency Output is Superior

- **Quasi-digital:** Frequency is intrinsically a digital quantity — no ADC is required at the front end.
- **Immune to amplitude noise:** The information is carried in *frequency*, not in voltage level.
- **High resolution:** Crystal oscillators and digital counters can resolve sub-Hz shifts.
- **Telemetric & remote-readable:** Frequency signals travel long distances without degradation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table — these are the most-tested equations in KTU ESE for Module 3.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural frequency (SDOF) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_{eff}}{m_{eff}}}$ | Hz | Foundation equation |
| 2 | Natural frequency (Euler-Bernoulli beam) | $f_n = \dfrac{\beta_n^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$ | Hz | $\beta_1 = 4.730$ (1st mode, cantilever) |
| 3 | Natural frequency (cantilever) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}} \approx 0.162 \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}\dfrac{t}{L^2}$ | Hz | $t$ = thickness, $L$ = length |
| 4 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f_{3dB}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k m}}{c}$ | dimensionless | $\Delta f_{3dB}$ = bandwidth at half-power |
| 5 | Frequency shift due to added mass | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx -\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}}$ | dimensionless | Linear approximation, valid for $\Delta m \ll m$ |
| 6 | Frequency shift due to axial stress (beam) | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx \dfrac{\sigma L^2}{4 E t^2}$ | dimensionless | For compressive/tensile axial load |
| 7 | Mass sensitivity (cantilever) | $S_m = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial m} = -\dfrac{f_0}{2 m_{eff}}$ | Hz/kg | Linear in $f_0$ |
| 8 | Pressure-induced stress on diaphragm | $\sigma = \dfrac{3 P r^2}{4 t^2}$ (clamped edge) | Pa | $r$ = diaphragm radius |
| 9 | Resonator figure of merit | $FOM = Q \cdot S_m$ | Hz/kg | Higher = better |
| 10 | Damping coefficient (Squeeze-film) | $c = \dfrac{\mu A_{eff}}{d^3}$ | N·s/m | $\mu$ = viscosity, $d$ = gap |
| 11 | Pull-in voltage (electrostatic) | $V_{pi} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | V | Sets upper drive limit |
| 12 | Eigenvalue coefficient (1st mode) | $\beta_1 L = 1.875$ | — | Cantilever |
| 13 | Eigenvalue coefficient (1st mode) | $\beta_1 L = 4.730$ | — | Clamped–clamped beam |
| 14 | Eigenvalue coefficient (1st mode) | $\beta_1 L = 3.927$ | — | Free–free beam |
| 15 | Resonant sensor sensitivity (general) | $S = \dfrac{\partial f_0}{\partial x}$ | Hz/unit | $x$ = measurand |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always write $f_0$ in **Hz** and convert angular frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ correctly. Many students lose 1–2 marks by mixing up $\omega$ and $f$ in the natural frequency equation.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

- **Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM):** The most commercially successful resonant sensor — used for thin-film thickness monitoring and biosensing.
- **MEMS Gyroscopes** (e.g., Analog Devices ADXRS): Vibrating ring or tuning-fork structure senses Coriolis force.
- **Inkjet print-heads** (HP, Canon): Piezoelectric resonant diaphragms eject ink drops.
- **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM):** Micro-cantilevers operate in resonant mode for imaging.
- **Gas chromatography detectors** and **biosensor arrays.**
- **MEMS microphones and ultrasonic transducers.**

> [!NOTE]
> The same physical structure can serve as an **actuator** (e.g., RF MEMS switch, micropump) or as a **sensor**, depending on whether the resonance is the input or the output. In a resonant sensor, the *physical measurand* perturbs the resonance; in a resonant actuator, an *electrical signal* drives the resonance to produce motion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exam-grade** — every algebraic step is shown. Practice reproducing them on paper for full marks.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Natural Frequency of an Euler–Bernoulli Cantilever Beam

**Starting assumption:** A uniform cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, density $\rho$, and Young's modulus $E$ oscillates in its fundamental flexural mode.

**Step 1 — Write the Euler–Bernoulli equation of motion:**

$$\rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} + EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} = 0$$

where:
- $w(x,t)$ is the transverse deflection.
- $A = w \cdot t$ is the cross-sectional area.
- $I = \dfrac{wt^3}{12}$ is the area moment of inertia.

**Step 2 — Assume harmonic (steady-state) solution:**

$$w(x,t) = W(x) e^{j\omega t}$$

Substitute into the PDE. The time derivative becomes $(j\omega)^2 = -\omega^2$. Thus:

$$-\rho A \omega^2 W(x) + EI \frac{d^4 W(x)}{dx^4} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into the standard 4th-order ODE:**

$$\frac{d^4 W(x)}{dx^4} - \beta^4 W(x) = 0, \quad \text{where} \quad \beta^4 = \frac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$$

**Step 4 — Write the general solution:**

$$W(x) = C_1 \cos(\beta x) + C_2 \sin(\beta x) + C_3 \cosh(\beta x) + C_4 \sinh(\beta x)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the cantilever boundary conditions:**

- At fixed end ($x = 0$): $W(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 + C_3 = 0$
- At fixed end ($x = 0$): $\dfrac{dW}{dx}\Big|_{x=0} = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 + C_4 = 0$
- At free end ($x = L$): bending moment = 0 $\Rightarrow \dfrac{d^2 W}{dx^2}\Big|_{x=L} = 0$
- At free end ($x = L$): shear force = 0 $\Rightarrow \dfrac{d^3 W}{dx^3}\Big|_{x=L} = 0$

**Step 6 — Apply the two free-end conditions, which together yield the transcendental eigenvalue equation:**

$$\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$$

**Step 7 — The smallest positive root of this transcendental equation is:**

$$\beta_1 L = 1.8751049 \quad \text{(universally, the first cantilever eigenvalue)}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the natural angular frequency $\omega_0$ using the definition of $\beta$:**

$$\omega_0 = \beta_1^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to natural frequency $f_0$ (in Hz):**

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\beta_1^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute the explicit cross-section terms $A = wt$ and $I = wt^3/12$:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot (wt^3/12)}{\rho \cdot (wt)}} = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{12 \rho}}$$

**Step 11 — Final compact cantilever resonance formula:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{12 \rho}} \approx 0.1615 \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical MEMS formula**. The first inverse-square dependence on length ($1/L^2$) and linear dependence on thickness ($t$) are favorite KTU viva questions: *"How would you double the resonant frequency of a cantilever?"* → Halve $L$ and multiply $L$ by $1/\sqrt{2}$ (or double $t$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency Shift Due to Mass Loading

**Starting assumption:** A small added mass $\Delta m$ is deposited at the position of maximum deflection (typically the free end of a cantilever or the antinode of a clamped-clamped beam). The original effective mass of the resonator is $m_{eff}$.

**Step 1 — Write the original natural frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{eff}}{m_{eff}}}$$

**Step 2 — Write the natural frequency after the mass is added:**

$$f_0' = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{eff}}{m_{eff} + \Delta m}}$$

(We assume $k_{eff}$ is unchanged — valid for small $\Delta m$.)

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $f_0'/f_0$:**

$$\frac{f_0'}{f_0} = \sqrt{\frac{m_{eff}}{m_{eff} + \Delta m}} = \left(1 + \frac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}}\right)^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the binomial expansion** $(1 + x)^{-1/2} \approx 1 - \dfrac{x}{2} + \dfrac{3x^2}{8} - \ldots$ for $|x| \ll 1$:

$$\frac{f_0'}{f_0} \approx 1 - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}} + \frac{3}{8}\left(\frac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}}\right)^2 - \ldots$$

**Step 5 — The fractional frequency shift is therefore:**

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_0} = \frac{f_0' - f_0}{f_0} \approx -\frac{1}{2}\frac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to obtain the added mass from the measured shift:**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta m \approx -\frac{2 m_{eff}}{f_0} \Delta f\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** A *decrease* in frequency ($\Delta f < 0$) corresponds to a *positive* added mass ($\Delta m > 0$) — consistent with the tuning-fork intuition. The negative sign is the heart of the mass-sensing principle.

**Step 7 — Sensitivity (in Hz/kg):**

$$S_m = \frac{\partial f_0}{\partial \Delta m} = -\frac{f_0}{2 m_{eff}} \quad [\text{Hz/kg}]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Design takeaway:** To maximize mass sensitivity, the resonator must be *light* (small $m_{eff}$) and have a *high* initial $f_0$. This is why nano-scale cantilevers and NEMS (Nano-Electro-Mechanical Systems) resonators are now the state of the art for single-molecule and even single-atom mass detection.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frequency Shift Due to Axial Stress (Force / Pressure Sensing)

**Starting assumption:** A clamped–clamped beam of length $L$, thickness $t$, width $w$, density $\rho$, Young's modulus $E$ is subjected to a uniform axial stress $\sigma$ (positive in tension, negative in compression).

**Step 1 — Modified Euler–Bernoulli equation with axial load:**

$$EI \frac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} + \sigma A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial x^2} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Assume harmonic solution $w(x,t) = W(x) e^{j\omega t}$. Substitute:**

$$\frac{d^4 W}{dx^4} + \frac{\sigma A}{EI}\frac{d^2 W}{dx^4\,?}\ldots \rightarrow \text{After substitution: } \frac{d^4 W}{dx^4} + \frac{\sigma A}{EI}\frac{d^2 W}{dx^2} - \frac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI} W = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mathematical structure yields a transcendental eigenvalue equation. For small axial stress ($\vert\sigma\vert \ll \sigma_{cr}$), a perturbation/series expansion gives the well-known closed-form result below.

**Step 3 — Standard result (perturbation solution, clamped-clamped beam):**

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx \frac{\sigma L^2}{4 E t^2}$$

**Step 4 — For a pressure sensor:** the axial stress is generated by a diaphragm coupled to the beam. For a clamped circular diaphragm of radius $r$ and thickness $t_d$ subjected to differential pressure $P$:

$$\sigma = \frac{3 P r^2}{4 t_d^2} \quad \text{(maximum stress at the edge)}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting, the fractional frequency shift becomes:**

$$\frac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx \frac{3 P r^2 L^2}{16 E t^2 t_d^2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign convention:** Tensile stress *raises* the frequency; compressive stress *lowers* it. The buckling limit $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 E I}{A L^2}$ must not be exceeded for stable operation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Quality Factor and Frequency Resolution

**Starting assumption:** The resonator is modelled as a damped SDOF system:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 1 — Steady-state amplitude near resonance:**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{F_0/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_0^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega\omega_0)^2}}$$

where $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{1}{2Q}$ is the damping ratio.

**Step 2 — The half-power (3 dB) bandwidth is the frequency range over which the amplitude is at least $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its peak value:**

$$\Delta f_{3dB} = \frac{f_0}{Q}$$

**Step 3 — The smallest detectable frequency shift (resolution) is set by the linewidth of the resonance peak itself:**

$$\delta f_{min} \approx \frac{f_0}{Q} \quad \text{(for SNR} = 1\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A higher $Q$ yields a narrower peak and thus a *smaller detectable* $\Delta f$. Practical MEMS pressure sensors target $Q > 10{,}000$ in vacuum to resolve sub-pascal pressure changes.

---

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Measurand | Mechanism | Governing Equation | Direction of $\Delta f$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Added mass $\Delta m$ | Inertia increase | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx -\dfrac{\Delta m}{2 m_{eff}}$ | **Down** |
| Axial tension $\sigma > 0$ | Stiffness increase | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx +\dfrac{\sigma L^2}{4 E t^2}$ | **Up** |
| Axial compression $\sigma < 0$ | Stiffness decrease | Same as above, negative sign | **Down** |
| Temperature rise $\Delta T$ | $E(T)$ decreases, $\rho$ decreases | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta E}{E} - \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\right)$ | Depends on material |
| Fluid viscosity $\mu$ | Damping increase | $Q \propto \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Peak broadens, $\Delta f \to 0$ |
| Force $F$ | Stress on beam | $\sigma = F/A$ | Up (tension) |
| Acceleration $a$ | Inertial force on seismic mass | $F = m_{s} a$ | Up or down with sign of $a$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Resonant Sensor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Measurand Input] --> B[MEMS Resonator Structure]
    C[Actuation Signal Generator AC Voltage] --> D[Actuator Electrostatic or Piezo or Thermal]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Mechanical Vibration at f0]
    E --> F[Readout Transducer Capacitive or Piezo or Piezoresistive]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    G --> H[Phase Locked Loop PLL]
    H --> I[Frequency Counter]
    I --> J[Digital Output Delta f]
    H --> D
    J --> K[Microprocessor or Display Unit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **PLL feedback path** (from H back to D) is the key closed-loop element. It tracks the resonance frequency continuously and forces the drive to follow any shift, producing a real-time frequency-output sensor.

## 4.2 Cantilever Resonant Sensor — Cross-Sectional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Substrate
        SUB[Silicon Substrate Handle Wafer]
    end
    subgraph Anchor
        ANC[Fixed Anchor Region]
    end
    subgraph Beam
        BML[Micro Cantilever Beam Length L Thickness t Width w]
        CMT[Optional Sensing Coating for Mass or Gas Detection]
    end
    subgraph Actuation
        ACT[Drive Electrode Below Beam AC Voltage Applied]
    end
    subgraph Readout
        RDT[Readout Electrode or Piezo Layer Capacitance Change Measured]
    end
    SUB --- ANC
    ANC --- BML
    BML --- CMT
    ACT --- BML
    BML --- RDT
```

## 4.3 Operating Sequence Flow (Frequency-Tracking Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Apply AC Drive at Estimated f0] --> S2[Measure Vibration Amplitude]
    S2 --> S3[Compare with Reference Phase 90 degrees]
    S3 --> S4{Resonance Locked}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Adjust Drive Frequency Up or Down]
    S5 --> S1
    S4 -- Yes --> S6[Read Locked Frequency f0 prime]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Delta f = f0 prime minus f0]
    S7 --> S8[Convert Delta f to Measurand Using Sensitivity Coefficient]
```

## 4.4 Functional Comparison of Three Resonator Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cantilever
        C1[One End Fixed] --> C2[Other End Free] --> C3[Maximum Deflection at Tip] --> C4[Highest Mass Sensitivity] --> C5[Lowest Q Typically]
    end
    subgraph Clamped Clamped Beam
        B1[Both Ends Fixed] --> B2[Maximum Strain at Mid Span] --> B3[Best for Axial Stress Sensors] --> B4[Higher Q than Cantilever]
    end
    subgraph Diaphragm
        D1[Edges Clamped] --> D2[Pressure Deflects Center] --> D3[Tension Increases Frequency] --> D4[Best for Pressure Sensors] --> D5[Lowest Q Among the Three]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Design rule of thumb:**
> - Want **mass sensitivity** → use **cantilever** (large tip deflection).
> - Want **pressure/force sensitivity** → use **diaphragm + clamped-clamped beam**.
> - Want **best Q-factor and stability** → use **ring or disk resonator** (e.g., wine-glass mode gyroscope).

## 4.5 Excitation & Detection Mechanism Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACT1[Electrostatic] --- ACT2[Piezoelectric] --- ACT3[Thermal] --- ACT4[Electromagnetic] --- ACT5[Optical]
    DET1[Capacitive] --- DET2[Piezoelectric] --- DET3[Piezoresistive] --- DET4[Optical Interferometric] --- DET5[Electromagnetic EMF]
    ACT1 --- DET1
    ACT2 --- DET2
    ACT4 --- DET5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **most common IC-compatible pairing** in silicon MEMS is **electrostatic actuation + capacitive detection** (monolithic, low power, CMOS-compatible). The **highest-sensitivity pairing** for research applications is **photothermal excitation + optical interferometric detection**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Define a resonant sensor. Why is frequency chosen as the output parameter in MEMS resonant sensors? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **resonant sensor** is a MEMS device in which the input measurand (mass, force, pressure, etc.) is converted into a measurable shift in the natural resonant frequency of a micro-mechanical structure.

Frequency is preferred as the output because:

1. **It is a quasi-digital quantity** — it can be measured with extreme precision using digital counters without requiring a high-resolution ADC.
2. **It is immune to amplitude noise, power-supply variations, and electromagnetic interference** — the information is encoded in *time*, not voltage level.
3. **Frequency signals can be transmitted over long distances** without degradation, enabling remote and telemetric sensing.
4. **High resolution** — sub-Hz frequency shifts are easily resolved when the resonator has a high quality factor $Q$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**List and briefly explain any three actuation mechanisms used to excite a MEMS resonator. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Electrostatic actuation:** An AC voltage applied between a fixed electrode and the movable structure produces a time-varying Coulomb force $F = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} V^2$ that drives the structure into vibration. CMOS-compatible and low-power.

2. **Piezoelectric actuation:** An AC voltage across a piezoelectric thin film (AlN, ZnO, PZT) deposited on the resonator generates strain via the converse piezoelectric effect, driving the structure. High coupling efficiency at MHz frequencies.

3. **Thermal (electrothermal) actuation:** A periodic current through a resistive element causes Joule heating and differential thermal expansion, producing a mechanical displacement. Produces large forces but lower bandwidth and higher power consumption.

*(Alternative: Electromagnetic actuation — Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the fundamental natural frequency of a uniform **Euler–Bernoulli cantilever beam** of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, density $\rho$, and Young's modulus $E$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A silicon cantilever (Young's modulus $E = 169$ GPa, density $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³) has length $L = 200 \,\mu\text{m}$, width $w = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$, and thickness $t = 5 \,\mu\text{m}$. Calculate its fundamental resonant frequency. If a mass of $\Delta m = 10$ pg is deposited at the tip, calculate the resulting frequency shift. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1.** Euler–Bernoulli equation of motion:
$$\rho A \dfrac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} + EI \dfrac{\partial^4 w(x,t)}{\partial x^4} = 0$$

**Step 2.** Assume harmonic solution $w(x,t) = W(x) e^{j\omega t}$. Substitution gives:
$$\dfrac{d^4 W}{dx^4} - \beta^4 W = 0, \quad \beta^4 = \dfrac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$$

**Step 3.** General solution:
$$W(x) = C_1 \cos \beta x + C_2 \sin \beta x + C_3 \cosh \beta x + C_4 \sinh \beta x$$

**Step 4.** Cantilever boundary conditions yield the transcendental equation:
$$\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$$

**Step 5.** Smallest positive root: $\beta_1 L = 1.875$.

**Step 6.** Natural frequency:
$$f_0 = \dfrac{\beta_1^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A}} = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{12 \rho}}$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1.** Compute the geometric factors:
- Cross-sectional area: $A = w \cdot t = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2$
- Moment of inertia: $I = \dfrac{wt^3}{12} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = 5.208 \times 10^{-22} \text{ m}^4$

**Step 2.** Compute the term $\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A}}$:
$$\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{169 \times 10^9 \times 5.208 \times 10^{-22}}{2330 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-10}}}$$

**Numerator:** $169 \times 10^9 \times 5.208 \times 10^{-22} = 8.802 \times 10^{-11}$

**Denominator:** $2330 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-10} = 5.825 \times 10^{-7}$

**Ratio:** $\dfrac{8.802 \times 10^{-11}}{5.825 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.511 \times 10^{-4}$

**Square root:** $\sqrt{1.511 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01229$ rad·s/m units simplify to $1/\text{s}$ scale → frequency in Hz:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \times 0.01229 = \dfrac{3.516}{6.283} \times 0.01229 \times 10^?$$

*Recomputing carefully using direct formula:*

$$f_0 \approx 0.1615 \cdot \dfrac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}} = 0.1615 \cdot \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{(200 \times 10^{-6})^2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{169 \times 10^9}{2330}}$$

$$\dfrac{t}{L^2} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-8}} = 125 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{169 \times 10^9}{2330}} = \sqrt{7.253 \times 10^7} = 8516 \text{ m/s}$$

$$f_0 = 0.1615 \times 125 \times 8516 = 171.9 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3.** Effective mass of a uniform cantilever (1st mode): $m_{eff} = 0.24 \, \rho A L = 0.24 \times 2330 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-10} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}$

$$m_{eff} = 0.24 \times 2330 \times 5 \times 10^{-14} = 2.796 \times 10^{-11} \text{ kg} \approx 28 \text{ pg}$$

**Step 4.** Frequency shift due to added mass $\Delta m = 10$ pg:

$$\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx -\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{2.796 \times 10^{-11}} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.3577 = -0.1789$$

$$\Delta f = -0.1789 \times 171{,}900 \approx -30{,}750 \text{ Hz} \approx -30.75 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note:** The fractional mass loading is $\Delta m / m_{eff} \approx 0.36$, which **violates the small-perturbation assumption** $\Delta m \ll m_{eff}$. The exact nonlinear formula should be used:
> $$f_0' = f_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{eff}}{m_{eff} + \Delta m}} = 171.9 \text{ kHz} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{28}{38}} = 171.9 \times 0.858 = 147.5 \text{ kHz}$$
> $$\Delta f = 147.5 - 171.9 = -24.4 \text{ kHz}$$
> **Examiner's note:** Always state the small-perturbation assumption explicitly; if violated, use the exact formula. **(Full 7 marks requires this caveat.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **resonant pressure sensor**. Derive the relation between frequency shift and applied pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Quality factor (Q)** in a MEMS resonator. Discuss the factors affecting Q in vacuum and in liquid environments, and state its importance in determining the resolution of a resonant sensor. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Applied Pressure P] --> D[Silicon Diaphragm Radius r Thickness td]
    D --> S[Axial Stress sigma on Coupled Clamped Clamped Beam]
    S --> F[Frequency Shift Delta f of Beam Resonator]
    F --> O[Digital Frequency Output Read by Counter]
```

**Working Principle:**

A thin silicon diaphragm deflects under applied pressure $P$. The deflection produces an axial stress $\sigma$ at the edges of the diaphragm, which is mechanically transferred to a clamped–clamped resonant beam anchored at the diaphragm edge. This axial stress changes the beam's effective stiffness and hence its natural frequency. The shift $\Delta f$ is measured using a PLL and output as the sensor reading.

**Derivation:**

For a clamped circular diaphragm (radius $r$, thickness $t_d$):
$$\sigma = \dfrac{3 P r^2}{4 t_d^2}$$

For a clamped–clamped beam under axial stress (small stress limit):
$$\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx \dfrac{\sigma L^2}{4 E t^2}$$

Substituting:
$$\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx \dfrac{3 P r^2 L^2}{16 E t^2 t_d^2}}$$

Hence the resonant pressure sensor sensitivity:
$$S_P = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial P} = \dfrac{3 f_0 r^2 L^2}{16 E t^2 t_d^2} \quad [\text{Hz/Pa}]$$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Definition of Quality Factor:**

The **Quality factor $Q$** of a resonator is a dimensionless measure of the sharpness of its resonance peak. Mathematically:
$$Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f_{3dB}} = 2\pi \dfrac{\text{Energy Stored per Cycle}}{\text{Energy Dissipated per Cycle}}$$

For a damped SDOF system: $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{km}}{c}$, where $c$ is the damping coefficient.

**Factors affecting Q:**

| Environment | Dominant Loss Mechanism | Typical $Q$ |
|---|---|---|
| **High vacuum ($< 10^{-6}$ Torr)** | Anchor (support) loss, thermoelastic damping, intrinsic material loss | $10^4$ – $10^5$ |
| **Atmospheric air** | Squeeze-film damping, air-viscous drag | $10^2$ – $10^3$ |
| **Liquid (water, bio-fluid)** | Squeeze-film + viscous drag of surrounding fluid | $1$ – $10^2$ |
| **High frequency (MHz)** | Anchor loss dominates | Decreases with $f_0$ |

**Importance in Resolution:**

The minimum detectable frequency shift is set by the resonance linewidth:
$$\delta f_{min} \approx \dfrac{f_0}{Q \cdot \text{SNR}}$$

A **high $Q$** → **narrower peak** → **smaller $\delta f_{min}$** → **higher resolution**. For example, a resonator with $f_0 = 100$ kHz and $Q = 10^4$ has $\delta f_{min} \approx 10$ Hz at SNR = 1, enabling detection of $\Delta m \approx 10^{-15}$ g (femtogram level).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student errors in Q-related questions:**
> 1. Confusing $Q$ with **damping ratio $\zeta$** (they are reciprocally related: $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$).
> 2. Forgetting that **anchor loss** is the dominant $Q$-limiting mechanism in vacuum-packaged MEMS, not air damping.
> 3. Stating $Q = f_0/\Delta f$ without defining $\Delta f$ as the **3-dB bandwidth**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks in Module 3 (Resonant Sensor) questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing $\omega$ and $f$:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ is **not** the same as $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$. A 1-mark penalty is common.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write **Hz** or **rad/s** explicitly. Numerical answers without units get zero.
> 3. **Skipping boundary conditions:** In any beam-resonance derivation, you must show all four boundary conditions — partial credit is forfeited otherwise.
> 4. **Ignoring the small-perturbation limit:** Always state $\Delta m \ll m_{eff}$ (or $\sigma \ll \sigma_{cr}$) before using the linearized frequency-shift formula.
> 5. **Drawing the wrong feedback direction:** The PLL feeds the **drive signal**, not the readout — a frequent schematic error.
> 6. **No diagrams:** KTU 2024 scheme mandates diagrams in 14-mark questions. A neat labeled block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision — go through this checklist before the exam.**

- ✅ **Resonant sensor definition:** MEMS device whose output is a *frequency* proportional to the measurand.
- ✅ **Canonical equation:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_{eff}}{m_{eff}}}$
- ✅ **Cantilever natural frequency:** $f_0 \approx 0.1615 \dfrac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ — *thickness up, length down* increases $f_0$.
- ✅ **Clamped–clamped eigenvalue:** $\beta_1 L = 4.730$.
- ✅ **Free–free eigenvalue:** $\beta_1 L = 3.927$.
- ✅ **Mass-loading formula:** $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx -\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\Delta m}{m_{eff}}$ — *negative* because added mass lowers frequency.
- ✅ **Stress-loading formula:** $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} \approx \dfrac{\sigma L^2}{4 E t^2}$ — *positive* (tension raises frequency).
- ✅ **Quality factor:** $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{\Delta f_{3dB}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{km}}{c}$.
- ✅ **Higher $Q$ → better resolution:** $\delta f_{min} \propto f_0/Q$.
- ✅ **Actuation methods:** Electrostatic, Piezoelectric, Thermal, Electromagnetic, Optical.
- ✅ **Detection methods:** Capacitive, Piezoelectric, Piezoresistive, Optical, Electromagnetic.
- ✅ **Diaphragm stress under pressure:** $\sigma = \dfrac{3 P r^2}{4 t_d^2}$ (clamped circular edge).
- ✅ **Silicon material constants:** $E \approx 169$ GPa, $\rho \approx 2330$ kg/m³.
- ✅ **Q in vacuum $\gg$ Q in air $\gg$ Q in liquid** (orders of magnitude drop).
- ✅ **Figure of merit:** $FOM = Q \cdot S_m$ — higher is better.
- ✅ **PLL is the standard closed-loop drive scheme** for tracking resonance.
- ✅ **Frequency is preferred over voltage** because it is quasi-digital, immune to amplitude noise, and high-resolution.
- ✅ **Cantilever = best for mass sensing; diaphragm + clamped-clamped beam = best for pressure sensing; ring = best $Q$.**
- ✅ **Always state the small-perturbation assumption** before using linearized formulas.
- ✅ **Resonant sensors and resonant actuators share the same physics** — only the input/output roles are swapped.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibratory Gyroscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Vibratory Gyroscope # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Vibratory Gyroscope (Coriolis MEMS Gyroscope):** A vibratory gyroscope is a micromachined inertial sensor that detects and measures angular rotation rate ($\Omega$) by exploiting the **Coriolis effect** on a vibrating proof mass. A primary (drive) resonator is excited into a steady-state mechanical oscillation; when the sensor body undergoes an angular rotation, a secondary (sense) resonator perpendicular to the drive direction experiences a Coriolis force that displaces the proof mass. The resulting sense-mode displacement is proportional to the input angular rate. The device is a **second-order resonant MEMS transducer** that converts mechanical angular motion into a measurable electrical signal (typically capacitance change). It is the workhorse inertial sensor in modern automotive Electronic Stability Programs (ESP), smartphones, drones, and inertial measurement units (IMUs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Syllabus Terminology You Must Memorize:** > - **Proof mass** — the suspended inertial element > - **Drive mode** — the driven primary resonance > - **Sense mode** — the perpendicular secondary resonance excited by Coriolis coupling > - **Coriolis force** — the fictitious inertial force $F_c = -2m(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$ > - **Mode-matched gyroscope** — when $\omega_d = \omega_s$ (highest sensitivity) > - **Bias stability / Scale factor** — the two key performance metrics ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Spinning Top" Analogy Imagine a child sitting on a rotating playground roundabout (merry-go-round) and repeatedly throwing a ball in a straight line. Because the roundabout rotates while the ball is in the air, the ball appears to curve sideways to the child. This sideways deflection is the **Coriolis effect**. A vibratory gyroscope works the same way: 1. The "child" is the **proof mass**, vibrating back-and-forth (the drive oscillation) — this is the "ball being thrown." 2. The "rotating roundabout" is the **sensor body**, rotating at rate $\Omega$ — this is the unknown we want to measure. 3. The "sideways curve" is the **Coriolis force**, which deflects the proof mass **perpendicular** to the drive direction. This deflection is the sensor's **output signal**, and its magnitude is directly proportional to $\Omega$. > [!TIP] > **Why "Vibratory"?** Unlike a spinning-wheel gyroscope (which uses angular momentum of a rotor), a vibratory gyroscope uses a *linear* oscillation. It has no rotating parts, making it ideal for MEMS fabrication and immune to wear — a critical advantage for billions of units produced annually. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded) | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | **Mechanical Quality factor** | $Q$ | **1,000 – 50,000** (vacuum) | | **Drive resonance frequency** | $\omega_d$ | **1 kHz – 50 kHz** | | **Bias stability** | $B$ | **0.1 – 10 °/hr** (consumer/tactical) | | **Scale factor** | $S_F$ | **mV/(°/s)** | | **Angular random walk** | $ARW$ | **°/√hr** | | **Coriolis force** | $F_c$ | **Newton** (fictitious) | --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "Why & How" A vibratory gyroscope always has **two orthogonal mechanical degrees of freedom (DOF)**: **Step 1 — Drive Mode (Primary Excitation)** - An electrostatic or piezoelectric actuator drives the proof mass along the $x$-axis into a stable sinusoidal oscillation at the drive resonant frequency $\omega_d$. - The drive amplitude $X_d$ is held constant by a closed-loop automatic gain control (AGC) loop. - The drive velocity is $\dot{x}_d(t) = X_d \omega_d \cos(\omega_d t)$. **Step 2 — Coriolis Force Generation** - When the entire sensor body rotates at angular rate $\vec{\Omega}$ (typically about the $z$-axis), the moving proof mass experiences the Coriolis force: $$F_c = -2m (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_d)$$ - With $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{z}$ and $\vec{v}_d = \dot{x}_d \hat{x}$, the cross-product gives $F_c = -2m \Omega \dot{x}_d \hat{y}$ — a force along the $y$-axis (perpendicular to drive). **Step 3 — Sense Mode (Secondary Response)** - This Coriolis force acts on the sense-mode resonator (also spring-mass-damper) along the $y$-axis. - The sense displacement $y_s$ is read out capacitively (combs or parallel plates) and is proportional to $\Omega$. - The sense output is demodulated at $\omega_d$ to recover the baseband angular rate. **Step 4 — Why it is proportional to $\Omega$** - The sense-mode transfer function, when excited at $\omega_d$, gives: $$y_s = \frac{2\Omega \omega_d X_d Q_s}{k_s} \quad \text{(at resonance)}$$ - Hence $y_s \propto \Omega$ — *the displacement is a direct measurement of rotation rate*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "two-mode coupling" is the heart of the device.** The drive mode provides the velocity reference; the sense mode provides the Coriolis displacement. Without mechanical coupling via Coriolis, there is no signal. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_c = 2m \vert \vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v} \vert$ | Coriolis force magnitude | N | | 2 | $m\ddot{x}_d + c_d \dot{x}_d + k_d x_d = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | Drive-mode EOM | — | | 3 | $m\ddot{y}_s + c_s \dot{y}_s + k_s y_s = F_c(t)$ | Sense-mode EOM | — | | 4 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural (resonant) frequency | rad/s | | 5 | $Q = \dfrac{m \omega_n}{c} = \dfrac{\sqrt{km}}{c}$ | Mechanical quality factor | dimensionless | | 6 | $x_d(t) = \dfrac{F_0/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$ | Steady-state drive amplitude | m | | 7 | $y_s(\Omega) = \dfrac{2\Omega \omega_d X_d Q_s}{k_s}$ | Sense displacement at resonance | m | | 8 | $H(s) = \dfrac{Y_s(s)}{\Omega(s)} = \dfrac{-2m s X_d(s)}{ms^2 + c_s s + k_s}$ | Sense transfer function | m·s | | 9 | $S_F = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{Q_s}{\omega_s X_d}$ | Scale factor (simplified) | V·s/° | | 10 | $ARW = \dfrac{\sqrt{4k_B T \omega_n m}}{S_F}$ | Angular Random Walk (noise floor) | °/√hr | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** When $\omega_d = \omega_s$ (mode-matched condition), sensitivity is maximized. Always state the mode-matching condition explicitly in derivation answers to earn full marks. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Required Performance | |---|---|---| | **Automotive (ESP, rollover detection)** | Vehicle stability control | Bias ~ 1–10 °/s | | **Smartphones / Wearables** | Screen rotation, gaming, AR/VR | Bias ~ 1–10 °/s | | **Drones / Robotics** | Attitude control, dead reckoning | Bias ~ 0.1–1 °/s | | **Aerospace / Tactical** | Inertial navigation, missile guidance | Bias < 0.01 °/hr | | **Medical** | Capsule endoscopy stabilization | Bias ~ 1 °/s | | **Industrial IoT** | Platform stabilization, structural health | Mid-range precision | --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Steady-State Drive-Mode Amplitude We begin with the single-DOF driven harmonic oscillator equation for the proof mass in the drive direction: $$m\ddot{x}_d + c_d \dot{x}_d + k_d x_d = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 1 — Normalize by mass** to put it into canonical form: $$\ddot{x}_d + \frac{c_d}{m}\dot{x}_d + \frac{k_d}{m} x_d = \frac{F_0}{m} \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Define standard parameters.** Introduce natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_d/m}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = c_d/(2\sqrt{k_d m}) = c_d/(2m\omega_n)$. Substituting: $$\ddot{x}_d + 2\zeta \omega_n \dot{x}_d + \omega_n^2 x_d = \frac{F_0}{m} \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Assume the steady-state particular solution** in the form $x_d(t) = X_d \sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Differentiating: $$\dot{x}_d = X_d \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi), \quad \ddot{x}_d = -X_d \omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute** into the canonical equation: $$-X_d \omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2\zeta \omega_n X_d \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) + \omega_n^2 X_d \sin(\omega t - \phi) = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 5 — Collect $\sin$ and $\cos$ coefficients** and solve for $X_d$ (using phasor addition — magnitude of complex impedance): $$X_d = \frac{F_0/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta \omega_n \omega)^2}}$$ **Step 6 — At resonance** ($\omega = \omega_n$), the denominator reduces to $2\zeta \omega_n^2$.
