Since $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$: $$X_{d,\text{res}} = \frac{F_0 Q}{k_d} = \frac{F_0 Q}{m \omega_n^2}$$ This $X_d$ is the constant-amplitude oscillation that drives the Coriolis force. **Key insight for KTU:** Higher $Q$ → larger $X_d$ → larger Coriolis displacement → higher sensitivity. ## 3.2 Derivation II — Coriolis Force and Sense-Mode Response **Step 1 — Establish the Coriolis force expression.** For a mass $m$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}_d$ in a rotating reference frame with angular velocity $\vec{\Omega}$, the Coriolis force in the lab frame is: $$\vec{F}_c = -2m (\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_d)$$ **Step 2 — Choose a coordinate system.** Let drive be along $\hat{x}$, sense along $\hat{y}$, rotation about $\hat{z}$: $\vec{\Omega} = \Omega \hat{z}$, $\vec{v}_d = \dot{x}_d \hat{x}$. Then: $$\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_d = \Omega \dot{x}_d (\hat{z} \times \hat{x}) = \Omega \dot{x}_d \hat{y}$$ Therefore: $$F_c(t) = -2 m \Omega \dot{x}_d(t)$$ **Step 3 — Sense-mode EOM.** The proof mass in the $y$-direction obeys: $$m\ddot{y}_s + c_s \dot{y}_s + k_s y_s = -2m\Omega \dot{x}_d(t)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the drive velocity** at resonance. From §3.1 with $\omega = \omega_d$: $$\dot{x}_d(t) = X_{d,\text{res}} \omega_d \cos(\omega_d t)$$ So the forcing becomes: $$F_c(t) = -2m\Omega X_{d,\text{res}} \omega_d \cos(\omega_d t) = F_c^{\max}\cos(\omega_d t)$$ with $F_c^{\max} = 2m\Omega X_{d,\text{res}} \omega_d$. **Step 5 — Solve the sense-mode EOM as a forced oscillator at frequency $\omega_d$.** Using the same canonical solution: $$Y_s = \frac{F_c^{\max}/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_{s,n}^2 - \omega_d^2)^2 + (2\zeta_s \omega_{s,n} \omega_d)^2}}$$ **Step 6 — Apply mode-matching** ($\omega_d = \omega_{s,n} = \omega_n$): $$Y_s = \frac{F_c^{\max} Q_s}{k_s} = \frac{2m\Omega X_{d,\text{res}} \omega_d Q_s}{k_s}$$ **Step 7 — Final compact result:** $$\boxed{\,Y_s = \frac{2 \Omega \, \omega_d \, X_{d,\text{res}} \, Q_s}{k_s}\,}$$ This is the central equation of every vibratory gyroscope problem. The output displacement is **linear in $\Omega$** — exactly what we want for an angular rate sensor. ## 3.3 Derivation III — Sense-Mode Transfer Function (Laplace Domain) **Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform** of the sense EOM with $\Omega$ as the input and $Y_s$ as the output: $$(ms^2 + c_s s + k_s) Y_s(s) = -2m s X_d(s) \cdot \Omega(s)$$ **Step 2 — Note:** $X_d(s)$ is the *Laplace transform of the drive motion*. The drive loop holds $X_d(t)$ at constant amplitude (modeled as a constant $X_0$ in phasor analysis). The *transfer function from $\Omega$ to $Y_s$* becomes: $$H(s) = \frac{Y_s(s)}{\Omega(s)} = \frac{-2m s X_0}{m s^2 + c_s s + k_s}$$ **Step 3 — Identify the standard second-order form** by dividing by $m$: $$H(s) = \frac{-2 X_0 s}{s^2 + 2\zeta_s \omega_{s,n} s + \omega_{s,n}^2}$$ **Step 4 — Frequency response.** Substitute $s = j\omega$ and evaluate magnitude at $\omega = \omega_d = \omega_{s,n}$ (mode-matched case). Denominator becomes $j 2\zeta_s \omega_n^2$, magnitude $2\zeta_s \omega_n^2$: $$\vert H(j\omega_n) \vert = \frac{2 X_0 \omega_n}{2\zeta_s \omega_n^2} = \frac{X_0 Q_s}{\omega_n}$$ Which recovers the time-domain result from §3.2 since $k_s = m\omega_n^2$: $$Y_s = \Omega \cdot \frac{X_0 Q_s}{\omega_n} = \frac{\Omega X_0 Q_s m}{k_s} \cdot \omega_n = \frac{2 \Omega \omega_n X_0 Q_s}{2 k_s} \quad (\text{after algebra — equivalent expression up to factor of 2 depending on convention})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Discrepancies of a factor of 2** in the final constant are common across textbooks. The exact numerical coefficient depends on whether the sense displacement is peak, peak-to-peak, or RMS, and whether the drive amplitude $X_d$ is total stroke or half-stroke. **Always write down your definitions at the start of a KTU derivation answer.** ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Scale Factor and Bias Stability Definitions **Step 1 — Scale Factor ($S_F$)** is defined as the ratio of output voltage to input angular rate: $$S_F = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{\Omega} = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{Y_s} \cdot Y_s(\Omega) = G_{\text{elec}} \cdot \frac{2 \omega_d X_d Q_s}{k_s}$$ where $G_{\text{elec}}$ is the transimpedance gain of the readout ASIC (V/m). **Step 2 — Bias stability** is the lowest drift of the output when $\Omega = 0$. It is governed by electronic noise and mechanical-thermal noise. The dominant noise is the **Brownian noise force**: $$F_{\text{noise,rms}} = \sqrt{4 k_B T c_s \Delta f}$$ The minimum detectable angular rate (Noise Equivalent Rate) is: $$\Omega_{\min} = \frac{F_{\text{noise}}}{2 m \omega_d X_d} = \sqrt{\frac{4 k_B T c_s \Delta f}{4 m^2 \omega_d^2 X_d^2}} = \frac{1}{m \omega_d X_d}\sqrt{k_B T c_s \Delta f}$$ This sets the **noise floor** of the sensor. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Vibratory Gyroscope The figure below maps the signal flow from physical rotation to electrical output. The two orthogonal mechanical resonators (drive and sense) are coupled only through the Coriolis force, which physically manifests the rotation rate. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DCO Drive Oscillator] --> B[Drive Amplifier] B --> C[Drive Comb Electrodes] C --> D[(Proof Mass
Drive Mode X)] D -- Coriolis Force F_c --> E[(Proof Mass
Sense Mode Y)] E --> F[Sense Comb Electrodes] F --> G[Charge Amplifier] G --> H[Synchronous Demodulator] A -. reference .-> H H --> I[Low Pass Filter] I --> J[Output Voltage V_out] J --> K[Angular Rate Omega] subgraph Mechanical D E end subgraph Electronic A B G H I end ``` ## 4.2 Operating-Mode State Machine The gyroscope operates in a closed-loop regime. The drive mode is held at constant amplitude (AGC), and the sense mode is demodulated coherently. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> StartUp StartUp --> DriveResonance: Apply drive force at f_d DriveResonance --> SteadyState: AGC locks X_d constant SteadyState --> SenseActive: Body rotates, F_c appears SenseActive --> Demodulation: Multiply by sin and cos of f_d Demodulation --> LPFandOutput: Extract I and Q baseband LPFandOutput --> AngularRate: V_out = S_F x Omega AngularRate --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block View of a Tuning-Fork Gyroscope A tuning-fork gyroscope uses two proof masses vibrating $180°$ out of phase. The differential motion cancels common-mode accelerations (linear shock, vibration) and doubles the sense signal. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TF[Tuning Fork Resonator] M1[Proof Mass 1
Drives at +X] -->|anti-phase| M2[Proof Mass 2
Drives at -X] M1 --> SC1[Sense Comb 1] M2 --> SC2[Sense Comb 2] end SC1 --> DIFF[Differential Amplifier] SC2 --> DIFF DIFF --> OUT[Cleaned Sense Signal
2 x F_c common-mode rejected] style M1 fill:#e1f5ff style M2 fill:#e1f5ff style DIFF fill:#fff4e1 style OUT fill:#e8f5e9 ``` ## 4.4 Common MEMS Gyroscope Topologies | Topology | Key Feature | Application | |---|---|---| | **Tuning Fork** | Two anti-phase masses, differential | Automotive, consumer | | **Wine-Glass Resonator** | Hemispherical shell, n=2 mode | High-performance tactical | | **Ring Resonator** | Annular ring, n=2 wine-glass mode | Navigation grade | | **Linear Comb Drive** | Simple linear X-Y structure | Low-cost consumer | | **Piezoelectric Disc** | Acoustic waves on a plate | Harsh environments | --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define Coriolis force. Explain its role in a vibratory gyroscope.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Coriolis Force:** It is a fictitious inertial force experienced by a mass moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a rotating reference frame of angular velocity $\vec{\Omega}$. It is given by $\vec{F}_c = -2m(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v})$. > **Role in Gyroscope:** In a vibratory gyroscope, the proof mass is driven into linear oscillation (drive mode). When the sensor body rotates, the Coriolis force deflects the proof mass perpendicular (sense mode) to the drive direction. The magnitude of this deflection is proportional to the input angular rate $\Omega$, allowing the device to function as a rotation-rate sensor. (3 marks) --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is "mode matching" in a vibratory gyroscope? Why is it desirable?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Mode matching** is the condition in which the natural frequencies of the drive mode ($\omega_d$) and the sense mode ($\omega_s$) are made equal: $\omega_d = \omega_s = \omega_n$. > **Why desirable:** When the drive frequency equals the sense natural frequency, the Coriolis force excites the sense resonator at its resonance, maximizing the sense displacement amplitude (by a factor of $Q_s$). This dramatically improves the scale factor and signal-to-noise ratio. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` #### **(a)** Derive the equation of motion of the drive mode of a vibratory gyroscope. Show that at resonance, the steady-state drive amplitude is $X_d = F_0 Q_d / k_d$. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the drive-mode equation of motion** (1 mark): For a proof mass $m$ suspended by spring $k_d$ and damper $c_d$, driven by an external force $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ along the $x$-axis: $$m\ddot{x}_d + c_d \dot{x}_d + k_d x_d = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 2 — Normalize** (1 mark): Divide by $m$ and define $\omega_n = \sqrt{k_d/m}$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c_d/m$: $$\ddot{x}_d + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x}_d + \omega_n^2 x_d = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Assume steady-state solution** $x_d(t) = X_d \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ (1 mark). **Step 4 — Substitute and solve** using phasor method (2 marks): After substitution and matching coefficients, the magnitude is: $$X_d = \frac{F_0/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$$ **Step 5 — Apply resonance condition** $\omega = \omega_n$. The denominator becomes $2\zeta\omega_n^2$ (1 mark): $$X_d = \frac{F_0}{2\zeta m \omega_n^2} = \frac{F_0}{c_d \omega_n} = \frac{F_0 Q_d}{k_d}$$ using $Q_d = 1/(2\zeta) = m\omega_n/c_d$ and $k_d = m\omega_n^2$. **Step 6 — Conclude** (1 mark): The drive amplitude is amplified by the quality factor $Q_d$ at resonance — a key design principle. *Q.E.D.* #### **(b)** A MEMS gyroscope has the following parameters: $m = 1 \mu$g, $k_d = k_s = 1$ N/m, $Q_d = Q_s = 1000$, $\omega_d = \omega_s = 2\pi \times 10$ kHz, and drive force amplitude $F_0 = 1 \mu$N. The device is mode-matched. Find (i) the drive amplitude $X_d$, and (ii) the sense displacement $Y_s$ when the input angular rate $\Omega = 100°$/s. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute drive amplitude at resonance** (2 marks): $$X_d = \frac{F_0 Q_d}{k_d} = \frac{(1\times 10^{-6})(1000)}{1} = 1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} = 1\ \text{mm}$$ **Step 2 — Convert angular rate to rad/s** (1 mark): $$\Omega = 100°/\text{s} \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.745\ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the sense-displacement formula** (mode-matched case) (2 marks): $$Y_s = \frac{2 \Omega \omega_d X_d Q_s}{k_s} = \frac{2 \times 1.745 \times (2\pi \times 10^4) \times (1\times 10^{-3}) \times 1000}{1}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate** (1 mark): $$Y_s = 2 \times 1.745 \times 62831.85 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 2.193\times 10^{5}\ \text{m}$$ Wait — this is non-physical. **Re-check units**: with $F_0 = 1\mu$N, $X_d$ must be tiny. Let me re-evaluate: with $k_d = 1$ N/m, $X_d = F_0 Q / k = (10^{-6})\cdot 1000/1 = 10^{-3}$ m = 1 mm. This is consistent. But $Y_s$ is huge, indicating the mode-matched formula with $Q_s = 1000$ gives enormous amplification. In practice, $X_d$ is electronically limited. **Conclusion:** The numerical values used here are illustrative; the key is the algebraic form. **Step 5 — Final answer** (1 mark): $$X_d = 1\ \text{mm}, \qquad Y_s = 2.19 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m} \quad (\text{illustrative, since parameters are non-physical})$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** When you quote $Y_s$, **always state the assumptions** (mode-matched, steady-state, no electronic AGC limiting). Half the marks lost in numerical problems come from missing these. A correct formula with a clear assumption statement earns 6/7; a number alone earns at most 3/7. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` #### **(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a vibratory gyroscope. Clearly identify the drive mode, sense mode, and the Coriolis coupling. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Block diagram** (3 marks). [Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above for the expected structure. Student should draw: Drive Oscillator → Drive Amp → Drive Electrodes → Proof Mass (X) → [Coriolis coupling] → Proof Mass (Y) → Sense Electrodes → Charge Amp → Demodulator → LPF → Output.] **Step 2 — Drive mode** (1.5 marks): The drive oscillator (typically at the drive resonant frequency) drives the proof mass along $x$ via comb electrodes. The amplitude $X_d$ is regulated by an AGC loop. The drive motion is purely mechanical and is the "reference" against which rotation is measured. **Step 3 — Sense mode** (1.5 marks): The $y$-axis degree of freedom is a separate mechanical resonator (spring-mass $k_s$, $c_s$, $m$). It is normally quiescent but is excited only when Coriolis force appears. **Step 4 — Coriolis coupling and demodulation** (1 mark): The Coriolis force $F_c = -2m\Omega \dot{x}_d$ couples the two modes. The sense displacement is detected as a capacitance change, amplified, and synchronously demodulated at $\omega_d$ to extract the baseband angular rate $\Omega$. #### **(b)** Derive the scale factor expression for a mode-matched vibratory gyroscope. Discuss the effect of $Q_s$ and drive amplitude $X_d$ on sensitivity. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Start with the mode-matched sense displacement** (2 marks): $$Y_s = \frac{2 \Omega \omega_d X_d Q_s}{k_s}$$ **Step 2 — Convert displacement to voltage using the readout gain** $G_{\text{elec}}$ (V/m) (1 mark): $$V_{\text{out}} = G_{\text{elec}} \cdot Y_s = G_{\text{elec}} \cdot \frac{2 \Omega \omega_d X_d Q_s}{k_s}$$ **Step 3 — Define scale factor** as $S_F = V_{\text{out}}/\Omega$ (1 mark): $$\boxed{\,S_F = \frac{2 G_{\text{elec}} \omega_d X_d Q_s}{k_s}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Discuss $Q_s$ effect** (1.5 marks): Sensitivity scales *linearly* with $Q_s$. Higher $Q_s$ (achieved by vacuum packaging, low anchor losses) directly improves scale factor. However, $Q_s$ also reduces bandwidth ($\Delta f = \omega_s/Q_s$) — a classic **sensitivity–bandwidth trade-off**. **Step 5 — Discuss $X_d$ effect** (1.5 marks): Sensitivity scales *linearly* with drive amplitude $X_d$. Larger $X_d$ → larger Coriolis force → larger sense signal. However, $X_d$ is limited by the **non-linearity of comb fingers**, **stiction**, and **dynamic range** of the readout. AGC is used to hold $X_d$ constant over temperature. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** In Part B answers, **always draw the block diagram even if not explicitly asked.** KTU examiners allot 2–3 marks specifically for a labeled diagram. A textual answer without a diagram is capped at ~60% of the total marks for that sub-part. Also, in derivation questions, never skip the "normalizing by $m$" step — this is a classic 1-mark deduction point. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] **Coriolis force formula:** $\vec{F}_c = -2m(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}_d)$ — always state both the direction (cross product) and the dependence on $m$, $\Omega$, and $v_d$. - [ ] **Two orthogonal modes:** Drive (X) and Sense (Y); they are coupled *only* by the Coriolis force. - [ ] **Drive EOM:** $m\ddot{x}_d + c_d\dot{x}_d + k_d x_d = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$; at resonance $X_d = F_0 Q_d/k_d$. - [ ] **Sense EOM:** $m\ddot{y}_s + c_s\dot{y}_s + k_s y_s = -2m\Omega \dot{x}_d$. - [ ] **Mode matching:** $\omega_d = \omega_s$ maximizes sensitivity by a factor of $Q_s$. - [ ] **Final output:** $Y_s = (2\Omega \omega_d X_d Q_s)/k_s$ — linear in $\Omega$ (this is why the device is called a *rate* sensor). - [ ] **Transfer function:** $H(s) = -2X_0 s / (s^2 + 2\zeta_s\omega_s s + \omega_s^2)$ — a high-pass characteristic (gyroscopes cannot measure DC rotation; they need a time-varying drive). - [ ] **Scale factor:** $S_F = 2 G_{\text{elec}} \omega_d X_d Q_s / k_s$. - [ ] **Performance metrics to remember:** Bias stability, scale factor, ARW, bandwidth, shock survival. - [ ] **Common architectures:** Tuning fork, ring resonator, wine-glass, linear comb, piezoelectric. - [ ] **Trade-offs:** Sensitivity vs. bandwidth ($Q$ trade-off), $X_d$ vs. linearity, vacuum $Q$ vs. damping, size vs. mass. - [ ] **KTU favorite trick question:** "Why does a vibratory gyroscope fail to measure DC rotation?" → *Because $\dot{x}_d$ is sinusoidal; its time average is zero, so the demodulated Coriolis signal at DC is zero. Gyroscopes measure *rate*, not absolute angle.* - [ ] **Key SI units reminder:** $\Omega$ in **rad/s** (convert °/s by multiplying $\pi/180$); $k$ in **N/m**; $m$ in **kg**; $c$ in **N·s/m**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** For any 14-mark question, the *formula sheet ratio* matters — typically **4 marks for the diagram, 6 marks for the derivation, 2 marks for the discussion, 2 marks for the numerical / conclusion**. Plan your answer accordingly. Always end with a one-line concluding sentence — examiners reward it.

topics.title: Capacitive and Piezo Resistive Pressure Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capacitive and Piezo Resistive Pressure Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Capacitive Pressure Sensor

A **Capacitive Pressure Sensor** is a MEMS device that transduces an applied mechanical pressure into a measurable change in electrical capacitance. The transduction mechanism relies on the physical deflection of a deformable diaphragm (typically a thin polysilicon or metal membrane) suspended over a fixed reference electrode, which alters the gap distance, the overlap area, or the dielectric properties of a parallel-plate capacitor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"A capacitive pressure sensor is a micromachined transducer in which an applied pressure produces a deflection of a thin diaphragm, modulating the capacitance formed between the diaphragm and a fixed substrate electrode. The change in capacitance is proportional to the applied pressure and forms the basis of measurement."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two parallel playing cards held a fraction of a millimeter apart, forming a tiny invisible reservoir of electrical charge. When you press the top card gently with your finger, it bends downward, the air gap shrinks, and suddenly the card-pair can "store" more electrical charge at the same voltage. The Capacitive Pressure Sensor works exactly like this — pressure squeezes a microscopic diaphragm closer to a base plate, and the resulting change in electrical charge-storage capacity (capacitance) tells us exactly how hard we pushed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants (SI Units):**
> - Vacuum Permittivity, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
> - Relative Permittivity of Air, $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.0006$
> - Relative Permittivity of Polysilicon, $\varepsilon_r \approx 11.7$
> - Young's Modulus of Silicon (used in diaphragm design), $E \approx 130$ **GPa**
> - Poisson's Ratio of Silicon, $\nu \approx 0.28$

---

## 1.2 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor

A **Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor** is a MEMS transducer that converts applied mechanical stress into a proportional change in electrical resistance. The physical principle exploited is the **piezoresistive effect**, discovered by Lord Kelvin in 1856 and extensively characterized in silicon by Charles S. Smith in 1954. In a typical KTU-syllabus configuration, four piezoresistors are diffused on a silicon diaphragm and wired in a **Wheatstone bridge** arrangement so that the differential output voltage is a linear function of the applied pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *"A piezoresistive pressure sensor uses the change in resistivity of semiconductor strain gauges embedded on a flexible diaphragm. The applied pressure strains the diaphragm, which alters the resistance of the gauges, and this resistance change is detected using a Wheatstone bridge circuit."*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a thin rubber sheet stretched across a small drum. Now glue four tiny strips of carbon-loaded rubber at specific points on the sheet. When you press down on the drum, the rubber sheet stretches in some places and compresses in others. The carbon strips change their electrical resistance — strips in stretched regions become *thinner and longer* (more resistance), while strips in compressed regions become *fatter and shorter* (less resistance). Wiring these four smart strips into a bridge circuit (a Wheatstone bridge) lets us measure the *difference* in resistance, which is directly proportional to the pressure applied. This is precisely how a piezoresistive pressure sensor works — silicon replaces the rubber, and doped silicon regions replace the carbon strips.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks (for board exam reference):**
> - Piezoresistive Coefficient of p-type Silicon ($\pi_{44}$) $\approx 138 \times 10^{-11}$ **Pa⁻¹**
> - Piezoresistive Coefficient of n-type Silicon ($\pi_{11}$) $\approx -102 \times 10^{-11}$ **Pa⁻¹**
> - Gauge Factor of Silicon (p-type): $G \approx 100$ to $200$ (compared to metal gauges with $G \approx 2$)

---

## 1.3 Comparative Overview

| Feature | Capacitive Sensor | Piezoresistive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Domain** | Capacitance change ($\Delta C$) | Resistance change ($\Delta R$) |
| **Read-out Circuit** | C–V converter, charge amplifier | Wheatstone bridge |
| **Sensitivity** | High (at small gaps) | Moderate |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | Low | High (requires compensation) |
| **Power Consumption** | Very Low | Moderate |
| **Diaphragm Material** | Polysilicon / Metal / Si | Monocrystalline Silicon |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low (most mature) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** **C**apacitive sensors respond to **C**hange in **C**harge storage (geometry). **P**iezoresistive sensors respond to **P**roperty (resistivity) change in doped regions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capacitive Pressure Sensor — Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

The operational logic can be broken into six engineering steps:

1. **Diaphragm Fabrication** — A thin membrane (square or circular) is released via surface or bulk micromachining above a sealed cavity etched into the substrate.
2. **Reference Cavity Sealing** — The cavity is sealed at a reference pressure (often vacuum, $\sim 10$ **mTorr**), establishing a known baseline capacitance $C_0$.
3. **Pressure Application** — External pressure deflects the diaphragm. For a square diaphragm clamped at all edges, the maximum center deflection $w_0$ is given by the classical plate-bending equation.
4. **Capacitance Modulation** — The deflection changes the gap $d$ between the diaphragm and the bottom electrode, modulating the parallel-plate capacitance.
5. **Capacitance Sensing** — A C–V converter or switched-capacitor ASIC measures the new value of $C$ and converts it to a voltage.
6. **Signal Output** — The voltage difference is linearized and scaled to produce a digital pressure reading (e.g., in kPa or psi).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - Step 1 ensures a deformable element — the "spring" of the system.
> - Step 2 sets a fixed reference for absolute (not differential) pressure measurement.
> - Step 3 converts mechanical energy into geometric deformation.
> - Step 4 is the actual **transduction** — geometry to capacitance.
> - Step 5 converts capacitance to a usable voltage signal.
> - Step 6 makes the output compatible with microcontrollers and buses (I²C, SPI).

### The Three Capacitive Sensing Modes

- **Gap-Sensing Mode:** $d$ changes with constant area $A$ and constant $\varepsilon_r$. Used in **most pressure sensors**.
- **Area-Sensing Mode:** $A$ changes with constant $d$ (e.g., lateral comb-finger structures). Used in **accelerometers and position sensors**.
- **Dielectric-Sensing Mode:** $\varepsilon_r$ of a fluid or gas between the plates changes (e.g., humidity, chemical sensors). Used in **chemical/biosensors**.

---

## 2.2 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor — Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

1. **Diaphragm Definition** — A square or circular silicon diaphragm (typically **400–500 µm** wide, **10–50 µm** thick) is formed by anisotropic wet etching (e.g., KOH or TMAH) or DRIE.
2. **Resistor Implantation** — Four piezoresistors are ion-implanted (typically Boron for p-type) at the **edges of the diaphragm**, where longitudinal and transverse stresses are maximum.
3. **Bridge Wiring** — Two resistors are oriented along the $\langle 110 \rangle$ direction (longitudinal piezoresistors), and two along the $\langle 1\bar{1}0 \rangle$ direction (transverse piezoresistors). They are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge**.
4. **Pressure-Induced Strain** — When pressure is applied, the diaphragm bows. The longitudinal resistors (at the diaphragm edges parallel to the edge) undergo **tensile strain** ($\Delta R > 0$), while the transverse resistors undergo **compressive strain** ($\Delta R < 0$).
5. **Bridge Unbalance** — The two opposite arms of the bridge increase in resistance while the other two decrease, producing a differential output voltage $V_{out} \neq 0$.
6. **Signal Amplification & Compensation** — Instrumentation amplifiers boost the millivolt-level signal. Temperature compensation circuits correct for the strong temperature dependence of piezoresistive coefficients.

### The Piezoresistive Effect in Silicon

The fractional change in resistivity of a semiconductor under stress is given by:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

where $\pi_l$ and $\pi_t$ are the **longitudinal** and **transverse** piezoresistive coefficients, and $\sigma_l$ and $\sigma_t$ are the corresponding stresses.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - **Capacitive sensors** dominate in **low-power and high-precision** applications: tire-pressure monitoring (TPMS), biomedical implants, barometric altimeters in smartphones, and HVAC controls.
> - **Piezoresistive sensors** dominate in **high-volume, cost-sensitive, harsh-environment** applications: automotive MAP/BMP sensors, industrial process control, oil & gas well-head monitoring, and medical catheters.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas — Memorize These for the 14-Mark Question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parallel-plate capacitance (basic) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $A$ = area, $d$ = gap | F |
| 2 | Capacitance after diaphragm deflection | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - w(x,y)}$ | $w(x,y)$ = local deflection | F |
| 3 | Small-signal linearized sensitivity | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx \dfrac{w_0}{d_0}$ | $w_0$ = center deflection | dimensionless |
| 4 | Center deflection, square diaphragm | $w_0 = \dfrac{0.01512 \, P \, a^4}{E \, t^3} \left(1 - \nu^2\right)^{-1}$ | $P$ = pressure, $a$ = half-side, $t$ = thickness, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | m |
| 5 | Center deflection, circular diaphragm | $w_0 = \dfrac{P \, a^4}{64 \, D}$, $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | $a$ = radius, $D$ = flexural rigidity | m |
| 6 | Capacitive sensitivity (small deflection) | $S_C = \dfrac{1}{C_0}\dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{0.01512 \, a^4}{E t^3 d_0 (1-\nu^2)}$ | Sensitivity | Pa⁻¹ |
| 7 | Piezoresistive fractional resistance change | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | $\pi_{l,t}$ = coefficients, $\sigma$ = stress | dimensionless |
| 8 | Gauge factor (general) | $G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | $\varepsilon$ = strain | dimensionless |
| 9 | Wheatstone bridge output | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\left(\dfrac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \dfrac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \dfrac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \dfrac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)$ | $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage | V |
| 10 | Bridge output (matched, with $\Delta R/R = G\varepsilon$) | $V_{out} = G \varepsilon V_{ex}$ | Linearized output | V |
| 11 | Maximum stress in square diaphragm (edge center) | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{0.308 \, P \, a^2}{t^2}$ | Edge stress | Pa |
| 12 | Sensitivity (full bridge, piezoresistive) | $S_P = \dfrac{V_{out}}{P \, V_{ex}} = G \varepsilon / P$ | Piezoresistive sensitivity | V/Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety:** All absolute-value / modulus expressions have been written using the symbol $\vert$ or described in words (e.g., *change in $R$*). Do **not** insert vertical pipes inside table cells — they will break rendering.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility — Choosing $t$ and $a$:**
> - For a **barometric** range ($\sim 100$ kPa), typical dimensions are $a = 500$ µm, $t = 20$ µm.
> - For a **high-pressure** oil-well sensor ($> 10$ MPa), the diaphragm is made thicker ($t = 50$ µm) and smaller in area.
> - The cube-dependence on $t$ in formula #4 means a 2× increase in thickness reduces deflection by **8×** — a critical design lever.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitance Change for a Parallel-Plate Pressure Sensor

We will derive the small-deflection sensitivity of a capacitive pressure sensor with a deformable upper plate (diaphragm) and a fixed lower plate.

### Step 1: Define the Geometry

Let:
- $A$ = electrode area (m²)
- $d_0$ = initial gap (m)
- $w(x,y)$ = local vertical deflection of the diaphragm (m)
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m (vacuum permittivity)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the medium (≈ 1 for air)

The differential capacitance between the diaphragm and the bottom electrode is given by:

$$C = \int_A \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d_0 - w(x,y)} \, dA$$

### Step 2: Apply the Small-Deflection Approximation

When the diaphragm deflection is very small compared to the initial gap ($w(x,y) \ll d_0$), we can use a first-order Taylor expansion:

$$\frac{1}{d_0 - w(x,y)} = \frac{1}{d_0} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{w(x,y)}{d_0}} \approx \frac{1}{d_0}\left(1 + \frac{w(x,y)}{d_0}\right)$$

This is valid because $\frac{w(x,y)}{d_0} \ll 1$ in MEMS pressure sensors (typically $< 0.1$).

### Step 3: Substitute and Integrate

Substituting the expanded form:

$$C = \int_A \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d_0}\left(1 + \frac{w(x,y)}{d_0}\right) dA$$

Splitting into two integrals:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d_0} \int_A dA \; + \; \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d_0^{\,2}} \int_A w(x,y) \, dA$$

### Step 4: Identify the Baseline Capacitance

The first integral is simply the area $A$. Therefore:

$$C = C_0 + \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d_0^{\,2}} \int_A w(x,y) \, dA$$

where $C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$ is the baseline (unpressurized) capacitance.

### Step 5: Express the Capacitance Change

The change in capacitance is:

$$\Delta C = C - C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d_0^{\,2}} \int_A w(x,y) \, dA$$

### Step 6: Define the Average Deflection

Let $\bar{w}$ denote the area-averaged deflection:

$$\bar{w} = \frac{1}{A}\int_A w(x,y) \, dA$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta C = C_0 \cdot \frac{\bar{w}}{d_0}$$

### Step 7: Final Sensitivity Expression

Dividing both sides by $C_0$:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\bar{w}}{d_0}$$

**This is the central sensitivity equation for a gap-sensing capacitive pressure sensor under small-deflection conditions.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Board Exam Favorites):**
> - Sensitivity is **inversely proportional** to the gap $d_0$. Halving the gap *doubles* the sensitivity.
> - Sensitivity is **proportional** to the average diaphragm deflection $\bar{w}$.
> - For a square diaphragm with parabolic deflection profile, $\bar{w} \approx \dfrac{w_0}{3}$ (where $w_0$ is the center deflection).
> - This linearization is valid only when $w_0/d_0 < 0.3$ to keep error below 5%.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Piezoresistive Bridge Output for a Pressure Sensor

We will derive the output voltage of a fully-active Wheatstone bridge with four piezoresistors under diaphragm stress.

### Step 1: Define the Resistor States

For a square diaphragm clamped at the edges with applied pressure $P$:

- Two resistors ($R_1, R_3$) are placed at edges with **tensile stress** $+\sigma$ (longitudinal).
- Two resistors ($R_2, R_4$) are placed at edges with **compressive stress** $-\sigma$ (transverse).

### Step 2: Express Each Resistance

The resistance after stress application is:

$$R_1 = R_0\left(1 + G\varepsilon_1\right) = R_0\left(1 + \pi_l \sigma\right)$$

$$R_2 = R_0\left(1 - G\varepsilon_2\right) = R_0\left(1 + \pi_t (-\sigma)\right)$$

$$R_3 = R_0\left(1 + G\varepsilon_3\right) = R_0\left(1 + \pi_l \sigma\right)$$

$$R_4 = R_0\left(1 - G\varepsilon_4\right) = R_0\left(1 + \pi_t (-\sigma)\right)$$

### Step 3: Apply the Wheatstone Bridge Equation

For a bridge excited by $V_{ex}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4}\right)$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Simplify (Small-Signal Approximation)

Assuming $\pi\sigma \ll 1$ (a valid assumption since $\pi \sigma \sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$):

$$R_1 + R_3 \approx 2R_0(1 + \pi_l \sigma)$$

$$R_2 + R_4 \approx 2R_0(1 + \pi_t (-\sigma))$$

### Step 5: Compute Each Voltage Divider Ratio

$$\frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} = \frac{1 + \pi_l \sigma}{2(1 + \pi_l \sigma)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\frac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4} = \frac{1 - \pi_t \sigma}{2(1 - \pi_t \sigma)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

### Step 6: Apply the Perturbation Expansion

Using $\dfrac{1 + a}{1 + b} \approx 1 + (a - b)$ to first order:

$$\frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} = \frac{1 + \pi_l \sigma}{2(1 + \pi_l \sigma)} = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + (\pi_l \sigma - \pi_l \sigma) + O(\sigma^2)\right]$$

But this simplification hides the asymmetry. Going back to the **fully expanded form**:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)$$

### Step 7: Substitute the Fractional Changes

For a fully-active symmetric bridge where the signs alternate:

$$\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} = +\pi_l \sigma, \quad \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} = -\pi_t \sigma$$

$$\frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} = +\pi_l \sigma, \quad \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4} = -\pi_t \sigma$$

### Step 8: Final Simplification

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\left(\pi_l \sigma - (-\pi_t \sigma) + \pi_l \sigma - (-\pi_t \sigma)\right)$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\left(2\pi_l \sigma + 2\pi_t \sigma\right) = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}\left(\pi_l + \pi_t\right)\sigma$$

### Step 9: Final Sensitivity Formula

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}\left(\pi_l + \pi_t\right)\sigma}$$

For a square diaphragm with $\sigma_{max} = 0.308 P a^2 / t^2$, the sensitivity becomes:

$$S_P = \frac{V_{out}}{P \cdot V_{ex}} = \frac{0.154 \, a^2 \, (\pi_l + \pi_t)}{t^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - Sensitivity scales as $a^2/t^2$ — i.e., **larger and thinner diaphragms** are more sensitive.
> - The factor $(\pi_l + \pi_t)$ is called the **effective piezoresistive coefficient**. For silicon on (100) wafer, $\pi_l + \pi_t = \pi_{44}/2$ for p-type resistors, and its magnitude is typically $\sim 70 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹.
> - Increasing $V_{ex}$ boosts sensitivity linearly, but also increases self-heating — a critical design trade-off.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A capacitive pressure sensor has $A = 1\text{ mm}^2$, $d_0 = 2\text{ µm}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$. When a pressure of $P = 100$ kPa is applied, the average diaphragm deflection is $\bar{w} = 0.1$ µm. Find $C_0$, $\Delta C$, and the fractional sensitivity.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_0$:**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(1 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = 4.427 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 4.427 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta C$:**

$$\Delta C = C_0 \cdot \frac{\bar{w}}{d_0} = (4.427 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot \frac{0.1 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta C = 4.427 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 0.05 = 2.21 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F} = 0.221 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sensitivity:**

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{0.1}{2} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

This is well within the linear range of the small-deflection approximation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Capacitive Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AP[Applied Pressure P] --> DF[Silicon Diaphragm]
    DF --> GM[Geometric Modulation of Gap d]
    GM --> CC[Parallel Plate Capacitor Structure]
    CC --> CV[C to V Converter ASIC]
    CV --> AM[Amplifier and Filter]
    AM --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> MCU[Microcontroller for Pressure Readout]
    RV[Reference Vacuum Sealed Cavity] -.-> CC
    EX[Excitation AC Signal 100 kHz to 1 MHz] --> CV
    MCU --> DS[Digital Pressure Display in kPa]
    TS[Temperature Sensor] --> CCOR[Onchip Compensation Logic]
    CCOR --> AM
```

**Description of the Flow:**
1. The applied pressure deflects the diaphragm.
2. The deflection changes the gap in the parallel-plate capacitor.
3. A C–V converter translates the change in capacitance to a voltage.
4. The amplifier/filter block conditions the signal.
5. The ADC digitizes the signal for the microcontroller.
6. Temperature compensation is applied to cancel thermal drift.
7. The final digital output is displayed as a pressure value.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AP[Applied Pressure P] --> SD[Silicon Diaphragm with Edges Clamped]
    SD --> SR[Stress Distribution on Diaphragm]
    SR --> PR1[Piezoresistor R1 Longitudinal at Edge]
    SR --> PR2[Piezoresistor R2 Transverse at Edge]
    SR --> PR3[Piezoresistor R3 Longitudinal at Opposite Edge]
    SR --> PR4[Piezoresistor R4 Transverse at Opposite Edge]
    PR1 --> WB[Wheatstone Bridge Network]
    PR2 --> WB
    PR3 --> WB
    PR4 --> WB
    WB --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    EX[DC Excitation Voltage Vex 3.3 V or 5 V] --> WB
    IA --> TC[Temperature Compensation Network]
    TC --> LP[Low Pass Filter]
    LP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP --> OUT[Calibrated Pressure Output in kPa or psi]
```

**Description of the Flow:**
1. The applied pressure creates a stress field in the silicon diaphragm.
2. Four piezoresistors placed at high-stress locations change their resistance.
3. The Wheatstone bridge converts differential resistance change to a voltage.
4. The instrumentation amplifier boosts the low-level signal.
5. Temperature compensation cancels the thermal drift of piezoresistive coefficients.
6. The low-pass filter removes high-frequency noise.
7. The ADC and DSP produce a calibrated, linearized output.

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB

Always label the **sealed cavity** and the **diaphragm thickness $t$**, as these are the most-marked features.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define a capacitive pressure sensor. With a neat sketch, explain its working principle. State two advantages over piezoresistive sensors.**

**Model Answer:**

A capacitive pressure sensor is a MEMS transducer in which the applied pressure deflects a thin diaphragm, thereby changing the capacitance between the diaphragm and a fixed reference electrode. The change in capacitance $\Delta C$ is proportional to the applied pressure $P$.

**Working Principle:**
A thin diaphragm is suspended over a sealed vacuum cavity, forming a parallel-plate capacitor with capacitance:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - w(x,y)}$$

When pressure deflects the diaphragm by $w(x,y)$, the effective gap reduces and capacitance increases. The capacitance change is measured by a C–V converter and converted to a pressure reading.

**Two Advantages over Piezoresistive Sensors:**
1. **Lower temperature sensitivity** — Capacitive sensors are intrinsically less affected by temperature since capacitance depends mainly on geometry, not on carrier mobility.
2. **Lower power consumption** — They can operate with very small AC excitation currents, making them ideal for battery-powered and implantable applications.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest Pattern]**
**What is the piezoresistive effect? Explain the role of the Wheatstone bridge in a piezoresistive pressure sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

The **piezoresistive effect** is the phenomenon in which the electrical resistivity of a material changes when mechanical stress is applied to it. In semiconductors like silicon, the effect is approximately **two orders of magnitude larger** than in metals, which makes silicon highly suitable for MEMS pressure sensors.

The fractional change in resistance is given by:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

**Role of the Wheatstone Bridge:**
A piezoresistive pressure sensor uses **four piezoresistors** connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. Two resistors are oriented longitudinally and two transversely on the silicon diaphragm. Under pressure, the resistance of two opposite arms increases while the other two decreases. The bridge converts this **differential resistance change** into a measurable differential output voltage:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}(\pi_l + \pi_t)\sigma$$

The Wheatstone bridge thus provides **temperature self-compensation** (since all four resistors experience the same temperature) and **linearized output** (since opposite arms cancel even-order nonlinearities).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module-end questions offer internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as two complete, independent alternative choices. Each has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Capacitive Pressure Sensor (Complete Module-3 Coverage)

#### **Part (a)** — Derive the sensitivity of a parallel-plate capacitive pressure sensor. [7 marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Consider two parallel plates of area $A$ separated by a gap $d_0$. The upper plate (diaphragm) deflects by $w(x,y)$ under pressure.

**Step 2 — Baseline Capacitance:**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance After Deflection:**
Differentiating the basic capacitance formula with respect to gap:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - w(x,y)} = C_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - w/d_0}$$

**Step 4 — Small-Deflection Approximation:**

$$\frac{1}{1 - w/d_0} \approx 1 + \frac{w}{d_0}$$

**Step 5 — Capacitance Change:**

$$\Delta C = C - C_0 = C_0 \cdot \frac{w}{d_0}$$

**Step 6 — Sensitivity Definition:**
The capacitive sensitivity is defined as the ratio of fractional capacitance change to applied pressure:

$$S_C = \frac{1}{P}\cdot\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{\bar{w}}{P \cdot d_0}$$

**Step 7 — Substituting Diaphragm Deflection:**
For a square diaphragm with clamped edges, $w_0 = \dfrac{0.01512 \, P \, a^4}{E t^3 (1 - \nu^2)}$, and the average deflection $\bar{w} = w_0/3$.

**Step 8 — Final Sensitivity Formula:**

$$\boxed{S_C = \frac{0.00504 \, a^4}{E t^3 d_0 (1 - \nu^2)}}$$

---

#### **Part (b)** — Compare capacitive and piezoresistive pressure sensors. Discuss two applications of each. [7 marks]

**Model Answer Table:**

| Parameter | Capacitive | Piezoresistive |
|---|---|---|
| Transduction | Gap change | Resistivity change |
| Output | $\Delta C$ | $\Delta R$ |
| Sensitivity | High (small gaps) | Moderate |
| Temp. Drift | Low | High (needs compensation) |
| Power | Very low | Moderate |
| Linearity | Good | Excellent |
| Cost | Moderate | Low |

**Applications of Capacitive Sensors:**
1. **Tire Pressure Monitoring Systems (TPMS)** — battery-powered, low-power CMOS-compatible.
2. **Barometric altimeters in smartphones** — high precision, low drift.
3. **Biomedical implants** (e.g., intracranial pressure sensors) — minimal heating.

**Applications of Piezoresistive Sensors:**
1. **Automotive MAP/BMP sensors** — robust, mature, cost-effective.
2. **Industrial process control** in oil & gas — high pressure, harsh media.
3. **Medical catheters and disposable blood-pressure transducers** — single-use, low-cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Piezoresistive Pressure Sensor (Alternative Choice)

#### **Part (a)** — Explain the working of a piezoresistive pressure sensor with a neat diagram. Derive the output voltage of a Wheatstone bridge. [7 marks]

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Diaphragm & Resistor Layout:**
A square silicon diaphragm of side $2a$ and thickness $t$ is formed by anisotropic etching. Four Boron-doped piezoresistors are implanted at the **midpoints of the four edges** of the diaphragm where stresses are maximum.

**Step 2 — Stress Distribution:**
For a square clamped diaphragm, the maximum stress occurs at the edge midpoints:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{0.308 \, P a^2}{t^2}$$

**Step 3 — Resistance Change:**
Each piezoresistor's resistance changes according to:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \pi_l \sigma_l + \pi_t \sigma_t$$

**Step 4 — Bridge Configuration:**
The four resistors form a Wheatstone bridge with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$. Resistors $R_1$ and $R_3$ (at opposite edges) experience **tensile stress**, while $R_2$ and $R_4$ (at the other two edges) experience **compressive stress**.

**Step 5 — Bridge Output Equation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_3}{R_1 + R_3} - \frac{R_4}{R_2 + R_4}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Linearized Result:**
Under small-signal assumption ($\Delta R \ll R$):

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{2}\left(\pi_l + \pi_t\right)\sigma_{max}}$$

**Step 7 — Substituting $\sigma_{max}$:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{0.154 \, V_{ex} (\pi_l + \pi_t) a^2 P}{t^2}$$

This is the **piezoresistive sensor output equation**, showing that:
- $V_{out} \propto P$ (linear with pressure).
- $V_{out} \propto a^2 / t^2$ (geometry-dependent).
- $V_{out} \propto V_{ex}$ (boosted by higher excitation, with self-heating trade-off).

---

#### **Part (b)** — Discuss the role of the Wheatstone bridge in cancelling temperature effects and common-mode errors. What is a quarter-bridge, half-bridge, and full-bridge configuration? [7 marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Temperature Compensation in a Full Bridge:**
In a fully-active Wheatstone bridge, all four piezoresistors are on the same silicon diaphragm and thus experience the **same temperature**. Since temperature changes shift all four resistances by the same factor, the **differential output remains unchanged**. This is called **common-mode rejection** of temperature.

**2. Common-Mode Error Cancellation:**
Pressure creates **differential strain** (tensile at two edges, compressive at the other two), while temperature creates **common-mode strain** (all resistors stretch equally). The bridge output depends only on the difference, automatically subtracting the common-mode component.

**3. Bridge Configurations:**

| Configuration | Active Resistors | Output Voltage | Sensitivity | Temperature Compensation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quarter-bridge** | 1 piezoresistor, 3 fixed | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Lowest | Poor (needs external compensation) |
| **Half-bridge** | 2 piezoresistors, 2 fixed | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2}\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Double the quarter-bridge | Better, but not perfect |
| **Full-bridge** | 4 piezoresistors | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Four times quarter-bridge | Best (intrinsic cancellation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For maximum sensitivity and inherent temperature compensation, the full-bridge configuration is used in commercial piezoresistive pressure sensors** (e.g., Honeywell, Bosch BMP series).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing up the two deflection formulas** — Square diaphragm ($0.01512$) vs. circular diaphragm ($1/64$). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for using the wrong constant.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/(1-\nu^2)$ factor** — This is the **flexural rigidity correction** and is mandatory. Forgetting it loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Using $|\Delta R|$ in tables** — This breaks markdown rendering AND confuses the examiner. Use words like *change in $R$* or the symbol $\Delta R$ explicitly.
> 4. **Failing to label the bridge polarity** — In derivations, always mark which resistors are tensile ($+$) and which are compressive ($-$). Examiners check for **sign convention**.
> 5. **Omitting the $V_{ex}/4$ factor** — The Wheatstone bridge output is $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / 4R)$ in the quarter-bridge and $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ in the full-bridge. Confusing these loses **2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Drawing a sensor without labeling the sealed cavity** — A "diaphragm floating in air" with no cavity is treated as a *conceptual error*, deducting **1 mark**.
> 7. **Stating $C = \varepsilon A/d$ without mentioning $\varepsilon_0$** — Always include the vacuum permittivity explicitly. The relative permittivity alone is **not sufficient**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read This the Night Before the Exam):**

- **Capacitive sensor** output: $C_0 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d_0$; sensitivity $\Delta C / C_0 = \bar{w} / d_0$.
- **Piezoresistive sensor** output: $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/2)(\pi_l + \pi_t)\sigma_{max}$ for a full bridge.
- **Square diaphragm** center deflection: $w_0 = \dfrac{0.01512 \, P a^4}{E t^3 (1 - \nu^2)}$.
- **Square diaphragm** max edge stress: $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{0.308 \, P a^2}{t^2}$.
- **Circular diaphragm** center deflection: $w_0 = \dfrac{P a^4}{64 D}$ where $D = E t^3 / [12(1 - \nu^2)]$.
- **Piezoresistive coefficients** for silicon: $\pi_{44} \approx 138 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ (p-type); $\pi_{11} \approx -102 \times 10^{-11}$ Pa⁻¹ (n-type).
- **Wheatstone bridge** output (full, matched): $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ when all four resistors are active.
- **Sensitivity scaling laws**: capacitive $\propto a^4/t^3$, piezoresistive $\propto a^2/t^2$.
- **Key design trade-off**: smaller gap $d_0$ → higher capacitive sensitivity but lower linear range; thinner diaphragm $t$ → higher deflection but lower burst pressure.
- **Temperature compensation**: full-bridge is intrinsically temperature-compensated; capacitive sensors need minimal compensation.
- **Real-world examples**: TPMS, smartphone barometers (capacitive); automotive MAP sensors, industrial pressure transmitters (piezoresistive).
- **Material constants to memorize**: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $E_{Si} = 130$ GPa; $\nu_{Si} = 0.28$.
- **Always show units in derivations** — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing SI units in the final answer.
- **Always draw the sealed cavity** in capacitive sensor diagrams — marks are reserved for this specific feature.
- **Sign convention**: longitudinal stress is positive (tensile), transverse stress is negative when oriented along the strain direction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro Actuator: Design of Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micro Actuator: Design of Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Micro Actuator** is a Micro-Electro-Mechanical System (MEMS) component that converts an input energy form (electrical, thermal, magnetic, or optical) into a controlled mechanical output in the form of **force**, **displacement**, **strain**, or **vibration** at the micro-scale (typically dimensions between **1 µm and 1 mm**). The **Design of Actuators** in the MEMS context refers to the systematic engineering process of selecting actuation principles, modelling electromechanical coupling, optimizing geometric parameters, predicting performance metrics, and ensuring manufacturability using standard microfabrication processes.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAET864)** framework, micro actuator design is taught as the logical counterpart to micro sensor design: while sensors *harvest* mechanical/thermal/chemical energy into electrical signals, actuators *inject* energy from the electrical/thermal domain back into the mechanical world to perform work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The design of micro actuators requires an understanding of **actuation principles, governing equations, material behaviour at the micro-scale, and the dominant forces at play (electrostatic, magnetic, thermal, piezoelectric, and shape-memory effects).**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a conductor controlling a tiny orchestra where every musician holds a thread attached to a movable mirror. You have five different tools to make the mirror dance:
- A **static-electricity spark gap** (electrostatic actuator) — like rubbing a balloon on hair; a voltage makes the hair stand up.
- A **bimetallic strip** (thermal actuator) — like the bimetallic coil in an old thermometer that bends when heated.
- A **magnet on a spring** (magnetic actuator) — like a relay coil pulling a metal lever.
- A **piezo buzzer** (piezoelectric actuator) — like the click of a lighter, where a crystal flexes when voltage is applied.
- A **muscle wire** (shape memory alloy) — like a "robot muscle" that remembers its original shape and contracts when heated.

In each case, the **input signal (voltage, current, heat)** is transduced into a **physical motion** of a suspended micro-structure — this is the essence of micro actuator design.

## 1.3 Why Micro Actuator Design is Unique

Designing actuators at the micro-scale is fundamentally different from the macro-scale because:

- **Surface forces dominate over volume forces** (e.g., electrostatic force scales as $L^2$ while inertial force scales as $L^3$).
- **Material behaviour changes** — silicon is no longer brittle; it behaves elastically with negligible plastic deformation.
- **Friction (stiction) and adhesion** become significant, leading to failure modes like **pull-in instability**.
- **Heat dissipation** is poor, so thermal actuators require careful power budgeting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** A well-designed micro actuator should deliver maximum **specific work output (work per unit volume)** with minimum **input power** and a **resonant frequency** matched to the application.

## 1.4 Classification of Micro Actuators

| Actuation Principle | Input Energy | Output Motion | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrostatic** | Voltage | In-plane / out-of-plane deflection | Comb drives, micromirrors, RF switches |
| **Piezoelectric** | Voltage | Sub-nm displacement | Precision positioning, inkjet |
| **Thermal (bimorph)** | Current/Heat | Large deflection, low force | Grippers, valves |
| **Magnetic (Lorentz/Coil)** | Current | High force, long stroke | Hard-disk head positioning, microfluidics |
| **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** | Heat / Current | Contraction (up to 8% strain) | Micro-grippers, surgical tools |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of Micro Actuators

The design of any micro actuator revolves around a **transduction mechanism** — a physical law that couples two energy domains. The designer must walk through five logical steps:

1. **Identify the application requirement** → stroke ($\Delta x$), force ($F$), bandwidth ($f_{3dB}$), power budget ($P$).
2. **Select the actuation principle** based on scaling laws, material compatibility, and fabrication constraints.
3. **Build a lumped-parameter model** (mass–spring–damper) of the mechanical structure.
4. **Derive coupled electromechanical equations** relating electrical input to mechanical output.
5. **Validate the design** against stability, pull-in, fatigue, and process tolerances.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** Electrostatic actuation is preferred for low-power, high-speed applications; thermal actuation is preferred for high-force, low-speed applications; magnetic actuation is preferred for large strokes in non-conductive media.

## 2.2 Electrostatic Actuator Design (Most Important for KTU)

### 2.2.1 Parallel-Plate Capacitor Actuator

A movable plate of area $A$ is suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$ at a gap $g$ from a fixed plate. Applying a voltage $V$ creates an attractive Coulomb force.

The **electrical energy stored** in the capacitor is:

$$U_e = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{(g - x)} V^2$$

where $x$ is the displacement of the movable plate towards the fixed plate.

The **electrostatic force** is obtained by differentiating energy with respect to displacement at constant $V$:

$$F_e = \frac{\partial U_e}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_V = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{(g - x)^2}$$

The **mechanical restoring force** of the suspension spring is:

$$F_m = k x$$

### 2.2.2 Pull-In Instability

Static equilibrium requires $F_e = F_m$. Solving yields:

$$\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{(g - x)^2} = k x$$

A critical voltage called the **Pull-In Voltage ($V_{PI}$)** exists beyond which the system is unstable and the movable plate snaps onto the fixed plate.

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$$

where $g_0$ is the initial gap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The pull-in occurs when the plate has travelled **1/3 of the initial gap**, i.e., $x_{PI} = g_0/3$. Beyond this, electrostatic force grows faster than the spring can restore, leading to snap-down.

### 2.2.3 Comb-Drive Actuator

Comb drives overcome the pull-in limitation of parallel-plate actuators because the capacitance changes linearly with displacement (not inversely with gap).

For a comb drive with $N$ fingers, overlap length $l$, finger gap $d$, and displacement $y$:

$$C = N \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t (l + y)}{d}$$

$$F_y = \frac{1}{2} N \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t}{d} V^2$$

The displacement under applied voltage is:

$$y = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t V^2}{2 k_y d}$$

## 2.3 Piezoelectric Actuator Design

Piezoelectric materials (PZT, AlN, ZnO, PVDF) generate strain when subjected to an electric field. The constitutive equations are:

$$S = s^E \, T + d \, E$$

$$D = d \, T + \varepsilon^T \, E$$

where:
- $S$ = mechanical strain, $T$ = mechanical stress
- $E$ = electric field, $D$ = electric displacement
- $s^E$ = elastic compliance at constant $E$
- $d$ = piezoelectric strain coefficient (e.g., $d_{31}$, $d_{33}$)
- $\varepsilon^T$ = permittivity at constant stress

The **free strain** produced by an applied field $E$ is:

$$S = d_{31} E = d_{31} \frac{V}{t_{piezo}}$$

where $t_{piezo}$ is the piezoelectric layer thickness.

## 2.4 Thermal Actuator Design

### 2.4.1 Bimorph (Bimetallic) Thermal Actuator

Two materials with different coefficients of thermal expansion ($\alpha_1, \alpha_2$) are bonded. Heating causes differential expansion, producing a bending motion.

The tip deflection for a cantilever of length $L$ and thickness $t$:

$$\delta = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2 t (1 + \frac{E_1 t_1}{E_2 t_2})}$$

For a symmetric bimorph, the maximum blocking force is:

$$F_{block} = \frac{3 E I \delta}{L^3}$$

### 2.4.2 Thermal Expansion (Pseudobimorph) Actuator

A heated arm expands against an asymmetric cold arm, causing lateral buckling and rotation.

$$\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$$

## 2.5 Magnetic Actuator Design

For a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field $B$:

$$F = B I L$$

For a micro-coil of $N$ turns in a gap field $B$:

$$F = N B I L$$

Power consumption is $P = I^2 R$ where $R$ is the coil resistance. This is the chief drawback of magnetic actuation at the micro-scale.

## 2.6 Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuator

Nitinol (Ni-Ti) wires undergo martensite-to-austenite phase transition upon heating, recovering up to **8% strain**.

$$\text{Strain recovery} = \varepsilon_{SMA} = 8\%$$

Actuation stress: $\sigma_{act} \approx 200$ MPa  
Typical activation temperature: $A_f \approx 70-100°C$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU ESE — they appear in 7-mark derivation sub-parts.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g}$ | F | Most fundamental MEMS equation |
| 2 | Electrostatic force | $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2} V^2 \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | N | General energy method |
| 3 | Parallel-plate force | $F_e = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{2(g-x)^2}$ | N | Pull-in at $x = g/3$ |
| 4 | Pull-in voltage | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$ | V | Key instability threshold |
| 5 | Comb-drive force | $F_y = \dfrac{1}{2} N \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t}{d} V^2$ | N | No pull-in limitation |
| 6 | Piezo strain | $S = d_{31} E$ | dimensionless | $d_{31}$ in m/V |
| 7 | Bimorph tip deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{2 t}$ | m | Cantilever geometry |
| 8 | Resonant frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | Lumped-mass model |
| 9 | Quality factor | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{km}}{b}$ | dimensionless | $b$ = damping coefficient |
| 10 | Bandwidth | $f_{3dB} = f_0 \sqrt{\sqrt{2}-1}\cdot\sqrt{1+...}$ | Hz | For 2nd-order system |
| 11 | Lorentz force | $F = B I L$ | N | Magnetic actuation |
| 12 | SMA recoverable strain | $\varepsilon_{SMA} \approx 8\%$ | dimensionless | Nitinol |
| 13 | Capacitive energy | $U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Stored energy |
| 14 | Spring PE | $U_{spring} = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$ | J | Mechanical storage |
| 15 | Damping force | $F_d = -b \dot{x}$ | N | Viscous damping model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Application | Actuation Principle | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| **DLP micromirror (TI DMD)** | Electrostatic | Fast ($\mu$s), low power, CMOS compatible |
| **Hard-disk read head** | Magnetic (voice coil) | Long stroke ($\sim$mm) |
| **Inkjet printhead (HP)** | Thermal / Piezo | High speed, compact |
| **AFM probe positioning** | Piezoelectric | Sub-nanometre precision |
| **Endoscopic capsule camera** | SMA | Large strain, biocompatible |
| **Optical cross-connect switches** | Electrostatic comb drive | Low power, integrated with ICs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** These derivations are written in the exact step-by-step style expected by a KTU board examiner when awarding full marks (7–14 mark sub-questions).

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pull-In Voltage for a Parallel-Plate Electrostatic Actuator

**Given:** A movable plate of area $A$ suspended by a linear spring of stiffness $k$ above a fixed plate, separated initially by a gap $g_0$. The medium has permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.

**To Find:** Expression for the **pull-in voltage** $V_{PI}$ and the **pull-in displacement** $x_{PI}$.

### Step 1: Set up the capacitance model

The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor as a function of the plate displacement $x$ (with $x = 0$ at initial position) is:

$$C(x) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{g_0 - x}$$

### Step 2: Express the total energy of the system

The total potential energy of the actuator system is the sum of the electrical energy stored in the capacitor and the mechanical strain energy in the spring:

$$U_{total}(x, V) = U_{elec}(x, V) + U_{mech}(x)$$

$$U_{total}(x, V) = \frac{1}{2} C(x) V^2 + \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$

$$U_{total}(x, V) = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{g_0 - x} + \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$

### Step 3: Apply the static equilibrium condition

At static equilibrium (assuming the system is biased at a constant voltage $V$), the net force on the movable plate is zero. The force is obtained by taking the negative gradient of the total energy:

$$F_{net} = -\frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial x} = 0$$

Differentiating term-by-term:

$$-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[\frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{g_0 - x}\right] - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[\frac{1}{2} k x^2\right] = 0$$

$$-\left[\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^2}\right] - [k x] = 0$$

This gives the **force-balance equation**:

$$\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^2} = k x \quad \text{...(Equilibrium Condition)}$$

### Step 4: Apply the marginal-stability (stability) condition

For the equilibrium to be marginally stable (i.e., a small perturbation neither grows nor decays), the second derivative of the total energy with respect to $x$ must also vanish:

$$\frac{\partial^2 U_{total}}{\partial x^2} = 0$$

Differentiating the equilibrium force equation with respect to $x$ (or differentiating $U_{total}$ twice), we get:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^3} - k = 0$$

$$\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2 = k (g_0 - x)^3 \quad \text{...(Stability Condition)}$$

### Step 5: Solve the two equations simultaneously

From the equilibrium condition:

$$V^2 = \frac{2 k x (g_0 - x)^2}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

From the stability condition:

$$V^2 = \frac{k (g_0 - x)^3}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

Equating the right-hand sides:

$$\frac{2 k x (g_0 - x)^2}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{k (g_0 - x)^3}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

$$2 k x (g_0 - x)^2 = k (g_0 - x)^3$$

Dividing both sides by $k (g_0 - x)^2$ (valid since $x \ne g_0$):

$$2 x = (g_0 - x)$$

$$\boxed{x_{PI} = \frac{g_0}{3}}$$

### Step 6: Substitute back to get $V_{PI}$

Substitute $x_{PI} = g_0/3$ into the stability condition equation:

$$V_{PI}^2 = \frac{k \left(g_0 - \frac{g_0}{3}\right)^3}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

$$V_{PI}^2 = \frac{k \left(\frac{2 g_0}{3}\right)^3}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** The factor $8/27 = (2/3)^3$ comes directly from the cubic term in the stability condition.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Static Displacement of a Comb-Drive Actuator

**Given:** A comb-drive with $N$ finger pairs, finger thickness $t$, finger overlap $l$, finger gap $d$, and applied voltage $V$. The suspension spring stiffness in the direction of motion is $k_y$.

### Step 1: Capacitance of a single finger pair

A single finger pair forms a parallel-plate capacitor with **changing overlap** (not changing gap). With displacement $y$:

$$C_{pair} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t (l + y)}{d}$$

### Step 2: Total capacitance of $N$ pairs

$$C_{total} = N \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t (l + y)}{d} = N \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t}{d}(l + y)$$

### Step 3: Electrostatic force from the energy method

$$F_y = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{d C_{total}}{dy} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \cdot N \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t}{d}$$

### Step 4: Equilibrium with the suspension spring

At static equilibrium, electrostatic force equals spring restoring force:

$$\frac{1}{2} N \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t V^2}{d} = k_y y$$

Solving for $y$:

$$\boxed{y = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t V^2}{2 k_y d}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Because $F_y$ is **independent of $y$**, the comb drive has no pull-in instability in the lateral direction. This is the primary advantage of comb drives over parallel-plate actuators.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever Beam Actuator

**Given:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, density $\rho$, and Young's modulus $E$.

### Step 1: Equivalent stiffness of a cantilever

The tip stiffness of a cantilever beam loaded at its tip is:

$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

### Step 2: Effective mass of the cantilever

For the fundamental mode shape, the effective mass is approximately:

$$m_{eff} \approx 0.24 \, \rho L w t$$

(The exact coefficient is $0.24$ for a uniform cantilever's first mode shape.)

### Step 3: Resonant frequency

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E w t^3 / 4 L^3}{0.24 \rho L w t}}$$

Simplifying:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{0.96 \rho L^4}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{0.96 \rho}}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \cdot 1.02}$$

For a quick estimate, the simpler form is:

$$f_0 \approx \frac{1.02}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}\left(\frac{t}{L^2}\right) \approx 0.162 \frac{t}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

For silicon ($E = 169$ GPa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³, $\sqrt{E/\rho} = 8518$ m/s):

$$f_0 (\text{kHz}) \approx 1.38 \times 10^3 \cdot \frac{t}{L^2} \quad (t, L \text{ in m})$$

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Practice problem to test the formulas above.**

**Question:** A parallel-plate electrostatic actuator has plate area $A = 200 \times 200 \;\mu m^2$, initial gap $g_0 = 2\;\mu m$, and is suspended by a spring of stiffness $k = 1$ N/m. The medium is air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). Calculate:
> (a) the pull-in voltage,
> (b) the pull-in displacement,
> (c) the static displacement at $V = 5$ V.

### Solution

**Given:** $A = 200 \times 200 \times 10^{-12} = 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m², $g_0 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $k = 1$ N/m, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_r = 1$.

**(a) Pull-in voltage:**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 1 \times (2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

Computing the numerator: $8 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 6.4 \times 10^{-17}$

Computing the denominator: $27 \times 8.854 \times 4 \times 10^{-20} = 956.2 \times 10^{-20} = 9.562 \times 10^{-18}$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-17}}{9.562 \times 10^{-18}}} = \sqrt{6.693} \approx 2.59 \text{ V}$$

**(b) Pull-in displacement:**

$$x_{PI} = \frac{g_0}{3} = \frac{2 \mu m}{3} \approx 0.667 \;\mu m$$

**(c) Static displacement at $V = 5$ V:**

Since $V = 5$ V $>$ $V_{PI} = 2.59$ V, the actuator has **already snapped down**. There is no stable static equilibrium; the plate is pulled onto the substrate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Pitfall:** Operating at $V > V_{PI}$ is dangerous because it leads to **stiction failure** — the plate remains stuck even after the voltage is removed. Always design with $V_{applied} < V_{PI}$.

## 3.5 Design Trade-off Matrix (Analytical Comparison)

| Parameter | Electrostatic | Piezoelectric | Thermal (Bimorph) | Magnetic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Voltage (~V) | Voltage (~V) | Current (mA) | Current (mA) |
| **Stroke** | Small (< 10 µm) | Very small (< 1 µm) | Large (> 50 µm) | Large (mm) |
| **Force** | Low (µN) | Medium (mN) | Low (mN) | High (mN–N) |
| **Speed** | Very fast (µs) | Very fast (µs) | Slow (ms) | Medium |
| **Power** | Negligible (CV²f) | Low | High (Joule heating) | High (I²R) |
| **CMOS compat.** | Excellent | Moderate | Good | Poor |
| **Vacuum compat.** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good |
| **Pull-in risk** | Yes (parallel plate) | No | No | No |
| **Cost driver** | Gap control | Material | Current | Coil fabrication |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question says *"compare the various actuation mechanisms"*, draw this table — examiners award 2–3 marks for a clean comparative presentation alone.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Micro Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Energy Source<br>Voltage / Current / Heat]
    B[Transduction Mechanism<br>Electrostatic / Piezo / Thermal / Magnetic / SMA]
    C[MEMS Mechanical Structure<br>Beam / Plate / Comb / Diaphragm]
    D[Mechanical Output<br>Force / Displacement / Strain]
    E[Feedback Sensor<br>Capacitive / Piezoresistive]
    F[Controller / Driver IC]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -.control signal.-> A

    classDef ioBox fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef trBox fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef meBox fill:#14532d,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef fbBox fill:#581c87,stroke:#581c87,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class A,D ioBox
    class B trBox
    class C,E meBox
    class F fbBox
```

## 4.2 Design Workflow for a Micro Actuator (KTU 5-Mark Answer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Application Requirements<br>Stroke / Force / Bandwidth / Power]
    B[Step 2: Select Actuation Principle<br>Electrostatic / Piezo / Thermal / Magnetic]
    C[Step 3: Choose Material System<br>Silicon / PZT / Polymer / SMA / Metal]
    D[Step 4: Lumped-Parameter Modelling<br>k, m, b, C, L, R]
    E[Step 5: Coupled Field Simulation<br>FEA: Electrostatic / Structural / Thermal]
    F[Step 6: Stability Check<br>Pull-in Voltage / Thermal Runaway / Fatigue]
    G[Step 7: Fabrication Process Design<br>Lithography / Etch / Release / Package]
    H[Step 8: Prototype Test & Iteration]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H -.feedback.-> A

    classDef stepBox fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0c4a6e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H stepBox
```

## 4.3 Parallel-Plate Electrostatic Actuator — Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PP[Parallel Plate Electrostatic Actuator]
        direction TB
        A1[Top Fixed Plate<br>Metal / Polysilicon]
        A2[Air Gap: g0]
        A3[Movable Plate<br>Suspended by Spring k]
        A4[Substrate / Anchor]
        A5[Spring Suspension]
    end

    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A3 --- A4
    A3 --- A5

    V1[Applied Voltage V] -.creates field.-> A1
    Fe[Electrostatic Force Fe<br>Fe = 0.5 eps A V squared over g squared] -.pulls down.-> A3
    Fs[Spring Force Fs = k x] -.restores.-> A3

    classDef mech fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef elec fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef force fill:#14532d,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class A1,A3,A4,A5 mech
    class V1,Fe,Fs elec
    class A2 force
```
