# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: Short answer (2 × 3 = 6 marks)
> - Part B: Two full questions of 14 marks each, with internal choice (answer ONE).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define the term "pull-in voltage" in the context of a parallel-plate electrostatic micro actuator. State the formula and explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer:**

The **pull-in voltage ($V_{PI}$)** of a parallel-plate electrostatic actuator is the critical voltage at which the movable plate becomes **unstable** and snaps onto the fixed plate due to a runaway increase in the attractive electrostatic force. It arises from the positive feedback between capacitance and gap reduction: as the plate moves, capacitance increases, which in turn increases the force, which moves the plate further.

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}$$

**Physical Significance:** It sets the **maximum usable voltage** of the actuator. Operating beyond $V_{PI}$ causes collapse (snap-down) and often permanent stiction failure. Design rule: keep $V_{applied} \le 0.7 \cdot V_{PI}$ for safe operation.

**[3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark significance]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Compare the actuation principles of comb-drive and parallel-plate electrostatic actuators in terms of stroke, force linearity, and pull-in behaviour.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Parallel-Plate | Comb-Drive |
|---|---|---|
| **Stroke** | Limited to $g_0/3$ (pull-in) | No inherent limit (long) |
| **Force–displacement** | $F \propto 1/(g-x)^2$ (nonlinear) | $F$ = constant (linear) |
| **Pull-in** | Yes (catastrophic) | No (in lateral direction) |
| **Capacitance variation** | Inversely with gap | Linearly with overlap |
| **Application** | Small gap, large force | Long stroke, low force |

The comb drive is preferred when **predictable linear force and long strokes** are required, e.g., in micro-grippers and variable capacitors.

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 marks each, internal choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **pull-in voltage** of a parallel-plate electrostatic micro actuator. Explain why the pull-in occurs at one-third of the initial gap. [7 marks]

**(b)** A parallel-plate electrostatic actuator has plate area $A = 100 \times 100 \;\mu m^2$, initial gap $g_0 = 3 \;\mu m$, and suspension spring stiffness $k = 0.5$ N/m. The gap is filled with air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). Determine:
   (i) the pull-in voltage,
   (ii) the static displacement when $V = 1$ V is applied. [7 marks]

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for energy equation, 2 marks for equilibrium and stability conditions, 1 mark for the final expression, 1 mark for the explanation of $x_{PI} = g_0/3$.

Consider a parallel-plate capacitor with movable plate suspended by a spring of stiffness $k$ above a fixed plate. The gap is $g_0 - x$ when the movable plate is displaced by $x$ towards the fixed plate.

**Total energy of the system:**

$$U_{total}(x, V) = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{g_0 - x} + \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$

**Equilibrium condition (zero net force):**

$$\frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^2} = k x$$

**Marginal stability condition (zero second derivative):**

$$\frac{\partial^2 U_{total}}{\partial x^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^3} = k$$

Dividing the stability condition by the equilibrium condition:

$$\frac{2}{g_0 - x} = \frac{1}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2x = g_0 - x \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{PI} = \frac{g_0}{3}$$

Substituting back:

$$V_{PI}^2 = \frac{k (g_0 - x_{PI})^3}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{k (2g_0/3)^3}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

$$\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}}}$$

**Why one-third?** Because the electrostatic force grows as $1/(g-x)^2$ (faster than linearly), while the spring force grows linearly. The two curves are tangent to each other (marginal stability) only when the displacement is exactly one-third of the initial gap. Beyond this, the electrostatic curve rises above the spring curve at every point, leading to a net restoring force that pulls the plate further down.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark given data, 2 marks for $V_{PI}$, 2 marks for static analysis, 2 marks for final result.

**Given:** $A = 100 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}$ m² = $10^{-8}$ m², $g_0 = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $k = 0.5$ N/m, $\varepsilon_r = 1$.

**(i) Pull-in voltage:**

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 0.5 \times (3 \times 10^{-6})^3}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

Numerator: $8 \times 0.5 \times 27 \times 10^{-18} = 108 \times 10^{-18} = 1.08 \times 10^{-16}$

Denominator: $27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-20} = 2.39 \times 10^{-18}$

$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{1.08 \times 10^{-16}}{2.39 \times 10^{-18}}} = \sqrt{45.2} \approx 6.72 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Static displacement at $V = 1$ V:**

Solve the force-balance equation $\frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^2}{(g_0 - x)^2} = k x$:

Substitute $V = 1$ V:

$$\frac{1}{2}\frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 \times 10^{-8} \times 1}{(3 \times 10^{-6} - x)^2} = 0.5 \, x$$

$$\frac{4.427 \times 10^{-20}}{(3 \times 10^{-6} - x)^2} = 0.5 \, x$$

For a small displacement ($x \ll g_0$), approximate $g_0 - x \approx g_0$:

$$x \approx \frac{4.427 \times 10^{-20}}{0.5 \times 9 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{4.427 \times 10^{-20}}{4.5 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 9.84 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} \approx 9.8 \text{ nm}$$

**Verification:** $x = 9.8$ nm $\ll g_0 = 3000$ nm, so the linear approximation is valid. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the working principle of a **comb-drive actuator**. Derive the expression for the **lateral force** and **static displacement** produced by an applied voltage. [7 marks]

**(b)** Compare the four major micro actuation principles (electrostatic, piezoelectric, thermal, and magnetic) in terms of stroke, force, speed, and power consumption. State one practical application of each. [7 marks]

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Comb-Drive Actuator [7 marks]

> **Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for capacitance, 1 mark for force, 1 mark for displacement, 1 mark for conclusion.

A **comb-drive actuator** consists of two interdigitated sets of comb fingers: one set is anchored (fixed) and the other is attached to a movable shuttle suspended by folded-flexure springs.

**Working Principle:** When a voltage $V$ is applied between the fixed and movable comb fingers, a lateral electrostatic force is generated due to the **change in overlap area** (not the gap). Because the capacitance varies **linearly** with the lateral displacement, the resulting force is **constant** (independent of $x$).

**Capacitance of one finger pair:**

$$C_{pair} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t (l + y)}{d}$$

where $l$ is the initial overlap, $y$ is the lateral displacement, $t$ is the finger thickness, and $d$ is the inter-finger gap.

**Total capacitance for $N$ finger pairs:**

$$C_{total} = N \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t (l + y)}{d}$$

**Lateral electrostatic force (energy method):**

$$F_y = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \frac{d C_{total}}{dy} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \cdot N \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t}{d}$$

$$\boxed{F_y = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t V^2}{2 d}}$$

**Static displacement under spring restoring force $k_y y$:**

$$y = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r t V^2}{2 k_y d}$$

**Key Advantage:** No pull-in instability in the lateral direction because the force is constant; the equilibrium becomes unstable only when the spring cannot hold the (constant) force, i.e., the limit is set by the maximum allowable voltage before the fingers touch laterally.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Actuation Principles [7 marks]

> **Mark Allocation:** 4 marks for table, 1 mark each for applications (4 × 1 = 4 marks).

| Actuation | Stroke | Force | Speed | Power | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrostatic** | Small (< 10 µm) | Low (µN) | Very fast (µs) | Negligible | DLP micromirror, RF switch |
| **Piezoelectric** | Sub-µm to µm | Medium (mN) | Very fast (ns) | Low | AFM scanner, inkjet printhead |
| **Thermal (Bimorph)** | Large (50+ µm) | Low (mN) | Slow (ms) | High (mW–W) | Micro-valve, micro-gripper |
| **Magnetic** | Very large (mm) | High (mN–N) | Medium (ms) | High (mW–W) | Hard-disk head, micro-pump |

**One-liner applications:**
- **Electrostatic →** Texas Instruments Digital Micromirror Device (DMD) for projection displays.
- **Piezoelectric →** Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) nanopositioning stage.
- **Thermal →** Thermo-pneumatic micro-valve in lab-on-chip.
- **Magnetic →** Voice-coil actuator in computer hard-disk drives.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the energy expression $U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$. This costs **1 full mark** in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing voltage-controlled (constant V) vs charge-controlled (constant Q) pull-in.** At constant V, pull-in is at $g_0/3$; at constant Q, it is at $g_0/2$. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Using $g$ instead of $(g_0 - x)$** when writing the capacitance as a function of displacement. The variable of differentiation is $x$, and the gap is reduced as the plate moves.
> 4. **In the numerical problem, not checking if $V_{applied} > V_{PI}$** before computing displacement. If yes, the system has snapped down and there is no static solution.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in the final answer. KTU examiners often deduct **0.5 mark** for missing units like V, N, µm.
> 6. **In comparison questions, providing descriptions instead of a structured table.** A table is worth 2–3 marks by itself; without it, marks are easily lost.
> 7. **In SMA questions, confusing austenite and martensite phases.** Austenite = high-temperature, high-stiffness, parent phase. Martensite = low-temperature, low-stiffness, twinned phase. Actuator is heated to convert martensite → austenite.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this list 30 minutes before entering the KTU ESE Hall — these are the highest-yield points.**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] A micro actuator converts **electrical/thermal/magnetic energy** into **mechanical motion** at the micro-scale.
- [ ] The **parallel-plate electrostatic actuator** is the most fundamental MEMS actuator.
- [ ] The **pull-in voltage** is the most-examined instability in electrostatic actuators: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8kg_0^3/(27\varepsilon A)}$.
- [ ] **Pull-in occurs at $x = g_0/3$** for constant-voltage bias.
- [ ] **Comb drives** avoid pull-in by having force **independent of displacement**.
- [ ] **Piezoelectric actuators** offer sub-nm precision but require high voltage or thin films.
- [ ] **Thermal bimorph actuators** offer the largest stroke but consume the most power.
- [ ] **Magnetic actuators** offer the highest force and longest stroke but require non-CMOS coils.
- [ ] **SMA (Nitinol)** actuators offer muscle-like strain (~8%) but slow response.

### Key Formulas (Memorize)
- [ ] Capacitance: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / g$
- [ ] Electrostatic force: $F = \frac{1}{2} V^2 (dC/dx)$
- [ ] Pull-in voltage: $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8kg_0^3/(27\varepsilon A)}$
- [ ] Comb-drive force: $F = N\varepsilon t V^2/(2d)$
- [ ] Resonant frequency: $f_0 = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$
- [ ] Cantilever tip stiffness: $k = Ewt^3/(4L^3)$
- [ ] Bimorph deflection: $\delta = 3L^2(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T / (2t)$
- [ ] Lorentz force: $F = BIL$

### Design Trade-offs to Mention
- [ ] Electrostatic: **low power, fast, small stroke, pull-in limited**
- [ ] Piezo: **high precision, low stroke, material-limited**
- [ ] Thermal: **large stroke, slow, high power**
- [ ] Magnetic: **high force, large stroke, high power, non-CMOS**
- [ ] SMA: **largest strain, slow, heating required**

### Numerical Problem Workflow
- [ ] List all given data with units.
- [ ] Identify which formula applies.
- [ ] Substitute and simplify step by step.
- [ ] Always verify the result's physical plausibility.
- [ ] Check $V_{applied}$ vs $V_{PI}$ before computing static displacement.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Do not confuse $g$ and $g_0$ in capacitance.
- [ ] Do not forget the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in $F = \frac{1}{2}V^2(dC/dx)$.
- [ ] Do not claim "no pull-in in comb drives" without specifying **lateral** direction.
- [ ] Do not use SI units inconsistently (mixing mm with m).
- [ ] In comparison answers, **always use a table** — it is worth 2–3 marks on its own.

### Cross-Module Connections
- [ ] **Module 1 (MEMS fundamentals):** Scaling laws — surface forces dominate, hence electrostatic actuation scales better than magnetic.
- [ ] **Module 2 (Fabrication):** Comb drives require **sacrificial layer release**; piezoelectric actuators require **sol-gel deposition or wafer bonding**.
- [ ] **Module 4 (Circuits & Systems):** Electrostatic actuators need **high-voltage driver circuits** (charge pumps); thermal actuators need **PWM current drivers**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuations Using Thermal Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Actuations Using Thermal Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Thermal Actuation in MEMS** refers to the class of micro-actuation mechanisms that exploit the **thermo-mechanical coupling** — specifically, the controlled expansion, contraction, or phase change of materials when subjected to a temperature gradient or Joule heating — to generate a mechanical displacement, force, or motion at the microscale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Thermal actuation converts electrical energy into heat (via Joule effect), and then converts this thermal energy into mechanical motion using the differential thermal expansion of structural materials, phase-change effects, or shape-memory phenomena. It is one of the *indirect* transduction mechanisms in MEMS, since the primary physical intermediate is *thermal energy*, not electrostatic or magnetic field energy.

### 1.2 Underlying Physical Principles

Thermal actuators leverage three principal physical phenomena:

1. **Differential Thermal Expansion** — Two materials with different Coefficients of Thermal Expansion (CTE, denoted $\alpha$ or $\beta$) expand by different amounts when heated, producing bending or extension.
2. **Joule Heating** — Resistive dissipation $P = I^2 R$ causes localized temperature rise.
3. **Phase/Memory Effects** — Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs) undergo martensite-to-austenite phase transition, producing large recoverable strains (up to 6–8 %).

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Bimetallic Strip

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Classic Bimetallic Thermostat:**
> Think of an old analog *iron thermostat* in your home. Two metal strips of brass and invar are riveted together. When the room gets hot, brass (high CTE) expands more than invar (low CTE). Because they are bonded, the strip is forced to *curl* into an arc. This curl is enough to break or close an electrical contact, switching the heater on or off.
>
> A MEMS thermal actuator is the **micro-miniaturized cousin** of this bimetallic strip — except that instead of room-temperature air, we pass an electric current through a thin-film resistor to do the heating, and the "switch" is a polysilicon beam that bends by *micrometers* rather than millimetres.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (Silicon) | $\alpha_{Si}$ | **2.6 × 10⁻⁶** | K⁻¹ |
| CTE (Aluminium) | $\alpha_{Al}$ | **23 × 10⁻⁶** | K⁻¹ |
| CTE (Polysilicon) | $\alpha_{polySi}$ | **2.8–3.2 × 10⁻⁶** | K⁻¹ |
| CTE (SU-8 polymer) | $\alpha_{SU-8}$ | **52 × 10⁻⁶** | K⁻¹ |
| Thermal conductivity of Silicon | $k_{Si}$ | **150** | W/m·K |
| Thermal conductivity of Air | $k_{air}$ | **0.026** | W/m·K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *mismatch* between $\alpha_{Si}$ ($\sim 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$) and $\alpha_{Al}$ ($\sim 23 \times 10^{-6}$) is nearly **a factor of 9**. This huge mismatch is exactly why bimorphs in MEMS almost always pair silicon (or polysilicon) with a metal — small temperature changes ($\Delta T \sim 100$ K) still yield usable deflections at the microscale.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Current Injection:** A controlled electrical current is passed through a microfabricated resistor (typically doped polysilicon, metal, or diffusion region).
2. **Joule Heating:** Power dissipation $P = I^2 R = V^2 / R$ converts electrical energy into heat *locally*, raising the temperature of the suspended microstructure.
3. **Thermal Expansion:** The heated material attempts to expand. Free thermal strain is $\epsilon_T = \alpha \, \Delta T$.
4. **Mechanical Constraint (Key for actuation):** The heated element is anchored at one end (or bonded to a low-CTE substrate), so expansion is converted into *bending* or *force*, not free length change.
5. **Heat Dissipation:** Heat is removed by conduction through the anchors and convection to the surrounding air/gas. A steady-state temperature rise $\Delta T$ is reached when input power equals heat loss.
6. **Mechanical Output:** The actuator produces either:
   - **Force** (when displacement is constrained) — typical 1–100 µN, **or**
   - **Displacement** (when free) — typical 0.1–20 µm.

### 2.2 Why Thermal Actuation? — The "Why"

| Property | Thermal | Electrostatic | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output Force | **High (µN–mN)** | Low (nN–µN) | Medium |
| Displacement | **Large (µm)** | Very small | Small (nm) |
| Drive Voltage | Low (5–20 V) | High (50–200 V) | High (50–500 V) |
| Power Consumption | **High (mW)** | Negligible | Low |
| Response Speed | Slow (ms) | Very fast (µs) | Fast |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Choose *thermal* actuation when you need **big force and big displacement at low voltage**; avoid it when you need **fast response** or **low power**.

### 2.3 Classification of Thermal Actuators

#### (a) Bimorph / Bimetal Cantilever
A cantilever beam composed of two materials with different $\alpha$. When heated, differential expansion produces a tip deflection $\delta$. Classic example: Si + Al cantilever.

#### (b) In-Plane Thermal Expansion Actuator (Hot-Arm Actuator)
A pair of asymmetric arms — a *thin hot arm* (high electrical resistance, high current, high $\Delta T$) and a *cold arm* (low resistance, low $\Delta T$). The hot arm expands more, pushing a slider/paddle. This is the workhorse of digital micromirrors and micro-grippers.

#### (c) Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuator
NiTi (Nitinol) wires/thin films undergo a reversible martensite ↔ austenite phase transition at a transition temperature $A_f$ (austenite finish). Recoverable strain up to **6–8 %** — *orders of magnitude* higher than thermal expansion.

#### (d) Thermal Bubble Actuator (Inkjet)
A thin-film resistor rapidly vaporizes ink, producing a bubble that ejects a droplet. Used in thermal inkjet printheads (HP, Canon).

#### (e) Phase-Change (Melting/Freezing) Actuator
Paraffin or low-melt-alloy expands on solid-liquid transition, providing large volume change.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: All formulas below are mandatory for KTU 2024 ESE problems. The vertical bar character `|` is intentionally avoided in table cells — use LaTeX `\vert` for absolute values and $\mid$ for "divides".**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\epsilon_T = \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\delta L = L_0 \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Free thermal elongation | m |
| 3 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{P \, R_{th}}{1}$ (steady state) | Temperature rise | K |
| 4 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{k \, A}$ | Thermal resistance of a slab | K/W |
| 5 | $P = I^2 R = V^2 / R$ | Joule heating power | W |
| 6 | $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{3 \, ( \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 ) \, \Delta T \, L^2}{2 \, t \, \left( 1 + \dfrac{E_1 t_1}{E_2 t_2} \right)}$ (bimorph) | Tip deflection of bimorph | m |
| 7 | $F_{block} = E \, A \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Blocked force (constrained thermal expansion) | N |
| 8 | $\sigma_T = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal stress (constrained) | Pa |
| 9 | $\kappa = \sqrt{ \dfrac{12 \, \sigma_T \, (1-\nu^2)}{E \, t^2} }$ | Curvature of bimetallic strip | m⁻¹ |
| 10 | $t_{response} \approx \dfrac{\rho c_p L^2}{k}$ | Thermal time constant (heat-up) | s |
| 11 | $\Delta x_{SMA} = L_0 \cdot \epsilon_{SMA}$ | SMA displacement (with $\epsilon_{SMA} \approx 0.06$) | m |
| 12 | $A_f = A_f^0 + \dfrac{\sigma}{C_A}$ | Clausius-Clapeyron shift in SMA transition temperature | K |

> **Boundary Condition Reminder:** For all steady-state thermal MEMS analysis, the *anchor temperature* is assumed to equal the substrate temperature $T_{sub}$ (Dirichlet boundary condition). The *surfaces* lose heat by convection: $q'' = h (T - T_{\infty})$ where $h$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient (typically $10$–$10^4$ W/m²·K in MEMS, depending on packaging).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Micro-grippers** for handling biological cells (e.g., sperm sorting).
- **RF MEMS switches** (thermal expansion closes the contact).
- **Adaptive optics / DMD (Digital Micromirror Devices)** — TI DMD uses electrostatic primarily, but thermal variants exist.
- **Thermal inkjet printheads** (HP, Canon, Epson consumer printers).
- **Optical bench alignment** — micro-positioners.
- **DNA stretching devices** in single-molecule biophysics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Free Thermal Strain and Elongation

**Starting principle:** For a small temperature change $\Delta T$, the *linear* thermal expansion of an isotropic solid is:

$$
\Delta L = L_0 \, \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

**Derivation logic:**

- At the atomic level, an increase in temperature increases the mean inter-atomic spacing.
- For *isotropic* materials, this manifests as linear expansion in *all* directions.
- The **strain** (dimensionless) is defined as $\epsilon_T \equiv \Delta L / L_0$.

$$
\epsilon_T \;=\; \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \;=\; \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

> **Engineering note:** This is valid only for *small* $\Delta T$ (typically $< 200$ K) where $\alpha$ is approximately constant. For larger temperature ranges, $\alpha$ becomes a function of $T$ and we must integrate.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bimorph Cantilever Tip Deflection

Consider a bilayer cantilever of length $L$, total thickness $t = t_1 + t_2$, with layers 1 (top) and 2 (bottom) having Young's moduli $E_1, E_2$, thicknesses $t_1, t_2$, and CTEs $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$. The structure is heated uniformly by $\Delta T$.

**Step 1 — Differential free expansion:**
Each layer, if *free*, would expand by:

$$
\delta L_1 = L \, \alpha_1 \, \Delta T, \qquad \delta L_2 = L \, \alpha_2 \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility (layers must remain bonded):**
The bonded pair must adopt a *common length*, so internal bending moments and forces develop. The bimorph bends into an arc of radius $R$.

**Step 3 — Curvature (Timoshenko bimetallic strip formula, 1925):**
Timoshenko derived the curvature $\kappa = 1/R$:

$$
\kappa \;=\; \frac{6 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, \Delta T \, (1 + m)^2}{h \, \left[ 3(1+m)^2 + (1 + mn) \left( m^2 + \dfrac{1}{mn} \right) \right]}
$$

where $m = t_1 / t_2$ and $n = E_1 / E_2$, and $h$ is the total thickness.

**Step 4 — Simplified for $m = 1$ and $n = 1$ (symmetric case):**

$$
\kappa \;=\; \frac{3 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, \Delta T}{2 \, t}
$$

**Step 5 — Tip deflection (small-angle approximation $\theta \approx L/R = \kappa L$):**

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{\kappa L^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{3 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, \Delta T \, L^2}{4 \, t}
$$

> **Refined form (asymmetric moduli):**
> The general compact form is:
> $$\delta_{tip} = \frac{3 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, \Delta T \, L^2}{2 t \left(1 + \dfrac{E_1 t_1}{E_2 t_2}\right)}$$
> This is the formula examiners expect you to quote for **unequal** layer thicknesses and moduli.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Temperature Rise of a Joule-Heated Beam

**Setup:** A suspended microbeam of length $L$, cross-section $A = w \cdot t$, electrical resistance $R_e$, suspended by two anchors at temperature $T_0$. Current $I$ flows through it.

**Heat balance (steady state):**
Power generated by Joule heating = Power lost by conduction through anchors.

$$
P = I^2 R_e = \frac{T_{beam} - T_0}{R_{th,anchor}}
$$

where the thermal resistance of the two anchor beams in parallel is:

$$
R_{th,anchor} = \frac{L_a}{2 \, k_{beam} \, A}
$$

(with $L_a$ the anchor length).

**Solving for $\Delta T$:**

$$
\Delta T \;=\; I^2 R_e \cdot \frac{L_a}{2 \, k_{beam} \, A}
$$

Substituting back into the bimorph formula:

$$
\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{3 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \, L^2 \, L_a \, I^2 R_e}{4 \, t \, k_{beam} \, A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The tip deflection of a thermal bimorph scales as the **square of the current** ($\delta \propto I^2$). This is the *fingerprint* of thermal actuation — it is highly nonlinear, which has implications for closed-loop control.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Blocked Force (Constrained Thermal Expansion)

When the actuator is *restrained* from moving (e.g., gripping an object), the thermal expansion generates a **force** instead of displacement. The mechanical strain $\epsilon_M$ must exactly cancel the thermal strain $\epsilon_T$:

$$
\epsilon_M + \epsilon_T = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \epsilon_M = -\alpha \, \Delta T
$$

The corresponding stress (uniaxial, free lateral expansion) is:

$$
\sigma_T = E \, \epsilon_M = -E \, \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

The **blocked force** is:

$$
F_{block} = \sigma_T \cdot A = E \, A \, \alpha \, \Delta T
$$

**Numerical sanity check (typical Si micro-beam):**
- $E_{Si} = 170$ GPa
- $A = 2\,\mu\text{m} \times 50\,\mu\text{m} = 10^{-10}$ m²
- $\alpha = 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹
- $\Delta T = 200$ K

$$
F_{block} = 170 \times 10^9 \cdot 10^{-10} \cdot 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 200 \;\approx\; 8.8 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{N} = 88 \; \mu\text{N}
$$

This matches the **typical µN-scale force** quoted in MEMS literature. ✓

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Displacement

For NiTi (Nitinol) wires/films, the recoverable strain upon heating above the austenite-finish temperature $A_f$ is:

$$
\epsilon_{SMA} \;\approx\; 0.06 \;\;(\text{i.e. } 6\%)
$$

The displacement of an SMA actuator of length $L_0$ is therefore:

$$
\Delta x_{SMA} = L_0 \cdot \epsilon_{SMA} = 0.06 \, L_0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** If a silicon beam ($L_0 = 1$ mm) were heated by $\Delta T = 200$ K, its free thermal expansion would only be $L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 1000 \cdot 2.6 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 200 = 0.52 \,\mu\text{m}$. An SMA wire of the same length gives $60\,\mu\text{m}$ — **a 100× larger displacement**. This is why SMA is preferred when large stroke is required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) and state its typical values for silicon and aluminium. Mention how differential CTE is exploited in a MEMS bimorph actuator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE), denoted $\alpha$, is defined as the fractional change in length per unit change in temperature:

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{L_0} \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{\Delta T} \quad [\text{K}^{-1}]
$$

**Typical values (1 mark):**
- Silicon: $\alpha_{Si} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹
- Aluminium: $\alpha_{Al} \approx 23 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹

**Bimorph exploitation (1 mark):** A bimorph cantilever is made by bonding two materials with *different* $\alpha$ values (e.g., polysilicon and aluminium). When the structure is heated, the layer with higher $\alpha$ expands more, forcing the cantilever to bend. This differential expansion is converted into a controllable tip deflection, which is the basis of thermal actuation in MEMS.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) actuator? Why is the displacement produced by an SMA actuator much larger than that produced by a conventional thermal bimorph?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) actuator uses a material (typically Ni–Ti / Nitinol) that can be plastically deformed in its low-temperature *martensite* phase and recovers its original shape upon heating above the *austenite finish* temperature $A_f$.

**Mechanism (1 mark):** Heating above $A_f$ triggers a solid-to-solid phase transformation from martensite to austenite, producing a recoverable strain of about 6–8 %.

**Comparison (1 mark):** A conventional bimorph relies on ordinary thermal expansion with $\epsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T \sim 10^{-3}$, whereas an SMA actuator produces $\epsilon_{SMA} \approx 0.06$, which is roughly **two orders of magnitude larger** for the same actuator length. This is why SMAs are preferred in applications needing large stroke (e.g., surgical tools, deployable space structures).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two full alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the steady-state temperature rise $\Delta T$ of a Joule-heated suspended microbeam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, electrical resistance $R_e$, and two anchors each of length $L_a$ and thermal conductivity $k$. Assume the anchors are the dominant heat-loss path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MEMS bimorph cantilever is made of polysilicon (layer 1, $\alpha_1 = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹, $E_1 = 160$ GPa, $t_1 = 2$ µm) and aluminium (layer 2, $\alpha_2 = 23 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹, $E_2 = 70$ GPa, $t_2 = 1$ µm). The cantilever is $L = 500$ µm long and is heated by $\Delta T = 150$ K. Calculate the tip deflection. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the energy balance. In steady state, the electrical power dissipated by Joule heating equals the rate of heat conducted away through the anchors:

$$
P_{Joule} = P_{conduction}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express the Joule heating power:

$$
P_{Joule} = I^2 R_e
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Express the conduction heat flow. The two anchors are in *parallel* thermally. The thermal resistance of a single anchor (slab conduction) is:

$$
R_{th,1} = \frac{L_a}{k \, A}
$$

where $A = w \cdot t$ is the cross-section. The two anchors in parallel give:

$$
R_{th,total} = \frac{R_{th,1}}{2} = \frac{L_a}{2 k A}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Heat flow through the anchors:

$$
P_{conduction} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th,total}} = \frac{2 k A \, \Delta T}{L_a}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Equate and solve for $\Delta T$:

$$
I^2 R_e = \frac{2 k A \, \Delta T}{L_a}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta T = \frac{I^2 R_e L_a}{2 k A}}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Discussion / boundary conditions. The anchor temperature is fixed at substrate temperature $T_0$ (Dirichlet BC). Surfaces lose additional heat by convection $q'' = h (T - T_\infty)$, often neglected if anchors are dominant.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Note physical insight. $\Delta T$ scales as $L_a$, so shorter, thicker anchors *reduce* the temperature rise needed for a given input — useful design rule for low-power thermal actuators.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 marks**

**Given data (1 mark):**
- $\alpha_1 = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹, $E_1 = 160$ GPa, $t_1 = 2$ µm = $2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\alpha_2 = 23 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹, $E_2 = 70$ GPa, $t_2 = 1$ µm = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $L = 500$ µm = $500 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\Delta T = 150$ K

**Formula to use (1 mark):**

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{3 (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \, \Delta T \, L^2}{2 \, t \, \left(1 + \dfrac{E_2 t_2}{E_1 t_1}\right)}
$$

(Note: layer 1 is the *top* in many conventions; the formula remains valid as long as the *differential* expansion is taken with the correct sign. Here we use $|\alpha_2 - \alpha_1|$ for magnitude.)

**Step 1 — Calculate total thickness (1 mark):**

$$
t = t_1 + t_2 = 2 + 1 = 3 \; \mu\text{m} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the modular ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{E_2 t_2}{E_1 t_1} = \frac{70 \times 10^9 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6}}{160 \times 10^9 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{70}{320} = 0.21875
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the bracket term (1 mark):**

$$
1 + 0.21875 = 1.21875
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in all values (1 mark):**

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{3 \cdot (23 - 3) \times 10^{-6} \cdot 150 \cdot (500 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.21875}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerator evaluation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Num} = 3 \cdot 20 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 150 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-8} = 3 \cdot 20 \cdot 150 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
= 225000 \times 10^{-14} = 2.25 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Step 6 — Denominator evaluation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Den} = 2 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.21875 = 7.3125 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result (1 mark):**

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-9}}{7.3125 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.077 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} \approx 308 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

> **Result:** The bimorph tip deflects by approximately **308 µm**, which is a *very large* deflection — about 60 % of the cantilever length. The small-angle approximation may break down here, and a more exact Timoshenko formula should be used for design accuracy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of an in-plane *hot-arm* thermal expansion actuator. State two design rules to maximise the deflection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A suspended polysilicon microbeam of length 1000 µm, width 5 µm, thickness 2 µm is used as a thermal actuator. A current of 5 mA is passed through it, and the beam resistance is measured as 1.2 kΩ. The two anchors, each 200 µm long, are the dominant heat-loss path. The thermal conductivity of polysilicon is 150 W/m·K. Calculate the steady-state temperature rise $\Delta T$ of the beam. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Hot-Arm Actuator — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Schematic description (2 marks):**

The in-plane hot-arm actuator (also called an *asymmetric* thermal actuator) consists of two parallel polysilicon arms anchored at one end and joined to a movable *paddle* at the other. The two arms are designed to have *very different electrical resistances* and *very different cross-sectional areas*:

- **Hot arm:** thin and narrow → high $R_e$ → large $I^2R$ → high $\Delta T$ → expands *more*.
- **Cold arm:** wide and thick → low $R_e$ → low $\Delta T$ → expands *little*.

Because the two arms are joined at the paddle, the differential expansion pushes the paddle laterally, producing an in-plane displacement.

**Step 2 — Working principle (2 marks):**

When a voltage $V$ is applied across the anchors, current flows preferentially through the low-resistance cold arm (current divider). The hot arm still carries appreciable current (due to its finite resistance) and dissipates more heat per unit volume. The hot arm elongates by $\Delta L_{hot} = L \alpha \Delta T_{hot}$ while the cold arm elongates much less. The *difference* is the actuator's output stroke.

**Step 3 — Two design rules for maximum deflection (3 marks):**

1. **Maximise the temperature asymmetry:** Make the hot arm *as thin as possible* and the cold arm *as wide as possible* to maximise $\Delta T_{hot} - \Delta T_{cold}$.
2. **Use long arms:** Stroke $\propto L$ (for unimorph) or $\propto L^2$ (for bimorph).
3. **Minimise anchor conduction loss** so that the Joule heat is retained in the hot arm (use long, thin anchors).
4. **Use materials with large differential CTE** if a bimorph design is chosen (e.g., Si + Al, or polysilicon + Au).

---

**(b) Numerical Problem — 7 marks**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $L = 1000$ µm $= 10^{-3}$ m (beam length, not needed in this formula but stated)
- $w = 5$ µm $= 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $t = 2$ µm $= 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $I = 5$ mA $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ A
- $R_e = 1.2$ kΩ $= 1200$ Ω
- $L_a = 200$ µm $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m (each anchor)
- $k = 150$ W/m·K

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{I^2 R_e L_a}{2 k w t}
$$

**Step 1 — Joule power (1 mark):**

$$
P = I^2 R_e = (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 1200 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1200 = 0.03 \; \text{W} = 30 \; \text{mW}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area (1 mark):**

$$
A = w \cdot t = 5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-11} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Conduction heat transfer per unit $\Delta T$ (1 mark):**

$$
P_{cond} = \frac{2 k A \, \Delta T}{L_a} = \frac{2 \cdot 150 \cdot 10^{-11} \cdot \Delta T}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve (1 mark):**

$$
0.03 = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{0.03}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2000 \; \text{K}
$$

**Step 5 — Discussion / sanity check (2 marks):**

> [!WARNING]
> **This answer is physically unreasonable!** A temperature rise of 2000 K would *melt* polysilicon (which melts at $\sim 1685$ K). The model assumption that *anchors are the only heat-loss path* breaks down; radiation and convection must be included. A more realistic answer would require an iterative design correction. KTU students should comment on this — examiners reward engineering judgement.

**Refined estimate (commentary, not for marks):** Including radiation ($q_{rad} = \epsilon \sigma T^4$ with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴) and natural convection ($h \sim 10$–$100$ W/m²·K), the steady-state $\Delta T$ in practice is typically **100–400 K** for such a device, which is consistent with the assumption used in 5.2 (b) above.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in thermal-actuator problems (deduct 1–2 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 3/2 in the bimorph tip-deflection formula** — quoting only the *curvature* result and forgetting to integrate once more for tip deflection.
> 2. **Mixing up layer indices in the differential $\alpha$** — the formula uses $(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)$, not the absolute sum. Take care with sign.
> 3. **Unit errors in $L_a / (kA)$** — thermal resistance has units K/W, not W/K. Cross-check by dimensional analysis: $[L] / ([k][A]) = \text{m} / (\text{W/m·K} \cdot \text{m}^2) = \text{m}^2 \text{K} / \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-1} = \text{K/W}$. ✓
> 4. **Treating the anchors as in series** instead of *parallel* — both anchors conduct heat simultaneously, halving the thermal resistance.
> 5. **Neglecting the $E_2 t_2 / (E_1 t_1)$ term in the bimorph formula** when the layer stiffnesses differ. Always include the term in the denominator.
> 6. **Forgetting that $\Delta T$ depends on $I^2$** — examiners love to ask why thermal actuators are *nonlinear* in current.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** Thermal actuation uses Joule heating + thermal expansion (or phase change) to produce mechanical motion.
- [ ] **Three core phenomena:** Differential thermal expansion, Joule heating, and shape-memory phase transition.
- [ ] **Free thermal strain:** $\epsilon_T = \alpha \, \Delta T$.
- [ ] **Blocked force (uniaxial):** $F = E A \alpha \Delta T$.
- [ ] **Bimorph tip deflection (Timoshenko):** $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{3(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T L^2}{2 t (1 + E_2 t_2 / (E_1 t_1))}$.
- [ ] **Steady-state temperature rise:** $\Delta T = \dfrac{I^2 R_e L_a}{2 k w t}$ (anchors dominant).
- [ ] **Two anchors are in *parallel* thermally** — divide their individual thermal resistance by 2.
- [ ] **Joule heating is proportional to $I^2$** → thermal actuators are *quadratically* nonlinear in current.
- [ ] **Thermal actuator $\delta \propto I^2$** — a *fingerprint* property.
- [ ] **Typical displacement range:** 0.1 – 20 µm (bimorph), up to 100+ µm (SMA).
- [ ] **Typical force range:** 1 – 100 µN (bimorph), up to mN (SMA).
- [ ] **Typical drive voltage:** 5 – 20 V (low voltage, unlike electrostatic).
- [ ] **Typical response time:** ms-scale (slow, due to thermal time constant $t_{response} \sim \rho c_p L^2 / k$).
- [ ] **SMA strain** $\approx 6\%$, i.e. **100× larger** than ordinary thermal expansion.
- [ ] **CTE of silicon** $\approx 2.6 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹; **CTE of aluminium** $\approx 23 \times 10^{-6}$ K⁻¹.
- [ ] **Vacuum packaging** eliminates convective loss → thermal actuators use *less power* in vacuum.
- [ ] **Applications:** micro-grippers, RF switches, inkjet printheads, adaptive optics, micro-positioners.
- [ ] **Advantages:** large force & displacement, low drive voltage, simple fabrication.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** high power consumption, slow response, nonlinear behaviour, heat-management challenges.

> **Final Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"compare thermal and electrostatic actuation"*, immediately mention: (i) voltage (low vs. high), (ii) force (high vs. low), (iii) speed (slow vs. fast), (iv) power (high vs. negligible), (v) nonlinearity (quadratic in $I$ vs. quadratic in $V$). Examiners love a clean comparison table.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape Memory Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shape Memory Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** is a class of smart/functional metallic materials that exhibit the ability to recover their original predetermined shape after being subjected to significant plastic (permanent) deformation when exposed to a specific thermal or mechanical stimulus. This unique phenomenon is known as the **Shape Memory Effect (SME)**, which is fundamentally driven by a reversible, solid-state, diffusionless crystallographic phase transition known as **Thermoelastic Martensitic Transformation (TMT)**.

In the context of **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)**, SMAs are categorized as **active smart materials** because they can convert thermal energy directly into mechanical work (actuation) or convert mechanical stress into a measurable electrical/pseudoelastic response (sensing). They are integral to the fabrication of micro-actuators, micro-grippers, and micro-valves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEAET864 Module 3):** Shape Memory Alloys are a sub-topic under **Smart Materials** in MEMS. You must understand (a) the two crystallographic phases, (b) the four critical transformation temperatures, and (c) the difference between the One-Way Effect (OWE) and Two-Way Effect (TWE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **crumpled aluminum foil "robot arm"** that is permanently bent. Now, instead of throwing it away, you **heat it gently with a hair dryer** — and astonishingly, the arm **snaps back to its original straight shape on its own**! That is the Shape Memory Effect.

At the microscopic level, the atoms of the alloy exist in one of two arrangements:
- **Cold state (Martensite):** The atoms are arranged in a "twin" configuration, which makes the alloy soft, ductile, and easy to bend. Think of a deck of cards lying flat — you can flex it.
- **Hot state (Austenite):** The atoms snap into a tighter, more symmetric arrangement. When this happens, all the bent "twins" re-orient to their original "un-bent" master configuration, pulling the macroscopic shape back to its original geometry.

So basically: **heat it → it remembers its past shape → it moves.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A normal metal when bent *permanently* deforms. An SMA, when bent, only *appears* deformed at low temperature. Heating it triggers an internal atomic "recall" that restores the original shape.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU 2024)

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Material Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Density of **Nitinol (Ni-Ti)** | **6.45 g/cm³** | Most common SMA |
| Transformation Strain | **≈ 6% – 8%** (recoverable) | Nitinol single crystal |
| Recoverable Stress | **100 – 800 MPa** | Nitinol |
| Latent Heat of Transformation | **≈ 19 kJ/kg** | Ni-Ti alloy |
| Electrical Resistivity (Austenite) | **≈ 100 μΩ·cm** | Nitinol |
| Young's Modulus (Austenite) | **≈ 30 – 90 GPa** | Nitinol |
| Typical Activation Temperature | **60 °C – 110 °C** | Biomedical / Actuator Ni-Ti |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Nitinol?** Discovered in 1959 by Buehler and Wang at the U.S. Naval Ordnance Laboratory, **Ni-Ti (Nitinol)** is the most widely used SMA because of its excellent fatigue resistance, biocompatibility, and high recoverable strain (~8%), making it ideal for MEMS and biomedical microdevices.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Crystallographic Phases

The entire behavior of an SMA is governed by reversible switching between two solid-state phases:

**A. Austenite Phase (Parent / High-Temperature Phase)**
- Crystallographic structure: **Body-Centered Tetragonal (BCT)** in Ni-Ti, often simplified as a **B2 (CsCl-type ordered cubic)** structure.
- Exists **above** the austenite finish temperature ($A_f$).
- Stiff, strong, and has a single, well-defined orientation variant.
- This is the "memory" — the geometry the alloy wants to return to.

**B. Martensite Phase (Product / Low-Temperature Phase)**
- Crystallographic structure: **Monoclinic (B19')** in Ni-Ti.
- Exists **below** the martensite finish temperature ($M_f$).
- Soft, ductile, and can exist in **24 possible self-accommodating twin variants** (crystallographic orientations).
- Easy to deform by re-orienting / detwinning these variants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The martensite formed in SMAs is **thermoelastic martensite** — it forms gradually with decreasing temperature and disappears reversibly on heating. This is fundamentally different from the **burst-type (irreversible)** martensite in plain carbon steels.

### 2.2 The Four Critical Transformation Temperatures

A KTU-favorite question is to draw/label the **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) curve**. Memorize these four temperatures:

| Symbol | Meaning | Phase Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $M_s$ | **Martensite Start** | Austenite begins → Martensite on cooling |
| $M_f$ | **Martensite Finish** | Transformation to Martensite completes |
| $A_s$ | **Austenite Start** | Martensite begins → Austenite on heating |
| $A_f$ | **Austenite Finish** | Transformation to Austenite completes |

The natural ordering is:
$$M_f < M_s < A_s < A_f$$

> [!NOTE]
> The gap between $A_s$ and $M_s$ defines the **thermal hysteresis** of the SMA. For Nitinol, this hysteresis is typically **30 °C – 50 °C**.

### 2.3 The Three Functional Effects (High-Yield for KTU)

| Effect | Stimulus | Description | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **One-Way Shape Memory Effect (OWE)** | Heat only | Material "remembers" only its high-temperature (austenite) shape. Cold shape must be set externally each cycle. | Fire-safety sprinklers, vascular stents |
| **Two-Way Shape Memory Effect (TWE)** | Heat and Cool alternately | Material remembers *both* hot and cold shapes. Achieved through special thermomechanical training. | MEMS micro-actuators, cyclic valves |
| **Pseudoelasticity (Superelasticity)** | Stress at constant $T > A_f$ | Large recoverable strain (up to 8%) upon loading/unloading, *without* any temperature change. | Eyeglass frames, orthodontic wires |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Clausius-Clapeyron: $\dfrac{d\sigma}{dT} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T \cdot \varepsilon_{tr}}$ | Stress-temperature equivalence (drives actuation) | MPa/°C |
| Transformation Strain: $\varepsilon_{tr} \approx 0.067$ (Nitinol) | Maximum recoverable strain | Dimensionless |
| Curie Temperature: $T_c$ ≈ 100 °C (Nitinol) | Limit above which SME is lost | °C |
| Hysteresis: $\Delta T = A_f - M_s$ | Width of thermal hysteresis loop | °C |
| Clausius-Clapeyron (alternative form): $\sigma = \sigma_0 + \beta(T - T_0)$ | Critical stress to induce martensite | MPa |
| Phase fraction rule: $f_A + f_M = 1$ | Sum of phase fractions is unity | — |
| Latent Heat: $L = T_{eq} \cdot \Delta S$ | Energy absorbed/released per transformation | J/kg |
| Thermal energy for actuation: $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat to raise SMA above $A_f$ | J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA for KTU problems:** The Clausius-Clapeyron relation shows that an applied stress is *thermodynamically equivalent* to a temperature change. This is why an SMA can be triggered either by heat or by mechanical load — they are conjugate variables in the free energy equation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in MEMS

- **Micro-actuators:** SMAs are deposited as thin films (e.g., sputtered Ti-Ni) on silicon substrates. When Joule-heated by passing a current, the film contracts and bends the substrate, producing displacement in the micrometer range with high force density.
- **Micro-grippers:** Used in surgical robotics for grasping micron-scale objects.
- **Micro-valves:** For fluidic MEMS, especially in biomedical Lab-on-Chip systems.
- **Vibration dampers / shock absorbers:** Pseudoelastic SMAs dissipate energy via stress-induced martensite formation.
- **Aerospace:** Morphing wings in micro-aircraft (MAVs) use SMA wires as artificial "muscles."

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Clausius-Clapeyron Slope for SMA Actuation

**Goal:** Show that an applied mechanical stress shifts the critical transformation temperature linearly. This is the *thermodynamic engine* of every SMA actuator.

**Step 1 — Define the thermodynamic potentials.**
The Gibbs free energy of each phase determines stability. For martensitic transformation under stress $\sigma$ at temperature $T$:

$$G_A(T, \sigma) = G_M(T, \sigma)$$

at thermodynamic equilibrium. The phase with the lower $G$ is stable.

**Step 2 — Express the equilibrium temperature at zero stress.**
Let $T_0$ be the equilibrium transformation temperature (also called the *transus temperature* or $T_{eq}$) at $\sigma = 0$. At this point:

$$G_A(T_0, 0) = G_M(T_0, 0)$$

**Step 3 — Introduce an applied stress.**
The applied stress does work on the system. The mechanical work density is $W = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon_{tr}$, where $\varepsilon_{tr}$ is the transformation strain. The new equilibrium condition becomes:

$$G_A(T, \sigma) - G_M(T, \sigma) = 0$$

Expanding each $G$ around $(T_0, 0)$ using a first-order Taylor series:

$$G_A = G_A(T_0, 0) + \left(\frac{\partial G_A}{\partial T}\right)_{T_0}(T - T_0) - S_A \sigma \varepsilon_A$$

$$G_M = G_M(T_0, 0) + \left(\frac{\partial G_M}{\partial T}\right)_{T_0}(T - T_0) - S_M \sigma \varepsilon_M$$

where the negative mechanical work term follows the convention that applied stress *lowers* the free energy of the favored phase.

**Step 4 — Apply the equilibrium condition.**
Subtracting and using $G_A(T_0, 0) = G_M(T_0, 0)$:

$$-(S_A - S_M)(T - T_0) = \sigma(\varepsilon_A - \varepsilon_M)$$

Defining $\Delta S = S_A - S_M$ and $\varepsilon_{tr} = \varepsilon_M - \varepsilon_A$:

$$-\Delta S \cdot (T - T_0) = -\sigma \cdot \varepsilon_{tr}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for stress as a function of temperature.**

$$\sigma = \frac{\Delta S}{\varepsilon_{tr}} (T - T_0)$$

Define the **stress-influence coefficient** $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta S}{\varepsilon_{tr}}$. Then:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \beta \, (T - T_0)}$$

This is the famous **Clausius-Clapeyron linear relation for SMAs**. It states that the critical stress to induce martensite increases linearly with temperature. For Nitinol, $\beta \approx 6.7$ MPa/°C.

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**
- For every **1 °C increase** in operating temperature, the critical stress for martensite induction rises by ~6.7 MPa.
- Conversely, **applying a stress of 67 MPa** raises the effective $M_s$ temperature by **10 °C** — meaning the alloy transforms to martensite at a *higher* temperature than it normally would.
- This explains both **shape memory** (heat-driven) and **pseudoelasticity** (stress-driven at $T > A_f$).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Phase vs. Stimulus Behavior

| Initial State | Applied Stimulus | Final Phase | Macroscopic Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Austenite ($T > A_f$) | **Cool below $M_f$** | Self-accommodated Martensite | No shape change; soft & detwinnable |
| Martensite ($T < M_f$) | **Apply stress → detwin** | Detwinned Martensite | Visible macroscopic strain ($\sim$6%) |
| Detwinned Martensite | **Heat above $A_f$** | Austenite | **Shape Memory Effect — original shape returns** |
| Austenite ($T > A_f$) | **Apply stress** | Stress-induced Martensite (SIM) | **Pseudoelasticity** (loading plateau) |
| Stress-induced Martensite (at $T > A_f$) | **Remove stress** | Austenite | **Superelastic recovery** to original shape |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A Nitinol SMA wire is used as a micro-actuator. The equilibrium transformation temperature is $T_0 = 25$ °C, and the stress-influence coefficient is $\beta = 6.7$ MPa/°C. The device must produce an actuation stress of 200 MPa. Find (a) the temperature to which the wire must be heated, and (b) the transformation strain energy stored per unit volume.

**Solution (a):**
Using the derived Clausius-Clapeyron formula:

$$T = T_0 + \frac{\sigma}{\beta} = 25 + \frac{200}{6.7} = 25 + 29.85 = 54.85 \text{ °C}$$

**Solution (b):**
The transformation strain energy density is:

$$w = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \cdot \varepsilon_{tr} = \frac{1}{2} (200 \times 10^6)(0.067) = 6.7 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This work density (~6.7 MJ/m³) is **~10× higher** than that of a typical human muscle (~0.04 MJ/m³), which is why SMAs are called "artificial muscles" in MEMS.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Phase Transformation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: SMA at High Temperature T greater than Af] --> A1[Parent Austenite Phase B2 Cubic]
    A1 --> A2{Cool below Ms}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Twinned Martensite B19 Prime - Soft - Detwinnable]
    A2 -- No --> A1
    A3 --> A4{Apply Mechanical Stress}
    A4 -- Yes --> A5[Detwinned Martensite - Visible Macroscopic Strain]
    A4 -- No --> A3
    A5 --> A6{Heat above Af}
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Austenite Restored - Original Shape Recovers]
    A6 -- No --> A5
    A1 --> B1{Apply Stress at T greater than Af}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Stress Induced Martensite SIM]
    B2 --> B3{Remove Stress}
    B3 -- Yes --> A1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Critical Temperature Hierarchy (DSC Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Cooling Path] --> P2[High T Austenite Region]
    P2 --> P3[Reach Ms - Martensite begins to nucleate]
    P3 --> P4[Reach Mf - Fully Twinned Martensite]
    P4 --> P5[Heating Path Starts]
    P5 --> P6[Reach As - Reverse transformation begins]
    P6 --> P7[Reach Af - Fully Austenite Restored]
    P7 --> P8[Thermal Hysteresis Gap = Af minus Ms]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Three Functional Effects Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Shape Memory Alloy Functional Modes] --> R1[One Way Effect OWE]
    R0 --> R2[Two Way Effect TWE]
    R0 --> R3[Pseudoelasticity SE]

    R1 --> R1A[Deform at low T]
    R1A --> R1B[Heat above Af]
    R1B --> R1C[Original Hot Shape Recovers]
    R1C --> R1D[To re-bend must apply external force again]

    R2 --> R2A[Special training cycles required]
    R2A --> R2B[Remembers BOTH cold and hot shapes]
    R2B --> R2C[Cyclic actuation possible by heating and cooling]

    R3 --> R3A[Operate at T greater than Af]
    R3A --> R3B[Apply Stress]
    R3B --> R3C[Large recoverable strain up to 8 percent]
    R3C --> R3D[Remove Stress]
    R3D --> R3E[Shape returns to original - No heat needed]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: SMA MEMS Micro-Actuator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Power Supply DC] --> S1[Joule Heating Element]
    S1 --> S2[SMA Thin Film Deposited on Silicon Cantilever]
    S2 --> S3[Phase Change to Austenite]
    S3 --> S4[Cantilever Bends - Mechanical Displacement]
    S4 --> S5[Micro Gripper or Micro Valve Actuates]
    S5 --> S6[Cut Power - SMA Cools]
    S6 --> S7[Return to Martensite Phase]
    S7 --> S8[Reset Position Achieved]
    S8 --> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Follow the arrows in sequence. In the first diagram, any path that returns to `A1` (Austenite) represents recovery. The hysteresis gap shown in the second diagram is the *irreversibility region* — a key source of energy loss in cyclic SMA actuators.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
Define **Shape Memory Effect (SME)**. Name the two crystallographic phases involved in the transformation of a Shape Memory Alloy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The **Shape Memory Effect (SME)** is the ability of a metallic alloy to recover its original, pre-deformed shape upon heating above a critical temperature, after being plastically deformed at a lower temperature.
- **(1 mark)** The two phases are:
  - **Austenite (Parent phase):** The high-temperature, high-symmetry, ordered cubic (B2) phase. This is the "remembered" shape.
  - **Martensite (Product phase):** The low-temperature, lower-symmetry (monoclinic B19' in Ni-Ti) phase. It exists in 24 self-accommodating twin variants, allowing easy deformation by detwinning.
- **(1 mark)** The transformation between them is a **diffusionless, displacive (shear-dominant) martensitic transformation** — atoms move cooperatively over distances smaller than an interatomic spacing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
List the **four critical transformation temperatures** of an SMA and arrange them in order. What does the gap between $A_s$ and $M_s$ represent?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1.5 marks)** The four critical temperatures are:
  1. **$M_s$** — Martensite Start (cooling)
  2. **$M_f$** — Martensite Finish (cooling)
  3. **$A_s$** — Austenite Start (heating)
  4. **$A_f$** — Austenite Finish (heating)
- **(0.5 mark)** Order: $M_f < M_s < A_s < A_f$.
- **(1 mark)** The gap $(A_s - M_s)$ or $(A_f - M_s)$ represents the **thermal hysteresis** of the SMA — a measure of the energy dissipated per thermomechanical cycle. For Nitinol, this is typically 30–50 °C.

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Note:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, Module 3 internal choice is given between two 14-mark questions. Both choices are provided below with complete model solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `OR` — Full Model Solution

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model QP, PEAET864]**
**(a)** With a neat labeled stress–strain diagram, explain the **Pseudoelasticity (Superelasticity)** behavior of a Shape Memory Alloy operating at $T > A_f$. Discuss the loading and unloading plateaus and the role of stress-induced martensite (SIM). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation** for SMAs and explain how it governs the **coupling between temperature and stress** in SMA actuators. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pseudoelasticity — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pseudoelasticity (or superelasticity) is the ability of an SMA to undergo large recoverable strain (up to ~8%) upon loading at a temperature $T > A_f$, with complete strain recovery upon unloading, **without any change in temperature**.

**Stress–Strain Diagram (2 marks):** At $T > A_f$, the stress–strain curve of an SMA exhibits three distinct regions:

1. **Elastic Region (Austenite):** Initial linear elastic loading of the parent austenite phase (Young's Modulus ~30–90 GPa for Nitinol).
2. **Stress Plateau (Loading):** Once the critical stress $\sigma_{SIM}$ (Stress-Induced Martensite start) is reached, the curve becomes nearly flat. During this plateau, the austenite progressively transforms into martensite by detwinning. This is a **constant-stress phase transformation**, not plastic yielding.
3. **Elastic Region (Martensite):** After full transformation, the detwinned martensite elastically loads until unloading begins.
4. **Unloading Plateau:** Upon unloading, the martensite is **thermodynamically unstable** at $T > A_f$ and reverts to austenite along a **lower stress plateau**, producing a large hysteresis loop.
5. **Final Recovery:** The strain returns to zero, and the specimen regains its original austenite shape.

**Role of SIM (2 marks):**
- Stress-Induced Martensite is a metastable martensite phase created purely by mechanical stress, without any change in temperature.
- Its nucleation is governed by the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation** — raising the applied stress is equivalent to lowering the temperature below $M_s$.
- On unloading, because the operating temperature is above $A_f$, the SIM is *not* thermodynamically stable and reverts to austenite, producing the "superelastic" spring-back.
- The area enclosed by the loading–unloading loop is the **dissipated energy per cycle**, useful for **damping applications**.

**Engineering Use (2 marks):** Pseudoelastic SMAs are used in:
- Orthodontic archwires (constant force over a wide displacement range).
- Eyeglass frames and stents.
- Seismic vibration dampers in civil structures.
- MEMS shock absorbers.

---

**(b) Clausius-Clapeyron Derivation — 7 marks**

**Statement (1 mark):** The Clausius-Clapeyron relation for SMAs states that the critical stress required to induce martensitic transformation is a **linear function of temperature**.

**Starting point (1 mark):** The Gibbs free energies of the two phases must be equal at equilibrium:
$$G_A(T, \sigma) = G_M(T, \sigma)$$

**Taylor expansion (2 marks):** Expanding around the zero-stress equilibrium temperature $T_0$:

$$-(S_A - S_M)(T - T_0) = \sigma(\varepsilon_M - \varepsilon_A)$$

**Final form (2 marks):** Defining $\Delta S = S_A - S_M$, $\varepsilon_{tr} = \varepsilon_M - \varepsilon_A$, and $\beta = \Delta S / \varepsilon_{tr}$:

$$\sigma = \beta (T - T_0)$$

**Engineering significance (1 mark):** This equation reveals the **dual-trigger nature** of SMAs:
- A **thermal trigger** (heat above $A_f$): restores austenite, recovers shape.
- A **mechanical trigger** (stress at $T > A_f$): induces SIM, enabling pseudoelasticity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `OR` — Full Model Solution

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model QP, PEAET864]**
**(a)** Compare **One-Way Effect (OWE)** and **Two-Way Effect (TWE)** in SMAs. Explain the **thermomechanical training** required to achieve TWE with a stress–temperature phase diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **applications of SMAs in MEMS**, with specific examples of micro-actuators and biomedical microdevices. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) OWE vs. TWE — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | One-Way Effect (OWE) | Two-Way Effect (TWE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Shapes Remembered** | Only the hot (austenite) shape | Both hot and cold shapes |
| **Cycling Stimulus** | Only heating | Heating AND cooling |
| **Training Required** | None (intrinsic) | Specialized thermomechanical training |
| **Strain Range** | Larger (up to 8%) | Smaller (typically 2–4%) |
| **Cyclic Stability** | Excellent | Degrades with cycles |
| **Typical Use** | One-time deployment (stents, sprinklers) | Cyclic actuators (valves, grippers) |

**Mechanism of OWE (1 mark):** In the OWE, the SMA "remembers" only its high-temperature austenite shape. At low temperature, the martensite can be deformed into *any* shape by detwinning. Upon heating above $A_f$, the alloy reverts to austenite and recovers the original memorized geometry. To repeat the cycle, the user must re-deform the alloy externally at low temperature.

**Mechanism of TWE (2 marks):** In the TWE, the alloy spontaneously alternates between two distinct shapes on heating and cooling. This is achieved through **thermomechanical training**, which involves:
- Repeated cycles of stress + temperature (e.g., 20–50 cycles).
- The training creates a population of **oriented dislocation networks** that act as internal stress fields, biasing the martensite variants to a specific orientation on cooling.
- After training, the cooling-induced martensite adopts a "trained" variant, producing a macroscopic shape change, while heating reverts it to austenite.
- The result is a reversible shape change between two pre-determined geometries.

**Stress-Temperature Phase Diagram (1 mark):** A typical SMA phase diagram shows:
- $M_s$, $M_f$, $A_s$, $A_f$ lines on the temperature axis at $\sigma = 0$.
- The Clausius-Clapeyron slopes rising linearly with stress.
- The region between $M_s$ and $A_s$ is the **transformation hysteresis band**.

---

**(b) SMA Applications in MEMS — 7 marks**

**1. Thin-Film SMA Micro-Actuators (2 marks):**
- SMA thin films (typically 1–10 µm thick) of Ti-Ni or Ti-Ni-Cu are deposited by **sputtering** on silicon or polymer substrates.
- When **Joule-heated** by passing a current through the film, the film undergoes martensite → austenite transformation, generating stresses up to 500 MPa and displacements of several micrometers.
- Application: **Micro-valves** for microfluidic systems, **optical switches** in telecom MEMS, and **read/write heads** in micro-scanners.

**2. SMA Wire-Based Micro-Grippers (2 marks):**
- Opposing SMA wires contract when heated, closing a micro-jaw with sub-millimeter precision.
- Used in **microsurgery** and **cell-manipulation** robots.
- Force output: ~50–150 mN; stroke: ~3–5% of wire length.

**3. Biomedical Microdevices (2 marks):**
- **Vascular stents:** Cooled in a compressed martensite state, inserted into an artery, then heated by body temperature to expand and scaffold the vessel.
- **Orthodontic archwires:** Apply constant corrective force due to the pseudoelastic plateau.
- **Endoscopic positioners:** SMA actuators steer the tip of an endoscope inside the body.
- **Drug-delivery micro-pumps:** SMA diaphragms act as cyclic pumps in implantable devices.

**4. Aerospace and Defense MEMS (1 mark):**
- **Morphing-wing MAVs:** SMA wires act as artificial muscles that change wing camber in flight.
- **Adaptive optics:** SMA actuators deform telescope mirrors to correct aberrations.
- **Inertial switches and latches:** SMAs provide one-time deployment mechanisms for aerospace payloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SME with magnetism.** SME is a thermal-mechanical phenomenon; magnetostriction is a magnetic phenomenon. (Lose 1 mark instantly for confusion.)
> 2. **Always label the four transformation temperatures in correct order** ($M_f < M_s < A_s < A_f$). Reversing $A_s$ and $M_s$ is a frequent mistake.
> 3. **Pseudoelasticity ≠ Plasticity.** Pseudoelasticity is *fully recoverable* and occurs at $T > A_f$. Plasticity is permanent and occurs in any metal.
> 4. **OWE vs. TWE:** Don't claim the untrained SMA has TWE. Without thermomechanical training, only OWE exists.
> 5. **Martensite in SMAs is NOT the brittle phase of steel.** It is a soft, ductile, twinned phase in SMAs. This is a high-frequency KTU conceptual trap.
> 6. **Units:** Stress-temperature slope $\beta$ must be in **MPa/°C** (or MPa/K). Writing it as just "MPa" loses a unit mark.
> 7. **In derivations, show the Taylor expansion step explicitly** — KTU examiners award partial credit for the expansion step, not just the final boxed equation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** SMAs are smart materials that recover original shape upon heating after apparent plastic deformation — driven by **thermoelastic martensitic transformation**.
- **Two Phases:** **Austenite (hot, B2 cubic)** and **Martensite (cold, B19' monoclinic, 24 variants)**.
- **Four Temperatures (in order):** $M_f < M_s < A_s < A_f$. The **hysteresis gap** $(A_f - M_s)$ is typically **30–50 °C** for Nitinol.
- **Three Effects:** **OWE** (heat only — remembers hot shape), **TWE** (heat + cool — remembers both, needs training), **Pseudoelasticity** (stress at $T > A_f$ — large recoverable strain, no heat needed).
- **Key Equation:** $\sigma = \beta(T - T_0)$, where $\beta \approx 6.7$ MPa/°C for Nitinol.
- **Key Material:** **Nitinol (Ni-Ti)** — density **6.45 g/cm³**, recoverable strain **~8%**, recoverable stress **up to 800 MPa**, biocompatible.
- **MEMS Use:** Thin-film sputtering on Si substrates → Joule heating → micro-actuators, micro-grippers, micro-valves, biomedical stents.
- **"Artificial Muscle":** Work density ~**6.7 MJ/m³**, ~10× human muscle.
- **Above $T_c$ (Curie temp ≈ 100 °C for Nitinol):** SME is lost.
- **Clausius-Clapeyron is the unifying equation** — it shows that stress and temperature are interchangeable triggers of the martensitic transformation.
- **Quick Mnemonic for the four temperatures:** **"M's come first when cooling, A's come first when heating"** → M's, then A's, with Ms in between.
- **For Part A answers:** Always state *both* the definition *and* the application in the same answer — KTU rewards multi-dimensional answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Topic — Shape Memory Alloys.** The next likely KTU topic in this module is **Piezoelectric Materials** or **Magnetostrictive Materials** — both are smart materials that complement SMAs in MEMS actuator design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Crystals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Crystals # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Piezoelectric Crystals ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Piezoelectricity (Greek: piezein = to press/pressurize)** is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials to interconvert mechanical energy (stress/strain) and electrical energy (charge/voltage). The **direct piezoelectric effect** generates an electric charge in response to applied mechanical stress, while the **converse (inverse) piezoelectric effect** produces mechanical strain when an electric field is applied. A material exhibiting both effects linearly is called a **piezoelectric crystal**. The phenomenon was first discovered in 1880 by **Jacques and Pierre Curie** in single-crystal **Rochelle salt** and **quartz**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Out of the **32 crystallographic point groups**, only **21 classes are non-centrosymmetric**. Of these, **20 are piezoelectric** (the cubic class *432* is non-piezoelectric). **10 of the 20 piezoelectric classes are also pyroelectric** (spontaneously polarised), and among those, the **ferroelectric class** exhibits a switchable spontaneous polarisation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Sponge" Imagine a special **sponge** that has tiny internal dipoles (like microscopic positive and negative poles locked in fixed positions). When you **squeeze** the sponge: - The dipoles are forced to align better, generating a net voltage on the surface — this is the **direct effect** (mechanical → electrical). - Conversely, when you connect a **battery** to the sponge, it physically **expands or contracts** by a tiny amount — this is the **converse effect** (electrical → mechanical). The sponge represents crystals like **quartz (SiO₂)** or **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)**. The key is that the crystal's **internal charge distribution lacks a centre of symmetry** — squeeze it, and the charges separate; apply a field, and the lattice stretches. ## 1.3 Why Symmetry Matters | Crystal Property | Requirement for Piezoelectricity | |---|---| | Crystal structure | Must be **non-centrosymmetric** (no inversion centre) | | Lattice type | Anisotropic (properties differ with direction) | | Polarity | Internal dipole moments must exist or be inducible | | Temperature | Below the **Curie temperature** T_c (polar phase stable) | > [!TIP] > **Common misconception:** "All crystals are piezoelectric." This is **false** — centrosymmetric crystals like NaCl (rock salt) or metals like copper CANNOT exhibit piezoelectricity because the inversion centre forces positive and negative charges to cancel under any stress. ## 1.4 Direct vs. Converse Effect — Schematic Picture | Effect | Input | Output | Symbol | |---|---|---|---| | **Direct (Generator/Sensor mode)** | Mechanical stress *T* | Electric displacement *D* | *D* = *d · T* | | **Converse (Motor/Actuator mode)** | Electric field *E* | Mechanical strain *S* | *S* = *d · E* | The same constant *d* (piezoelectric charge/strain coefficient) couples both effects — a consequence of the **thermodynamic Maxwell reciprocity**. ## 1.5 Classification of Piezoelectric Materials **Natural (single-crystal) piezoelectric materials:** - **Quartz (SiO₂)** — point group *32*, the benchmark natural piezoelectric - **Rochelle salt (KNaC₄H₄O₆·4H₂O)** — historically first discovered, very high *d* but fragile - **Tourmaline** — naturally polar, used in pressure gauges - **Lithium Niobate (LiNbO₃)** and **Lithium Tantalate (LiTaO₃)** — strong piezoelectrics for SAW devices **Synthetic (polycrystalline / ceramic) piezoelectric materials:** - **PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate (Pb[ZrₓTi₁₋ₓ]O₃)** — industry workhorse, *d*₃₃ ≈ 300–600 pC/N - **Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃)** — classic ferroelectric perovskite - **PVDF — Polyvinylidene fluoride** — a *polymer* piezoelectric, flexible - **PMN-PT (Lead Magnesium Niobate–Lead Titanate)** — relaxor ferroelectric with ultra-high coupling > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU exam factoid:** The *d*₃₃ coefficient of PZT (~500 pC/N) is roughly **1000× larger** than that of quartz (~2.3 pC/N). That is why PZT dominates MEMS actuators while quartz dominates frequency-stable resonators. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Piezoelectric Tensor — A Structured View The piezoelectric effect is **anisotropic** — its magnitude depends on the direction of stress/field relative to the crystal axes. This is captured by a **third-rank tensor** *d_ijk*, which is conventionally contracted into a **3 × 6 matrix** *d_ij*: $$ d_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} d_{11} & d_{12} & d_{13} & d_{14} & d_{15} & d_{16} \\ d_{21} & d_{22} & d_{23} & d_{24} & d_{25} & d_{26} \\ d_{31} & d_{32} & d_{33} & d_{34} & d_{35} & d_{36} \end{pmatrix} $$ - **First index *i* = 1,2,3** → direction of *E*-field / *D*-polarisation (x, y, z). - **Second index *j* = 1…6** → stress/strain component (3 normal + 3 shear, using Voigt notation). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering convention (IEEE 1987):** The first index of *d_ij* refers to the **electric** quantity (polarisation direction), and the second refers to the **mechanical** quantity (stress/strain direction). Always quote *d_ij* in this format. ## 2.2 Key Physical Coefficients Used in KTU Problems | Coefficient | Symbol | Definition | Typical Units | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Charge coefficient** | *d_ij* | Charge generated per unit applied force | C/N (= m/V) | Sensor sensitivity | | **Voltage coefficient** | *g_ij* | Electric field generated per unit stress | V·m/N | Sensor figure of merit | | **Strain coefficient** | *d_ij* | Strain per unit applied electric field | m/V | Actuator stroke | | **Stress coefficient** | *e_ij* | Stress per unit electric field | N/V·m | Actuator force | | **Stiffness coefficient** | *c^E_ij* | Elastic stiffness at constant *E* | N/m² | Mechanical rigidity | | **Compliance coefficient** | *s^E_ij* | Elastic compliance at constant *E* | m²/N | Mechanical softness | | **Permittivity (clamped)** | *ε^T_ij* | Dielectric constant at constant *T* | F/m | Electrical polarisability | | **Electromechanical coupling** | *k* | Energy conversion efficiency | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Overall FOM | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Most-tested formula:** The **electromechanical coupling coefficient** *k* relates converted energy to input energy: > $$k^2 = \frac{\text{Stored mechanical energy}}{\text{Input electrical energy}} = \frac{d^2}{s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T}$$ > For PZT, *k*₃₃ ≈ **0.6–0.7** (≈ 60–70% energy conversion — exceptionally high). ## 2.3 Constitutive Equations of Piezoelectricity (IEEE Standard) The full linear piezoelectric constitutive relations couple mechanical and electrical variables: $$ \begin{aligned} D_i &= d_{ij} \, T_j + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij} \, E_i && \text{(Direct effect — sensor)} \\ S_i &= s^{E}_{ij} \, T_j + d_{ij} \, E_i && \text{(Converse effect — actuator)} \end{aligned} $$ **Where:** - *D_i* = electric displacement (C/m²) - *T_j* = mechanical stress (N/m²) - *E_i* = electric field (V/m) - *S_i* = mechanical strain (dimensionless) - *d_ij* = piezoelectric coefficient matrix (C/N or m/V) - *ε^T_ij* = permittivity at constant stress (F/m) - *s^E_ij* = elastic compliance at constant electric field (m²/N) > [!TIP] > **Examiner's mental model:** If you are asked "Derive the constitutive equations", remember the four canonical forms — *D* & *S* expressed in (*T*, *E*) is only ONE of them. The other three are *D* & *T* in (*S*, *E*), *E* & *S* in (*T*, *D*), and *E* & *T* in (*S*, *D*). Each form uses a different set of constants: (*d, ε^T, s^E*), (*e, ε^S, c^E*), (*g, β^T, s^D*), (*h, β^S, c^D*). ## 2.4 Crystal Symmetry of Quartz (Point Group 32) Quartz (SiO₂) is the **canonical KTU example**. Its non-vanishing independent piezoelectric coefficients are: $$ d_{ij}^{\text{quartz}} = \begin{pmatrix} d_{11} & -d_{11} & 0 & d_{14} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -d_{14} & -2d_{11} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} $$ with *d*₁₁ = **2.3 pC/N** and *d*₁₄ = **−0.67 pC/N**. > [!WARNING] > Note that for quartz, *d*₃₁ = *d*₃₂ = *d*₃₃ = 0.
