This is why quartz is a poor actuator but a brilliant **resonator** — its longitudinal mode (*d*₁₁) is highly stable, with Q-factors routinely exceeding 10⁶. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | *D* = *d · T* + *ε^T · E* | Direct constitutive (sensor) | C/m² | | 2 | *S* = *s^E · T* + *d · E* | Converse constitutive (actuator) | dimensionless | | 3 | *k²* = *d² / (s^E · ε^T)* | Electromechanical coupling | dimensionless | | 4 | *g* = *d / ε^T* | Voltage coefficient | V·m/N | | 5 | *f_r* = *N / t* (bulk mode) | Resonant frequency (thickness mode) | Hz | | 6 | *N* = (1/2)·√(*c^E_33 / ρ*) | Frequency constant for thickness mode | Hz·m | | 7 | *λ_s* = √(*c^E_33 · h / ρ*)? | SAW wavelength | m | | 8 | Curie–Weiss: *ε* = *C / (T − T_c)* | Permittivity near T_c | F/m | | 9 | Mass sensitivity: Δ*f*/*f* = −*c_m · Δm* | QCM / SAW mass-loading | dimensionless | | 10 | Piezo voltage: *V* = *g₃₃ · σ₃ · t* | Open-circuit voltage across thickness *t* | V | > [!NOTE] > The **resonant frequency of a thickness-mode resonator** is *f_r = v / (2t)*, where the longitudinal acoustic velocity is *v = √(c^E_33 / ρ)* and *t* is the slab thickness. This is the basis of **MEMS piezoelectric resonators** used as timing references and RF filters. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MEMS Resonators & Filters:** Quartz and AlN-based FBAR/BAW filters in every smartphone RF front-end (2.4 GHz Wi-Fi, 5G bands). - **Piezoelectric Inkjet Printers:** PZT actuators deform ink chambers by sub-micron strokes, ejecting picolitre droplets. - **Energy Harvesters:** PZT cantilever beams scavenge vibration energy (e.g., from shoes, machinery) to power IoT nodes. - **SAW Sensors:** Surface Acoustic Wave devices detect mass loading (biosensors, gas sensors) using piezoelectric substrates. - **Scanning Probe Microscopy:** *d*₃₃ piezoelectric tube scanners position AFM tips with sub-nanometre precision. - **Medical Ultrasound:** PZT arrays in transducers convert electrical pulses into acoustic imaging waves. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: The Constitutive Equations from Thermodynamic Free Energy ### Step 1 — Start with the appropriate thermodynamic potential The Helmholtz free energy density of a piezoelectric crystal is a function of strain *S* and electric field *E*: $$\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{F}(S, E)$$ ### Step 2 — Take the first-order Taylor expansion around the reference state (*S* = 0, *E* = 0) $$ \mathcal{F}(S, E) = \frac{1}{2} c^{E}_{ij} S_i S_j + \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon^{S}_{ij} E_i E_j - e_{ijk} S_i E_k $$ **Explanation of terms:** - $\frac{1}{2} c^{E}_{ij} S_i S_j$ → elastic strain energy stored at constant electric field. - $\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon^{S}_{ij} E_i E_j$ → electrical energy stored at constant strain. - $-e_{ijk} S_i E_k$ → **piezoelectric coupling energy** (the cross term). ### Step 3 — Derive the thermodynamic conjugates The stress is the partial derivative of $\mathcal{F}$ with respect to strain at constant field: $$T_j = \left. \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial S_j} \right|_{E}$$ Applying the derivative term-by-term: $$ \begin{aligned} T_j &= c^{E}_{ij} S_i - e_{kij} E_k \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — The electric displacement is the negative partial derivative with respect to E at constant strain $$D_k = -\left. \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial E_k} \right|_{S}$$ Differentiating: $$ \begin{aligned} D_k &= \varepsilon^{S}_{kj} E_j + e_{kij} S_i \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Re-express in the (*T*, *E*) form (solving for *D* and *S*) Invert the elastic equation to get *S* in terms of *T* and *E*: $$S_i = s^{E}_{ij} T_j + d_{ijk} E_k$$ Substitute into the *D* equation: $$D_k = \varepsilon^{T}_{kj} E_j + d_{kij} T_j$$ ### Step 6 — Final canonical form (Voigt notation) $$ \begin{aligned} D_i &= d_{ij} \, T_j + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij} \, E_i \\ S_i &= s^{E}_{ij} \, T_j + d_{ij} \, E_i \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why both forms exist:** There are **four thermodynamically allowed constitutive forms**, depending on which pair of variables (*D, E* and *S, T*) are chosen as independent. The form used most often in KTU problems is the (*T*, *E*) form because both inputs are easily controllable in the lab. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II: Electromechanical Coupling Coefficient *k* ### Step 1 — Definition in terms of energy densities The **squared coupling factor** is the ratio of stored *mutual* (piezoelectric) energy to the geometric mean of purely mechanical and purely electrical stored energies: $$k^2 = \frac{U_{\text{mutual}}^2}{U_{\text{mech}} \cdot U_{\text{elec}}}$$ ### Step 2 — Express the three energy densities in terms of material constants At maximum mutual energy, the coupled energy is proportional to *d*: $$U_{\text{mutual}} \propto d$$ Purely mechanical energy (with *E* = 0): $$U_{\text{mech}} = \frac{1}{2} s^E T^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; U_{\text{mech}} \propto s^E$$ Purely electrical energy (with *T* = 0): $$U_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon^T E^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; U_{\text{elec}} \propto \varepsilon^T$$ ### Step 3 — Assemble the ratio $$k^2 = \frac{d^2}{s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T}$$ ### Step 4 — Numerical illustration for PZT-5A | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | *d*₃₃ | 374 × 10⁻¹² C/N | | *s^E*₃₃ | 18.8 × 10⁻¹² m²/N | | *ε^T*₃₃ / *ε*₀ | 1700 | | *ε^T*₃₃ | 1700 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹² = 1.505 × 10⁻⁸ F/m | Plug in: $$k_{33}^2 = \frac{(374 \times 10^{-12})^2}{(18.8 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.505 \times 10^{-8})}$$ Step-by-step arithmetic: - Numerator: *d²* = (374)² × 10⁻²⁴ = 139876 × 10⁻²⁴ ≈ 1.399 × 10⁻¹⁹ (C/N)² - Denominator: *s^E · ε^T* = 18.8 × 1.505 × 10⁻²⁰ = 28.29 × 10⁻²⁰ ≈ 2.829 × 10⁻¹⁹ - Ratio: *k²* = 1.399 / 2.829 ≈ 0.4945 - *k*₃₃ = √0.4945 ≈ **0.703** > [!TIP] > **KTU interpretation:** A coupling coefficient of 0.70 means **~70% of input electrical energy can be converted to mechanical energy** in one cycle — PZT is among the most efficient electromechanical transducers known. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III: Open-Circuit Voltage from a Piezoelectric Disc ### Step 1 — Set up the geometry Consider a **PZT disc** of thickness *t*, electroded on the two flat faces (z-direction). An axial compressive stress *σ*₃ is applied. ### Step 2 — Direct piezoelectric effect gives the surface charge density $$D_3 = d_{33} \, \sigma_3$$ ### Step 3 — Open-circuit voltage across the electrodes Since the electrodes are open, *D*₃ is constant, and the voltage *V* relates to the field as: $$E_3 = V / t$$ For a linear dielectric, *D*₃ = *ε^T*₃₃ · *E*₃, so: $$D_3 = \varepsilon^T_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{t} = d_{33} \, \sigma_3$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for V using the *g*-coefficient Rearranging: $$V = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t}{\varepsilon^T_{33}} \cdot \sigma_3 = g_{33} \cdot \sigma_3 \cdot t$$ ### Step 5 — Numerical example Suppose a PZT disc has *t* = 1 mm = 10⁻³ m, *g*₃₃ = 24 × 10⁻³ V·m/N, and an applied pressure of 1 MPa = 10⁶ N/m². $$ \begin{aligned} V &= (24 \times 10^{-3}) \times (10^6) \times (10^{-3}) \\ V &= 24 \times 10^{0} \\ V &= \mathbf{24 \text{ V}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU mistake:** Forgetting the *thickness factor t*. The voltage across a piezoelectric disc is *NOT* just *g·σ* — the *electric field* is *g·σ*, but the *voltage* is the field × thickness. Many students lose 1–2 marks by missing this. --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV: Resonant Frequency of a Thickness-Mode Quartz Resonator ### Step 1 — Wave equation in a piezoelectric plate For longitudinal waves travelling along the thickness *z*, the 1-D wave equation is: $$\rho \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^{E}_{33} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}$$ where *u* is the mechanical displacement, *ρ* is the density, and *c^E*₃₃ is the elastic stiffness at constant *E*. ### Step 2 — Acoustic velocity The longitudinal acoustic velocity is: $$v = \sqrt{\frac{c^{E}_{33}}{\rho}}$$ ### Step 3 — Boundary conditions (stress-free surfaces) The two faces at *z* = 0 and *z* = *t* are mechanically free, so the stress vanishes there. The standing-wave solution requires: $$f_n = \frac{n}{2t} \sqrt{\frac{c^{E}_{33}}{\rho}}, \quad n = 1, 3, 5, \dots$$ The **fundamental** (*n* = 1) thickness-mode frequency is: $$f_1 = \frac{1}{2t} \sqrt{\frac{c^{E}_{33}}{\rho}}$$ ### Step 4 — Quartz numerical value For quartz, *c^E*₃₃ = 86.8 GPa = 8.68 × 10¹⁰ N/m², *ρ* = 2650 kg/m³, and *t* = 0.1 mm = 10⁻⁴ m. $$ \begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{\frac{8.68 \times 10^{10}}{2650}} = \sqrt{3.275 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5722 \text{ m/s} \\ f_1 &= \frac{5722}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.861 \times 10^{7} \text{ Hz} \approx \mathbf{28.6 \text{ MHz}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **operating principle of every quartz crystal oscillator** in digital watches, microcontrollers, and RF circuits. The frequency can be tuned precisely by varying *t* — every micron of thickness shift moves the frequency by hundreds of kHz. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Piezoelectric Material to Use? | Application | Best Material | Why | Typical *d*₃₃ or *k* | |---|---|---|---| | Frequency-stable resonator | **Quartz (AT-cut)** | Near-zero tempco, ultra-high Q | *k* ≈ 0.10 | | High-strain actuator | **PZT-5H** | Largest *d*₃₃ | *d*₃₃ ≈ 593 pC/N | | High-temperature sensor | **LiNbO₃** | T_c ≈ 1210 °C | *d*₃₃ ≈ 6 pC/N | | Flexible sensor / wearable | **PVDF** | Polymer, can be bent | *d*₃₃ ≈ 33 pC/N | | RF MEMS / FBAR | **AlN** | CMOS-compatible, low loss | *d*₃₃ ≈ 5 pC/N | | Ultra-low-noise transducer | **PMN-PT** | Highest *k*₃₃ | *k*₃₃ ≈ 0.92 | | Biosensor (mass loading) | **Quartz QCM** | Stable, inert, biocompatible | *k* ≈ 0.10 | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Piezoelectric Transduction ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Mechanical Stress Input] --> B{Piezoelectric Crystal
Non-centrosymmetric lattice} B --> C[Direct Effect
D = d times T] C --> D[Charge Amplifier] D --> E[Output Voltage Signal
SENSOR MODE] F[External Voltage Source] --> G{Electric Field Applied} G --> B B --> H[Converse Effect
S = d times E] H --> I[Mechanical Strain / Displacement
ACTUATOR MODE] ``` ## 4.2 Symmetry-Based Classification of the 32 Crystal Classes ```mermaid flowchart TB R[32 Crystallographic Point Groups] --> S1[11 Centrosymmetric
Not piezoelectric] R --> S2[21 Non-centrosymmetric] S2 --> S3[1 cubic class 432
Not piezoelectric] S2 --> S4[20 Piezoelectric Classes] S4 --> S5[10 Pyroelectric Classes
Spontaneously polarised] S5 --> S6[Ferroelectric Subset
Switchable polarisation] S4 --> S7[10 Non-pyroelectric
e.g. Quartz class 32] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) Sensor ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SensorHead[QCM Sensor Head] E1[Top Electrode Au] --> Q[AT cut Quartz Disc
thickness t] Q --> E2[Bottom Electrode Au] end Osc[Oscillator Circuit
5 to 10 MHz] -->|drives| E1 E2 --> FreqCount[Frequency Counter] FreqCount --> DAQ[Data Acquisition] DAQ --> Out[Delta f output
proportional to mass loading] Abs[Analyte Molecules
adsorb on electrode] -.->|mass loading| E1 ``` ## 4.4 Piezoelectric MEMS Actuator — Cantilever Beam Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Cantilever[PZT Cantilever Beam] Sub[Si or Metal Substrate] --> BE[Bottom Electrode] BE --> PZ[PZT Active Layer
1 to 5 micrometre] PZ --> TE[Top Electrode] end V[in] -->|V_drive| TE TE --> PZ PZ -->|d31 transverse strain| TipDef[Tip Deflection
delta z] TipDef --> MechOut[Mechanical Work Output] ``` ## 4.5 Energy-Flow Diagram — Direct vs. Converse Coupling ```mermaid flowchart TB MechEnergy[Mechanical Energy
stress times strain] -->|applied T| CP[Crystal Lattice
Piezo Coupling] ElecEnergy[Electrical Energy
field times displacement] -->|applied E| CP CP -->|Direct path| DE[Dielectric + Piezo Charge] CP -->|Converse path| SE[Elastic + Piezo Strain] DE --> Meas1[Sensed by electrodes] SE --> Meas2[Sensed as displacement] ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading tip for KTU exam:** When asked to "draw the block diagram", always include (1) the **input** quantity, (2) the **piezoelectric element** explicitly, (3) the **transduction equation** on the arrow, and (4) the **output** quantity. Examiners look for these four anchors. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Define the direct and converse piezoelectric effects. Why are these effects only observed in non-centrosymmetric crystals?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Direct effect (1 mark):** Generation of an electric displacement *D* (and hence surface charge) in a crystal when subjected to mechanical stress *T*. Mathematically: *D* = *d · T* + *ε^T · E*. - **Converse effect (1 mark):** Production of mechanical strain *S* in a crystal when subjected to an external electric field *E*. Mathematically: *S* = *s^E · T* + *d · E*. - **Non-centrosymmetry requirement (1 mark):** In a centrosymmetric crystal, the inversion centre forces every dipole created by an applied stress to be cancelled by an equal and opposite dipole. The net polarisation remains zero for all stress states, so the effect is forbidden by symmetry. The 20 non-centrosymmetric point groups (out of 32) allow a non-zero piezoelectric tensor, hence piezoelectricity is observed. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Differentiate between piezoelectric coefficient *d*₃₃ and *d*₃₁. Give typical values for PZT and quartz.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** *d*₃₃ is the **longitudinal** coefficient — charge generated per unit force, both along the poling axis (z, index 3). *d*₃₁ is the **transverse** coefficient — charge generated along the z-axis due to a stress applied perpendicular to it (along x, index 1). - **Typical values (1.5 marks):** For PZT-5A, *d*₃₃ ≈ 374 pC/N, *d*₃₁ ≈ −171 pC/N. For quartz, *d*₁₁ ≈ 2.3 pC/N, with *d*₃₃ = 0 (no longitudinal coefficient along the optic axis). - PZT shows both because of poling-induced anisotropy; quartz is uniaxial with the unique axis *c* (z) being non-piezoelectric. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer ONE full question, but EITHER choice A OR choice B. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a)** Starting from the Helmholtz free energy of a piezoelectric crystal, derive the canonical constitutive equations relating electric displacement *D*, stress *T*, strain *S*, and electric field *E*. Clearly define each material constant that appears. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PZT-5H disc of diameter 10 mm and thickness 1 mm is subjected to a uniform compressive force of 100 N along its thickness direction. Given *d*₃₃ = 593 pC/N, *ε^T*₃₃ / *ε*₀ = 3400, and *ε*₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m, calculate the (i) charge generated and (ii) the open-circuit voltage developed across the electrodes. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the Helmholtz free energy density as a function of strain *S* and electric field *E*: $$\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{F}(S_i, E_k)$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Expand to second order: $$\mathcal{F} = \frac{1}{2} c^{E}_{ij} S_i S_j - e_{ijk} S_i E_k + \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon^{S}_{ij} E_i E_j$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Take thermodynamic derivatives to obtain stress and electric displacement: $$T_j = \left.\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial S_j}\right|_{E} = c^{E}_{ij} S_i - e_{kij} E_k$$ $$D_k = -\left.\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial E_k}\right|_{S} = e_{kij} S_i + \varepsilon^{S}_{kj} E_j$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Re-express in the (*T*, *E*) form by inverting the elastic equation: $$S_i = s^{E}_{ij} T_j + d_{ijk} E_k \quad ; \quad D_k = d_{kij} T_j + \varepsilon^{T}_{kj} E_j$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** In Voigt notation (matrix form), and identify constants: - *d_ij* — piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N or m/V) - *s^E_ij* — elastic compliance at constant *E* (m²/N) - *ε^T_ij* — permittivity at constant stress (F/m) - *c^E_ij* — elastic stiffness at constant *E* (N/m²) - *e_ijk* — piezoelectric stress coefficient (N/V·m) Final boxed answer: $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} D_i &= d_{ij} T_j + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij} E_i \\ S_i &= s^{E}_{ij} T_j + d_{ij} E_i \end{aligned} } $$ --- #### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Geometry and stress (1.5 marks):** - Disc area: $A = \pi r^2 = \pi (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$ - Applied force: *F* = 100 N - Stress: $\sigma_3 = F / A = 100 / 7.854 \times 10^{-5} = 1.273 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2$ **Step 2 — Charge generated (2.5 marks):** Using the direct effect *D*₃ = *d*₃₃ · *σ*₃: $$D_3 = (593 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.273 \times 10^{6}) = 7.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ C/m}^2$$ Total surface charge: $$Q = D_3 \cdot A = (7.55 \times 10^{-4}) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-5}) = 5.93 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 59.3 \text{ nC}}$$ **Step 3 — Permittivity value (1 mark):** $$\varepsilon^T_{33} = 3400 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 3.01 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/m}$$ **Step 4 — Open-circuit voltage (2 marks):** Using *V* = *Q / C*, where *C = ε^T_33 · A / t*: $$C = \frac{(3.01 \times 10^{-8}) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-5})}{10^{-3}} = 2.365 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F} \approx 2.37 \text{ nF}$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{5.93 \times 10^{-8}}{2.365 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 25.07 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V \approx 25.1 \text{ V}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall #1):** Students often confuse the **two geometric factors**. Disc thickness *t* = 1 mm is for **voltage** (V = E·t). Disc area *A* is for **charge** (Q = D·A). Mixing these up is the #1 source of dimensional errors in piezoelectric problems. Always write *A* and *t* explicitly in your solution. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the structure of the piezoelectric coefficient matrix *d_ij*. For point group *32* (quartz), write down the matrix and identify the independent non-zero coefficients. Comment on why quartz is the preferred material for resonator applications despite its low *d* values. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A quartz thickness-mode resonator of thickness *t* = 0.05 mm is required to operate at 66 MHz (5G Wi-Fi band). Given the elastic stiffness *c^E*₃₃ = 86.8 GPa and density *ρ* = 2650 kg/m³, (i) calculate the expected resonant frequency for the fundamental thickness mode and (ii) comment on whether the design specification is achievable. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Structure of the matrix (2 marks):** The piezoelectric tensor is **third-rank** with 27 components *d_ijk*. By Voigt contraction of the mechanical indices (1–6), it becomes a **3 × 6 matrix** *d_ij*: - *i* = 1, 2, 3 → direction of polarisation (x, y, z). - *j* = 1…6 → direction of applied stress or strain (1=xx, 2=yy, 3=zz, 4=yz, 5=xz, 6=xy). - 18 independent components in the most general case. **Step 2 — Quartz matrix (2 marks):** $$ d_{ij}^{\text{quartz}} = \begin{pmatrix} d_{11} & -d_{11} & 0 & d_{14} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -d_{14} & -2d_{11} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} $$ Independent non-zero coefficients: **only two** — *d*₁₁ ≈ 2.3 pC/N and *d*₁₄ ≈ −0.67 pC/N. **Step 3 — Why quartz is preferred for resonators (3 marks):** - **Mechanical Q-factor** of quartz is extraordinarily high (Q > 10⁶), meaning very low acoustic damping. - **Temperature stability:** The **AT-cut** orientation of quartz has a near-zero temperature coefficient of frequency around 25 °C (cubic temperature term vanishes), making it ideal for stable oscillators. - **Frequency accuracy** depends on *t*, which can be polished to ±1 µm precision, giving ppm-level frequency control. - **Linear response** with negligible hysteresis (unlike PZT ferroelectrics). - Quartz is **not** a good actuator because *d*₃₃ = 0, so no longitudinal actuation along the optic axis — but this is exactly what allows stable thickness-mode resonance. --- #### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Acoustic velocity (2 marks):** $$v = \sqrt{\frac{c^E_{33}}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{86.8 \times 10^{9}}{2650}} = \sqrt{3.275 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5722 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Fundamental resonant frequency (3 marks):** $$f_1 = \frac{v}{2t} = \frac{5722}{2 \times 0.05 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{5722}{10^{-4}} = 5.722 \times 10^{7} \text{ Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_1 \approx 57.22 \text{ MHz}}$$ **Step 3 — Comparison with the target (2 marks):** Target = 66 MHz; calculated = 57.22 MHz. The design is **NOT achievable with a single 0.05 mm plate**. Two options: - (i) **Reduce the thickness** to $t_{\text{new}} = v / (2 f_{\text{target}}) = 5722 / (2 \times 66 \times 10^{6}) \approx 43.4 \text{ µm}$. - (ii) **Use a higher-order mode** (3rd overtone) — but this requires impedance matching and has higher loss. - (iii) **Switch material** — use AlN with higher *c^E*₃₃ (≈ 395 GPa) to raise the frequency for the same thickness. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall #2):** In thickness-mode resonator problems, students often write the wave equation as $\partial^2 u/\partial t^2 = c \cdot \partial^2 u/\partial x^2$ and forget the **density** *ρ* in the velocity. Always include $v = \sqrt{c/\rho}$, not just $v = c$. A second common error is using *n* = 1,2,3,... instead of only **odd integers** (1, 3, 5, ...) for stress-free surfaces. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Master Pitfall List > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 places where students lose marks on piezoelectric problems:** > 1. **Confusing the direction of indices in *d_ij*** — IEEE 1987 convention has electric index FIRST. > 2. **Forgetting that *d* = 0 in centrosymmetric crystals** — even though they may have a polar axis. > 3. **Mixing up sensor vs. actuator equations** — the direct effect is *D = d T*, not *S = d E*. > 4. **Wrong units** — *d_ij* is in C/N **OR** m/V (numerically equal), not C/m² or V/m. > 5. **Ignoring poling direction in PZT** — the "3" axis in *d*₃₃ is the poling axis, not an arbitrary direction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print & Memorise Before Exam:** - ⭐ **Piezoelectricity** = coupling of mechanical and electrical energy in **non-centrosymmetric** crystals. - ⭐ **Direct effect:** Stress → Charge (used in sensors, QCMs, microphones). - ⭐ **Converse effect:** Voltage → Strain (used in actuators, inkjets, nanopositioners). - ⭐ **Curie brothers** (1880) discovered the effect in **Rochelle salt** and **quartz**. - ⭐ Out of **32 crystal classes**, **20 are piezoelectric**; of these, **10 are pyroelectric**, and a subset of pyroelectrics are **ferroelectric** (switchable polarisation). - ⭐ **Quartz (SiO₂, point group 32):** two independent coefficients, *d*₁₁ ≈ 2.3 pC/N, *d*₁₄ ≈ −0.67 pC/N; *d*₃₃ = 0. - ⭐ **PZT:** industry standard for high-*d* actuators; *d*₃₃ up to 593 pC/N (PZT-5H); coupling *k*₃₃ ≈ 0.7. - ⭐ **PVDF:** a *polymer* piezoelectric — flexible, suitable for wearable sensors. - ⭐ **Constitutive equations (IEEE 1987 form):** *D* = *d·T* + *ε^T·E*; *S* = *s^E·T* + *d·E*. - ⭐ **Coupling coefficient:** $k^2 = d^2 / (s^E \cdot \varepsilon^T)$ — energy conversion efficiency. - ⭐ **Voltage coefficient:** *g* = *d / ε^T*; open-circuit voltage *V* = *g·σ·t*. - ⭐ **Resonance:** $f_n = (n/2) \sqrt{c^E/\rho} / t$ for thickness mode; *n* odd only. - ⭐ **Curie temperature** *T_c*: above it, the polar phase disappears, and the material becomes paraelectric (piezoelectric effect vanishes). - ⭐ **MEMS applications:** FBAR/SAW filters, energy harvesters, inkjet heads, AFM scanners, ultrasound transducers, gyroscopes. - ⭐ **Symmetry rule:** inversion centre → no piezoelectricity. Always check the point group first. - ⭐ **Reciprocity:** the *same* matrix *d_ij* couples *D ↔ T* and *S ↔ E* — a consequence of Maxwell relations in thermodynamics. > **End of Notes — Piezoelectric Crystals (Module 3)**

topics.title: Electrostatic Forces (Parallel Plate, Comb Drive) Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic Forces (Parallel Plate, Comb Drive) Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electrostatic Actuators** are MEMS transducers that convert electrical energy into mechanical motion (or vice versa) by exploiting the **Coulombic attractive force** generated between two electrically charged conductors separated by a dielectric medium (usually air or vacuum). They are the most widely used actuation mechanism in microsystems because they are **fast, low-power, CMOS-compatible, and integrable** with on-chip circuitry.

Two canonical topologies dominate the MEMS literature:

1. **Parallel-Plate Actuator** – A movable plate suspended above a fixed counter-electrode by a mechanical spring; the electrostatic attraction pulls the plate toward the substrate as voltage is applied across the gap.
2. **Comb-Drive Actuator** – A set of interdigitated "fingers" anchored alternately to the movable and fixed structures; lateral motion is produced by electrostatic fringing-field interaction in the inter-finger gaps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Under *Actuation Mechanisms in MEMS*, electrostatic actuation using parallel-plate and comb-drive topologies is treated as a **high-weightage topic** for both Continuous Evaluation (CE) and End Semester Examinations (ESE). Expected cognitive levels: **Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4)** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine rubbing a balloon on your hair: tiny charges transfer, and the balloon **sticks to the wall** even though gravity is pulling it down. The wall and balloon are not in contact — an invisible "pull" acts across a small gap. That is precisely how an electrostatic MEMS actuator works.

- The **substrate** is the wall.
- The **suspended microstructure** is the balloon.
- The **applied voltage** is the friction-induced charge.
- The **restoring spring** is gravity pulling the balloon back.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants You MUST Memorise for KTU:**
> - Vacuum permittivity: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
> - Relative permittivity of air: **εᵣ ≈ 1.0006**
> - Relative permittivity of SiO₂ (often used as dielectric): **εᵣ ≈ 3.9**

The key engineering insight is that the **electrostatic force scales as V²** (always attractive, independent of voltage polarity). This is both an advantage (works with AC or DC, no current required) and a limitation (force becomes negligible at very low voltages).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Energy Method

For any electrostatic transducer, the force along a generalised coordinate **x** is obtained from the change in stored electrostatic energy **U** as the geometry changes.

$$
F_x \;=\; +\frac{1}{2}\,V^{2}\,\frac{dC(x)}{dx}
$$

This is the **constant-voltage** (voltage-source) form. The constant-charge form is:

$$
F_x \;=\; -\frac{1}{2}\,\frac{Q^{2}}{dC(x)/dx} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2}\,V^{2}\,\frac{dC(x)}{dx}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the sign?** With a stiff voltage source, the supply delivers energy as capacitance increases, so the force does **positive work** (attractive). We therefore use the +½ V² dC/dx form throughout KTU derivations.

The general solution procedure is:
1. Express the **device capacitance C(x)** as a function of displacement.
2. Differentiate: **dC/dx**.
3. Multiply by **½ V²** to obtain force.

### 2.2 Parallel-Plate Actuator

For two plates of overlap area **A** separated by a variable gap **g(x) = g₀ − x** (where x is the displacement of the movable plate *toward* the fixed plate):

$$
C(x) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g_0 - x}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{dC}{dx} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{(g_0 - x)^{2}}
$$

And the electrostatic (attractive) force is:

$$
F_e(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{\varepsilon_0 A\,V^{2}}{(g_0 - x)^{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** As the plate moves toward the substrate, the gap shrinks and the force **grows nonlinearly** (∝ 1/gap²). This non-linear growth is what causes the famous **pull-in instability**.

### 2.3 Comb-Drive Actuator

A comb drive has **N** movable fingers, each flanked by two stationary fingers. The lateral overlap length is **L + x** (where L is the nominal overlap and x is the lateral displacement). The fringing-field capacitance per gap is **ε₀ h (L + x) / g**, with **h** being the structural thickness and **g** the inter-finger gap.

Total capacitance:

$$
C(x) \;\approx\; \frac{2N\,\varepsilon_0 h\,(L + x)}{g}
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{dC}{dx} \;=\; \frac{2N\,\varepsilon_0 h}{g} \;=\; \text{constant}
$$

Therefore the lateral force:

$$
F_{\text{comb}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,V^{2}\,\frac{2N\,\varepsilon_0 h}{g} \;=\; \frac{N\,\varepsilon_0 h\,V^{2}}{g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU result:** The comb-drive force is **independent of displacement x** (a constant-force actuator). This eliminates the pull-in catastrophe and gives a **linear electrical-to-mechanical transfer function** — the dominant reason comb drives are preferred in precision MEMS (gyroscopes, accelerometers, optical scanners).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parallel-plate capacitance | Cₚₚ | ε₀A / (g₀ − x) | F | Neglects fringing |
| 2 | Parallel-plate electrostatic force | Fₚₚ | ½ ε₀ A V² / (g₀ − x)² | N | Attractive (toward fixed plate) |
| 3 | Small-signal PP force | Fₚₚ | ½ ε₀ A V² / g₀² | N | When x ≪ g₀ |
| 4 | Comb-drive capacitance | C_c | 2 N ε₀ h (L + x) / g | F | Includes both flanks |
| 5 | Comb-drive force | F_c | N ε₀ h V² / g | N | **Constant — independent of x** |
| 6 | Mechanical restoring force | F_k | k x | N | k = effective spring constant |
| 7 | Static equilibrium condition | — | k x = ½ ε₀ A V² / (g₀ − x)² | — | Solve for x(V) |
| 8 | Pull-in displacement | x_pi | g₀ / 3 | m | Exactly 1/3 of gap |
| 9 | Pull-in voltage (PP) | V_pi | √(8 k g₀³ / (27 ε₀ A)) | V | Stable range: 0 ≤ x ≤ g₀/3 |
| 10 | Energy stored in capacitor | U | ½ C V² | J | For constant V |
| 11 | Capacitor energy density | u | ½ ε₀ E² | J/m³ | E = V / gap |
| 12 | Fringe-field correction (Comb) | α | ≈ 1.04 − 1.06 | — | Empirical (matches KTU design problems) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In the formula table, the **pull-in voltage is the single most-tested expression** in this module. Memorise both the derivation path *and* the final form: V_pi = √(8 k g₀³ / 27 ε₀ A). Do **not** confuse it with V_pi = √(2 k g₀³ / ε₀ A), which is a common wrong answer from students mis-deriving the stability condition.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Parallel-plate actuators** → Variable capacitors (RF MEMS switches, tunable filters), optical MEMS (digital micromirror devices — DMD), pressure sensors, micro-relays.
- **Comb drives** → MEMS gyroscopes (sense-mode and drive-mode), linear accelerometers, optical scanners, micro-grippers, atomic-force microscope (AFM) nanopositioners, and force-balanced accelerometers (FBAs) used in automotive airbag systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Electrostatic Force on a Parallel-Plate Capacitor

We model the device as a plate of area **A** suspended by a linear spring of stiffness **k** at an initial gap **g₀** from a fixed counter-electrode. A voltage **V** is applied across the gap.

**Step 1: Capacitance as a function of displacement**

When the movable plate shifts by **x** (toward the fixed plate), the gap becomes:

$$
g(x) \;=\; g_0 - x
$$

Hence the capacitance is:

$$
C(x) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g(x)} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g_0 - x}
$$

**Step 2: Energy stored at constant voltage**

The electrostatic energy stored in the capacitor at applied voltage V (assuming an ideal voltage source) is:

$$
U(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,C(x)\,V^{2} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - x)}
$$

**Step 3: Electrostatic force from the co-energy gradient**

The mechanical work done by the actuator in an incremental displacement dx is **dW = F dx = −dU**. Therefore the force in the +x direction is:

$$
F_e(x) \;=\; -\frac{dU}{dx} \;=\; -\frac{d}{dx}\!\left[\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2(g_0 - x)}\right]
$$

Computing the derivative:

$$
F_e(x) \;=\; -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2}\cdot \frac{d}{dx}\!\left[(g_0 - x)^{-1}\right] \;=\; -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2}\cdot \left[+\frac{1}{(g_0 - x)^{2}}\right]
$$

Rearranging the sign (the force is in the **+x direction**, i.e. attractive, toward the fixed plate):

$$
F_e(x) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - x)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4: Sign convention and physical interpretation**

The positive sign means that **increasing x** (closing the gap) **reduces** U. The voltage source is delivering energy (the device *attracts*). The force magnitude grows hyperbolically as x → g₀.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is non-linear:** dC/dx is itself a function of x. Doubling V quadruples the force, but halving the gap quadruples the force as well. This super-linear response is the origin of pull-in.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Pull-In Voltage for a Parallel-Plate Actuator

The pull-in instability occurs when the electrostatic force curve and the mechanical restoring-force curve cease to intersect in a stable manner.

**Step 1: Set up static equilibrium**

The mechanical restoring force is **F_k = kx** (Hooke's law). Equilibrium requires:

$$
k x \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - x)^{2}}
\tag{1}
$$

**Step 2: Stability criterion**

A small perturbation δx must not cause runaway motion. Linearising around equilibrium, the *net* stiffness must be **non-negative**:

$$
k_{\text{net}} \;=\; k - \frac{dF_e}{dx} \;\geq\; 0
$$

Compute dF_e/dx:

$$
\frac{dF_e}{dx} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{(g_0 - x)^{3}}
$$

**Step 3: Critical stability condition (touching point of tangency)**

At the pull-in threshold, the two force-vs-displacement curves become tangent — both equality and the stability condition hold simultaneously:

$$
k - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{(g_0 - x)^{3}} \;=\; 0
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
k \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{(g_0 - x)^{3}}
\tag{2}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for the pull-in displacement**

Divide equation (2) by equation (1):

$$
\frac{k}{k x} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2} / (g_0 - x)^{3}}{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2} / [2(g_0 - x)^{2}]}
$$

Simplify the right-hand side:

$$
\frac{1}{x} \;=\; \frac{2}{g_0 - x}
$$

Cross-multiply:

$$
g_0 - x \;=\; 2x \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; g_0 \;=\; 3x \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{x_{pi} \;=\; \dfrac{g_0}{3}}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back to get V_pi**

Insert x = g₀/3 into equation (1):

$$
k \cdot \frac{g_0}{3} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{pi}^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - g_0/3)^{2}}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\frac{k g_0}{3} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{pi}^{2}}{2\,(2g_0/3)^{2}}
$$

Simplify the denominator: (2g₀/3)² = 4g₀²/9. Therefore:

$$
\frac{k g_0}{3} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{pi}^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{9}{4 g_0^{2}}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\frac{k g_0}{3} \;=\; \frac{9\,\varepsilon_0 A V_{pi}^{2}}{8\,g_0^{2}}
$$

Cross-multiply and isolate V_pi²:

$$
V_{pi}^{2} \;=\; \frac{8\,k\,g_0^{3}}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\boxed{V_{pi} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{8\,k\,g_0^{3}}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation of pull-in:**
> - The actuator is **stable only for 0 ≤ x ≤ g₀/3**.
> - Beyond x = g₀/3, any further increase in voltage causes the plate to snap abruptly to the substrate — a **catastrophic instability** with no intermediate equilibrium.
> - This limits the **usable travel range** of parallel-plate actuators to **only one-third of the initial gap**, a major design constraint.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Comb-Drive Force (Lateral, Constant-Force Actuator)

Consider a comb drive with **N** movable fingers, each of height **h** (out-of-plane) and overlap **L + x** (in-plane, where x is the lateral displacement). The gap between adjacent fingers is **g**.

**Step 1: Capacitance per gap**

For each gap (left or right of a movable finger), the parallel-plate approximation gives:

$$
C_{\text{gap}} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 h (L + x)}{g}
$$

This is only the parallel-plate contribution; a fringing multiplier α ≈ 1.05 is often added in practice, but for KTU derivations α is taken as **1**.

**Step 2: Total device capacitance**

Each movable finger faces **two** stationary gaps, so the contribution per finger is **2 C_gap**. With N movable fingers:

$$
C(x) \;=\; \frac{2 N \varepsilon_0 h (L + x)}{g}
$$

**Step 3: Differentiate with respect to x**

$$
\frac{dC}{dx} \;=\; \frac{2 N \varepsilon_0 h}{g}
$$

**Crucial observation:** dC/dx is a **constant** — it does not depend on x. The comb drive is therefore a *linear* electrostatic transducer.

**Step 4: Compute the force**

$$
F_{\text{comb}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,V^{2}\,\frac{dC}{dx} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,V^{2}\cdot \frac{2 N \varepsilon_0 h}{g}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{comb}} \;=\; \frac{N\,\varepsilon_0\,h\,V^{2}}{g}}
$$

**Step 5: Verify the absence of pull-in**

For a parallel-plate actuator we had F_e ∝ 1/(g₀ − x)², which diverges as the gap closes. For the comb drive, F is a constant — independent of x. Setting this constant equal to the spring force kx gives:

$$
k x \;=\; \frac{N \varepsilon_0 h V^{2}}{g}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
x(V) \;=\; \frac{N \varepsilon_0 h V^{2}}{k g}
$$

The displacement grows **linearly with V²**, with no instability. The actuator is stable for all x up to the geometric limit (where fingers begin to disengage). Hence comb drives offer:

| Property | Parallel-Plate | Comb-Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Force profile | ∝ 1/(gap − x)² (nonlinear) | **Constant** (linear) |
| Stable travel | ≤ g₀/3 (~33 % of gap) | **~Full overlap length** |
| Pull-in voltage | Exists at finite V | **Does not exist** in the lateral direction |
| Voltage required | High (often > 30 V) | **Lower** (~ 5–20 V typical) |
| Capacitance change | C ∝ 1/(g₀ − x) | C ∝ (L + x) — **linear** |
| Fabrication | Simple | Slightly more complex |
| Typical application | RF switches, DMD | Gyroscopes, accelerometers, scanners |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A MEMS parallel-plate actuator has plate area A = 200 µm × 200 µm, initial gap g₀ = 2 µm, and an effective spring constant k = 1 N/m. Compute (a) the pull-in voltage and (b) the displacement at V = 5 V.

**Solution:**

(a) Pull-in voltage:

$$
V_{pi} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{8\,k\,g_0^{3}}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}}
\;=\; \sqrt{\frac{8 \cdot 1 \cdot (2\times 10^{-6})^{3}}{27 \cdot 8.854\times 10^{-12} \cdot (200\times 10^{-6})^{2}}}
$$

Numerator: 8 × 1 × 8×10⁻¹⁸ = 6.4 × 10⁻¹⁷

Denominator: 27 × 8.854×10⁻¹² × 4×10⁻⁸ = 9.562×10⁻¹⁸

$$
V_{pi} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-17}}{9.562 \times 10^{-18}}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{6.69} \;\approx\; 2.59 \text{ V}
$$

(b) Static displacement at V = 5 V: Since V = 5 V > V_pi = 2.59 V, the actuator has **already pulled in** at 5 V — there is no static equilibrium in the open-gap regime. The plate snaps to the substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical point:** This numerical example shows that parallel-plate actuators are inherently **low-voltage, low-travel** devices — a key design trade-off discussed in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Parallel-Plate Actuator (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[SUSPENSION SPRING K]:::spring --> B[MOVABLE TOP PLATE]
    B --> C[AIR GAP G0 MINUS X]:::gap
    C --> D[FIXED BOTTOM PLATE]:::fixed
    E[VOLTAGE SOURCE V APPLIED]:::volt --> B
    E --> D
    F[ELECTROSTATIC FORCE FE ATTRACTIVE DOWNWARD]:::force --> B
    G[RESTORING FORCE FK UPWARD]:::springf --> B
    H[NET FORCE FNET EQUALS FE MINUS FK]:::net --> B
    I[DISPLACEMENT X OF MOVABLE PLATE]:::disp --> B

    classDef spring fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef gap fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef fixed fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#fff
    classDef volt fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef force fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef springf fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000
    classDef disp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **blue spring** (K) holds the movable plate.
- The **red voltage source** charges both plates.
- The **green attractive force** pulls the plate down; the **light-blue restoring force** pushes it back.
- The **gold net-force block** represents the dynamical balance.
- The **pink displacement node** outputs x — the measurable mechanical variable.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Comb-Drive Actuator (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    P[ANCHOR PAD LEFT]:::anchor --> F1[FIXED FINGER 1]:::fixed
    P --> F2[FIXED FINGER 2]:::fixed
    P --> F3[FIXED FINGER 3]:::fixed
    Q[ANCHOR PAD RIGHT]:::anchor2 --> M1[MOVABLE FINGER 1]:::mov
    Q --> M2[MOVABLE FINGER 2]:::mov
    Q --> M3[MOVABLE FINGER 3]:::mov
    R[FOLDED BEAM SUSPENSION K]:::beam --> Q
    S[VOLTAGE SOURCE V]:::volt --> P
    S --> Q
    T[LATERAL FORCE FCOMB CONSTANT]:::force --> Q
    U[DISPLACEMENT X IN PLANE]:::disp --> Q

    classDef anchor fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef fixed fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#fff
    classDef mov fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B,color:#fff
    classDef beam fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef volt fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef force fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef disp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef anchor2 fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Purple** = fixed fingers, anchored to the substrate.
- **Steel-blue** = movable fingers, attached to a common shuttle (right anchor).
- The right shuttle is suspended by a **folded-beam spring (K)** and translates laterally by **x**.
- The **voltage source** is applied between the left and right anchors.
- The output is a **constant lateral force** (green) producing **in-plane displacement** (pink).

---

### 4.3 Pull-In Phenomenon — Force-Displacement Stability Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[INITIAL GAP G0]:::init --> B[SPRING FORCE F_K LINEAR]
    A --> C[ELECTROSTATIC FORCE F_E HYPERBOLIC]
    B --> D[EQUILIBRIUM POINTS X0 AND X1]:::eq
    C --> D
    D --> E{PULL IN AT X EQUALS G0/3}:::decision
    E -->|STABLE| F[0 TO G0/3 USABLE]:::stable
    E -->|UNSTABLE| G[X GREATER THAN G0/3 CATASTROPHIC]:::unstable
    F --> H[STABLE OPERATING REGION]:::region
    G --> I[PLATE SNAPS TO SUBSTRATE]:::snap

    classDef init fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef eq fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef stable fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef unstable fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef region fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef snap fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** The hyperbolic electrostatic curve eventually overtakes the linear spring curve. The last *stable* intersection is at **x = g₀/3**. Beyond that, no intersection exists — the plate accelerates uncontrollably toward the substrate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an electrostatic actuator. Why is the electrostatic force in a MEMS device independent of the polarity of the applied voltage?**

**Model Answer:**

An *electrostatic actuator* is a MEMS device that produces mechanical displacement by exploiting the **Coulombic attractive force** between two conductors held at different potentials, separated by a dielectric (typically air). The force arises from the gradient of the stored electrostatic energy with respect to displacement.

The force is given by **F = ½ V² (dC/dx)**, which depends on the **square of voltage**. Since V² is always positive regardless of sign, the force is **always attractive and independent of polarity**. This makes electrostatic actuators compatible with both DC and AC drive signals.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for force expression, 1 for polarity-independence explanation.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State any three advantages of a comb-drive actuator over a parallel-plate actuator.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Constant force profile** — the lateral force is independent of displacement x (F = N ε₀ h V² / g), giving a linear electrical-mechanical transfer function.
2. **No pull-in instability** — since the force does not diverge with displacement, the actuator is stable over the full overlap range.
3. **Larger usable travel** — the only geometric limit is the disengagement of fingers, allowing strokes of several microns.
   *(Supplementary advantages: lower drive voltage, easier fabrication using surface micromachining, well-suited for differential/sense-mode operation.)*

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correctly stated advantage.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, sub-divided into two 7-mark parts. Students must answer **either the whole of Choice A or the whole of Choice B**.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the electrostatic force acting on the movable plate of a parallel-plate MEMS actuator as a function of displacement. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Capacitance model (2 marks)**

Let the plate area be A, initial gap g₀, and downward displacement x. The instantaneous gap is (g₀ − x).

$$
C(x) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g_0 - x}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored (2 marks)**

At constant applied voltage V (ideal source):

$$
U(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,C(x)\,V^{2} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - x)}
$$

**Step 3 — Force from co-energy gradient (3 marks)**

The attractive force in the +x direction (toward the fixed plate) is:

$$
F_e(x) \;=\; -\frac{dU}{dx} \;=\; -\frac{d}{dx}\!\left[\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - x)}\right]
$$

Differentiating using d/dx[(g₀ − x)⁻¹] = +(g₀ − x)⁻²:

$$
F_e(x) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - x)^{2}}
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{F_e(x) \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - x)^{2}}}
$$

The force grows hyperbolically as the gap closes, doubling in magnitude when the voltage is increased by a factor of √2.

---

**(b) What is the pull-in instability? Derive the pull-in voltage and the pull-in displacement for a parallel-plate actuator with linear spring constant k. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Equilibrium equation (2 marks)**

Spring force F_k = kx balances the electrostatic force:

$$
k x \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2\,(g_0 - x)^{2}} \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Stability condition (1 mark)**

For stable equilibrium: k ≥ dF_e/dx, where:

$$
\frac{dF_e}{dx} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{(g_0 - x)^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Tangency (pull-in) condition (2 marks)**

At pull-in, equality and stability hold simultaneously:

$$
k \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{pi}^{2}}{(g_0 - x_{pi})^{3}} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

Divide (ii) by (i): 1/x = 2/(g₀ − x) ⟹ **x_pi = g₀/3**.

**Step 4 — Substitute back to find V_pi (2 marks)**

Insert x = g₀/3 into (i):

$$
k \cdot \frac{g_0}{3} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V_{pi}^{2}}{2\,(2g_0/3)^{2}}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
V_{pi} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{8\,k\,g_0^{3}}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}}
$$

**Final boxed results:**

$$
\boxed{x_{pi} \;=\; \frac{g_0}{3}}\qquad\boxed{V_{pi} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{8\,k\,g_0^{3}}{27\,\varepsilon_0 A}}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Part B (Question A):**
> 1. **Sign errors in force derivation** — students often drop the negative sign from −dU/dx and end up with F = −½V² dC/dx. KTU key insists on the **attractive (positive)** form. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the stability condition** — dF_e/dx has a factor of 2 relative to F_e/x. Mis-applying this yields x_pi = g₀/2 instead of g₀/3. (–3 marks)
> 3. **Units of ε₀** — must be F/m, not F/cm. Mixing units silently loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not stating the assumptions** — ideal voltage source, no fringing, plate motion only along x. Adding one sentence on assumptions gains 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a comb-drive actuator. Derive the expression for the lateral electrostatic force. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Description (1 mark)**

A comb drive consists of two sets of interdigitated fingers: one set anchored to the substrate (fixed) and the other set attached to a movable shuttle suspended by mechanical springs. When a voltage is applied between the two sets, lateral fringing-field capacitance develops in the inter-finger gaps, producing a horizontal attractive force.

**Step 2 — Capacitance expression (2 marks)**

For N movable fingers, each of height h, overlap (L + x), and inter-finger gap g:

$$
C(x) \;=\; \frac{2 N \varepsilon_0 h (L + x)}{g}
$$

(The factor 2 accounts for the two gaps flanking each movable finger.)

**Step 3 — Differentiate and find force (2 marks)**

$$
\frac{dC}{dx} \;=\; \frac{2 N \varepsilon_0 h}{g} \;=\; \text{constant}
$$

$$
F_{\text{comb}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,V^{2}\,\frac{dC}{dx} \;=\; \frac{N\,\varepsilon_0 h V^{2}}{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Schematic and physical explanation (2 marks)**

Refer to the Mermaid schematic in **Section 4.2**. The constant force pulls the shuttle laterally until balanced by the folded-beam spring. The actuator is linear and stable.

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{comb}} \;=\; \frac{N\,\varepsilon_0\,h\,V^{2}}{g}}
$$

---

**(b) Compare the parallel-plate and comb-drive actuators with respect to (i) force-displacement profile, (ii) pull-in behaviour, (iii) typical applications, and (iv) usable travel range. (7 marks)**

| S.No. | Comparison Parameter | Parallel-Plate Actuator | Comb-Drive Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Force profile | Non-linear, F ∝ 1/(g₀ − x)² | **Constant**, F = N ε₀ h V² / g |
| (ii) | Pull-in | Occurs at x = g₀ / 3; **catastrophic** | **No pull-in** in lateral direction |
| (iii) | Typical applications | RF MEMS switches, DMD, variable capacitors | MEMS gyroscopes, accelerometers, optical scanners, micro-grippers |
| (iv) | Usable travel | ≤ g₀ / 3 (≈ 33 % of gap) | Full overlap length L (often several µm) |
| (v) | Capacitance gradient | Non-linear in x | **Linear** in x |
| (vi) | Drive voltage | High (typically 20–100 V) | Lower (typically 5–20 V) |
| (vii) | Fabrication complexity | Simpler | Slightly more complex (more fingers, anchors) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Comb drives are preferred for applications requiring **linear response and large stable travel** (e.g., gyroscopes, accelerometers). Parallel-plate actuators are preferred where **large force per unit area** is needed over a tiny gap (e.g., RF switches, optical displays).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Part B (Question B):**
> 1. **Confusing "constant force" with "no force"** — the comb-drive force is constant *with respect to displacement*, not zero. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Omitting the factor of 2 in the capacitance** — students often write C = N ε₀ h (L+x)/g, missing that each movable finger has **two** gaps. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Comparing the wrong quantities** — the question asks for *force–displacement* profile; giving the formula alone without qualitative description loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Stating "no pull-in" without justification** — must explain *why* (force independent of x). Just stating it is worth only 1 of 2 marks.
> 5. **Applications in only one domain** — listing 2 RF and 2 inertial applications gains full credit; mixing domains shows breadth.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Electrostatic actuation** = Coulombic force between charged conductors; force ∝ **V²**, always attractive, polarity-independent.
- **Energy method** is the universal tool: F = ½ V² (dC/dx) at constant voltage.
- **Parallel-plate force** = ½ ε₀ A V² / (g₀ − x)² — non-linear, **hyperbolic in x**.
- **Comb-drive force** = N ε₀ h V² / g — **constant in x**, the key advantage.
- **Pull-in displacement** = **g₀/3** (one-third of the initial gap) — derived from the tangency of mechanical and electrostatic force curves.
- **Pull-in voltage** = **√(8 k g₀³ / 27 ε₀ A)** — must be quoted with **units** (volts) and **assumptions** (ideal voltage source, no fringing).
- **Comb drives have NO pull-in** in the ideal lateral model because F is independent of x — equilibrium kx = F_constant is solvable for *any* x up to geometric disengagement.
- **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** — always in SI; the most common unit-conversion mistake.
- **Folding beam suspensions** are used in comb drives for in-plane linear compliance.
- **Effective spring constant k** depends on suspension geometry: for a folded beam of length L_f, width w, thickness t, and Young's modulus E, k ≈ E t (w/L_f)³ (per beam).
- **Fringing fields** add a multiplicative correction α ≈ 1.05 to comb-drive capacitance; in KTU problems, take α = 1 unless stated otherwise.
- **Usable travel** of a parallel-plate actuator is limited to **33 %** of the gap; this is the chief reason comb drives dominate inertial sensors.
- **Common KTU mistakes**: wrong sign in force, missing the 2 in 2 N ε₀ h (L+x)/g, using g₀ instead of (g₀ − x) in pull-in, mixing F/m with F/cm units.
- **Industry relevance**: Bosch, STMicroelectronics, Analog Devices, and InvenSense all use comb drives in production MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers — a key talking point in viva voce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In 14-mark questions, the KTU examiner's marking scheme reserves **2 marks** for "diagram/schematic quality" and "presentation/assumptions stated". Always draw (or describe) the structure, label all symbols, state assumptions explicitly, and box the final formula. Students who do this consistently gain 1–2 extra marks per answer — over a full paper, that is the difference between an S grade and an A grade.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micromechanical Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micromechanical Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Micromechanical Pump** (or MEMS micropump) is a microscale fluid-handling device fabricated using MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) fabrication technologies, designed to transport, meter, or mix precise volumes of fluids (typically in the microliter to picoliter range) through microchannels. These pumps integrate mechanical moving parts (membranes, valves, diaphragms) and actuators (piezoelectric, electrostatic, thermal) on a single silicon, polymer, or glass substrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A micromechanical pump is a microfluidic actuator that converts electrical, mechanical, or thermal energy into controlled fluid motion through microchannels, characterized by flow rates typically ranging from **0.1 µL/min to 10 mL/min** and operating pressures up to several atmospheres.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **straw in a juice box**. When you blow into the straw, a one-way valve opens and pushes juice up and out. Micromechanical pumps work in exactly the same way, but:
- The "straw" is a microchannel **~100 µm wide** (thinner than a human hair).
- The "blowing" is replaced by a **vibrating membrane** (piezoelectric or electrostatic) that pulses thousands of times per second.
- The "one-way valve" is a **microfabricated check valve** etched into silicon.
- The whole assembly fits on a chip the size of a **fingernail** (~1 cm²).

Think of it as a **"heart on a chip"** — a tiny mechanical heart that pulses fluid through artificial blood vessels (microchannels) for applications like drug delivery, lab-on-a-chip diagnostics, and micro-cooling of processors.

## 1.3 Classification of Micromechanical Pumps

Micropumps are broadly classified into two categories:

| Category | Sub-types | Working Principle |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Mechanical Pumps** | Reciprocating (Peristaltic, Piezoelectric, Electrostatic, Electromagnetic) | Physical displacement of fluid by moving parts |
| **Non-Mechanical Pumps** | Electroosmotic, Electrohydrodynamic, Capillary, Bubble-driven | Fluid motion through electrokinetic/thermal effects |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU?**
> Out of all subtypes, **Piezoelectric micropumps** and **Peristaltic micropumps** are the most frequently asked in university exams because they represent the canonical displacement-based micropump architecture.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics

The following performance metrics are **standardized across the MEMS literature** and are routinely tested in KTU exams:

- **Flow Rate (Q)**: Volume of fluid delivered per unit time, typically **µL/min** or **nL/s**.
- **Back Pressure (ΔP)**: Maximum pressure the pump can overcome, typically **10 kPa – 100 kPa**.
- **Power Consumption**: Usually **1 mW – 100 mW** for battery operation.
- **Self-priming capability**: Ability to start from an empty (air-filled) state.
- **Biocompatibility**: Critical for biomedical use (e.g., insulin pumps).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Reciprocating Displacement Pump

The dominant micromechanical pump topology is the **reciprocating displacement pump**, which operates in two phases:

### Phase 1: Supply (Suction) Stroke
1. The actuator (piezoelectric disc, electrostatic comb, etc.) **deflects the membrane downward**.
2. This **increases** the chamber volume.
3. The **inlet check valve opens** (low pressure side).
4. The **outlet check valve closes** (high pressure side).
5. Fluid is **drawn into the chamber** from the inlet reservoir.

### Phase 2: Pump (Delivery) Stroke
1. The actuator **deflects the membrane upward**.
2. This **decreases** the chamber volume.
3. The **inlet check valve closes**.
4. The **outlet check valve opens**.
5. Fluid is **pushed out** of the chamber into the outlet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Valve Action**
> Valves work due to pressure asymmetry. When chamber pressure > outlet pressure → outlet valve opens. When chamber pressure < inlet pressure → inlet valve opens. This passive rectification of AC membrane motion into DC net flow is the core innovation of mechanical micropumps.

## 2.2 The Peristaltic Micropump (No-Valve Architecture)

In peristaltic micropumps, the fluid is pumped by a **traveling wave of contractions** along a flexible tube — analogous to how the human esophagus moves food to the stomach.

- Three or more actuators are arranged sequentially along the tube.
- By activating them in a phase-shifted sequence (0°, 120°, 240°), a **peristaltic wave** propagates along the channel.
- Fluid is pushed forward in a continuous, pulse-free flow.

**Why use peristaltic?** Eliminates check valves (which are failure-prone in MEMS), provides bidirectional flow, and allows gentle handling of sensitive fluids (blood, proteins).

## 2.3 Actuation Mechanisms — A Detailed Comparison

| Actuation Type | Principle | Force Output | Response Time | Voltage Required |
|----------------|-----------|--------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Piezoelectric** | Inverse piezoelectric effect (PZT ceramic disc) | **High** (N-scale) | **Fast** (µs) | **50 – 200 V** |
| **Electrostatic** | Coulomb attraction between charged plates/combs | Moderate | Fast | 30 – 100 V |
| **Electromagnetic** | Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor in B-field | High | Moderate | 1 – 5 V (low voltage!) |
| **Thermopneumatic** | Heating-induced gas expansion | Moderate | Slow | 2 – 5 V |
| **Shape Memory Alloy** | Phase transition of NiTi alloy | Very High | Slow | 1 – 3 V |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **exam-critical**. Master every variable and unit.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables |
|---|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_h}$ | **Flow rate** = Pressure drop / Hydraulic resistance |
| 2 | $R_h = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^{4}}$ | Hydraulic resistance of a **circular microchannel** (Hagen-Poiseuille) |
| 3 | $R_h = \dfrac{12 \mu L}{w h^{3}} \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{192 h}{\pi^{5} w} \sum_{n=1,3,5}^{\infty} \dfrac{\tanh(n\pi w / 2h)}{n^{5}}\right]$ | Hydraulic resistance of a **rectangular microchannel** |
| 4 | $\Delta V = \pi r^{2} \cdot d$ | Volumetric displacement per stroke of a circular membrane (d = deflection) |
| 5 | $Q = \Delta V \cdot f$ | **Average flow rate** = Stroke volume × Actuation frequency |
| 6 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Maximum generated pressure = Actuator force / Membrane area |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds number** for flow regime identification |
| 8 | $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P_{wet}}$ | **Hydraulic diameter** of channel |
| 9 | $f_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}}$ | **Resonance frequency** of membrane (k = stiffness, m_eff = effective mass) |
| 10 | $F_{piezo} = d_{31} \cdot E \cdot A \cdot V$ | Force generated by piezoelectric actuator (d₃₁ = charge coefficient) |
| 11 | $F_{elec} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} A V^{2}}{g^{2}}$ | Electrostatic force (parallel plate) |
| 12 | $P_{vapor} = P_{0} \exp\left(-\dfrac{2\sigma V_{m}}{r R T}\right)$ | **Kelvin equation** — capillary pressure in micropumps |

> Where:
> - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
> - $L$ = channel length (m)
> - $r$ = channel radius (m)
> - $w, h$ = channel width, height (m)
> - $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
> - $v$ = mean velocity (m/s)
> - $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m)
> - $\varepsilon_{0}$ = permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|--------------------|----------|
| **Biomedical** | Implantable drug delivery (insulin, analgesics), microdialysis |
| **Lab-on-a-Chip** | Precise reagent mixing for PCR, ELISA assays |
| **Microelectronics Cooling** | Forced liquid cooling of high-power CPUs/GPUs |
| **Fuel Cells** | Hydrogen/oxygen recirculation in micro fuel cells |
| **Aerospace** | Propellant management in microthrusters for CubeSats |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Flow Rate of a Reciprocating Micropump

### Step 1: Define the Chamber Geometry
Consider a cylindrical pump chamber of radius $r$ capped with a flexible membrane.

### Step 2: Compute the Stroke Volume
The membrane deflects by a maximum distance $d$ at its center (for small deflections, $d \ll r$). The displaced volume equals the volume under the deflected membrane:

$$\Delta V = \pi r^{2} \cdot d$$

> **Logic**: We approximate the deflected membrane as a flat piston (valid only for $d \ll r$). For more accurate calculation, integrate the deflection profile $w(r) = w_{0}\left(1 - r^{2}/a^{2}\right)^{2}$ over the membrane area.

### Step 3: Apply Valve Rectification Efficiency
Not all fluid delivered in the supply stroke returns through the inlet; some is delivered to the outlet. Define the **valve efficiency** $\eta_{v}$:

$$\eta_{v} = \dfrac{V_{delivered}}{\Delta V}$$

Typically, $\eta_{v}$ = 0.6 – 0.9 for well-designed check valves.

### Step 4: Compute Time-Averaged Flow Rate
For a sinusoidal actuation of frequency $f$ (Hz), the average flow rate over one cycle is:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \Delta V \cdot f \cdot \eta_{v} \\[6pt]
  &= \pi r^{2} \cdot d \cdot f \cdot \eta_{v}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Pressure-Flow Relationship
When delivering against a back-pressure $\Delta P$, the effective flow rate drops:

$$Q = \pi r^{2} d f \eta_{v} \left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_{max}}\right)$$

This is a **linear approximation** valid for moderate back-pressures ($P_{max}$ = maximum no-flow pressure the pump can generate).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Flow in a Rectangular Microchannel

This derivation is the **most commonly tested** in KTU exams.

### Step 1: Setup the Navier-Stokes Equation
For steady, incompressible, fully-developed flow in a rectangular channel of width $w$ and height $h$, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:

$$\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$$

### Step 2: Apply No-Slip Boundary Conditions
At the four walls ($y = \pm h/2$, $z = \pm w/2$), velocity $u = 0$.

### Step 3: Solve via Separation of Variables
The general solution is an infinite Fourier series:

$$u(y,z) = \dfrac{4 h^{2}}{\mu \pi^{3}} \left(-\dfrac{dP}{dx}\right) \sum_{n=1,3,5}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^{(n-1)/2}}{n^{3}} \left[1 - \dfrac{\cosh(n\pi z / h)}{\cosh(n\pi w / 2h)}\right] \cos\left(\dfrac{n\pi y}{h}\right)$$

### Step 4: Integrate to Obtain Volumetric Flow Rate
$$Q = \int_{-w/2}^{w/2} \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} u(y,z) \, dy \, dz$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form — Hydraulic Resistance
$$\begin{aligned}
R_{h} &= \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q} \\[6pt]
      &= \dfrac{12 \mu L}{w h^{3}} \left[1 - \dfrac{192 h}{\pi^{5} w} \sum_{n=1,3,5}^{\infty} \dfrac{\tanh(n\pi w / 2h)}{n^{5}}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic of every term**:
> - $12\mu L / w h^{3}$ = leading-order parallel-plate result
> - The summation term = **finite-width correction** (accounts for the sidewalls; vanishes when $w \gg h$)
> - **Key insight**: $R_{h}$ scales as $1/h^{3}$ — halving the channel height increases resistance by **8×**!

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Numerical Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is representative of **real-world MEMS micropumps** published in peer-reviewed literature. KTU problems often quote similar numbers.

| Parameter | Value Range | Units |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Chamber radius ($r$) | 2 – 10 | mm |
| Membrane deflection ($d$) | 5 – 50 | µm |
| Actuation frequency ($f$) | 10 – 1000 | Hz |
| Channel width ($w$) | 50 – 500 | µm |
| Channel height ($h$) | 20 – 200 | µm |
| Hydraulic resistance ($R_{h}$) | 10⁸ – 10¹¹ | Pa·s/m³ |
| Flow rate ($Q$) | 1 – 1000 | µL/min |
| Back pressure ($P_{max}$) | 10 – 100 | kPa |
| Reynolds number ($Re$) | 1 – 100 | dimensionless |
| Power consumption | 1 – 500 | mW |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Reciprocating Micropump Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Voltage Source] --> B[Function Generator]
    B --> C[High Voltage Amplifier]
    C --> D[Piezoelectric Disc Actuator]
    D --> E[Membrane Diaphragm]
    E --> F[Pump Chamber]
    F --> G{Inlet Check Valve}
    G --> H[Inlet Reservoir]
    F --> I{Outlet Check Valve}
    I --> J[Outlet Microchannel]
    J --> K[Delivery Point]
```

> **Reading this diagram**: The function generator produces a low-voltage sinusoidal waveform (e.g., 1 V), which the high-voltage amplifier boosts to 100 V to drive the piezoelectric disc. The disc vibrates the membrane, creating pulsating pressure in the chamber, rectified by the two check valves into net flow from reservoir to delivery.

## 4.2 State Diagram — Peristaltic Pump Activation Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state "Actuator 1 ON" as s1
    state "Actuator 2 ON" as s2
    state "Actuator 3 ON" as s3
    state "All OFF Reset" as s0
    [*] --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s0
    s0 --> s1
```

> **Reading this diagram**: In a 3-phase peristaltic pump, only one actuator is ON at a time, and the ON state rotates sequentially from Actuator 1 → Actuator 2 → Actuator 3. This produces a traveling wave that pushes fluid forward (rightward, in the channel orientation).

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Pump Stroke Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StrokeA[Supply Stroke]
        A1[Membrane deflects DOWN] --> A2[Chamber volume INCREASES]
        A2 --> A3[Inlet valve OPENS]
        A3 --> A4[Outlet valve CLOSES]
        A4 --> A5[Fluid DRAWN IN from inlet]
    end

    subgraph StrokeB[Delivery Stroke]
        B1[Membrane deflects UP] --> B2[Chamber volume DECREASES]
        B2 --> B3[Inlet valve CLOSES]
        B3 --> B4[Outlet valve OPENS]
        B4 --> B5[Fluid PUSHED OUT to outlet]
    end

    StrokeA --> StrokeB
    StrokeB --> StrokeA
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopLayer[Top Layer - Silicon Glass Cap]
        L1[Inlet Port]
        L2[Outlet Port]
    end

    subgraph MiddleLayer[Middle Layer - Membrane and Chamber]
        L3[Piezoelectric Disc]
        L4[Silicon Membrane]
        L5[Pump Chamber Cavity]
        L6[Inlet Check Valve]
        L7[Outlet Check Valve]
    end

    subgraph BottomLayer[Bottom Layer - Microchannel]
        L8[Micromachined Microchannel]
    end

    L1 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L7
    L7 --> L2
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L7 --> L8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a micromechanical pump. List the two main categories with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
A micromechanical pump is a micro-fabricated device that transports precise volumes of fluid through microchannels using MEMS actuators. The two main categories are:
1. **Mechanical / Displacement Pumps** — Example: Piezoelectric reciprocating pump with check valves.
2. **Non-Mechanical Pumps** — Example: Electroosmotic pump using DC electric field.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for classification with example)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State Hagen-Poiseuille's equation for flow through a circular microchannel. What does it predict about the role of channel radius?**

**Model Answer:**
$$\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$$
The pressure drop $\Delta P$ is **inversely proportional to the fourth power of the radius**. Thus, halving the channel radius increases the pressure drop by **16×**, making microchannels inherently very resistive.

*(3 marks: 1 for equation, 2 for fourth-power dependence explanation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block schematic, explain the working principle of a reciprocating displacement-type micromechanical pump using a piezoelectric actuator. Discuss the role of check valves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the average flow rate of the pump. A micropump has a chamber radius of 4 mm, a peak membrane deflection of 15 µm, and operates at 100 Hz with a valve efficiency of 0.8. Calculate the average flow rate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
A reciprocating displacement pump uses a **piezoelectric disc** bonded to a flexible silicon membrane. When a sinusoidal voltage (typically 50–200 V) is applied, the piezo disc expands/contracts, deflecting the membrane by 5–50 µm. This causes the chamber volume to alternately increase and decrease.

Two passive **check valves** (inlet and outlet) rectify the alternating pressure into a net unidirectional flow:
- During **supply stroke** (membrane down): chamber pressure drops below inlet pressure → inlet valve opens → fluid enters.
- During **delivery stroke** (membrane up): chamber pressure exceeds outlet pressure → outlet valve opens → fluid exits.

The check valves therefore act as a **mechanical rectifier**, converting AC membrane motion into DC flow.

*(Marks: 2 for schematic, 3 for working, 2 for valve role)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1**: State the formula for stroke volume of a circular membrane:
$$\Delta V = \pi r^{2} \cdot d$$

**Step 2**: Substitute $r = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m and $d = 15 \times 10^{-6}$ m:
$$\Delta V = \pi \cdot (4 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \cdot 15 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\Delta V = \pi \cdot 16 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 15 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\Delta V = 240\pi \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{3}$$
$$\Delta V = 7.5398 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^{3} = 753.98 \text{ nL}$$

**Step 3**: Apply the average flow rate formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \Delta V \cdot f \cdot \eta_{v} \\
  &= 7.5398 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 100 \cdot 0.8 \\
  &= 6.032 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: Convert to µL/min:
$$Q = 6.032 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{9} \times 60 = 3.62 \text{ µL/min}$$

**Answer**: $Q \approx 3.62$ µL/min

*(Marks: 2 for formula, 3 for substitution and calculation, 2 for final answer and units)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare peristaltic and check-valve based micropumps. Explain why peristaltic pumps are preferred for handling biological fluids. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a rectangular microchannel of width $w = 200$ µm, height $h = 50$ µm, and length $L = 10$ mm carrying water ($\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s), calculate the hydraulic resistance and the flow rate for a pressure drop of $\Delta P = 10$ kPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Feature | Peristaltic Pump | Check-Valve Pump |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Valves | No mechanical valves | Two check valves required |
| Flow direction | Bidirectional | Unidirectional only |
| Valves wear | None | Susceptible to fatigue/fouling |
| Flow pulsation | Smooth, pulse-free | Pulsatile |
| Fabrication complexity | Higher (3+ actuators) | Lower (2 actuators) |
| Biocompatibility | High (no dead zones) | Moderate (valve seat crevices) |

Peristaltic pumps are preferred for biological fluids (blood, plasma, proteins) because:
1. **No check valves** = no dead volume or crevices where cells/proteins can accumulate and clot.
2. **Gentle, continuous flow** avoids hemolysis (red blood cell rupture).
3. **Self-priming** and sterile operation.
4. Easy to **sterilize** because the fluid only contacts a smooth tube.

*(Marks: 3 for comparison table, 4 for biocompatibility reasoning)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1**: Check the aspect ratio. $w/h = 200/50 = 4$ (moderate). For $w/h \geq 4$, the sidewall correction term is small. We use the simplified formula:

$$R_h \approx \dfrac{12 \mu L}{w h^{3}}$$

**Step 2**: Substitute values:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_h &= \dfrac{12 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3})}{(200 \times 10^{-6})(50 \times 10^{-6})^{3}} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{12 \times 10^{-5}}{200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 125000 \times 10^{-18}} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{12 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-17}} \\[6pt]
    &= 4.8 \times 10^{12} \text{ Pa·s/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3**: Calculate flow rate:
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_h} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{3}}{4.8 \times 10^{12}} \\[6pt]
  &= 2.083 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4**: Convert to µL/min:
$$Q = 2.083 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^{9} \times 60 = 0.125 \text{ µL/min}$$

**Answer**: $R_h \approx 4.8 \times 10^{12}$ Pa·s/m³; $Q \approx 0.125$ µL/min

*(Marks: 2 for formula selection, 3 for substitution, 2 for final numerical answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit errors**: Mixing µm and mm, or converting Pa·s/m³ incorrectly. Always check SI units before submitting.
> 2. **Forgetting valve efficiency $\eta_{v}$**: Many students compute $Q = \pi r^{2} d f$ without multiplying by $\eta_{v}$. This results in an **overestimation** of flow rate by 10–40%.
> 3. **Wrong formula for rectangular channel**: Using the circular Hagen-Poiseuille for a rectangular channel is a common error. Always use $R_h = 12\mu L / (w h^{3})$ for rectangular channels.
> 4. **Not defining hydraulic diameter**: When asked to compute Reynolds number in a non-circular channel, students forget $D_h = 4A/P_{wet}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Micromechanical Pumps**

- ✅ A **micromechanical pump** is a MEMS device that delivers **µL/min-scale** fluid flow through microchannels.
- ✅ Two main classes: **Mechanical (displacement)** and **Non-Mechanical (electrokinetic)**.
- ✅ Reciprocating pumps use **piezoelectric / electrostatic / electromagnetic** actuators to vibrate a membrane.
- ✅ **Check valves** act as mechanical rectifiers, converting AC membrane motion into DC net flow.
- ✅ **Peristaltic pumps** use a phase-shifted sequence of actuators to generate a traveling wave — preferred for biomedical fluids due to absence of valves.
- ✅ Flow rate: $Q = \pi r^{2} d f \eta_{v}$ for circular chamber.
- ✅ Hagen-Poiseuille (circular): $\Delta P = 8\mu L Q / (\pi r^{4})$.
- ✅ Hydraulic resistance (rectangular): $R_h = 12\mu L / (w h^{3})$ for $w \gg h$.
- ✅ Reynolds number in micropumps is typically **Re < 100** → flow is **laminar**.
- ✅ Key actuation voltages: Piezo (50–200 V), Electrostatic (30–100 V), Electromagnetic (1–5 V).
- ✅ Performance metrics: **Flow rate (Q)**, **Back pressure ($\Delta P$)**, **Power consumption**, **Self-priming**.
- ✅ Applications: **Drug delivery**, **Lab-on-a-chip**, **Micro-cooling**, **Micro fuel cells**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nanoelectronics and Quantum Mechanics: Atomic Structures and Quantum Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Nanoelectronics and Quantum Mechanics: Atomic Structures and Quantum Mechanics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Quantum Mechanics** is the fundamental branch of physics that describes the behavior of matter and energy at atomic and subatomic scales, where classical Newtonian mechanics fails. In the context of **nanoelectronics**, quantum mechanics provides the theoretical foundation for understanding how electrons behave when confined to dimensions comparable to their de Broglie wavelength (typically **1–100 nm**). **Atomic Structure** refers to the organized arrangement of protons, neutrons, and electrons within an atom.
