The quantum mechanical model describes electrons not as orbiting particles (Bohr model) but as standing waves described by **wavefunctions** $\psi(x, y, z, t)$ whose probability density is given by $\vert \psi \vert^2$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** At the nanoscale, classical physics breaks down and quantum phenomena such as **quantum confinement**, **wave-particle duality**, and **energy quantization** dominate the electronic behavior of materials. ### 1.2 Key Atomic Models — Evolution of Thought | Model | Year | Core Idea | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | Dalton's Model | 1803 | Atom as indivisible sphere | No substructure | | Thomson's "Plum Pudding" | 1904 | Electrons embedded in positive sphere | Could not explain scattering | | Rutherford's Nuclear Model | 1911 | Dense nucleus + orbiting electrons | Electron acceleration → radiation (unstable) | | Bohr's Model | 1913 | Quantized circular orbits | Only works for hydrogen | | **Quantum Mechanical Model** | 1926 | Electron as wavefunction (Schrödinger) | Probabilistic interpretation | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pond Ripples" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — Electron as a Standing Wave** > Imagine dropping a stone into a circular pond. The ripples form standing wave patterns — only certain wavelengths "fit" cleanly around the boundary. Similarly, when an electron is confined in an atom or nanostructure, only specific standing-wave patterns (called **orbitals** or **modes**) are allowed. Each allowed pattern corresponds to a discrete energy — this is **energy quantization**. **Why is this important for nanoelectronics?** - In a **bulk** material, electrons have many allowed wavelengths → nearly continuous energy bands. - In a **nanostructure** (quantum well, wire, or dot), the dimensions restrict which waves fit → energy becomes **discrete**. - This is why nanoscale transistors, quantum dots, and single-electron devices behave fundamentally differently from their bulk counterparts. ### 1.4 Fundamental Constants Used in Nanoelectronics - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** - Reduced Planck's constant: $\hbar = h / 2\pi = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** - Electron rest mass: $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg** - Elementary charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** - Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Mandate:** Mastery of the **Schrödinger equation**, **particle-in-a-box problem**, and the resulting **density of states** equations (for 3D, 2D, 1D, 0D) is mandatory for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Pillars of Quantum Mechanics **Pillar 1 — Wave-Particle Duality (de Broglie, 1924)** Every particle has an associated wavelength called the de Broglie wavelength: $$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv}$$ where $p = mv$ is the momentum. For electrons accelerated through a potential $V$ (in volts), this becomes: $$\lambda \text{ (nm)} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** For an electron accelerated through **1 V**, $\lambda \approx 1.227$ nm — comparable to atomic spacings! This is why electron-beam lithography and electron microscopy can resolve atomic features. **Pillar 2 — Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle (1927)** It is fundamentally impossible to simultaneously know both the exact position and exact momentum of a particle: $$\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}$$ A common engineering form used in KTU problems: $$\Delta x \cdot \Delta v \geq \frac{\hbar}{2m}$$ In energy–time form: $$\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}$$ ### 2.2 The Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation The master equation governing quantum systems: $$\hat{H}\psi(x) = E\psi(x)$$ Expanded in 1D: $$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2\psi(x)}{dx^2} + V(x)\psi(x) = E\psi(x)$$ where: - $\hat{H}$ = Hamiltonian operator (total energy operator) - $\psi(x)$ = wavefunction - $V(x)$ = potential energy function - $E$ = total energy eigenvalue ### 2.3 The Particle-in-a-Box (Infinite Potential Well) A foundational model for **quantum confinement** — the heart of nanoelectronics. **Setup:** An electron confined in a 1D box of length $L$ with infinitely high walls at $x = 0$ and $x = L$. **Boundary conditions:** - $\psi(0) = 0$ - $\psi(L) = 0$ **Solutions (after applying boundary conditions):** $$\psi_n(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}\sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right), \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ $$E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2mL^2} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8mL^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Nanoelectronics Insight:** Energy $E_n \propto 1/L^2$. As $L$ shrinks to nanoscale, the **energy spacing between levels grows enormously** — this is the essence of quantum confinement! ### 2.4 Density of States (DOS) — Engineering Tool The **Density of States** $g(E)$ gives the number of available electron states per unit energy per unit volume. As dimensionality reduces (3D → 2D → 1D → 0D), the DOS function changes shape: | Dimensionality | System Example | Density of States $g(E)$ | Behavior near band edge | |---|---|---|---| | **3D (Bulk)** | Transistor channel | $g_{3D}(E) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$ | $g(E) \propto \sqrt{E}$ (parabolic) | | **2D (Quantum Well)** | HEMT, MQW laser | $g_{2D}(E) = \dfrac{m}{\pi\hbar^2}$ (step-like) | Constant above $E_c$ | | **1D (Quantum Wire)** | Nanowire FET | $g_{1D}(E) = \dfrac{1}{\pi\hbar}\sqrt{\dfrac{2m}{E}}$ | $\propto 1/\sqrt{E}$ (van Hove singularity) | | **0D (Quantum Dot)** | Single-electron transistor | $g_{0D}(E) = 2\delta(E - E_n)$ | Discrete delta functions | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** A 0D quantum dot has **discrete energy levels** like an artificial atom — this is exploited in **quantum-dot LEDs (QLEDs)**, **single-photon sources**, and **biomedical tags**. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | de Broglie wavelength | $\lambda = h / mv$ | $m$ in kg, $v$ in m/s | | 2 | de Broglie (electron, V) | $\lambda \text{ nm} = 1.227 / \sqrt{V}$ | $V$ in volts | | 3 | Heisenberg position-momentum | $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar / 2$ | SI units | | 4 | Heisenberg energy-time | $\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \hbar / 2$ | SI units | | 5 | Schrödinger (1D, time-indep.) | $-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\psi'' + V\psi = E\psi$ | SI units | | 6 | Particle-in-a-box eigenenergy | $E_n = n^2 h^2 / (8mL^2)$ | $L$ in m, $n = 1, 2, \ldots$ | | 7 | PIB wavefunction | $\psi_n = \sqrt{2/L}\,\sin(n\pi x / L)$ | $0 \leq x \leq L$ | | 8 | Energy spacing (PIB) | $\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = 3h^2 / (8mL^2)$ | SI units | | 9 | DOS 3D | $g_{3D} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$ | states/J/m³ | | 10 | DOS 2D | $g_{2D} = m / (\pi\hbar^2)$ | states/J/m² | | 11 | DOS 1D | $g_{1D} = \dfrac{1}{\pi\hbar}\sqrt{\dfrac{2m}{E}}$ | states/J/m | | 12 | DOS 0D | $g_{0D} = 2\delta(E - E_n)$ | states | | 13 | Quantum confinement condition | $L \leq \lambda_{dB}$ | Confinement onset | | 14 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu = hc / \lambda$ | $\nu$ in Hz, $\lambda$ in m | ### 2.6 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering - **Transistor scaling:** As MOSFET gate length approaches $\sim$5 nm, electrons experience quantum confinement in the channel → classical drift-diffusion models fail. - **Quantum cascade lasers:** Exploit discrete sub-band transitions in quantum wells. - **Tunnel diodes and resonant tunneling devices:** Use quantum tunneling (a direct consequence of the Schrödinger equation with finite potential barriers). - **Single-electron transistors:** Operate on the Coulomb blockade principle, a quantum-mechanical effect. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world use:** Quantum mechanical models underpin **TCAD simulation tools** (Sentaurus, ATLAS, nextnano) used by every semiconductor fab — Intel, TSMC, Samsung — to design sub-7 nm chips. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: de Broglie Wavelength for an Electron Accelerated Through Potential $V$ **Step 1 — Energy gained by electron:** An electron accelerated through potential $V$ gains kinetic energy equal to $eV$ (work done by the field): $$eV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$ **Step 2 — Solve for velocity $v$:** $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m}}$$ **Step 3 — Apply de Broglie relation** $\lambda = h / (mv)$: $$\lambda = \frac{h}{m\sqrt{2eV/m}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2meV}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute constants** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C: $$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times V}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute numerical coefficient** (in meters): $$\lambda = \frac{1.227 \times 10^{-9}}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ meters} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Particle in a 1D Infinite Potential Well **Step 1 — Set up the Schrödinger equation inside the well** ($0 < x < L$, where $V = 0$): $$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = E\psi$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to a standard ODE form:** $$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = -\frac{2mE}{\hbar^2}\psi = -k^2\psi$$ where we define $k^2 = 2mE/\hbar^2$. **Step 3 — General solution of the ODE** (oscillator equation): $$\psi(x) = A\sin(kx) + B\cos(kx)$$ **Step 4 — Apply first boundary condition** $\psi(0) = 0$: $$0 = A\sin(0) + B\cos(0) \implies B = 0$$ So: $$\psi(x) = A\sin(kx)$$ **Step 5 — Apply second boundary condition** $\psi(L) = 0$: $$0 = A\sin(kL)$$ **Step 6 — Non-trivial solution** ($A \neq 0$) requires: $$\sin(kL) = 0 \implies kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ (Excluding $n = 0$ because it would give $\psi = 0$ everywhere — no particle.) **Step 7 — Solve for allowed $k$ values:** $$k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L}$$ **Step 8 — Substitute back into energy expression** $E = \hbar^2 k^2 / (2m)$: $$E_n = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2 = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2mL^2} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8mL^2}$$ **Step 9 — Determine normalization constant** $A$: Using $\int_0^L \vert \psi \vert^2 dx = 1$: $$A^2 \int_0^L \sin^2\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right) dx = A^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 1 \implies A = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}$$ **Final normalized wavefunction:** $$\boxed{\psi_n(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}\sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)}$$ **Final energy eigenvalues:** $$\boxed{E_n = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8mL^2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Gap Between Consecutive Levels (PIB) The energy difference between the $n$-th and $(n+1)$-th level is: $$\Delta E = E_{n+1} - E_n = \frac{(n+1)^2 h^2}{8mL^2} - \frac{n^2 h^2}{8mL^2}$$ $$\Delta E = \frac{h^2}{8mL^2}\left[(n+1)^2 - n^2\right] = \frac{h^2}{8mL^2}(2n + 1)$$ For the lowest transition ($n = 1 \to n = 2$): $$\Delta E_{1\to 2} = \frac{3h^2}{8mL^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Check:** For an electron in a 10 nm quantum well, $E_1 \approx 0.038$ eV, and $\Delta E_{1\to 2} \approx 0.113$ eV — large enough to be observed at room temperature ($k_BT \approx 0.025$ eV). ### 3.4 Derivation: Density of States in 3D **Step 1 — Count states in k-space.** Each allowed quantum state occupies a volume $(2\pi/L)^3$ in k-space. The number of states inside a sphere of radius $k$ is: $$N(k) = 2 \times \frac{\text{Volume of sphere}}{\text{Volume per state}} = 2 \cdot \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi k^3}{(2\pi/L)^3} = \frac{k^3 V}{3\pi^2}$$ (Factor 2 is for spin degeneracy.) **Step 2 — Express $k$ in terms of energy.** For free electrons: $$E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m} \implies k = \frac{\sqrt{2mE}}{\hbar}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$N(E) = \frac{V}{3\pi^2}\left(\frac{2mE}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate to get density of states** $g(E) = dN/dE$: $$g_{3D}(E) = \frac{dN}{dE} = \frac{V}{3\pi^2} \cdot \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$$ $$\boxed{g_{3D}(E) = \frac{V}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}}$$ **Per unit volume** (divide by $V$): $$g_{3D}(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$$ ### 3.5 Derivation: Density of States in 2D (Quantum Well) **Step 1 — States in 2D k-space.** Allowed states in a 2D plane of area $A$ fill a disk of radius $k_\parallel$. Number of states: $$N(k) = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi k_\parallel^2}{(2\pi/L)^2} = \frac{A k_\parallel^2}{2\pi}$$ **Step 2 — Energy-momentum relation** (with sub-band offset $E_c$): $$E - E_c = \frac{\hbar^2 k_\parallel^2}{2m}$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate:** $$\frac{dN}{dE} = \frac{A}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{d(k_\parallel^2)}{dE} = \frac{A}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2m}{\hbar^2}$$ $$\boxed{g_{2D}(E) = \frac{mA}{\pi\hbar^2}}$$ This is **constant** above the sub-band edge — a step-like function (each new sub-band adds another step). ### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** An electron is confined in a 1D infinite potential well of width $L = 5$ nm. Calculate (a) the ground-state energy, (b) the first excited-state energy, (c) the wavelength of a photon emitted when the electron transitions from $n = 2$ to $n = 1$. **Solution:** **(a) Ground state ($n = 1$):** $$E_1 = \frac{h^2}{8mL^2} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}{8 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times (5 \times 10^{-9})^2}$$ Numerator: $(6.626)^2 \times 10^{-68} = 43.90 \times 10^{-68}$ Denominator: $8 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 25 \times 10^{-18} = 1821.8 \times 10^{-49} = 1.8218 \times 10^{-46}$ $$E_1 = \frac{43.90 \times 10^{-68}}{1.8218 \times 10^{-46}} = 24.10 \times 10^{-22} \text{ J} \approx 0.150 \text{ eV}$$ **(b) First excited state ($n = 2$):** $$E_2 = 4 E_1 = 4 \times 0.150 = 0.601 \text{ eV}$$ **(c) Transition energy and photon wavelength:** $$\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = 0.601 - 0.150 = 0.451 \text{ eV} = 0.451 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 7.22 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J}$$ $$\lambda = \frac{hc}{\Delta E} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{7.22 \times 10^{-20}} = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{7.22 \times 10^{-20}} = 2.75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda \approx 2.75\ \mu\text{m} \text{ (infrared)}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** This wavelength lies in the **infrared** region, demonstrating that **quantum confinement in nanostructures shifts emitted photon energy** — the foundation of tunable quantum-dot lasers and LEDs. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Evolution of Atomic Models ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Dalton 1803
Indivisible Sphere] --> A1[Thomson 1904
Plum Pudding Model] A1 --> A2[Rutherford 1911
Nuclear Model with Orbits] A2 --> A3[Bohr 1913
Quantized Circular Orbits] A3 --> A4[de Broglie 1924
Wave Particle Duality] A4 --> A5[Heisenberg 1927
Uncertainty Principle] A5 --> A6[Schrodinger 1926
Wavefunction and Quantum States] A6 --> A7[Dirac 1928
Relativistic Quantum Mechanics] style A0 fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#1f6feb style A1 fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#1f6feb style A2 fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#1f6feb style A3 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#d97706 style A4 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#d97706 style A5 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#d97706 style A6 fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#2da44e style A7 fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#2da44e ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Particle-in-a-Box Setup and Solutions ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Setup[Setup: Infinite 1D Well] V1[V = infinity
x = 0]:::wall V2[V = 0
0 less than x less than L]:::well V3[V = infinity
x = L]:::wall end Setup --> BC[Apply Boundary Conditions
psi at 0 equals 0
psi at L equals 0] BC --> ODE[Second Order ODE
d2psi over dx2 equals k squared psi] ODE --> Sol[Solution Family
psi_n equals A sin of n pi x over L] Sol --> Energy[Quantized Energy Levels
E_n equals n squared h squared over 8 m L squared] Energy --> Quant[Quantum Confinement
Smaller L means Larger E_n
Larger Spacing] classDef wall fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#d73a49 classDef well fill:#ddf4ff,stroke:#0969da classDef quant fill:#dafbe1,stroke:#2da44e ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Density of States Hierarchy (3D → 0D) ```mermaid flowchart TD D3[Dimensionality 3
Bulk Semiconductor
g of E proportional to sqrt E
Parabolic DOS]:::d3 D2[Dimensionality 2
Quantum Well
Constant Step like DOS
Each subband adds a step]:::d2 D1[Dimensionality 1
Quantum Wire
DOS proportional to 1 over sqrt E
Van Hove singularities]:::d1 D0[Dimensionality 0
Quantum Dot
Discrete Delta functions
Artificial Atom]:::d0 D3 --> D2 D2 --> D1 D1 --> D0 D0 --> APP[Applications
QLED Displays
Single Electron Transistor
Quantum Computing Qubits
Biosensors]:::app classDef d3 fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#0969da classDef d2 fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#0969da classDef d1 fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#0969da classDef d0 fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#0969da classDef app fill:#fff0c1,stroke:#9a6700 ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Schrödinger Equation Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT[Input Physical System
Potential V of x
Boundary Conditions]:::in SCHR[Schrodinger Equation
H psi equals E psi]:::core SOLVE[Mathematical Solver
ODE with BCs]:::calc OUT1[Wavefunction psi of x
Shape and Nodes]:::out OUT2[Energy Eigenvalues E_n
Discrete Allowed Levels]:::out OUT3[Probability Density
mod psi squared]:::out APP1[Device Design
Quantum Well Laser]:::app APP2[Material Characterization
Band Gap Engineering]:::app APP3[Nanostructure Modeling
TCAD Simulation]:::app INPUT --> SCHR SCHR --> SOLVE SOLVE --> OUT1 SOLVE --> OUT2 SOLVE --> OUT3 OUT1 --> APP1 OUT2 --> APP2 OUT3 --> APP3 classDef in fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#d73a49 classDef core fill:#fff0c1,stroke:#9a6700 classDef calc fill:#ddf4ff,stroke:#0969da classDef out fill:#dafbe1,stroke:#2da44e classDef app fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#6e6e6e ``` ### 4.5 Energy Level Visualization (Conceptual Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Bulk[3D Bulk] B1[Continuous Energy Band]:::bulk end subgraph QWell[2D Quantum Well] QW1[Sub band 1]:::sub QW2[Sub band 2]:::sub QW3[Sub band 3]:::sub end subgraph QDot[0D Quantum Dot] QD1[Discrete Level 1]:::dot QD2[Discrete Level 2]:::dot QD3[Discrete Level 3]:::dot end Bulk --> QWell QWell --> QDot classDef bulk fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#0969da classDef sub fill:#fff0c1,stroke:#9a6700 classDef dot fill:#dafbe1,stroke:#2da44e ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State and explain Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle. Mention its significance in nanoelectronics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle states that it is fundamentally impossible to simultaneously determine both the exact position and exact momentum of a microscopic particle with arbitrary precision. $$\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}$$ where $\Delta x$ is the uncertainty in position, $\Delta p$ is the uncertainty in momentum, and $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck's constant. **Significance in nanoelectronics (1 mark each):** - As device dimensions shrink to a few nanometers, confining an electron to a smaller $\Delta x$ necessarily increases $\Delta p$, leading to **higher kinetic energy** (confinement energy). - This is the origin of **quantum confinement** effects in MOSFETs below 10 nm. - It explains why classical trajectories break down at the nanoscale and why quantum mechanical modeling is essential. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define de Broglie wavelength. Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through 150 V.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** de Broglie postulated that every moving particle has an associated wavelength: $$\lambda = \frac{h}{mv} = \frac{h}{p}$$ **Calculation (2 marks):** Using the engineering shortcut: $$\lambda \text{ (nm)} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}$$ $$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{150}} = \frac{1.227}{12.247} \approx 0.1002 \text{ nm}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda \approx 0.10 \text{ nm}}$$ This value is comparable to atomic spacing, confirming the wave nature of electrons in nanostructures. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the energy eigenvalues and normalized wavefunctions for a particle confined in a one-dimensional infinite potential well of width $L$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An electron is confined in a 1D infinite potential well of width 2 nm. Calculate: (i) The ground state energy $E_1$ in eV. (ii) The energy of the first excited state $E_2$ in eV. (iii) The wavelength of the photon emitted when the electron decays from $n = 2$ to $n = 1$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** The time-independent Schrödinger equation inside the well ($0 < x < L$, $V = 0$): $$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = E\psi \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + k^2\psi = 0, \quad \text{where } k^2 = \frac{2mE}{\hbar^2} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ General solution: $$\psi(x) = A\sin(kx) + B\cos(kx) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ Apply BC $\psi(0) = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$; thus $\psi(x) = A\sin(kx)$. Apply BC $\psi(L) = 0 \Rightarrow A\sin(kL) = 0$, giving $kL = n\pi$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ **(1 mark)** Therefore: $$k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L}, \quad E_n = \frac{\hbar^2 k_n^2}{2m} = \frac{n^2\pi^2\hbar^2}{2mL^2} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8mL^2} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ Normalization $\int_0^L \vert\psi\vert^2 dx = 1$ gives $A = \sqrt{2/L}$. **(1 mark)** Final answer: $$\boxed{\psi_n(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}\sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right), \quad E_n = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8mL^2}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** Given: $L = 2$ nm $= 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $m = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s. **(i) Ground state energy $E_1$:** **(2.5 marks)** $$E_1 = \frac{h^2}{8mL^2} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}{8 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times (2 \times 10^{-9})^2}$$ Numerator: $43.90 \times 10^{-68}$ Denominator: $8 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 4 \times 10^{-18} = 291.5 \times 10^{-49}$ $$E_1 = \frac{43.90 \times 10^{-68}}{2.915 \times 10^{-46}} = 1.506 \times 10^{-22} \text{ J} \times \frac{1 \text{ eV}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}$$ $$\boxed{E_1 \approx 0.940 \text{ eV}}$$ **(ii) First excited state $E_2$:** **(2 marks)** $$E_2 = 4 E_1 = 4 \times 0.940 = 3.76 \text{ eV}$$ **(iii) Photon wavelength for $n = 2 \to n = 1$:** **(2.5 marks)** $$\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = 3.76 - 0.94 = 2.82 \text{ eV} = 2.82 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 4.518 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$ $$\lambda = \frac{hc}{\Delta E} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4.518 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{4.518 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda \approx 4.40 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 440 \text{ nm (blue light)}}$$ --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** What is meant by density of states? Derive expressions for the density of states in 3D, 2D, 1D, and 0D systems and discuss their significance in nanostructures. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, explain why classical physics fails at the nanoscale. An electron is confined to a region of size 1 nm. Estimate the minimum uncertainty in its velocity. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Density of States Derivation (7 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The density of states $g(E)$ is defined as the number of allowed electron quantum states per unit energy per unit volume (or area/length for lower-dimensional systems). **3D Derivation (1.5 marks):** Number of states in k-space sphere of radius $k$: $$N(k) = 2 \cdot \frac{4\pi k^3/3}{(2\pi/L)^3} = \frac{V k^3}{3\pi^2}$$ With $E = \hbar^2 k^2 / (2m)$, $k = \sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$. Substituting and differentiating: $$g_{3D}(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$$ **2D Derivation (1.5 marks):** $$g_{2D}(E) = \frac{m}{\pi\hbar^2} \quad \text{(step function, constant per sub-band)}$$ **1D Derivation (1.5 marks):** $$g_{1D}(E) = \frac{1}{\pi\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{2m}{E}} \quad \text{(with van Hove singularities)}$$ **0D (1 mark):** $$g_{0D}(E) = 2\delta(E - E_n) \quad \text{(discrete delta peaks like an artificial atom)}$$ **Significance (1 mark):** - Bulk 3D systems have a continuous parabolic DOS. - 2D quantum wells have step-like DOS — basis of HEMTs and quantum well lasers. - 1D nanowires exhibit van Hove peaks — useful in photodetectors. - 0D quantum dots have discrete DOS — basis of QLEDs, single-electron devices, qubits. **(b) Heisenberg and Nanoscale (7 marks):** **Conceptual (3 marks):** Classical physics assumes a particle has simultaneously well-defined position and momentum, allowing predictable trajectories. At the nanoscale, $\Delta x$ becomes comparable to the de Broglie wavelength, so the uncertainty $\Delta p \geq \hbar/(2\Delta x)$ is no longer negligible. This produces: - **Wave-like behavior** (interference, diffraction of electrons). - **Quantized energy levels** in confined regions. - **Tunneling** through classically forbidden barriers. - Breakdown of the classical **drift-diffusion** model used in conventional transistors. **Numerical (4 marks):** Given: $\Delta x = 1$ nm $= 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $m = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg. Using $\Delta x \cdot m \cdot \Delta v \geq \hbar / 2$: $$\Delta v \geq \frac{\hbar}{2 m \Delta x} = \frac{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$\Delta v \geq \frac{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}{1.8218 \times 10^{-39}}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta v \geq 5.78 \times 10^{4} \text{ m/s}}$$ This is a substantial fraction of typical electron drift velocities in semiconductors ($\sim 10^5$ m/s), confirming that quantum effects cannot be ignored at 1 nm confinement. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing $h$ with $\hbar$** — Use $\hbar$ in angular momentum/energy formulas and $h$ when the original equation has $h$ (e.g., de Broglie). Mixing them loses 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting normalization** — Wavefunctions must be normalized; examiners deduct 1–2 marks if $\int \vert\psi\vert^2 dx = 1$ is not verified. > 3. **Wrong units for $L$ in numerical problems** — Always convert nm to meters before substitution; a common mistake is using $L$ in nm directly with $h$ in J·s, producing wildly wrong answers. > 4. **Boundary conditions** — For infinite well, $\psi(0) = \psi(L) = 0$ leads to $B = 0$ and quantized $k$. Skipping this loses full marks. > 5. **2D/1D/0D DOS units** — Examiners check whether you wrote the per-unit-volume form correctly. State explicitly whether it's per J·m³, J·m², or J·m. > 6. **Numerical constants** — Memorize $h$, $\hbar$, $m_e$, $e$, $c$, and $hc = 1240$ eV·nm. The last one is extremely useful for photon energy calculations. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Quantum mechanics governs nanoelectronics** because at dimensions below ~100 nm, the de Broglie wavelength of electrons becomes comparable to device size. - **de Broglie wavelength:** $\lambda = h/mv = 1.227/\sqrt{V}$ nm (for electrons). Wave nature must be considered. - **Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle:** $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$. Smaller confinement ⇒ larger momentum spread ⇒ higher kinetic energy. - **Time-independent Schrödinger Equation:** $\hat{H}\psi = E\psi$ is the master equation of quantum systems. - **Particle-in-a-Box** is the foundational model for quantum confinement: - Wavefunction: $\psi_n = \sqrt{2/L}\sin(n\pi x/L)$ - Energy: $E_n = n^2 h^2/(8mL^2) \propto 1/L^2$ - **Density of States** changes fundamentally with dimensionality: - 3D: $g(E) \propto \sqrt{E}$ - 2D: $g(E) = $ constant (step) - 1D: $g(E) \propto 1/\sqrt{E}$ (van Hove peaks) - 0D: $g(E) = 2\delta(E - E_n)$ (discrete) - **Engineering relevance:** Underpins MOSFET scaling limits, quantum well lasers, quantum dot LEDs, single-electron transistors, resonant tunneling diodes, and quantum computers. - **Key constants to memorize:** - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s - $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s - $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg - $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C - $hc = 1240$ eV·nm (very useful) - $1\text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J - **Quick energy formula for electron in 1D well of width $L$ (nm):** $$E_n \text{ (eV)} = \frac{37.6 \, n^2}{L^2 \text{ (nm}^2)}$$

topics.title: Basics of Molecular and Nanostructure Dynamics: Schrodinger Equation and Wave Function Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Molecular and Nanostructure Dynamics: Schrodinger Equation and Wave Function Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schrödinger Equation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The Schrödinger equation is the fundamental **postulate of non-relativistic quantum mechanics** that describes how the quantum state (represented by the wave function $\Psi$) of a physical system evolves in time and space. For a single particle of mass $m$ moving in a potential field $V(\vec{r},t)$, the time-dependent Schrödinger equation (TDSE) is:
> $$i\hbar \frac{\partial \Psi(\vec{r},t)}{\partial t} = \hat{H}\,\Psi(\vec{r},t)$$
> where $\hat{H} = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2 + V(\vec{r},t)$ is the **Hamiltonian operator** and $\hbar = h/2\pi$ is the **reduced Planck's constant**.

The **wave function** $\Psi(\vec{r},t)$ is a complex-valued mathematical function that completely characterizes the quantum state of a particle. It encodes all measurable information about the system through the application of quantum mechanical operators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Born's Statistical Interpretation (Max Born, 1926):**
> The squared magnitude of the wave function, $\vert \Psi(\vec{r},t) \vert^2 = \Psi^*(\vec{r},t)\,\Psi(\vec{r},t)$, represents the **probability density** of finding the particle at position $\vec{r}$ at time $t$. This is the cornerstone of probabilistic quantum mechanics and the basis of all nanoelectronic device modeling.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Diffused Pebble"

Imagine throwing a small pebble into a calm pond. The classical view says the pebble is *somewhere*—a definite point we could track. The quantum view (Schrödinger equation) says the pebble is *smeared out* across the entire pond as a "ripple cloud." We cannot pinpoint where it is, but the wave function $\Psi$ tells us the **probability** of finding it at any spot.

- Where the ripples are **tall** (large $\vert \Psi \vert^2$) → particle is **likely** to be found.
- Where the ripples are **flat** (small $\vert \Psi \vert^2$) → particle is **unlikely** to be there.
- The Schrödinger equation is the **mathematical rule** that tells us how this ripple cloud moves, splashes, and interferes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **nanostructures** (sizes 1–100 nm), the ripple cloud becomes comparable in size to the structure itself, so quantum effects dominate. This is why MEMS engineers MUST understand the Schrödinger equation when designing **quantum wells, quantum dots, RTDs (Resonant Tunneling Diodes), and FinFETs**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants Used Throughout

| Symbol | Quantity | Value |
|--------|----------|-------|
| $h$ | Planck's constant | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** |
| $\hbar$ | Reduced Planck's constant | **$1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** |
| $m_e$ | Free electron mass | **$9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg** |
| $e$ | Electron charge | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The free electron mass $m_e$ and reduced Planck's constant $\hbar$ are the two most frequently used constants in all nanoelectronic Schrödinger-based derivations. Memorize their orders of magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Forms of the Schrödinger Equation

The Schrödinger equation exists in two complementary forms, both heavily tested in KTU examinations:

### (A) Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE)

$$i\hbar \frac{\partial \Psi(\vec{r},t)}{\partial t} = \left[-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2 + V(\vec{r},t)\right]\Psi(\vec{r},t)$$

- **Why:** Describes **dynamic, time-evolving** quantum states (e.g., electron wave packet traversing a CNT channel).
- **How:** The Hamiltonian $\hat{H}$ acts on the wave function, producing the rate of change of $\Psi$ in time, scaled by $i\hbar$.

### (B) Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation (TISE)

$$\hat{H}\,\psi(\vec{r}) = E\,\psi(\vec{r}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2\psi + V(\vec{r})\psi = E\,\psi$$

- **Why:** When the potential $V$ does **not** depend on time, the wave function separates and the energy $E$ becomes a **conserved, definite quantity**. This is the eigen-equation.
- **How:** TISE is an **eigenvalue problem**. Solutions $\psi$ are eigenfunctions and energies $E$ are eigenvalues (the only allowed energy levels!).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Trick Question:** "Why is energy quantized?"
> **Answer:** The time-independent Schrödinger equation is a Sturm–Liouville eigenvalue problem with specific **boundary conditions** (continuity of $\psi$ and $\psi'$) imposed by the physical walls of the nanostructure. Only specific discrete energies $E_n$ produce wave functions that satisfy ALL boundary conditions.

## 2.2 The Hamiltonian Operator — "The Energy Bookkeeper"

The Hamiltonian operator is the **total energy operator** of the system:

$$\hat{H} = \underbrace{-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2}_{\hat{T}\;\text{(Kinetic)}} + \underbrace{V(\vec{r})}_{\text{Potential}}$$

- The Laplacian $\nabla^2 = \partial^2/\partial x^2 + \partial^2/\partial y^2 + \partial^2/\partial z^2$ encodes curvature in space.
- The more "wiggly" $\psi$ is (high curvature), the higher the kinetic energy.

## 2.3 Core Quantum Mechanical Postulates (KTU 2024)

| # | Postulate | Mathematical Form |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **State Postulate** | System is fully described by $\Psi(\vec{r},t)$ |
| 2 | **Born Interpretation** | $P(\vec{r},t)\,d\tau = \vert\Psi\vert^2 d\tau$ |
| 3 | **Normalization** | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\vert\Psi\vert^2\,d\tau = 1$ |
| 4 | **Expectation Value** | $\langle \hat{A}\rangle = \int \Psi^*\,\hat{A}\,\Psi\,d\tau$ |
| 5 | **Eigenvalue Equation** | $\hat{A}\psi_a = a\psi_a$ |
| 6 | **Uncertainty** | $\Delta A\,\Delta B \geq \tfrac{1}{2}\vert\langle[\hat{A},\hat{B}]\rangle\vert$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Following Table is the MOST Important Resource for KTU University Exam Success on this Topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Use / Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | TDSE (1D) | $i\hbar\,\partial\Psi/\partial t = -(\hbar^2/2m)\,\partial^2\Psi/\partial x^2 + V\Psi$ | General time evolution |
| 2 | TISE (1D) | $-(\hbar^2/2m)\,\psi'' + V\psi = E\psi$ | Stationary states |
| 3 | Probability density | $\rho = \vert\Psi\vert^2 = \Psi^*\Psi$ | Born's rule |
| 4 | Normalization | $\int \Psi^*\Psi\,d\tau = 1$ | All bound states |
| 5 | Energy of particle in 1D box | $E_n = \dfrac{n^2\pi^2\hbar^2}{2mL^2}$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ |
| 6 | Wave function in 1D box | $\psi_n(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{L}}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$ | $0 \leq x \leq L$ |
| 7 | Expectation of position | $\langle x\rangle = \int_0^L \psi^* x\,\psi\,dx$ | Statistical average |
| 8 | Expectation of energy | $\langle E\rangle = \int \psi^*\hat{H}\psi\,d\tau$ | Equals $E$ for eigenstates |
| 9 | Heisenberg Uncertainty | $\Delta x\,\Delta p \geq \hbar/2$ | All quantum systems |
| 10 | de Broglie wavelength | $\lambda = h/p = h/\sqrt{2mE}$ | Particle-wave duality |
| 11 | Probability current | $J = (\hbar/m)\,\text{Im}(\Psi^*\nabla\Psi)$ | Tunneling, transport |
| 12 | Continuity equation | $\partial\rho/\partial t + \nabla\cdot\vec{J} = 0$ | Charge conservation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** When asked for "energy of electron in 1D box", students often forget to substitute the **effective mass** $m^*$ for the free electron mass $m_e$ in semiconductor nanostructures. For GaAs, $m^* = 0.067\,m_e$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FinFETs and MOSFETs (sub-10 nm):** Channel electrons behave as **2D electron gas (2DEG)** in quantum wells. TISE with triangular well gives sub-band energies.
- **Resonant Tunneling Diodes (RTD):** Schrödinger equation in a double-barrier potential gives **transmission resonances** — the basis of THz oscillators.
- **Quantum Dots (QD):** Particle-in-a-spherical-box solution yields discrete energy levels used in **QD LEDs and single-photon sources**.
- **Carbon Nanotubes:** Band structure derived from Schrödinger equation applied to rolled graphene lattice gives **metallic vs. semiconducting** CNTs.
- **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM):** Direct application of **wave-function overlap and tunneling** between tip and sample.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation from TDSE (Separation of Variables)

This is a **standard 14-mark derivation** in KTU 2024. We derive the TISE by assuming a separable form of the wave function when the potential is time-independent.

### Step 1 — State the TDSE in 1D

$$i\hbar\,\frac{\partial \Psi(x,t)}{\partial t} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\,\frac{\partial^2 \Psi(x,t)}{\partial x^2} + V(x)\,\Psi(x,t)$$

### Step 2 — Assume Separable Solution

For a **time-independent potential** $V(x,t) = V(x)$, we postulate:

$$\Psi(x,t) = \psi(x)\cdot\phi(t)$$

Substitute into TDSE:

$$i\hbar\,\psi(x)\,\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\,\phi(t)\,\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + V(x)\,\psi(x)\,\phi(t)$$

### Step 3 — Divide Both Sides by $\psi(x)\phi(t)$

$$i\hbar\,\frac{1}{\phi}\,\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\,\frac{1}{\psi}\,\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + V(x)$$

### Step 4 — Recognize Two Independent Functions of Different Variables

The left side is a **pure function of $t$** only; the right side is a **pure function of $x$** only. For them to be equal for ALL $x$ and $t$, both must equal a separation constant $E$ (with units of energy).

**Time Part:**

$$i\hbar\,\frac{d\phi}{dt} = E\,\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\phi}{\phi} = -\frac{iE}{\hbar}\,dt$$

Integrating:

$$\phi(t) = e^{-iEt/\hbar}$$

**Space Part:**

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\,\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + V(x)\,\psi = E\,\psi$$

### Step 5 — Combine the Separated Solutions

$$\boxed{\Psi(x,t) = \psi(x)\,e^{-iEt/\hbar}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight (KTU loves this!):** The time factor $e^{-iEt/\hbar}$ has unit magnitude, so $\vert\Psi\vert^2 = \vert\psi\vert^2$ — the probability density is **time-independent**. Such states are called **stationary states**, and they are the eigenstates of the Hamiltonian.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Particle in a 1D Infinite Potential Well (Quantum Box)

This is the **most-tested problem** in KTU nanoelectronics. A particle of mass $m$ is confined to a region $0 \leq x \leq L$ with $V = \infty$ outside.

### Step 1 — Write the Potential Function

$$V(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \leq x \leq L \\ \infty, & x < 0 \text{ or } x > L \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Apply TISE Inside the Well ($V=0$)

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\,\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = E\,\psi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = -k^2\psi$$

where $k^2 = \dfrac{2mE}{\hbar^2}$.

### Step 3 — General Solution of the Differential Equation

$$\psi(x) = A\sin(kx) + B\cos(kx)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions

Since the potential is infinite outside, the wave function must vanish at the walls:

$$\psi(0) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 0$$

$$\psi(L) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A\sin(kL) = 0$$

For a non-trivial solution ($A \neq 0$):

$$\sin(kL) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

> [!NOTE]
> The integer $n$ is the **quantum number**. Only certain values of $k$ (and hence $E$) are allowed — this is **energy quantization**.

### Step 5 — Quantized Wave Vector and Energy

$$k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L}$$

$$E_n = \frac{\hbar^2 k_n^2}{2m} = \frac{n^2\pi^2\hbar^2}{2mL^2}$$

Using $\hbar = h/2\pi$, this is equivalently:

$$E_n = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8mL^2}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Normalization to Find $A$

$$\int_0^L \vert\psi_n\vert^2\,dx = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A^2\int_0^L \sin^2\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)dx = 1$$

Since $\int_0^L \sin^2(n\pi x/L)\,dx = L/2$:

$$A^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}$$

### Step 7 — Final Normalized Solutions

$$\boxed{\psi_n(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right), \qquad E_n = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8mL^2}, \qquad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Valued Conclusion (write this verbatim for full marks):**
> The Schrödinger equation, together with the physical boundary conditions, **forces the energy to take discrete values**. The ground state ($n=1$) has **non-zero** energy $E_1 = h^2/8mL^2$ — this is the **zero-point energy**, a purely quantum effect with no classical analogue. As $L \to \infty$, $E_n \to 0$ and the spectrum becomes continuous (classical limit).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Expectation Value of Position for a Particle in a Box

### Step 1 — Definition of Expectation Value

$$\langle x\rangle = \int_0^L \psi_n^*(x)\,x\,\psi_n(x)\,dx$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Normalized Wave Function

$$\langle x\rangle = \frac{2}{L}\int_0^L x\,\sin^2\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)dx$$

### Step 3 — Use the Identity $\sin^2\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$

$$\langle x\rangle = \frac{2}{L}\int_0^L x \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(2n\pi x/L)}{2}\,dx$$

$$= \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L x\,dx - \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L x\cos\!\left(\frac{2n\pi x}{L}\right)dx$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Each Integral

**First integral:**

$$\int_0^L x\,dx = \frac{L^2}{2}$$

**Second integral (using integration by parts):**

Let $u = x$, $dv = \cos(2n\pi x/L)\,dx$, so $du = dx$, $v = \frac{L}{2n\pi}\sin(2n\pi x/L)$.

$$\int_0^L x\cos\!\left(\frac{2n\pi x}{L}\right)dx = \left[\frac{xL}{2n\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{2n\pi x}{L}\right)\right]_0^L - \int_0^L \frac{L}{2n\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{2n\pi x}{L}\right)dx$$

At $x = L$: $\sin(2n\pi) = 0$; at $x = 0$: $\sin(0) = 0$. So the boundary term vanishes. The remaining integral is:

$$-\frac{L}{2n\pi}\left[-\frac{L}{2n\pi}\cos\!\left(\frac{2n\pi x}{L}\right)\right]_0^L = \frac{L^2}{4n^2\pi^2}\left[\cos(2n\pi) - \cos(0)\right] = \frac{L^2}{4n^2\pi^2}(1-1) = 0$$

### Step 5 — Combine Results

$$\boxed{\langle x\rangle = \frac{1}{L}\cdot\frac{L^2}{2} - 0 = \frac{L}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The average position of the particle is exactly the **center of the box**, regardless of quantum number $n$. This is a consequence of the symmetric probability density $\vert\psi_n\vert^2$ about $x = L/2$.

---
