# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🅰️ PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define nanostructures. Classify them based on the number of dimensions confined to the nanoscale.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** A nanostructure is a material or engineered object in which at least one physical dimension lies in the range **1 nm to 100 nm** ($10^{-9}$ m to $10^{-7}$ m), where size-dependent quantum and surface effects dominate the physical properties.

**Classification (2 Marks):**

| Class | Confinement | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **0D** — Quantum Dot | All 3 axes | CdSe nanoparticles, C₆₀ |
| **1D** — Quantum Wire | 2 axes | Carbon nanotubes, Si nanowires |
| **2D** — Quantum Well | 1 axis | Graphene, MoS₂, GaAs/AlGaAs QW |
| **3D** — Bulk Nanomaterial | 0 axes (but nanocrystalline) | Nanocrystalline metals |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is Molecular Dynamics? State the equation that is repeatedly solved in any MD simulation.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Molecular Dynamics (MD) is a deterministic, atomistic simulation method that numerically solves Newton's equations of motion for a system of interacting atoms to generate a time-resolved trajectory of particle positions and velocities.

**Governing Equation (1.5 Marks):**

$$m_i \frac{d^2 \mathbf{r}_i}{dt^2} = \mathbf{F}_i = -\nabla_i U(\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{r}_N)$$

This second-order ODE is integrated using a finite-difference scheme (typically **Velocity-Verlet**) with a time step $\Delta t \approx 1\,fs$.

---

## 5.2 🅱️ PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### **Question 3A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **equilibrium interatomic separation** from the Lennard-Jones potential. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With suitable derivations, explain the **Velocity-Verlet algorithm** used in MD simulations. Show why it is preferred over the basic Verlet algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q3A(a) (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Lennard-Jones Potential Expression (1 Mark):**

$$U(r) = 4\epsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $r$ (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{dU}{dr} = 4\epsilon \left[ -12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + 6 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Set $dU/dr = 0$ for equilibrium (1 Mark):**

$$12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} = 6 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}}$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification (1 Mark):**

$$2 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{6}} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad r^{6} = 2 \sigma^{6}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{r_{min} = 2^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.122 \sigma}$$

**Step 6 — Discussion (1 Mark):**
- $r_{min}$ is the natural bond length of the pair.
- At $r = \sigma$, $U = 0$ (not equilibrium, just zero-crossing).
- For $r < r_{min}$, the repulsive $r^{-12}$ term dominates — strong Pauli repulsion.
- For $r > r_{min}$, the attractive $r^{-6}$ term dominates — van der Waals attraction.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q3A(b) (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Taylor Expansion of Position (2 Marks):**

$$\mathbf{r}(t + \Delta t) = \mathbf{r}(t) + \mathbf{v}(t)\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{a}(t)\Delta t^2 + \tfrac{1}{6}\mathbf{b}(t)\Delta t^3 + O(\Delta t^4)$$

$$\mathbf{r}(t - \Delta t) = \mathbf{r}(t) - \mathbf{v}(t)\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{a}(t)\Delta t^2 - \tfrac{1}{6}\mathbf{b}(t)\Delta t^3 + O(\Delta t^4)$$

**Step 2 — Add and Solve for $\mathbf{r}(t + \Delta t)$ (1 Mark):**

$$\mathbf{r}(t + \Delta t) = 2\mathbf{r}(t) - \mathbf{r}(t - \Delta t) + \mathbf{a}(t)\Delta t^2 + O(\Delta t^4)$$

This is the **basic Verlet** scheme.

**Step 3 — Velocity-Verlet Form (2 Marks):**

$$\mathbf{r}(t + \Delta t) = \mathbf{r}(t) + \mathbf{v}(t)\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{a}(t)\Delta t^2$$

Compute new force → new acceleration $\mathbf{a}(t + \Delta t) = \mathbf{F}(t + \Delta t) / m$.

$$\mathbf{v}(t + \Delta t) = \mathbf{v}(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\big[\mathbf{a}(t) + \mathbf{a}(t + \Delta t)\big]\Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Advantages of Velocity-Verlet over Basic Verlet (2 Marks):**

| Property | Basic Verlet | Velocity-Verlet |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity at integer times | Requires extra $O(\Delta t^0)$ calc | Direct $O(\Delta t^2)$ result |
| Storage | Needs $\mathbf{r}(t)$ and $\mathbf{r}(t - \Delta t)$ | Needs $\mathbf{r}(t)$ and $\mathbf{v}(t)$ only |
| Numerical stability | Same symplectic property | Same symplectic property |
| Compatibility with thermostats | Poor (no direct $v$) | Excellent (Nosé-Hoover) |

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - Students often **confuse the equilibrium separation** with $r = \sigma$. The correct answer is $r_{min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$ — losing **1 mark** otherwise.
> - Students frequently **skip the time-reversal/symplectic argument** when justifying Velocity-Verlet — losing **2 marks** if the reasoning is not present.
> - The Velocity-Verlet position update is sometimes written *with* a $\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{a}(t+\Delta t)\Delta t^2$ correction term — this is **incorrect**; only the basic Verlet uses the centered form.

---

### **Question 3B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **— ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain the **Lennard-Jones potential** in detail. Show that the force between two LJ atoms vanishes at $r = 2^{1/6}\sigma$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **classification of nanostructures** with energy density of states diagrams. What is quantum confinement and how does it affect the bandgap of a semiconductor quantum dot? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q3B(a) (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Statement of the Potential (1 Mark):**

$$U_{LJ}(r) = 4\epsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Physical Origin of Each Term (2 Marks):**
- **Repulsive term ($r^{-12}$):** arises from **Pauli exclusion** — overlapping electron clouds of two atoms raise the energy steeply. The exponent 12 is a computational convenience; the true Born-Mayer repulsion is exponential. $r^{-12}$ is preferred because it is the square of the $r^{-6}$ term, making the gradient calculation cleaner.
- **Attractive term ($r^{-6}$):** arises from **London dispersion forces** — instantaneous dipole-induced dipole interactions. The $r^{-6}$ dependence is derived from second-order perturbation theory of correlated electron fluctuations.

**Step 3 — Parameters (1 Mark):**
- $\sigma$ = distance at which $U_{LJ} = 0$ (zero of potential).
- $\epsilon$ = depth of the well, equal to the binding energy at $r = r_{min}$.

**Step 4 — Derive the Force (2 Marks):**

$$F(r) = -\frac{dU}{dr} = \frac{24\epsilon}{r} \left[ 2\left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Force = 0 (1 Mark):**

Setting the bracket to zero:

$$2\left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} = \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{r = 2^{1/6}\sigma \approx 1.122\sigma}$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q3B(b) (7 Marks):

**Step 1 — Classification by Dimensionality (2 Marks):**

| Class | Confined Axes | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 0D | 3 | Quantum dot |
| 1D | 2 | Nanowire, CNT |
| 2D | 1 | Graphene, MoS₂ |
| 3D | 0 | Bulk nanocrystalline |

**Step 2 — DOS Description (2 Marks):**
- **3D:** $g(E) \propto E^{1/2}$ (parabolic)
- **2D:** $g(E) = $ constant step
- **1D:** $g(E) \propto E^{-1/2}$ (van Hove singularities)
- **0D:** $g(E) = \sum_n \delta(E - E_n)$ (discrete)

**Step 3 — Quantum Confinement Definition (1 Mark):** Quantum confinement occurs when the size of a structure in a given direction becomes comparable to or smaller than the de Broglie wavelength of charge carriers, causing their energy levels to become quantized in that direction.

**Step 4 — Bandgap of a Quantum Dot (2 Marks):**

$$E_g^{\text{nano}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2} - \frac{1.786 e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r R}$$

- First correction (confinement) $\propto R^{-2}$: dominates at small $R$, **increases** $E_g$ (blueshift).
- Second correction (Coulomb) $\propto R^{-1}$: reduces $E_g$ slightly.

**Conclusion:** Smaller dots → larger bandgap → emission wavelength shifts to blue. This is the principle behind **tunable color quantum-dot LEDs**.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - Many students **forget to mention the Coulomb correction** in the bandgap formula, which is worth **1 mark** in a 7-mark question.
> - Students often state *"bandgap decreases"* for smaller dots — this is **wrong** for the dominant $R^{-2}$ term; the net $E_g$ **increases** (blueshift).
> - For the DOS, the 0D case is frequently drawn as a *continuous* curve — it must be drawn as **discrete delta functions**.

---

## 5.3 🧾 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 Important Things to Remember — "The KTU Nanostructures & MD Pocket Card"**

- ✅ **Nanostructure definition:** at least one dimension in the range $\mathbf{1\,nm - 100\,nm}$.
- ✅ **Four classes** by confinement: 0D (dot), 1D (wire), 2D (well), 3D (bulk nano).
- ✅ **DOS scaling:** 3D → $E^{1/2}$, 2D → step, 1D → $E^{-1/2}$, 0D → $\delta$-function.
- ✅ **Lennard-Jones potential:** $U_{LJ} = 4\epsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$.
- ✅ **Equilibrium separation:** $r_{min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma \approx 1.122\sigma$ (NOT $r = \sigma$).
- ✅ **Force on LJ atom:** $F = \frac{24\epsilon}{r}\big[2(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^{6}\big]$.
- ✅ **Cutoff radius:** $r_c \approx 2.5\sigma$ to save CPU.
- ✅ **Particle-in-box energy:** $E_n = n^2\pi^2\hbar^2/(2mL^2)$.
- ✅ **Quantum dot bandgap:** $E_g^{\text{nano}} = E_g^{\text{bulk}} + \hbar^2\pi^2/(2\mu R^2) - 1.786 e^2/(4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r R)$.
- ✅ **Confinement term scales as $R^{-2}$** — dominant at small dot radii.
- ✅ **MD governing equation:** $m_i \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_i = -\nabla_i U$.
- ✅ **Velocity-Verlet** is **symplectic** and **time-reversible** — preferred for long simulations.
- ✅ **MD time step:** $\Delta t \approx 1\,fs$ (smaller than the fastest vibrational period).
- ✅ **Newton's third law** is exploited in MD to halve the force computation cost.
- ✅ **Periodic boundary conditions** eliminate surface artifacts in bulk MD.
- ✅ **Ensemble terminology:** NVE (microcanonical), NVT (canonical, e.g., Nosé-Hoover), NPT (isothermal-isobaric).
- ✅ **Thermostats:** Nosé-Hoover, Berendsen, Langevin.
- ✅ **Barostats:** Parrinello-Rahman, Berendsen.
- ✅ **Cohesive energy of FCC Ar (LJ):** $\mathbf{-8.61\,\epsilon}$ per atom.
- ✅ **Lindemann criterion for melting:** $\delta_L = \sqrt{\langle u^2 \rangle}/a > 0.1$ indicates the solid is molten.
- ✅ **Reduced LJ units:** $r^* = r/\sigma$, $T^* = k_BT/\epsilon$, $\rho^* = \rho\sigma^3$ — used in universal phase diagrams.
- ✅ **Three types of MD integrators to know:** Euler (unstable), Verlet (basic), Velocity-Verlet (production standard).
- ✅ **Energy conservation in NVE** is the canonical correctness test for an MD code.

---

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Last-Minute KTU Exam Mantra:**
> *"Lennard-Jones min at $2^{1/6}\sigma$, Velocity-Verlet is symplectic, smaller dot bigger bandgap, MD time step is one femtosecond."* — recite three times before entering the exam hall. ✍️

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molecular Wires and Molecular Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET864 - MEMS AND NANOELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Molecular Wires and Molecular Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Molecular Wires — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **molecular wire** is a single molecule or an assembly of conjugated organic/inorganic molecules that serves as a one-dimensional conductive pathway, transporting charge carriers (electrons or holes) between two electrodes, components, or active regions of a molecular electronic device with a well-defined and reproducible conductance.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, molecular wires belong to the broader umbrella of **molecular electronics** — a sub-discipline of nanoelectronics where individual molecules act as the active electronic elements (resistors, diodes, switches, transistors) rather than lithographically patterned semiconductor blocks.

The two governing figures-of-merit for any molecular wire are:
- **Electrical conductance (G)** — measured in Siemens (S), typically in the range of **10⁻⁸ S to 10⁻⁴ S** for single molecules.
- **Tunneling attenuation factor (β)** — measured in **Å⁻¹** or **nm⁻¹**, quantifying how rapidly conductance decays with molecular length.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Nano-Cable" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a microscopic electrical cable — not made of copper, but a single organic molecule. Just as a copper wire carries electrons from your switch to your bulb, a molecular wire carries electrons from one nano-electrode to another. However, instead of free-electron conduction in a metal, the electrons **tunnel or hop** through a conjugated π-electron "ladder" of the molecule.

A useful everyday picture is the **rope bridge**:
- The **two ends** are the metallic electrodes (source and drain).
- The **rope bridge planks** are the conjugated double/single bonds (π-orbitals).
- An electron crossing the bridge is like a person walking plank-by-plank. If the bridge is longer, fewer planks mean lower traffic — that is **exponential decay of conductance**.

## 1.3 Molecular Circuits — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **molecular circuit** is a functional network of two or more molecular electronic components (wires, rectifiers, switches, transistors, logic gates) integrated on a single molecular platform or across a self-assembled monolayer, performing Boolean or analog signal-processing operations at the molecular scale.

The canonical sub-elements of a molecular circuit are:
1. **Molecular wire** – passive conductor
2. **Molecular rectifier (diode)** – asymmetric I–V element (Aviram–Ratner model)
3. **Molecular switch** – bistable element toggled by voltage, light, or chemical input
4. **Molecular field-effect transistor (FET)** – gate-controlled three-terminal device
5. **Molecular logic gate** – AND, OR, NOT implemented via supra-molecular chemistry

## 1.4 Why Molecular Electronics?

> [!NOTE]
> **Motivation for KTU Examiners:**
> - **Miniaturization limit of CMOS:** Top-down silicon lithography is approaching ~2 nm physical gate lengths. Molecular-scale devices provide a **bottom-up alternative** governed by self-assembly.
> - **Footprint:** A single molecule (≈ 1–3 nm) replaces thousands of transistors.
> - **Functionality-by-design:** Quantum-chemical synthesis allows tuning of HOMO–LUMO gap, conductance, and rectification ratio *a priori*.
> - **Cost and throughput:** Self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) yield billions of devices per cm² simultaneously.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Range | Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Elementary charge | $e$ | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |
| Planck constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴** | J·s |
| Reduced Planck constant | $\hbar$ | **1.055 × 10⁻³⁴** | J·s |
| Quantum of conductance | $G_0 = 2e^2/h$ | **7.748 × 10⁻⁵** | S (≈ 12.9 kΩ⁻¹) |
| β for oligophenylene wires | $\beta$ | **0.1 – 0.5** | Å⁻¹ |
| β for alkane (saturated) wires | $\beta$ | **0.8 – 1.0** | Å⁻¹ |
| β for oligothiophene wires | $\beta$ | **0.1 – 0.2** | Å⁻¹ |
| Typical SAM thickness | $L$ | **1 – 3** | nm |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Charge-Transport Mechanisms in Molecular Wires

A molecular wire does not behave like a classical metallic wire. The transport regime is determined by the relative magnitudes of:
- The electronic coupling $\Gamma$ between the molecule and the electrodes,
- The thermal energy $k_B T$,
- The applied bias $V$.

The three canonical regimes are:

**(a) Coherent Tunneling (Off-Resonant)**
- Applies for short, strongly-coupled molecules ($L \lesssim 3$ nm).
- The electron wavefunction decays exponentially through the HOMO–LUMO gap.
- Governs the **Landauer–Büttiker** formalism and yields the **β-decay law**.

**(b) Incoherent Hopping (Charge Localization)**
- Applies for long molecules ($L \gtrsim 3$ nm) or weakly coupled electrodes.
- Electron "jumps" between localized redox sites — thermally activated.
- Conductance scales as $G \propto 1/L$ (linear decay) rather than exponential.

**(c) Coulomb Blockade / Resonant Tunneling**
- Applies when the molecular energy levels are quantized (single-electron transistor regime).
- Requires very low temperature and ultra-small junctions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The *coherent tunneling* regime is the most heavily tested, because it produces the cleanest analytical relationship between conductance and molecular length.

## 2.2 Energy-Level Alignment in a Molecular Junction

In equilibrium, when a molecule is chemically bonded to two metal electrodes, three energy scales must align:

1. **Electrode Fermi level** ($E_F$) — typically **−4.5 eV to −5.0 eV** for Au, Ag.
2. **HOMO of the molecule** — highest occupied molecular orbital.
3. **LUMO of the molecule** — lowest unoccupied molecular orbital.

The energy difference between $E_F$ and the nearest molecular orbital defines the **injection barrier** $\phi$:

- If $E_F - E_{HOMO} < E_{LUMO} - E_F$, the junction is **HOMO-dominated** (hole transport, p-type).
- If $E_{LUMO} - E_F < E_F - E_{HOMO}$, the junction is **LUMO-dominated** (electron transport, n-type).

This alignment is crucial for molecular rectifiers and switches.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **cheat-sheet** for solving any KTU 2024 numerical or analytical problem on this topic. **Memorize these equations verbatim.**

| # | Formula | Meaning of Each Term | Boundary / Limit |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $G = G_0 \, e^{-\beta L}$ | Length-dependent conductance of a tunneling wire | Coherent tunneling regime |
| 2 | $G_0 = \dfrac{2e^2}{h}$ | Quantum of conductance ≈ 12.9 kΩ⁻¹ | Single-channel, spin-degenerate |
| 3 | $R = R_c + \dfrac{1}{G_0} e^{\beta L}$ | Total resistance = contact + molecular | $L$ in Å, $R$ in Ω |
| 4 | $T(E) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{2\kappa (a-a_0)}}$ | Transmission probability across a barrier | $a_0$ = barrier width |
| 5 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m\phi}}{\hbar}$ | Decay constant inside the barrier | $\phi$ = barrier height in J |
| 6 | $I = \dfrac{2e}{h} \int T(E) [f_L - f_R] \, dE$ | Landauer current through a single channel | $f_{L,R}$ = Fermi functions |
| 7 | $R_{rect} = \dfrac{\vert I_{+V} \vert}{\vert I_{-V} \vert}$ | Rectification ratio (molecular diode) | $R_{rect} > 1$ for rectification |
| 8 | $E_{HOMO-LUMO} = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda_{abs}}$ | Optical HOMO–LUMO gap from absorption | $\lambda_{abs}$ in meters |
| 9 | $G_{hop} \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$ | Hopping conductance for long wires | $L \gtrsim 3$ nm |
| 10 | $\Delta E = E_{HOMO} - E_{F}$ | Hole injection barrier | Typical: 0.5 – 1.5 eV |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse $\beta$ (decay constant, units of length⁻¹) with the dimensionless transmission coefficient. Always carry units and remember that **$\beta$ is a *property* of the molecular backbone**, not the electrode.

## 2.4 Classification of Molecular Wires

KTU expects students to recognize and tabulate the major families of molecular wires:

| Family | Representative Molecule | Typical β (Å⁻¹) | Conduction Mechanism |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Oligo-phenylene** | $p$-quaterphenyl | 0.3 – 0.5 | Tunneling through π-system |
| **Oligo-thiophene** | $\alpha$-sexithiophene (6T) | 0.1 – 0.2 | Highly delocalized π-system |
| **Oligo-phenylene-ethynylene (OPE)** | OPE-3, OPE-4 | 0.2 – 0.4 | Rigid rod, low disorder |
| **Oligo-phenylene-vinylene (OPV)** | OPV-3 | 0.1 – 0.3 | Excellent π-conjugation |
| **Alkane (saturated)** | Octanedithiol HS-(CH₂)₈-SH | 0.8 – 1.0 | Through-bond tunneling |
| **DNA duplex** | Poly(dA)-poly(dT) | 0.1 – 1.0 (debated) | Coherent vs hopping |
| **Carbon nanotube (CNT)** | (5,5) armchair SWCNT | Effectively 0 | Ballistic / sub-band transport |

## 2.5 The Landauer–Büttiker Framework (KTU High-Priority)

For a single-molecule junction at low bias and low temperature, the conductance is:

$$
G = G_0 \, T(E_F)
$$

where $T(E_F)$ is the **transmission probability** at the electrode Fermi level evaluated in equilibrium. For a one-dimensional rectangular tunnel barrier of height $\phi$ and width $a$:

$$
T(E) \approx \exp(-2\kappa a), \quad \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(\phi - E)}}{\hbar}
$$

For a molecular wire, we identify $a \equiv L$ (molecular length) and combine with $G_0$:

$$
G = G_0 \, e^{-\beta L}, \quad \text{where } \beta = 2\kappa
$$

This is the **fundamental transport law** of molecular electronics.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DNA-templated circuits** for bio-sensing (detection of single base-pair mismatches via β-shift).
- **Molecular interconnects** in post-CMOS logic (Intel, IBM research roadmaps 2017–2024).
- **Single-molecule OLEDs** using oligophenylene emitters.
- **Molecular-scale rectifiers** for RFID antenna rectification at 13.56 MHz.
- **Wearable/flexible electronics** — SAM-based junctions are mechanically compliant.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conductance Decay Law $G = G_0 e^{-\beta L}$

**Goal:** Starting from the time-independent Schrödinger equation for an electron crossing a 1-D rectangular potential barrier, derive the length-dependent conductance of a molecular wire.

### Step 1 — Set up the potential landscape

Consider a free electron of mass $m$ and energy $E$ incident on a 1-D rectangular barrier of height $\phi > E$ and width $L$, representing the HOMO–LUMO gap of a molecular wire sandwiched between two metallic electrodes.

$$
V(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \phi, & 0 \le x \le L \\ 0, & x > L \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve the time-independent Schrödinger equation

Inside the barrier ($0 \le x \le L$), the Schrōdinger equation is:

$$
-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m} \frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + \phi \,\psi = E \, \psi
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2m(\phi - E)}{\hbar^2} \psi
$$

Define the **decay constant** inside the barrier:

$$
\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(\phi - E)}}{\hbar}
$$

So the equation becomes:

$$
\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = \kappa^2 \psi
$$

### Step 3 — General solution inside the barrier

The general solution is a linear combination of growing and decaying exponentials:

$$
\psi(x) = A \, e^{\kappa x} + B \, e^{-\kappa x}
$$

For a thick barrier ($L$ large), the growing exponential $A e^{\kappa x}$ cannot be matched to an outgoing wave on the right. We therefore set $A \approx 0$, leaving:

$$
\psi(x) \approx B \, e^{-\kappa x}, \quad 0 \le x \le L
$$

### Step 4 — Transmission amplitude at the exit

The wavefunction at the right boundary $x = L$ is:

$$
\psi(L) = B \, e^{-\kappa L}
$$

The probability current is proportional to $\vert \psi \vert^2$, so the **transmission probability** scales as:

$$
T(E) \propto \vert \psi(L) \vert^2 \propto e^{-2\kappa L}
$$

In the **opaque-barrier limit** ($2\kappa L \gg 1$), the prefactor is approximately unity and:

$$
T(E) \approx e^{-2\kappa L}
$$

### Step 5 — Identify the molecular decay constant

For a molecular wire, define the **empirical tunneling attenuation factor**:

$$
\beta \equiv 2\kappa
$$

Hence:

$$
T(E_F) = e^{-\beta L}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Landauer formula

The zero-bias, low-temperature conductance of a single-channel junction is:

$$
G = G_0 \, T(E_F) = G_0 \, e^{-\beta L}
$$

with:

$$
G_0 = \frac{2e^2}{h} \approx 7.748 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{S} = (12.9 \ \text{k}\Omega)^{-1}
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{\,G(L) = \frac{2e^2}{h} \exp(-\beta L) = (12.9 \ \text{k}\Omega)^{-1} \, e^{-\beta L}\,}
$$

### Step 7 — Total resistance in a real junction

The contact resistance $R_c$ at each metal–molecule interface is non-zero. The total resistance is:

$$
R_{total} = R_c + \frac{h}{2e^2} e^{\beta L} = R_c + \frac{e^{\beta L}}{G_0}
$$

For a typical Au–thiol contact, $R_c \approx 10$–$100 \ \text{k}\Omega$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A self-assembled monolayer of 1,4-benzenedithiol is measured in an STM break-junction at 4.2 K. The molecular length is **L = 0.7 nm**, and the tunneling decay constant is **β = 0.41 Å⁻¹**. The contact resistance is **R_c = 50 kΩ**. Calculate the conductance and the total resistance.

**Solution:**

$$
L = 0.7 \ \text{nm} = 7 \ \text{Å}
$$

$$
\beta L = 0.41 \ \text{Å}^{-1} \times 7 \ \text{Å} = 2.87
$$

$$
G = G_0 \, e^{-\beta L} = (12.9 \ \text{k}\Omega)^{-1} \times e^{-2.87}
$$

$$
e^{-2.87} \approx 0.0568
$$

$$
G \approx \frac{0.0568}{12.9 \ \text{k}\Omega} = \frac{1}{227.1 \ \text{k}\Omega} \approx 4.40 \ \mu\text{S}
$$

$$
R_{molecular} = \frac{1}{G} \approx 227.1 \ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{total} = R_c + R_{molecular} = 50 + 227.1 = 277.1 \ \text{k}\Omega
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final numerical answer:** $G \approx 4.40 \ \mu\text{S}$ and $R_{total} \approx 277 \ \text{k}\Omega$. This is consistent with experimentally reported values for benzene-1,4-dithiol (BBDT) junctions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Aviram–Ratner Molecular Rectifier

**Goal:** Show that a D-σ-A molecule (Donor–σ-bridge–Acceptor) is intrinsically asymmetric in its I–V characteristic.

### Step 1 — Energy diagram of a D-σ-A molecule

In the Aviram–Ratner model, the molecule has the form:
$$
D - (\text{CH}_2)_n - A
$$
where $D$ is an electron-rich donor (e.g., tetrathiafulvalene) and $A$ is an electron-deficient acceptor (e.g., tetracyanoquinodimethane, TCNQ). The σ-bridge is a saturated alkyl chain that electronically decouples D from A.

The equilibrium energy diagram is:

- $E_{HOMO}^D$ lies **above** $E_F$ of the electrode.
- $E_{LUMO}^A$ lies **below** $E_F$ of the electrode.
- $E_{HOMO}^D - E_{LUMO}^A$ defines the **intra-molecular gap**.

### Step 2 — Apply forward bias

At **forward bias** (anode on the D side, cathode on the A side):
- The energy levels of $A$ shift **down** relative to those of $D$.
- The HOMO of $D$ aligns with the LUMO of $A$ — providing a **resonant tunneling channel**.
- Electron flow: anode → HOMO(D) → LUMO(A) → cathode. **Current flows easily.**

### Step 3 — Apply reverse bias

At **reverse bias** (anode on the A side, cathode on the D side):
- The energy levels of $D$ shift **down** relative to $A$.
- The HOMO of $D$ is now **far below** the LUMO of $A$.
- No resonant alignment is available — electrons must traverse a wider barrier.
- **Current is suppressed.**

### Step 4 — Resulting I–V curve

The asymmetry yields a diode-like characteristic:

$$
I(V) = I_{sat} \left( e^{eV / \eta k_B T} - 1 \right) \quad \text{(forward)}
$$

$$
I(V) = -I_{sat}' \left( e^{-eV / \eta k_B T} - 1 \right) \quad \text{(reverse)}, \quad I_{sat}' \ll I_{sat}
$$

The rectification ratio is:

$$
R_{rect}(V) = \frac{\vert I(+V) \vert}{\vert I(-V) \vert} \gg 1
$$

Experimentally, $R_{rect} \approx 2$ to $10$ is reported for Aviram–Ratner-type rectifiers at room temperature.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Wire vs. Rectifier vs. Switch

| Property | Molecular Wire | Molecular Rectifier | Molecular Switch |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Function** | Passive conductor | Asymmetric I–V | Bistable conductance |
| **Mechanism** | Coherent tunneling | Resonant vs. off-resonant | Redox / conformational |
| **Governing parameter** | $\beta$ | $\phi_D - \phi_A$ | Switching ratio $G_{on}/G_{off}$ |
| **Typical $G$** | $10^{-8}$–$10^{-4}$ S | $10^{-8}$–$10^{-6}$ S | $10^{-10}$ (off) – $10^{-6}$ S (on) |
| **Geometry** | Linear, symmetric | D-σ-A asymmetric | Rotaxane / catenane |
| **Key equation** | $G = G_0 e^{-\beta L}$ | $R_{rect} = \vert I_{+V}/I_{-V} \vert$ | $G_{on}/G_{off} \gg 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Single-Molecule Junction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Electrode Au Left] --> B[Contact Thiol S Anchor]
    B --> C[Molecular Wire Conjugated Pi System]
    C --> D[Contact Thiol S Anchor]
    D --> E[Drain Electrode Au Right]
    E --> F[External Measurement Ammeter]
    F --> A

    G[Gate Electrode Optional FET] -.-> C

    H[Bias Voltage Vsd] --> A
```

**Description:** Two Au electrodes are bridged by a thiol-anchored conjugated molecule. The bias $V_{SD}$ drives current $I_{SD}$ through the wire. For three-terminal molecular transistors, a gate electrode tunes the molecular energy levels.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Charge-Transport Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Electron Enters Molecular Junction] --> Q1{Is Temperature High Enough for Activation}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is Molecular Length Less Than 3 nm}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Coherent Tunneling]
    R1 --> R1E[Equation G equals G0 exp beta L]
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Off Resonant Tunneling Weakly Coupled]
    R1 -- No Wait --> RR1[Coherent Tunneling]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Is Molecule Longer Than 3 nm}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Incoherent Hopping]
    R3 --> R3E[Equation G scales as 1 over L]
    Q3 -- No --> R4[Thermally Assisted Tunneling]
```

**Description:** The decision tree maps the operational regime of a molecular wire based on length and temperature. KTU questions frequently ask: *"Given a 5 nm oligophenylene at 300 K, which regime dominates?"* The answer: **incoherent hopping** because $L > 3$ nm at room temperature.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Aviram–Ratner Rectifier Energy Diagram (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Forward_Bias
        A1[Anode Au] --> A2[Donor HOMO Level]
        A2 --> A3[Acceptor LUMO Level]
        A3 --> A4[Cathode Au]
    end

    subgraph Reverse_Bias
        B1[Anode Au] --> B2[Acceptor LUMO Level]
        B2 --> B3[Donor HOMO Level]
        B3 --> B4[Cathode Au]
    end

    Forward_Bias -- Current Flows Easily --> Result1[High Forward Current]
    Reverse_Bias -- Resonant Alignment Lost --> Result2[Low Reverse Current]
    Result1 --> Rect[Rectification Ratio Greater Than 1]
    Result2 --> Rect
```

**Description:** Functional block diagram illustrating the asymmetry of the D-σ-A molecule under forward and reverse bias. The forward branch provides a resonant tunneling path, while the reverse branch blocks current.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — Molecular Circuit: Wire + Rectifier + Switch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Signal Molecular Wire 1] --> R[Rectifier D Sigma A]
    R --> S[Switch Bistable Rotaxane]
    S --> W2[Output Wire Molecular Wire 2]
    W2 --> OUT[Output Electrode Au]

    GATE[External Stimulus Light or Voltage] -.-> S
```

**Description:** A complete molecular signal-processing chain: input wire → rectifier (asymmetric element) → switch (gated element) → output wire. This is the **block-level functional architecture** of a hypothetical molecular circuit that performs rectification with on/off control.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Pattern]

> **Define a molecular wire. List any four families of molecular wires with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A molecular wire is a single molecule or molecular assembly that acts as a one-dimensional conductive channel for transporting charge carriers between two electrodes in a molecular electronic device, characterized by its conductance $G = G_0 e^{-\beta L}$.

Four families are:
1. **Oligophenylenes** — example: $p$-quaterphenyl (4 phenyl rings).
2. **Oligothiophenes** — example: $\alpha$-sexithiophene (6T).
3. **Oligo(phenylene ethynylenes) (OPE)** — example: OPE-3 with three phenyl-ethynylene units.
4. **Alkanedithiols (saturated)** — example: HS-(CH₂)₈-SH (octanedithiol).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]

> **State the Aviram–Ratner model for a molecular rectifier. What is the rectification ratio?**

**Model Answer:**

The Aviram–Ratner model proposes a **D-σ-A** (Donor–sigma-bridge–Acceptor) molecular architecture that mimics a $p$–$n$ junction diode. The donor HOMO lies above the electrode Fermi level, and the acceptor LUMO lies below it, with the saturated σ-bridge electronically decoupling the two. Under forward bias, the donor HOMO aligns with the acceptor LUMO, allowing resonant electron transfer. Under reverse bias, this alignment is lost and current is suppressed.

The **rectification ratio** is:

$$
R_{rect} = \frac{\vert I(+V) \vert}{\vert I(-V) \vert}
$$

A value $R_{rect} > 1$ indicates asymmetric (rectifying) behavior.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> **(a)** Derive the length-dependent conductance equation $G = G_0 e^{-\beta L}$ for a molecular wire operating in the coherent tunneling regime. Identify each term and discuss the physical meaning of the decay constant $\beta$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A self-assembled monolayer of oligophenylene wires has the following measured conductances:
> - $L = 1.0$ nm, $G = 1.10 \times 10^{-6}$ S
> - $L = 1.5$ nm, $G = 1.20 \times 10^{-7}$ S
>
> Calculate the tunneling decay constant $\beta$ in Å⁻¹ and the contact conductance $G_0$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Starting from the 1-D Schrōdinger equation for an electron of energy $E$ incident on a rectangular barrier of height $\phi > E$ and width $L$:

$$
-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m} \frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + (\phi - E)\psi = 0
$$

Define $\kappa = \sqrt{2m(\phi - E)}/\hbar$. In the opaque-barrier limit, the transmission probability is:

$$
T(E) \approx e^{-2\kappa L}
$$

The Landauer zero-bias conductance is:

$$
G = G_0 \, T(E_F) = G_0 \, e^{-\beta L}, \quad G_0 = \frac{2e^2}{h}
$$

Here, $\beta = 2\kappa$ is the **tunneling attenuation factor** (units of length⁻¹). Physically, $\beta$ characterizes how strongly the molecular backbone screens the electron wavefunction; a smaller $\beta$ implies better π-conjugation and longer effective conjugation length. Typical values range from **0.1 Å⁻¹** (highly delocalized oligothiophenes) to **1.0 Å⁻¹** (saturated alkanes).

**Model Solution (b):**

Taking the ratio of conductances at two lengths:

$$
\frac{G_1}{G_2} = \frac{e^{-\beta L_1}}{e^{-\beta L_2}} = e^{\beta (L_2 - L_1)}
$$

Substituting values (with $L_1 = 10$ Å and $L_2 = 15$ Å):

$$
\frac{1.10 \times 10^{-6}}{1.20 \times 10^{-7}} = e^{\beta (15 - 10)}
$$

$$
9.167 = e^{5\beta}
$$

Taking natural log:

$$
\ln(9.167) = 5\beta
$$

$$
2.216 = 5\beta \Rightarrow \beta = 0.443 \ \text{Å}^{-1}
$$

Now compute $G_0$ using either data point. Using $L_1 = 10$ Å:

$$
G_0 = G_1 \, e^{\beta L_1} = (1.10 \times 10^{-6}) \times e^{0.443 \times 10}
$$

$$
e^{4.43} \approx 84.0
$$

$$
G_0 \approx 1.10 \times 10^{-6} \times 84.0 \approx 9.24 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{S}
$$

This is reasonably close to the universal quantum conductance $G_0 = 7.748 \times 10^{-5}$ S, confirming the validity of the model.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert nm → Å. **β must be in Å⁻¹ or nm⁻¹** with consistent units of $L$.
> 2. Using $G_0 = e^2/h$ (single spin channel) instead of $2e^2/h$ (spin-degenerate).
> 3. Sign error in $\ln(G_1/G_2) = \beta(L_2 - L_1)$.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> **(a)** With a neat energy-band diagram, explain the operation of an Aviram–Ratner molecular rectifier. Derive an expression for the rectification ratio. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the operation of a **molecular switch** based on a [2]rotaxane. Discuss the role of external stimuli (light, redox potential, pH) in switching. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

In the Aviram–Ratner D-σ-A rectifier, the energy levels are arranged as:

- $E_{HOMO}^{Donor} > E_F$ of electrode
- $E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor} < E_F$ of electrode
- The σ-bridge presents a tunneling barrier between donor and acceptor.

**Under forward bias:** $E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}$ shifts down and aligns with $E_{HOMO}^{Donor}$. Electrons flow: anode → $E_{HOMO}^{Donor}$ → $E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}$ → cathode. Current is large.

**Under reverse bias:** $E_{HOMO}^{Donor}$ shifts down and away from $E_{LUMO}^{Acceptor}$. No resonant channel exists. Current is small.

The rectification ratio is:

$$
R_{rect} = \frac{\vert I(+V) \vert}{\vert I(-V) \vert} = \frac{I_{forward}}{I_{reverse}}
$$

For an ideal diode:

$$
I(V) = I_{sat}\left( e^{eV/\eta k_B T} - 1 \right)
$$

$$
R_{rect}(V) = \frac{e^{eV/\eta k_B T} - 1}{e^{-eV/\eta k_B T} - 1} \approx e^{2eV/\eta k_B T} \quad \text{for } eV \gg \eta k_B T
$$

**Model Solution (b):**

A [2]rotaxane consists of:
- A **dumbbell-shaped axle** with two recognition sites (e.g., a benzidine and a bipyridinium station).
- A **macrocyclic ring** (e.g., a crown ether) that can shuttle between the two stations.

In the **ground state**, the ring encircles the thermodynamically preferred station. Upon application of an external stimulus:
- **Redox stimulus:** Electrochemical oxidation of the preferred station moves the ring to the second station, changing the molecular dipole and conductance.
- **Photochemical stimulus:** UV light induces a cis–trans isomerization in an azobenzene station, switching the ring position.
- **pH stimulus:** Protonation/deprotonation changes the binding affinity of the stations.

The two states correspond to two distinct conductance values:

$$
G_{on} \quad \text{and} \quad G_{off}, \quad \text{with switching ratio} \quad \frac{G_{on}}{G_{off}} \approx 10 - 1000
$$

This bistability is the foundation of **molecular memory** and **molecular logic gates**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often confuse rotaxanes with catenanes. **Rotaxane** = ring on a dumbbell (mechanical bond, no covalent ring–axle link). **Catenane** = two interlocked rings.
> 2. The rectification ratio must be **dimensionless** and **positive**. Sign errors in the diode equation are common.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Molecular wire** = conjugated molecule that transports electrons via **coherent tunneling**.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $G = G_0 e^{-\beta L}$ with $G_0 = 2e^2/h \approx 12.9 \ \text{k}\Omega^{-1}$.
- [ ] **$\beta$ is a *molecular* property**, not an electrode property. Smaller $\beta$ → better wire.
- [ ] **β ranges:** 0.1–0.2 Å⁻¹ (oligothiophenes), 0.3–0.5 Å⁻¹ (oligophenylenes), 0.8–1.0 Å⁻¹ (alkanes).
- [ ] **Three transport regimes:** coherent tunneling ($L < 3$ nm, low $T$), incoherent hopping ($L > 3$ nm, high $T$), Coulomb blockade (single-electron, ultra-low $T$).
- [ ] **Landauer formula:** $G = G_0 T(E_F)$. Single-channel, spin-degenerate.
- [ ] **Aviram–Ratner rectifier** = D-σ-A architecture. Resonant alignment at forward bias, off-resonant at reverse bias.
- [ ] **Rectification ratio** $R_{rect} = \vert I(+V)/I(-V) \vert$.
- [ ] **Molecular switch** = bistable conductance element (rotaxane, catenane, azobenzene).
- [ ] **Switching ratio** $G_{on}/G_{off} \gg 1$ defines memory/latch operation.
- [ ] **Self-assembled monolayer (SAM)** is the standard fabrication platform for molecular junctions.
- [ ] **Energy alignment** is critical: $E_{HOMO}$ and $E_{LUMO}$ vs. electrode $E_F$ determines injection barrier.
- [ ] **HOMO–LUMO gap** can be measured optically: $E_g = hc/\lambda_{abs}$.
- [ ] **Single-molecule measurement techniques:** STM break junction, MCBJ (mechanically controllable break junction), conducting-AFM, graphene–molecule–graphene junctions.
- [ ] **Total resistance:** $R_{total} = R_c + e^{\beta L}/G_0$. Contact resistance is non-negligible.
- [ ] **Hopping regime:** $G \propto 1/L$ (linear), not exponential — this is a *signature* of long-wire behavior.
- [ ] **CNT and DNA wires** are special cases; CNTs are effectively ballistic ($\beta \to 0$).
- [ ] **Major exam-relevant molecules:** 1,4-benzenedithiol (BBDT), oligophenylene, oligothiophene, OPE, TTF-σ-TCNQ (rectifier), [2]rotaxane (switch).
- [ ] **Practical figures:** $G \sim 10^{-8}$–$10^{-4}$ S; $R_{rect} \sim 2$–$10$; switching ratio $\sim 10$–$10^3$.
- [ ] **Always convert units** — $L$ in Å when $\beta$ is in Å⁻¹.
